column2: REDOC - Digital Tutor to make easier learning the STEM subject
column4: Redooc is a revolutionizing website trains your brain in math and sciences, like a gym does with your muscles.  Redooc, through a successful use of innovative gaming and technology, stimulates an health dose of competition among kids of different age ranges.  Redooc has opened a platform entirely dedicated to the school, where you can find exercises, video lessons, tips and simulations useful for both teachers and students. Redooc provides support to professors with video lessons and interactive exercises explained and organised as a great game online.

column2: SAMPA - Service of registration and promotion of the adherence to medications in elderly patients
column4: Medication non-adherence among elderly and chronic patients leads to poorer health outcomes and higher healthcare costs. At the University Hospital Miguel Servet (HUMS) in Zaragoza, outpatient pharmacy processes were fragmented, manual, and prone to dispensing and coordination errors. The SAMPA project was implemented to improve treatment adherence, streamline medication management, and enhance patient experience. Stakeholders include hospital pharmacists, clinicians, outpatient staff, and elderly patients. The solution involved a comprehensive digital transformation of the outpatient pharmacy unit. Core components include an integrated electronic prescription platform, automated storage and dispensing robots, full traceability across prescribing and dispensing stages, appointment scheduling, and adherence monitoring systems. These components are interconnected with hospital information systems, enabling end-to-end visibility of medication flows and patient interactions. Supporting tools such as a patient app, improved web services, and dedicated communication points facilitate engagement and follow-up. The project has been implemented at HUMS and has reduced dispensing errors, improved workflow efficiency, and enhanced accessibility for patients. SAMPA demonstrates how integrated digital systems can deliver measurable operational and quality improvements in hospital outpatient care.

column2: Smart Dementia project
column4: The eHealth technology (a wristwatch and app with which the whereabouts of the patients can be traced) is used to provide elderly people with Alzheimer more freedom. The technology enables the nurses and the institution to trace the elderly better, thus giving them more freedom to move around. The technology fits in a policy of the organisation to work with life cycles. This means that a team decides how much freedom a specific patient can have. The aim is to grant as much freedom as possible, given the patient health and mental situation. If people can move around more, they stay fit longer both physically and mentally, the organisation argues. The organisation also emphasizes that the ICT tool is above all a supportive tool and that the most important element still is the (risk) assessment of the patient. The eHealth innovation was an example of how such technologies can support health professionals, as personal health data is collected, stored and communicated between relevant stakeholders. In general, in health technology, patient data is used to provide more precise and personalized health care (e.g. personalized interventions, predicating and preventing diseases). In this specific case of health technology, a mobile device, mobile sensor, and wearables were used to increase the health and wellbeing of users. This device with sensor technology helps Alzheimer patients to walk around more freely again in a nursing home and outside the nursing home through GPS tracking, which would otherwise be impossible for those suffering from dementia. In that sense, the ICT technology is important and vital for the innovation.

column2: Track AI
column4: The objective of the Track AI project is to incorporate artificial intelligence (AI) into the DIVE medical device so it can “estimate the probability of the patient having a certain pathology”. The innovation was created due to the difficulty in exploring the visual function (e.g. vision) among children, but it can be used for both collaborative and non-collaborative patients (i.e. patients who are able to collaborate with physicians as opposed to patients – such as children – who are unable to collaborate with physicians). The partnership allows technology to be shared between organisations and combines their experience in the health and technological fields. The innovation in this project consists of the development of a specific app that uses AI in a Huawei P30 mobile phone in order to analyse data from visual explorations using the DIVE-Medical device. This innovation helps physicians to determine whether or not a patient has a visual impairment by comparing patient gaze patterns with the database generated by previous diagnoses (which can be accessed online and offline). In some cases, it also makes it possible to estimate the probability of a particular pathology. The project is focused on non-collaborative patients, but it can also be used in collaborative patients (i.e. patients that are able to collaborate with physicians as opposed to patients – such as children – who are unable to collaborate with physicians).

column2: MasterMind - Basque Country
column4: The objective of the MasterMind project was “to make high quality treatment for depression more widely available for adults suffering from the illness by the use of ICT” (http://mastermind-project.eu). Collaboration by 23 partners framed in a European H2020 project was created in order to accomplish this objective. The innovation in this project was a Computerised Cognitive Behaviour Therapy (CCBT) for patients with mild and moderate depression. It allows people with these illnesses to receive distance therapy, involving fewer face-to-face appointments with health professionals. The main component of the innovation is a web application “Overcome your depression”, which can be used through a computer, tablet or smartphone. By using this application, patients can receive therapy in six modules in the form of text, videos, images, graphs, etc. The application makes patients perform tests, which then serve to communicate the evolution of their illness to the health professionals. The health professionals check these tests periodically and receive an alarm if a patient’s results are a cause for concern.

column2: Trentino Salute 4.0: TREC+
column4: TreC + consolidates and puts into service the trials that have been tested in recent years in Trentino together with citizens and health professionals. Access to online health services is guaranteed by a single platform and a single app in which a constant exchange of data produced by patients and doctors flows and in which the level of care that is offered to citizens remains unchanged, constant and Safe. It integrates the innovative vision for the Artificial Intelligence of the future with the collaboration wiith FBK research center. The design and experimenting of the part of TreC + that wants to become increasingly intelligent and integrated with applications such as virtual coaches, predictive models, chatbots, etc., which use artificial intelligence has been made with FBK research center. The TreC + ecosystem is based on Open Source technologies and thanks to an open architectural model it is possible to connect proprietary tools and solutions while keeping the health data at the center of the system, rigorously kept in the APSS archives and under public control, so as to guarantee the citizen security, protection and full availability of data concerning him. Thanks to the system specifications, the solution developed by APSS is now reusable in other healthcare contexts (regional / provincial / ASL and hospitals). It can be integrated with the management systems in use at other regional healthcare companies (CUP, FSE, Payments, existing telemonitoring systems), thus activating a television service extended to the entire  population.

column2: KEYCRIME: Crime Analysis for the next generation Predictive Policing
column4: With Keycrime, the software created by a policeman from the Milan police headquarters to analyze thefts and robberies committed in the city. Recently this has been trasformed in a new company with the same name and the software named Delia. Delia is a software from the KeyCrime company that supports police officers in crime prevention and enforcement operations. By analyzing thousands of data, the aim is to compare different crimes to determine which ones were committed by the same person or by the same group of people. Software has been tested in production in Italy by the State Police in Milan, to combat the phenomenon of robberies.
column8: Polizia di Stato - Questura di Milano

column2: Seamless Flow: facial recognition of airport passengers
column4:
Seamless FloW is a technology produced by the Portuguese company Vision Box used at many airports – including Fiumicino, Malpensa and Linate. The aim is the management of passengers' digital identities and automatic recognition through a biometric identification (facial recognition).
The recognition takes place through IoT tools that acquire the image of the passenger's face together with the passport and ticket data. These data are then correlated with each other and kept for the entire stay of the passenger at the airport, who will no longer have to show documents.
The system makes use of dedicated access gates, with cameras and facial recognition software to identify passengers in this way.

column2: Shareart: measure the public's satisfaction in front of the works of art exhibited in museum
column4: ShareArt is the monitoring system through which it would be possible to measure the public's satisfaction in front of the works of art exhibited in museums, tested by the Sustainability Department of Production and Territorial Systems of ENEA in collaboration with the Bologna Museums Institution. This software works through a camera that automatically detects the faces that look in the direction of the work and allows the system to acquire data concerning people and information related to their behavior during the observation of the work of art. Through the use of Big Data and convolutional neural networks, the system is able to correlate the data referable to the fruition of the works with those relating to the characteristics of people to infer useful information.

column2: Respondus:  assessment software for remote management of students (university)
column4: Respondus is a proctoring software. Proctoring is a tool that allows you to control the student's device at exams or questions and to acquire data to decree whether that is copying or not. During use, proctoring allows you to control the student's device and acquire a whole set of data that allow the algorithm to decree whether the child is copying or not. Respondus captures images of the face and videos of test students, identifying and flagging moments when unusual and/or suspicious behavior is revealed by recording video and snapshots taken at regular intervals to track abnormal behavior.
column8: Multiple Italian Universities (Cattolica, Bocconi, Roma Tre etc.)

column2: SARI (Automatic Image Recognition System)
column4: SARI (Automatic Image Recognition System) is a facial recognition software, in use at the Ministry of the Interior and Central Anti-Crime Directorate of the State Police since 2017. The software is produced and supplied to the Ministry by the company Parsec 3.26 S.r.l. The SARI system consists of two components: SARI Enterprise and SARI Real-Time. The first component aims to facilitate the search and identification of people, starting from images of the face already in possession of the Police database. The second is a facial recognition software, which thanks to the use of cameras can recognize people on a watch-list in real time.

column2: EU-JAV European Joint Action on Vaccination
column4:
The vaccine conversation monitoring platform was, in this initial phase of the project, developed in three languages: Italian, French and Spanish. The platform, accessible via credentials, monitors information flows from Twitter, Reddit, Wikipedia and Google Trends in real time. The platform allows the collection, analysis, aggregation and display of data extracted from different sources. This is possible thanks to the construction of the platform on three different levels (layers): the Data layer, the Analysis layer and the Visualization layer. The Data layer constantly collects raw data from the web and social media, pre-processes them through an initial screening and an initial cleaning of the raw data, obtained through the relative Application Programmer Interface, API: Twitter API, Reddit API, Google Trends API. To collect the data, keyword filters were used, created and validated through a structured framework, on the basis of recurrence and relevance criteria (6). The Analysis layer has the function of analyzing the processed and cleaned data through the generation of graphs, thus making the extracted data understandable and relating them to each other. The processes that allow the geolocalization of tweets take place on this layer, and two processes based on machine learning algorithms: Influencer analysis and Early-signal detection: Influencer analysis makes it possible to identify the most influential and most active users in the conversation about vaccines on Twitter, with the aim of monitoring the crucial "nodes" of the communities involved, both in vaccine-friendly communities and in the no vax community; Early-signal detection allows the interception of users who use new hashtags, therefore users new to the topic of vaccines. The hashtag (created by inserting the # key in front of the desired word or phrase, for example, #vaccini or #iononmivaccino) is a tool that serves as a thematic aggregator on some social networks and was introduced by Twitter as a keyword signal and to draw users' attention to some particular words or themes.
Finally, the Visualization layer allows the visualization of the results, makes the extrapolated information understandable and communicates it in a simple but representative way, in order to understand the displayed data more easily.
column8: ISS- ITALY Istituto Superiore di Sanità

column2: CLIP (Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training) for the Italian Language
column4: In this project propose the first CLIP model trained on Italian data, that in this context can be considered a low resource language. Using a few techniques, we have been able to fine-tune a SOTA Italian CLIP model with only 1.4 million training samples. Italian CLIP model is built upon the pre-trained Italian BERT model provided by dbmdz and the OpenAI vision transformer.

column2: iBorderCtrl: Virtual Policeman
column4: iBorderCtrl (Intelligent Portable Control System) is an innovative project that aims to enable faster and thorough border control for third country nationals crossing the land borders of EU Member States (MS), with technologies that adopt the future development of the Schengen Border Management. iBorderCtrl includes software and hardware technologies ranging from portable readers and scanners, various emerging and novel subsystems for automatic controls, highly reliable wireless networking for mobile controls, and secure backend storage and processing. One of the main goals of the project is to design and implement a comprehensive system that adopts mobility concepts and consists of a two-stage-procedure, designed to reduce cost and time spent per traveller at the border crossing station. iBorderCtrl also focuses on the land border crossing points: road, walkway, train stations. It addresses the better facilitation of thorough checking required for third country nationals that intend to cross EU borders.

column2: NUMEL.AI - A communication tool for neurological disorders
column4:
Numel AI-based viewer creates an immersive Augmented Reality experience for doctors and patients. Patients have a better view of their condition by looking at themselves with a special lens. It allows to explore the disorder and how it has evolved during time by directly using medical imaging and other patient data (load up MR images directly from your healthcare system CD)
It use patient medical imaging data to automatically generate their 3D model and display it on their body in real-time with AR. Doctors and patients can use it directly or register and save data in the cloud for a more convenient exploration 
Keywords:  Medical Imaging Import, Body Tracking, Augmented Reality 3D Rendering, Artificial Intelligence for Disorder Identification, Scalable API-Based Cloud Platform, Easily Extensible

column2: AI Robort Judge for small claims in Estonia
column4: The project want to implement a “robot judge” that could adjudicate small claims disputes of less than €7,000 (about $8,000). Officials hope the system can clear a backlog of cases for judges and court clerks. The project is in its early phases and a pilot started in 2019 focusing on contract disputes. In concept, the two parties will upload documents and other relevant information, and the AI will issue a decision that can be appealed to a human judge. Many details are still to be worked out. Velsberg says the system might have to be adjusted after feedback from lawyers and judges.

column2: MIREL - Mining and reasoning with legal text
column4: The MIREL project will create an international and inter-sectorial network to define a formal framework and to develop tools for MIning and REasoning with Legal texts, with the aim of translating these legal texts into formal representations that can be used for querying norms, compliance checking, and decision support. MIREL addresses both conceptual challenges, such as the role of legal interpretation in mining and reasoning, and computational challenges, such as the handling of big legal data, and the complexity of regulatory compliance. It bridges the gap between the community working on legal ontologies and NLP parsers and the community working on reasoning methods and formal logic. Moreover, it is the first project of its kind to involve industrial partners in the future development of innovative products and services in legal reasoning and their deployment in the market. MIREL promotes mobility and staff exchange between SMEs to academies in order to create an inter-continental interdisciplinary consortium in Law and Artificial Intelligence areas including Natural Language Processing, Computational Ontologies, Argumentation, and Logic & Reasoning.

column2: Predictive maintenance for wind farms
column4: A service to minimize the damage suffered in the company by the downtime of a windpower machine and better manage the efficiency of the production process. This purpose is achieved thanks to software system for predictive maintenance based on machine learning algorithms. The benefit by planning interventions and developing targeted solutions; which on the one hand reduce costs and downtime, on the other have a positive impact on the quality of the production system.

column2: Lombardia Chatbot Coronavirus
column4: This solution was an automatic info-chatbot. It allowed you to access a guided path which, based on the selected symptoms, lead to the choice of the most correct actions to take, based on the guidelines of the Ministry of Health and of the international bodies responsible for managing the emergency. The tool was available by the Regional Company for Innovation and Purchasing (ARIA SpA). The service immediately aroused great interest, with very high access numbers.

column2: Sentilo BCN - Plataforma de Sensors i Actuadors de Barcelona
column4: The Barcelona City Council, through the Municipal Institute of Informatics (IMI), started in November 2012 a project conceived for define the strategy and the necessary actions  in order to achieve global positioning Barcelona as a reference in the field of Smart Cities. Sentilo is an open source sensor and actuator platform designed to fit in the Smart City architecture of any city who looks for openness and easy interoperability. It is built, used, and supported by an active and diverse community of cities and companies that believe that using open standards and free software is the first smart decision a Smart City should take. In order to avoid vertical solutions, Sentilo is designed as a cross platorm with the objective of sharing information between heterogeneous systems and to easily integrate legacy applications.

column2: SKRINJA 2.0 - Business Intelligence in the civil service (BI)
column4: The ICT department of the Ministry of Public Administration implemented a data warehouse and business intelligence system that is configured as a horizontal service on Government Computer Cloud  to promote data-driven decision making in public administration. The Ministry also handles the procurement activities of the country and therefore there is a prospect of utilising the system in the procurement procedures as well.

column2: ProZorro, Bid prediction
column4: The ProZorro team is using Artificial Intelligence and machine learning in its work with the aim of developing a model that would predict the likelihood of a tender happening and being succesful. Another tool is able to predict the probability that a particular tender participant will leave the platform before completing the tender.

column2: ProZorro, CPV Prediction
column4: Prozorro has developed an AI tool that can predict the correct CPV codes and unit of measurement. The tool has not yet been implemented. It aims to make the platforms that provide the user-interface for organisations using the Prozorro data easier to use, reducing mis-classifications and errors.

column2: DoZorro AI tool
column4: The monitoring portal DoZorro is a platform where each participant in the system (supplier, customer, controlling authority, citizen) can provide feedback to the government customer or supplier. The new version of the platform provides software, which through its AI capabilities, is able to identify tenders with a high risk of corruption. The results of the beta test of this system were that 26% more tenders with unfounded selection of the winner were identified, 37% more tenders with groundless disqualification, and 298% more with participants' conspiracy. The system is intended to complement and support the work done by experts - making their work more efficient.

column2: Kommers (KAI) semantics check tool
column4: An AI assistant is being integrated into the Primonas Kommers e-procurement platform. This tool can do a semantics check on requirements and other text in the procurement material which will decrease the risk of bidders misunderstanding the tender material and hence reduce the bidder risk. Kommers has public sector (as well as private sector) clients that will be able to access this functionality.

column2: State Treasury e-invoicing plans
column4: The State Treasury of Finland plans to automate e-invoice processing by utilising AI and robotics. It is believed that AI tools could analyse an invoice within seconds to find the necessary information such as an agreement number and amount. Currently, Finland has identified the most significant challenge to be that IT companies and other operators need to be aware of what data needs to be in the correct place to achieve ultimate automation goals.

column2: YPO, Procurement Information Provider
column4: YPO has introduced a chatbot - the Procurement Information Provider (PIP) to help public sector buyers navigate their way through YPO's services and discover the frameworks on offer. The chatbot can provide information from other areas of the website and direct users to relevant procurement teams.

column2: Dashboard to Explore state spending in Finland
column4: The "explore state spending" website allows citizens and companies to explore data on government spending. Users can search according to different types of procurement spending (e.g. ICT procurement, administrative services, machinery, equipment and transport equipment, etc). AI technology is used in order to categorise the procurement spending. Other forms of analysis using AI technologies are also done on the data for the benefit just of procurement authorities.

column2: eNest solution
column4: Under the new eNest solution, verification of information provided by suppliers against official records will be done automatically during the tendering process. The system will use the European Single Procurement Document as a standard, which will mean that service providers do not need to attach official records to tenders.

column2: Palkeet, RPA Project

Tasks that have been automated include purchase invoice validation and supplier register maintenance etc. This was the first major Robotic Process Automation (RPA) implementation in Finnish public sector. The project is part of a wider digitalisation initiative by the Finnish government.

column2: Ministry of Finance, mTender

column2: SUVE - Chatbot for questions about the emergency situation and the coronavirus
column4: The websites of several government agencies implemented Suve, an automated chatbot which provided answers to frequently asked questions about general services. The chatbot provided answers to questions in Estonian and English and uses information from the official sources of government agencies.

column2: KRATID - detecting traffic anomalies and incidents of the Estonian data exchange layer (X-Road)
column4: The Information System Authority of Estonia is using machine learning for detecting anomalies and incidents on the traffic of the Estonian data exchange layer - X-Road.  Predictive analytics is used to decide where to send the police for traffic regulation. The system is well used in cities; however the algorithm does not yet work well in rural areas.  The Estonian Unemployment Insurance Fund matches job seekers with open positions using AI. It helps job seekers get matched with the right job. The current job matching algorithm is developed further with job seeker profiling algorithm.  Estonian Agricultural Registers and Information Board is using machine learning to detect land mowing. Satellite images are analysed to detect whether agricultural land has been mowed. This is necessary as mowing land is one of the requirements for receiving government grants. Before satellite image analysis this work was carried out manually using on-spot control.  In line with the Digital Agenda 2020 for Estonia, the government encourages the use of AI applications and test projects. The aim is to have at least 50 use cases in public sector by 2020. Interestingly, Estonia has also launched a task force to collect ideas from the general public on how to best make use of AI for the development of Estonia's economy and state.

column2: COVID19AragonBot
column4: One of the contributions of the ITAINNOVA Technological Institute of Aragon in the pandemic was the launch of a response service called COVID19AragonBot, carried out by the Government of Aragon and by our center. It was accessed through the social network Telegram @ COVID19AragonBot. The idea arose in the face of the collapse of the citizen service telephones and, given that there was a lot of recurring questions, Chatbot guided it to more complicated and more specific question.

column2: Covid-bot - France

The Covid-bot allows respondents to assess their risk of COVID-19 contamination by filling a questionnaire.

column2: Automated parking control
column4: In Amsterdam, the number of cars allowed to park in the city is limited, keeping the city liveable and accessible. The municipality checks whether a parked car has the right to be parked, for example, because parking fees have been paid via a parking meter or app, or because the owner has a parking permit. Enforcement is done with the help of scan cars equpiped with cameras, automating the process of license plate identification and background checks with specific scanning equipment and AI-based identification service. The service is currently in use for more than 150,000 street parking spaces in the City of Amsterdam.

column2: Reporting issues in public space
column4: When someone encounters rubbish or a maintenance issue on the street or in a park, they can report this to the municipality via an online reporting system. A dangerous traffic situation or disturbance from people or cafes can also be reported. This system used to be a collection of drop-down menus, from which the user would pick the category that best suited their report. The department responsible for a certain category would then take care of the report. However, as the municipality is a complex organisation, there are countless categories. Many times the wrong category would be chosen, resulting in delays. Now, an algorithm recognizes certain keywords, for example, waste and sidewalk. From these keywords, it determines which category it belongs to, and ultimately, which department within the municipality should examine the case. As a result, there are fewer administrative steps for the person reporting on the issue. Also, the report can be processed much faster, because it arrives at the right department more quickly.

column2: Parking chatbot
column4: The parking chatbot is a customer service channel of city’s parking services. Service provides automated answers to the parking-related questions of city residents and visitors. The service is available at the city parking website of Helsinki.

column2: Health center chatbot
column4: The chatbot provides health and illness-related advice easily without queuing. Chatbot directs the users to the right digital health services and advises on questions related to dental, mental health, substance abuse and social services. The service answers the most frequently asked questions and direct to the sources of reliable information based on usersâ€™ needs. The chatbot is integrated with other digital services of health centres. Through it, customers may be directed to, for example, the cityâ€™s digital services, Omaolo, Omakanta or social counselling chat. The service enables transactions around the clock anonymously and securely. All health centers use the service in Helsinki, and it covers all services of the health centers. The clinical guidance of the service is based on national practices of evidence-based treatments, the Current Care guidelines and the emergency care evaluation criteria. The service utilises natural language processing based Artificial Intelligence to analyse the searches entered by the customer and to identify the intended service need, as well as to find the respective instructions. The service expands the accessibility of health center services by enabling remote transactions around the clock. The service enhances the customer experience of digital services for health centers by providing a single channel for all digital services. The service streamlines the service production of health centers by automating the service on the frequently asked questions and by the automated collection of background information. The service also has positive effects on harmonising the quality of the service and on improving the employee experience of counselling services.

column2: Oodi's book recommendation service Obotti

The service is aimed at all Central Library Oodis customers and can be downloaded as a mobile application for Android and IoS devices. The service recommends books to the customer based on contextual analysis that the customer would not otherwise find. The purpose of the service is to present the material available in Oodi library to customers interestingly and to make it easier for customers to find interesting and new material. The recommended materials are freely available in the selections of the Central Library Oodi at the time of the recommendation. The service is divided thematically into six chatbots, each recommending content according to their theme. You can ask the service for recommendations using ready-made answer options, or in free search terms in the form of text or speech. The service utilises contextual analysis of natural language to determine the customerâ€™s interests, as well as to produce corresponding book recommendations.

The technical basis and server hosting is financed and operated by the City of Bonn and GovBot. That's why no one has to worry or invest anything themselves. The developer environment is ready and will be further expanded in the coming months.

column2: REBUILD -  ICT toolbox providing support to both migrants and refugees
column4: REBUILD (ICT-enabled integration facilitator and life-rebuilding guidance) aims at designing and developing an ICT toolbox providing support to both migrants and refugees in Europe for accessing existing services, and local service providers interacting with migrants and refugees in enhancing their service provision. The technological ambition is to use AI-based solutions (user profiling, skills/needs matching, chatbots) for the development of a usable, accessible, smart ecosystem for both the target groups. column2: NADINE -  platform to promote migrant inclusion
column4: NADINE is a platform to promote migrant inclusion. It aims to harness the potential of big data and Artificial Intelligence to better understand the supply and demand of skills brought to Europe through migration. The NADINE platform aims to use open data to gain a better understanding of migrant realities and the challenges associated with their integration. Data about local services and migrants will feed user-friendly tools for employability and career guidance, whilst assessing supply and demand of migrants skills. The platform will also focus on the social, educational and cultural needs of migrants and how these fit to existing services offered by local hosts. Overall, the system will allow public authorities and guidance professionals to offer a personalised service to migrants based on real needs and the opportunities available to them. The NADINE system will be based around two key banks of a data repository of information about host societies and migrants from which a range of services will be produced. As users access the services, more data will be captured to enhance the existing banks and feed reliable information into the system.

column2: Housing Assistance Payment Robotics Pilot
column4: Limerick City and County Council operate the Housing Assistance Payment Shared Service Centre (HAP SSC) on behalf of the local government sector and are continuously striving for ways to improve their business. The organisation deals with more than 47,000 tenancies and 27,000 landlords, makes payments in excess of 400million, and collects more than 100million in rents. By 2021 it is anticipated that the HAP SSC will deal with 80,000 tenancies, 55,000 landlords and 600million in payments. In order to help deal with these volumes, the deployment of Robotic Process Automation (RPA) in the HAP Shared Service Centre was investigated. During 2018 a proof of concept pilot project was carried out by Agilisys, examining 3 micro processes within the centre. The proof of concept project demonstrated that opportunities for efficiencies and savings can be achieved through the deployment of RPA. Applying the outcome of the proof of concept project to a business case, it was concluded that if 10 complex processes could be replaced by RPA, then considerable savings could be made, while at the same time improving services to all key stakeholders. The HAP SSC is now seeking to identify existing processes where RPA may be immediately deployed, as well as opportunities for further business systems integration and automation within the HAP SSC.

column2: Artificial intelligence and video surveillance to control the distance at the airport
column4: COVID-19 restrictions severely limited in-person contact in hospitals, isolating patients from families and complicating communication between healthcare staff. Hospitals in Turin launched a pilot to explore how remote presence technologies could mitigate social isolation while maintaining safety protocols. Stakeholders included patients, relatives, healthcare professionals, hospital administrators, and telecom providers, with the objective of preserving human interaction and collaboration despite physical distancing requirements. From 2020 to 2021, the pilot deployed Double Robotics Double3 mobile robots connected via a 5G network. These remotely controlled robots enabled high-definition video communication and autonomous movement within hospital wards. Secure network connectivity ensured low latency and reliable audio-visual interaction, while simple user interfaces allowed staff and families to connect without specialised training. The robots integrated with hospital connectivity infrastructure and operated under strict data protection policies. The pilot improved patient well-being by restoring contact with families and supported collaboration among medical staff across wards. It reduced unnecessary physical movement within hospitals and demonstrated the value of high-bandwidth connectivity for healthcare environments. The solution proved reliable and scalable, highlighting potential long-term applications for telepresence in clinical care, training, and hospital operations beyond emergency contexts.

column2: Natural language processing for Land Registry documentation in Sweden
column4: The handwritten documents were of low quality and resolution which made analysis challenging. To fix this, the SLR carried out pre-processing work on the handwritten documents to improve the quality of the input data. The SLR then used handwritten text recognition (HTR) to extract information from the handwritten documents. The SLR then used a neural network to apply word corrections and associations to complete any sentences with words not captured by HTR. Once SLR had extracted the text, they used an AI model to highlight key features in the document, for example location, names and summary.

column2: How the Ministry of Justice used AI to compare prison reports
column4: The Ministry of Justice trained a neural network on the prison reports to track how people use specific words in prison contexts. The algorithm groups words with similar meanings to form an ‘intelligent search’ tool. New reports are automatically added to the tool’s library so the data remains up-to-date. This means staff can rapidly uncover information buried in the reports and identify trends.

column2: AI-for-Covid Predictive diagnostics made in Italy
column4: During the Covid-19 pandemic, hospitals faced significant pressure to rapidly assess newly admitted patients and prioritise clinical resources. Clinicians needed early, reliable indicators to identify which patients were at higher risk of respiratory deterioration and might require intensive care or mechanical ventilation. This use case addresses this challenge by supporting radiologists and hospital physicians with a decision-support service based on chest X-ray examinations performed at admission. The objective is to improve early risk stratification, optimise care pathways, and enable timely clinical interventions for patients with Covid-19–related pulmonary involvement. The service is implemented through advanced machine learning techniques applied to diagnostic imaging. Predictive models trained on large datasets of chest X-ray images and associated clinical outcomes analyse radiological patterns linked to disease progression. The system processes imaging data captured at hospital admission and generates a risk score indicating the likelihood of pulmonary worsening. The platform is integrated into existing radiology and hospital information systems, enabling seamless access for clinicians without disrupting established diagnostic workflows. The solution improves the timeliness and consistency of clinical decision-making by providing early insight into disease progression risk. This supports more effective allocation of intensive care resources, reduces uncertainty in patient triage, and enhances overall care quality. By enabling earlier interventions for high-risk patients, the platform contributes to better patient management, increased operational efficiency, and improved resilience of healthcare services during periods of high demand.

column2: How we used deep learning to structure GOV.UK's content
column4: We used Natural Language Processing to make the text content on the page machine readable. We used this and the page metadata (like date published and department) to learn patterns that could be used to predict which sub-branches an untagged page should be organised in. In some cases, a GOV.UK page is tagged to more than one sub-branch, so we implemented a multilabel model to be able to do exactly that.

column2: How the Department for Transport used AI to improve MOT testing
column4:
The DVSA applied a clustering model against garage test data from the last 3 months, as there was no labelled data available. The clustering model grouped MOT centres based on the behaviour they show when conducting MOT tests.
The DVSA created a risk score for each garage in Great Britain. The risk score combined the output of the clustering algorithm with historical data about how frequently garages had been disciplined for not applying correct MOT standards. This allowed the DVSA to rank garages and their testers, and helped the DVSA identify regional trends. The DVSA updated their garage test data every 3 months. This allowed the DVSA to ensure the model used fresh data whilst also providing stability to garage ratings, providing examiners 3 month windows with which to visit garages.

column2: How DFID used satellite images to estimate populations
column4:
DFID has deployed the programme in Nigeria, Zambia, Mozambique and Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). DFID has also conducted scoping missions to Tanzania, Ethiopia, and South Sudan. The model - called GRID3 - is being used to:
develop a hybrid census model which combines population estimates for small areas of uniform, detailed grids with modelled population estimates support developing countries as they plan their full censuses plan vaccination campaigns and other services
support developing countries such as DRC safely gather population estimates for areas in conflict DFID is now exploring whether they can also use mobile phone data to provide more accurate local level population estimates.

It delivers faster responses, reduces call volumes, and enhances user experience through simple, mobile-first interaction, strengthening the city’s capacity to communicate effectively at scale.

It also frees HR staff to concentrate on later, more qualitative parts of the hiring process.

column2: London Police Facial Recognition System
column4: The technology London will deploy goes beyond many of the facial recognition systems used elsewhere, which match a photo against a database to identify a person. The new tools use software that can immediately identify people on a police watch list as soon as they are filmed on a video camera.

column2: The Municipality of Oslo explores chatbots to respond to citizen requests
column4: Working collaboratively, Oslo and Capgemini created an interdisciplinary team to develop proof of concept solutions. Beginning with the internal automated chatbot interface, the team tested the solution by having the municipality employees ask the robot to confirm their employment via chat. If successful, the request triggered the generation of a confirmation document through an existing automated process and then delivered final confirmation in the chat window as soon as resources were available.

column2: Chatbot - City of Vienna
column4: The City of Vienna website provides comprehensive, detailed content, while the WienBot is all about information for immediate use. People looking for specific information while out and about need a different kind of content to those accessing the web from home. The WienBot is aimed at an audience that wants a smart way of consuming information quickly and on the go, but without foregoing the personal component. The chatbot can also reply in proper Viennese dialect.

column2: London TravelBot
column4: Transports for London created an automated service on Facebook Messenger to help you check the status of your Tube, rail or bus route. The service is free and uses latest data. The status checker is available for all Tube lines, DLR, London Overground, TfL Rail, trams and all bus routes. It can be used on Facebook Messenger.

column2: Virtual assistant equipped with artificial intelligence for Parkinson's patients and their families
column4: Campus Bio-Medico University Hospital and IBM join forces to create "Parkinson bot", a virtual assistant, based on artificial intelligence, capable of answering questions related to Parkinson's 24 hours a day every day.

column2: Sohjoa - Baltic Sea Region transitioning into eco-friendly autonomous last mile public transport
column4: Urban areas across the Baltic Sea region face growing pressure to provide sustainable, accessible, and efficient last-mile public transport. Cities need solutions that reduce emissions while maintaining safety, reliability, and public trust in new mobility services. This use case addresses these challenges by piloting eco-friendly autonomous minibuses on real urban routes, involving local authorities, transport operators, technology providers, and passengers, with the objective of evaluating the feasibility of autonomous last-mile public transport, supporting the transition to greener mobility, and generating evidence to inform future transport policies and deployments. The service is implemented through autonomous driving technologies that enable small electric buses to operate on predefined demonstration routes, using a combination of sensors such as cameras, lidar, and radar to continuously scan their surroundings, detect obstacles, and make driving decisions including slowing down or stopping when required. Control software processes sensor data in real time to support safe navigation in mixed traffic environments, while a trained operator remains on board during all pilot operations to supervise the system, intervene if necessary, and ensure compliance with safety regulations, with operational data collected to support system evaluation and refinement. The pilots provide valuable insights into the technical, operational, and regulatory aspects of autonomous public transport, demonstrating potential reductions in environmental impact, improved last-mile connectivity, and increased efficiency in urban transport networks, while the supervised deployment model helps build public confidence and supports the development of scalable, low-emission mobility solutions for cities planning future autonomous transport services.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Finland Migration Service Chatbot
column4: The Finnish Immigration Service faced sustained pressure to respond to large volumes of repetitive public enquiries related to residence permits, application processing times, and contact information, particularly during periods of high migration demand. Citizens and applicants required timely, consistent information in both Finnish and English, while staff resources were limited. This use case addressed the need to improve access to basic immigration information and reduce pressure on customer service channels, involving the Finnish Immigration Service, applicants, and the general public. The objective was to provide a scalable, always-available information service integrated into the migri.fi website. The solution was implemented through a rule-based and language-processing chatbot embedded across all website pages, designed to recognise common user queries and retrieve predefined responses from official information sources. The chatbot supported bilingual interaction and was integrated with existing web content management systems. During its pilot phase, the service improved accessibility to information, reduced simple enquiries handled by staff, and enhanced user convenience. Although the chatbot has since been decommissioned, it demonstrated the value of automated digital front-line services in public administration and informed later approaches to online service design.
column40: New chatbot answers questions about processing times and contact details

column2: Virtual Digital Assistant with the Revenue Commissioners of Ireland
column4: The Revenue Commissioners of Ireland handle large volumes of telephone enquiries related to tax clearance certificates, creating operational pressure and delays for both staff and taxpayers. Many of these requests are repetitive and transactional in nature. This use case addresses the need to streamline telephone-based services while maintaining accuracy and trust, involving Revenue, taxpayers, and contact centre staff. The objective is to improve efficiency in handling general information requests and simple applications. The service is implemented as a voice-driven virtual assistant that recognises spoken queries, retrieves relevant information, and in some cases processes tax clearance certificate applications directly. It integrates with Revenue’s back-end systems to verify eligibility and status, operating within existing telephony infrastructure. The pilot demonstrates improved call handling efficiency, shorter response times, and reduced workload for human agents. It enhances accessibility for users who prefer voice interaction and supports more consistent service delivery, while freeing staff to focus on complex cases with higher added value.

column2: University La Sapienza launches the first voice "assistant":  Infostud-Watson
column4: Large universities manage complex administrative interactions with students, including exam bookings, certification requests, and general study-related enquiries, which can overwhelm traditional service desks. Sapienza University sought to simplify access to student services and modernise interaction channels. This use case involves the university administration, students, and technology partners, with the objective of improving usability and reducing administrative friction. Infostud-Watson is implemented as a mobile application that enables students to interact with university systems through voice commands and natural language dialogue. The assistant is connected to existing academic and administrative systems, allowing actions such as exam booking and certificate generation, and continuously adapts its responses based on usage patterns. The solution improves convenience by enabling hands-free interaction, reduces queues and manual processing, and enhances the overall student experience. It supports scalable service delivery across a large student population and strengthens digital engagement within the university ecosystem.

column2: Project “Sibyl A.I. Virtual" with Regione Puglia
column4: Public administrations increasingly need efficient and accessible channels to manage interactions with citizens, respond to enquiries, and deliver services in a timely manner. Regional institutions face high volumes of requests, fragmented communication channels, and rising expectations for digital public services. This use case addresses these challenges by creating a unified digital platform to support interactions between the Regione Puglia and citizens, involving public sector staff, local businesses, and end users, with the objective of improving access to information and services, streamlining communication processes, and enhancing the overall quality and responsiveness of institutional services. The platform is implemented through a virtual assistance system based on natural language processing and conversational interfaces, with language models trained on institutional content, public service information, and frequently asked questions to understand user requests and generate accurate, context-aware responses. The system integrates with existing public administration information systems and digital service portals, enabling automated handling of routine enquiries while allowing escalation to human operators when required, and using interaction data to continuously refine the knowledge base and improve response accuracy. The solution improves the efficiency and scalability of citizen support services by reducing response times and relieving administrative staff from repetitive tasks, while enhancing user experience through consistent, always-available access to information. For the administration, it supports cost-effective service delivery, greater transparency, and improved capacity to meet growing demand for digital public services, contributing to the modernisation of public sector operations in the region.

column2: Artificial intelligence: high technology and innovation to make life easier for citizens
column4: The Municipality of Trieste sought to modernise citizen interaction with local administration by simplifying access to demographic services, such as registry information and administrative procedures. Prior to the initiative, citizens relied heavily on in-person visits or phone calls, leading to queues, delays, and fragmented communication. The use case involved the Municipality of Trieste, regional authorities, and citizens, with the objective of providing real-time information through the institutional website and improving service efficiency. The solution was implemented as a conversational chatbot developed by Insiel SpA and co-financed by the Friuli Venezia Giulia Region. It was connected to municipal information systems and structured knowledge bases covering demographic services, enabling automated responses to common questions. During its initial operation in early 2020, the chatbot improved response times, increased transparency, and reduced manual workload for staff. However, the service was later discontinued. Despite this, the project provided valuable insights into citizen expectations, system integration challenges, and the organisational changes required to sustain digital assistance services in local government.

column2: Robots to be used in UK care homes to help reduce loneliness
column4: Loneliness and social isolation are persistent challenges in residential care homes, particularly among older adults with limited mobility or family contact. A UK pilot explored whether social robots could complement care staff by providing companionship and daily interaction for residents. Stakeholders included care home residents, carers, management, families, and technology providers, with the objective of improving emotional well-being without replacing human care. Conducted between 2019 and 2020, the pilot introduced “Pepper” wheeled humanoid robots into selected care homes. The robots were pre-programmed and gradually adapted to residents’ preferences, enabling simple conversations, music playback, reminders, and interactive activities. Sensors and speech interfaces supported autonomous navigation and engagement, while learning components allowed the robots to personalise interactions over time. The systems operated locally within care facilities and were supervised by staff. The pilot showed positive effects on resident engagement and mood, particularly for individuals with limited social contact. Care staff reported that robots supplemented, rather than replaced, human interaction and could be deployed flexibly. While not a substitute for personal care, the solution demonstrated potential to scale as a supportive tool to reduce loneliness and enhance quality of life in long-term care settings.

column2: Caterina, the first (virtual) municipal employee for identity cards and changes of residence
column4: The Municipality of Siena aimed to reduce administrative burdens on citizens and improve access to essential municipal services such as identity cards, change of residence, and electoral card renewals. Long queues and office visits were a source of frustration, particularly for elderly residents. This use case involved the municipality, residents, and service providers, with the objective of enabling citizens to complete administrative procedures remotely. Caterina was implemented as a virtual assistant accessible online, designed to guide users through specific administrative workflows and provide procedural information. The system relied on structured process logic, predefined decision trees, and integration with municipal service information. While active, Caterina reduced unnecessary in-person visits, improved convenience, and demonstrated strong public interest in digital municipal services. The service has since been discontinued, but it highlighted the potential of virtual assistants to support administrative simplification and informed subsequent digitalisation efforts within Italian municipalities.

column2: Anna, a bot for processing Expense Claim
column4: The Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU) and Visma has agreed to test letting a bot automatically process expense claims. The university is the first organisation to implement this functionality, financed by the NTNU digitalisation program. The functionality is not available anymore.
column8: Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU)

column2: Clean Air Optimisation in Madrid Metro
column4: Ensuring good air quality in underground transport systems is essential for passenger health and comfort, yet traditional ventilation systems often operate on static rules that do not respond efficiently to real conditions. Madrid Metro sought to improve air quality while optimising energy consumption. This use case involves the metro operator, passengers, and technology providers, with the objective of dynamically managing ventilation to maintain high air quality standards. The solution is implemented through a self-learning ventilation control system that processes environmental sensor data from stations to adjust airflow in real time. The system integrates with existing ventilation infrastructure and continuously adapts to changing conditions such as passenger density and external air quality. The result is improved air quality, more efficient energy use, and reduced operational costs. Passengers benefit from a healthier environment, while the operator gains a more sustainable and responsive infrastructure management approach.
column8: Madrid Metro (Department of Public Works, City Planning, and Transportation)

column2: Bebot – Vienna AirportCity Virtual Assistant
column4: Airports must provide clear, up-to-date information to diverse users, particularly during disruptions such as public health emergencies, while managing high volumes of enquiries. Vienna AirportCity introduced a digital assistant to support visitors navigating transport, facilities, and Covid-19-related requirements. This use case involves airport operators, travellers, and businesses, with the objective of improving information access and reducing pressure on staff. Bebot is implemented as a web-based chatbot accessible via mobile and desktop through on-site Wi-Fi and the airport website. It delivers structured responses on transport options, directions, facility services, and health regulations, integrated with airport information systems. The service enhances user experience by providing immediate guidance, improves operational efficiency by reducing routine enquiries, and supports consistent communication in a complex, high-traffic environment.

column2: AI technology speeds up historical image handling at Hungary's National Archive
column4: Hungary’s National Audiovisual Archive (NAVA) faced the challenge of organising and preserving millions of historical images and audiovisual records forming the country’s twentieth-century cultural memory. Manual classification was slow, costly, and unable to keep pace with digitisation efforts. Stakeholders included archivists, historians, public broadcasters, and citizens, with the objective of accelerating cataloguing while maintaining archival quality. Between 2019 and 2021, a pilot deployed cloud-based image analysis services to classify large image collections automatically. Visual recognition models were trained to detect objects, scenes, and contextual features, generating metadata to support indexing and retrieval. The system integrated with existing archive databases and digitisation workflows, enabling human archivists to validate and refine outputs rather than start from scratch. The pilot significantly reduced processing time for image classification, allowing millions of records to be indexed efficiently. It improved searchability and long-term preservation planning while lowering operational costs. Archivists retained editorial control, ensuring trust and reliability. The approach demonstrated strong scalability and laid the foundation for sustainable management of national audiovisual heritage.

column2: Unlocking the potential of crowdsourcing for public decision-making with artificial intelligence
column4: Public administrations increasingly use online participation platforms to collect citizen input, but processing thousands of contributions manually limits their impact on decision-making. This use case addresses the challenge of transforming large-scale citizen feedback into actionable insights, involving civil servants, policymakers, and citizens. The objective is to support evidence-based public decisions while maintaining inclusive participation. CitizenLab’s platform implements advanced text analysis techniques to automatically classify, cluster, and summarise citizen contributions by topic, location, and demographic traits. Dashboards present emerging trends and patterns, integrated into participation workflows used by public authorities. The solution significantly reduces analysis time, improves insight quality, and enables officials to respond more effectively to citizen priorities. It strengthens trust in participatory processes, enhances transparency, and scales citizen engagement without proportional increases in administrative effort.

column2: Artificial intelligence dives into thousands of WWII photographs
column4: Researchers in history, computer science, and the social sciences face significant challenges when analysing large collections of historical photographs, as manual examination is time-consuming and limits the scale of inquiry. Understanding patterns of authorship, perspective, and visual style in wartime imagery is important for historical interpretation, archival research, and cultural analysis. This use case addresses these challenges through an interdisciplinary study that examines thousands of Second World War photographs, involving academic researchers and cultural heritage institutions, with the objective of uncovering latent patterns in visual content and advancing new methods for large-scale historical image analysis. The study is implemented using computer vision and pattern recognition techniques applied to extensive datasets of digitised wartime photographs, with models trained to extract visual features related to composition, subject matter, and contextual elements. These models analyse similarities and recurring visual signatures across images, enabling the system to distinguish and attribute photographs to individual photographers based solely on image content, without relying on metadata. The results demonstrate that large-scale automated image analysis can reveal meaningful stylistic and contextual patterns in historical archives, including the ability to identify photographers with a high degree of accuracy. This approach significantly expands the analytical capacity of historical research, supports more systematic exploration of visual archives, and opens new opportunities for interdisciplinary collaboration and data-driven insights in the study of cultural heritage.

column2: U.K. to use AI to spot dangerous side effects in the COVID-19 vaccinations
column4: Large-scale vaccination programmes generate vast volumes of safety reports that regulators must review to identify potential adverse effects and protect public health. During the Covid-19 vaccination rollout, the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) faced the challenge of monitoring tens of thousands of reports within tight timeframes, far beyond what could be efficiently handled through manual analysis alone. This use case addresses the need for timely and reliable pharmacovigilance by supporting the MHRA in detecting early safety signals from reported side effects, involving regulators, healthcare providers, and the public, with the objective of ensuring vaccine safety while maintaining public confidence in the vaccination programme. The solution is implemented through a machine learning system developed under a $1.96 million contract awarded to the UK arm of Genpact, which ingests large volumes of reported side-effect data from multiple sources and applies statistical learning and pattern-recognition techniques to identify unusual trends, correlations, or clusters that may indicate potential safety concerns. Models are trained on historical vaccination and adverse-event data to distinguish normal background reporting patterns from signals requiring further investigation, and the platform integrates with existing regulatory reporting systems to enable continuous monitoring and prioritisation of cases for expert review. The approach significantly improves the scalability and responsiveness of vaccine safety monitoring, enabling the MHRA to manage an expected volume of 50,000 to 100,000 reports of suspected side effects per 100 million doses administered over a six- to 12-month period, while reducing manual workload, accelerating the identification of potential risks, supporting faster regulatory decision-making, and strengthening the overall reliability of pharmacovigilance processes to deliver safer vaccination campaigns and improved public health outcomes.
column8: U.K. Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)

column2: Aurora, the new virtual assistant of the South East Tuscany AUSL is online
column4: Healthcare organisations face high demand for reliable public information, particularly across large geographic areas. The South East Tuscany Local Health Authority aimed to improve access to health-related information for citizens in Siena, Arezzo, and Grosseto. This use case involved the health authority, technology providers, and residents, with the objective of delivering easy-to-use digital information services. Aurora was implemented as a 3D virtual avatar accessible via web and mobile devices, providing scripted responses to health service enquiries through a conversational interface. The system integrated visual interaction with structured information repositories. During its pilot phase, Aurora improved accessibility and visibility of health information and showcased innovative digital communication methods. The service has since been discontinued, but it demonstrated early experimentation with virtual interfaces in healthcare communication and contributed to organisational learning on digital citizen engagement.
column8: Azienda USL Toscana Sud Est (Regional Health structure)

column2: Self-driving buses roll into Gothenburg
column4: Cities seeking to modernise public transport must balance sustainability goals, service accessibility, and the safe introduction of new mobility technologies. In Gothenburg, local authorities and transport operators aimed to explore innovative solutions that could enhance connectivity within key employment and innovation districts while reducing environmental impact. This use case focuses on the trial deployment of autonomous electric shuttles to provide public transport services within the Lindholmen Industrial and Science Park, involving the city, the transport operator Keolis, and passengers, with the objective of assessing the feasibility, safety, and operational value of self-driving buses in an urban setting. The service is implemented through autonomous electric shuttle vehicles operating on a dedicated 1.8-kilometre route, using onboard sensors, navigation systems, and control software to perceive their environment and drive without direct human control. The shuttles operate under supervised conditions during a 4.5-month trial period that began on 18 January, allowing for real-world testing, data collection, and system validation while ensuring passenger safety and regulatory compliance. The trial provides insights into the performance, reliability, and user acceptance of autonomous public transport, demonstrating the potential for low-emission, flexible mobility services in urban areas. It supports evidence-based decision-making on future deployments, contributes to innovation in public transport provision, and helps position Gothenburg as a testbed for sustainable and scalable autonomous mobility solutions.

column2: Data-Driven Risk Analysis to Prevent Tax Evasion in Italy
column4: Tax authorities face persistent challenges in identifying and preventing tax evasion, particularly as economic activities and financial relationships become increasingly complex. Traditional risk assessment methods often struggle to detect sophisticated patterns of non-compliance early enough to support effective enforcement and voluntary compliance. This use case addresses these challenges by modernising tax evasion risk analysis for the Italian Revenue Agency, involving the Ministry of Finance, its in-house technology partner SOGEI, and European Union funding bodies, with the objective of strengthening preventive identification of taxpayers at high risk of non-compliance and improving the effectiveness of fiscal oversight. The initiative is implemented through a data-driven system that applies machine learning, network analysis, and advanced data visualisation techniques to large volumes of fiscal, administrative, and economic data. Analytical models examine relationships between individuals, companies, and transactions to uncover hidden networks and anomalous behaviours associated with evasion risks, while visualisation tools support analysts in interpreting complex results and prioritising cases. The system integrates with existing tax administration processes and information infrastructures, enabling experimentation, validation, and gradual operational adoption. The project enhances the accuracy and timeliness of risk assessments, enabling more targeted controls and more efficient use of investigative resources. By shifting towards preventive and evidence-based analysis, it supports reduced tax evasion, improved compliance, and stronger public finances, while increasing the transparency, scalability, and analytical capacity of the Revenue Agency’s risk management processes.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Autonomous Tram Depot (AStriD) – Digital Depot Operations in Potsdam
column4: Urban transport operators face rising pressure to increase efficiency while maintaining safety and reliability in depot operations. The ASTRiD project addresses this challenge by developing a digital tram depot capable of supporting autonomous vehicle movements within controlled environments. Stakeholders include Verkehrsbetrieb Potsdam (ViP), Siemens Mobility, transport authorities, and national research funders. The objective is to automate routine depot manoeuvres, such as moving trams through washing facilities or into sidings, reducing manual labour and operational risk. The project integrates autonomous vehicle control systems, depot infrastructure sensors, digital signalling, and safety supervision mechanisms. Trams are equipped with perception and navigation systems that interpret depot layouts and execute predefined manoeuvres under supervision. The system interfaces with depot management software and safety controls to ensure compliance with operational constraints. According to public reporting, a key demonstration phase was successfully completed as part of the three-year research project funded under Germany’s mFund programme. While the project remains at pilot level, it has validated the technical feasibility of autonomous depot operations and provided a foundation for future scaling in urban rail systems.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Manchester uses artificial intelligence to gain more insight into active travel
column4: City regions face increasing pressure to promote walking and cycling while ensuring that investments in active travel infrastructure are evidence-based. Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) planned a regional monitoring initiative to gain better insight into how pedestrians and cyclists use segregated routes, junctions, and priority corridors. Stakeholders included TfGM, local authorities, urban planners, and residents. The objective was to obtain continuous, anonymised data on active travel flows to support infrastructure planning, safety interventions, and evaluation of policy measures without relying on manual surveys. The planned solution involved the installation of around 100 roadside sensors supplied by Vivacity Labs. These sensors combine optical and signal-based detection to count pedestrians, cyclists, and vehicles in real time while processing data locally to avoid personal identification. Aggregated flow data would be transmitted to central dashboards and combined with contextual information such as time of day and location. According to public reporting, elements of the sensor deployment were initiated, but the programme was framed as an exploratory pilot rather than a fully institutionalised system. The initiative demonstrated the potential for scalable, low-intrusion monitoring of active travel, supporting more responsive transport planning and contributing to wider smart mobility strategies, even though full regional rollout was not confirmed at the time.

column2: Creating an AI-driven mobile virtual assistant and common public services platform
AI-SCORE, a project to calculate prognostic risk from Covid-19
