column2: Computer vision e-scooter technology to be trialled in UK city
Rome conducts smart city trial of intelligent vision sensors
column4: Welfare Ticket – Blockchain-Based Social Security Platform for the Gig Economy (status: conceptual design, not implemented)

column2: Dublin to host AI-powered e-scooter research project
column4: Dublin faces growing challenges in managing shared micro-mobility services, particularly e-scooters, as their rapid adoption raises concerns around road safety, pavement congestion, parking compliance, and integration with existing transport systems. City authorities, transport planners, mobility operators, researchers, and citizens all have a stake in ensuring that e-scooters contribute positively to sustainable urban mobility rather than creating new risks or inefficiencies. The objective of this use case is to improve how e-scooters are deployed, used, and regulated across the city by generating evidence-based insights that support safer operations, better infrastructure planning, and more effective policy decisions. The project applies advanced data analytics and machine learning techniques to data collected from e-scooters, including location, speed, braking events, trip duration, and interaction with the urban environment, combined with contextual data such as road layouts, traffic conditions, and weather. Computer vision and predictive models are used to analyse rider behaviour, identify unsafe patterns, optimise parking zones, and simulate different regulatory or infrastructure scenarios. These models are integrated into a research platform that connects mobility operators, city systems, and analytical tools to enable continuous experimentation and evaluation. The initiative supports improved rider safety, reduced pavement clutter, and more efficient use of public space, while providing the city with robust evidence to guide regulation and investment. It enhances service reliability and user experience, enables scalable management of micro-mobility, and delivers long-term value by helping Dublin design safer, more sustainable urban transport systems.

column2: Helsinki trials AI and automation as part of its drive to digital services
column4: Labour platforms and gig workers often operate outside traditional social security frameworks, creating gaps in insurance coverage and administrative complexity for short-term or task-based work. In Italy, a concept known as the “Welfare Ticket” was developed to address these challenges by enab

column2: Chatbot Comune di Collegno (TO)
column4: The Municipality of Collegno introduced a chatbot to support citizens seeking information on the Citizen Public Registry and separate waste collection services, aiming to reduce administrative workload and improve responsiveness. Stakeholders included municipal departments, residents, and service operators. The chatbot was implemented on the municipal website using predefined conversational flows linked to service-specific information. While the objective was to simplify access to information, the service faced usability and content limitations, leading to poor user satisfaction and eventual failure. The chatbot was later publicly acknowledged as unsuccessful and discontinued. Despite this outcome, the initiative highlighted critical success factors for digital assistants, including content quality, maintenance, and alignment with real user needs, offering valuable lessons for future public-sector digital services.

column2: MARVEL project in Trento: monitoring crowd in the city
column4: Urban authorities face growing challenges in ensuring public safety across busy urban environments, where large crowds, transport hubs, and shared public spaces can give rise to accidents, anti-social behaviour, and criminal activity. In Trento, local authorities and law enforcement needed tools to monitor multiple public areas simultaneously and respond rapidly to potentially dangerous situations while making efficient use of surveillance infrastructure. This use case addresses these needs by supporting the municipality and local police in monitoring public spaces such as streets, squares, subways, and parking areas, with the objective of identifying anomalous or risky situations, including fights, robberies, drug dealing, and traffic accidents, and enabling timely operational responses. The solution is implemented through a smart monitoring platform that analyses video and audio streams from distributed sensors and cameras across the city, applying advanced pattern recognition and anomaly detection techniques to identify events of interest within crowds and specific locations. Detected incidents trigger automated alerts to the police operational centre and generate a customised visual interface that highlights relevant camera feeds, supporting situational awareness and rapid decision-making. The system is tested across five trial cases, including crowd monitoring, detection of criminal or anti-social behaviour, subway monitoring, parking surveillance, and focused analysis of specific areas. The use case enhances public safety by enabling faster detection and response to incidents, improving coordination between monitoring systems and law enforcement, and increasing the effectiveness and scalability of urban surveillance, while supporting safer and more resilient city environments.
column8: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas

column2: Chatbot Covibot Provincia Trento (TN)
column4: During the Covid-19 pandemic, the Province of Trento needed to rapidly disseminate accurate and up-to-date information on testing, isolation rules, quarantine measures, and vaccination campaigns. Call centres and health services were under exceptional pressure. This use case involved the provincial health authority, citizens, and healthcare providers, with the objective of ensuring reliable information access at scale. Covibot was implemented as an informational chatbot connected to official health guidance and FAQs, accessible online to answer pandemic-related questions. The system enabled rapid updates as regulations changed and reduced pressure on human-operated channels. While no longer active, Covibot played a critical role during the emergency phase by improving information accessibility, reducing misinformation, and supporting public health communication under crisis conditions.
column8: Autonomous Province of Trento - Health Agency

column2: Belgian city uses artificial intelligence to tackle noise pollution
column4: Excessive traffic noise poses health and safety risks for residents, yet traditional enforcement relies on sporadic checks and citizen complaints. Genk aimed to better quantify and address noise pollution along a busy street. This use case involves city authorities, residents, and technology providers, with the objective of enabling data-driven noise management. The solution deploys microphones and cameras as roadside sensors to measure sound levels and identify excessively noisy vehicles, combining acoustic thresholds with vehicle recognition. Data is transmitted to authorities to support informed enforcement and policy decisions. The system improves accuracy and objectivity in noise monitoring, reduces manual enforcement effort, and provides evidence to address resident concerns, supporting a healthier urban environment.

column2: INPS project in the UNESCO World Top 10 for artificial intelligence
column4: INPS receives millions of certified emails (PEC) each year, creating delays and inefficiencies in routing messages to the appropriate departments. This use case addresses the need to streamline internal communication flows while improving response times for citizens and businesses. It involves INPS, its staff, and service users, with the objective of optimising message handling and operator workload. The system is implemented to automatically analyse the content of incoming PEC messages and route them to the most appropriate specialised operators within INPS. Integrated with existing communication and case management systems, it enables faster triage and prioritisation. The solution significantly reduces processing times, improves consistency in responses, and allows staff to focus on higher-value activities. Its recognition by UNESCO reflects its impact on efficiency, scalability, and service quality in large-scale public administration.

column2: iVoting in Poland - A cyberdemocracy tool
column4: Local governments increasingly seek secure, transparent, and inclusive ways to engage citizens in decision-making processes such as participatory budgeting, public consultations, petitions, and internal council voting. Traditional paper-based or in-person voting methods are costly, slow, and limit participation, particularly for digitally active citizens. This use case addresses these challenges by enabling municipalities to conduct surveys, polls, civic budget voting, and statutory civic initiatives online, involving local administrations, elected bodies, and citizens, with the objective of strengthening participation, trust, and efficiency in local democracy. The solution is implemented through a secure digital voting platform that creates electronic citizen identities using advanced cryptography and distributed ledger technology. Multi-level authentication allows citizens to vote via computers or smartphones, while decentralised notary servers validate and record voting events, ensuring transparency, auditability, and resistance to manipulation. Voting protocols support both secret and public ballots, depending on the use case, and integrate with municipal applications. The platform increases participation rates, reduces administrative costs, and accelerates decision-making processes. It enhances transparency and trust in civic processes, supports scalable deployment across municipalities, and provides a robust digital foundation for modern, participatory local governance.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: TrueBee (Digital forensics and blockchain technology against deceptive deepfake imaging in social media)
column4: The rapid spread of manipulated and deceptive images on social media has created significant challenges for journalists, platforms, institutions, and citizens seeking to assess the authenticity and provenance of digital content. Traditional verification methods struggle to keep pace with the volume and sophistication of falsified imagery, undermining trust in online information. This use case addresses these challenges by supporting the identification, classification, and long-term traceability of manipulated images, involving researchers, media professionals, and technology providers, with the objective of enabling reliable verification and increasing transparency in digital media ecosystems. The project combines image analysis techniques for recognising and classifying falsified or manipulated images with a distributed ledger system that records classification results in an immutable register. Each analysed image is processed to detect signs of manipulation and assigned a verification outcome, which is then permanently stored to preserve the history, provenance, and processing chain of the multimedia content. The register is made accessible for consultation, allowing users to verify whether an image has been assessed and to review its authenticity status. The approach strengthens the reliability of digital forensics by ensuring traceability and tamper resistance, improves confidence in media verification processes, and supports the development of trustworthy tools to combat misinformation, contributing to greater transparency and accountability in online content sharing.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Nidi Gratis - Public Service for Free Nursery
column4: Access to early childhood education can be limited by administrative complexity and financial barriers, particularly for low-income families. Public authorities must ensure that subsidies for nursery services are distributed fairly, efficiently, and transparently, while coordinating multiple stakeholders. This use case addresses the challenge of managing free nursery provision by simplifying access to benefits and improving oversight, involving regional authorities, municipalities, service providers, and families. The objective is to ensure equitable access to childcare services while reducing administrative burden and misuse of public funds. The service is implemented through a digital subsidy management system based on distributed ledger technology, where eligibility criteria, entitlements, and service usage are securely recorded. Transactions between families and authorised nursery providers are validated automatically, ensuring that public funding is used exclusively for eligible services. The system integrates with regional administrative platforms and service registries. The solution improves transparency and traceability of subsidies, reduces processing times, and limits errors or fraud. It enhances user experience for families and providers, supports scalable public service delivery, and strengthens trust in the management of social welfare programmes.

column2: Lombardia supply chain traceability system
column4: Food supply chains of animal origin require high levels of transparency and traceability to ensure food safety, regulatory compliance, and consumer trust. Regional authorities and industry stakeholders face difficulties in reliably tracking products across complex, multi-actor supply chains, particularly when data is fragmented or vulnerable to tampering. This use case addresses these challenges by supporting the Lombardy Region’s strategic objective to modernise agri-food traceability systems, involving public authorities, producers, and supply chain operators, with the objective of improving traceability, accountability, and trust throughout the food lifecycle. The experiment implements a digital traceability system based on distributed ledger technology to record and share information on the origin, processing, and movement of food products of animal origin. Data generated at different stages of the supply chain is securely recorded in a shared, tamper-resistant register, enabling authorised stakeholders to access consistent and verifiable information. The system is designed to integrate with existing supply chain and regulatory processes, supporting interoperability and gradual adoption. The initiative improves transparency and reliability in food traceability, reduces the risk of fraud and data manipulation, and strengthens consumer confidence, while providing a scalable foundation for innovation in agri-food systems and supporting the region’s broader strategy for research, innovation, and digital transformation.
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain

column2: Project PININ – Blockchain-Based Traceability for Agri-Food Supply Chains
column4: The project aims to build a distributed and decentralized infrastructure based on blockchain for agri-food products tracking throughout the whole supply chain. CSI is one of the partner, together with the University of Turin and Consoft Sistemi. Currently, the automated traceability data collection systems adopted by agri-food companies are different and not homogeneous. There are many applications that penalize the supply chain efficiency, the traced data accuracy and the individual user experience. Users who are forced to use different sites or apps manually inputting the product lot code.

column2: BLINC: BLockchain INclusive for digital Citizenships
column4: Migrants and refugees often interact with multiple public and private services during their integration journey, facing fragmented documentation processes, limited portability of credentials, and difficulties in managing personal records securely. These challenges can slow access to services, employment, housing, and social inclusion opportunities. This use case addresses these issues by supporting immigrants with a digital document wallet that brings together self-generated certificates and documents issued by public administrations and private service providers, involving migrants, public authorities, and service organisations, with the objective of simplifying document management, strengthening trust, and accelerating social inclusion. The solution is implemented through a mobile-first digital wallet based on distributed ledger technology, where credentials and documents are securely stored and linked to the individual. The system supports granular privacy controls, enabling users to selectively share specific information with authorised parties, while recording verifiable traces of interactions and certifications that can support access to services or hospitality initiatives. The wallet is accessible from mobile devices and other connected platforms, ensuring flexibility and continuity of access. The approach improves usability and trust in digital identity management, reduces administrative friction, and empowers users with greater control over their personal data, contributing to faster integration pathways and more inclusive digital public services.
column8: CSI Piemonte (Inhouse ICT of Regione Piemonte - Italy)

column2: Blockcerts academic credentials
column4: Verifying academic qualifications across institutions and borders is often slow, costly, and prone to fraud, creating challenges for graduates, employers, and education authorities. The Maltese government sought to modernise credential verification by enabling citizens to securely prove their educational achievements. This use case involves the Ministry for Education and Employment, educational institutions, students, and employers, with the objective of providing trustworthy, portable, and easily verifiable academic records. The solution is implemented using the Blockcerts open standard, which covers the full lifecycle of academic credentials, from issuance to verification. Certificates are digitally issued by authorised institutions, anchored on a blockchain infrastructure, and stored under the control of the individual. Verification can be performed independently by third parties without contacting the issuing institution. The service improves efficiency by eliminating manual verification processes, reduces fraud, and empowers citizens with ownership of their credentials. It supports international recognition of qualifications, enhances labour market mobility, and delivers long-term value through a scalable, standards-based digital education infrastructure.

column2: The Land Registry in the blockchain - testbed
column4: Property transactions involve high financial value and legal complexity, making security, transparency, and trust critical for buyers, sellers, banks, and public authorities. Traditional real estate transaction processes are often slow, costly, and exposed to risks such as disputes over ownership and delays in settlement. This use case addresses these challenges by rethinking how property transfers and mortgage deeds are recorded and executed in Sweden, involving the national land registry, financial institutions, technology providers, and telecom operators, with the objective of increasing trust, reducing transaction times, and lowering operational risk. The testbed is implemented by recording property transactions and related documents on a distributed ledger shared among authorised stakeholders, enabling secure, tamper-resistant registration of ownership changes and mortgage agreements. Smart contract mechanisms support the automation of transaction steps, while integration with existing digital land registry systems ensures legal validity and continuity with established processes. The initiative demonstrates the potential to significantly speed up settlements, reduce administrative costs, and minimise disputes, while improving transparency and reliability in real estate transactions and laying the groundwork for scalable digital transformation of land administration.
column8: Mapping, Cadaster and Land Registration Authority

column2: Veramo: decentralised identity
column4: Governments increasingly require secure and user-centric digital identity solutions to enable access to online public services while protecting personal data. Citizens also seek greater control over how their identity information is shared with public and private organisations. This use case addresses these needs through the development of a decentralised digital identity framework initially adopted by the City of Zug, involving public authorities, citizens, and technology developers, with the objective of enabling trusted authentication for e-government services and selective data sharing under citizen control. The solution is implemented through a decentralised identity infrastructure built on distributed ledger technology, where individuals manage their own identifiers and credentials without reliance on a central authority. The system enables selective disclosure, allowing users to share only specific attributes with selected organisations, while cryptographic mechanisms ensure integrity and authenticity. The evolution of the initiative into open-source projects such as Veramo supports broader interoperability and adoption across different services and platforms. The approach strengthens privacy, increases trust in digital interactions, and empowers citizens with ownership of their personal data, supporting more secure, flexible, and scalable digital identity ecosystems.
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: EBSILUX - Infrachain Project for digital academic diplomas in Luxembourg
column4: Educational mobility and cross-border employment require high levels of trust in academic qualifications, yet verification processes remain fragmented across countries and institutions. Luxembourg aimed to align national diploma systems with European infrastructure to support transparency and interoperability. This use case involves schools, universities, students, employers, and public authorities, with the objective of delivering secure, cross-border digital academic certificates. The solution is implemented by integrating Luxembourg into the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI) for the Diplomas use case. Academic credentials are issued and verified through a permissioned blockchain governed by Infrachain’s framework, which ensures privacy, cybersecurity, legal compliance, and business continuity. Independent nodes operate the infrastructure, separating network and service layers to support multiple public and private services. The system improves trust and efficiency in diploma verification, reduces administrative costs, and supports seamless cross-border recognition of qualifications. It strengthens European digital sovereignty, enhances labour mobility, and provides a robust, interoperable foundation for digital education services.

column2: Pension infrastructure
column4: Pension administration involves complex transactions, multiple stakeholders, and high operational costs, often lacking transparency for citizens. A pilot pension infrastructure project explored whether a shared digital ledger could modernise pension administration. Stakeholders included pension funds, administrators, employers, and citizens, with the objective of increasing transparency while reducing costs. Conducted between 2016 and 2018, the pilot used blockchain technology to manage individual pension balances and transactions. Each participant held a personal account, with contributions and entitlements recorded as immutable transactions. Smart contracts governed rules for updates and transfers, integrating with existing pension systems. The pilot demonstrated improved transparency, traceability, and administrative efficiency. It reduced reconciliation efforts and highlighted potential long-term cost savings, while offering citizens clearer insight into their pension status.

column2: Stadjerspas smart vouchers
column4: Municipalities must ensure that social benefits reach intended recipients and are spent on approved purposes, yet paper-based voucher systems are inefficient and difficult to control. The Municipality of Groningen sought to modernise its discount scheme for low-income citizens, known as Stadjerspas. This use case involves the municipality, beneficiaries, and authorised service providers, with the objective of improving targeting, transparency, and efficiency of public spending. The solution is implemented through a blockchain-based smart voucher system where eligibility criteria, spending limits, and authorised providers are encoded in smart contracts. Vouchers are issued digitally and can be redeemed for specific services such as sports, cultural activities, or energy-related investments. Transactions are automatically validated and recorded, ensuring compliance with programme rules. The system reduces administrative overhead, prevents misuse of funds, and provides real-time oversight for the municipality. It improves user experience for citizens and providers, enhances accountability, and delivers a scalable model for targeted social support.

column2: Blockchain pilot - Open Data notarization & change protocol
column4: Public administrations publish large volumes of open data that must remain accurate, authentic, and tamper-proof to maintain public trust and support reuse. The City of Vienna addressed the risk of data manipulation and version ambiguity by securing its open data infrastructure. This use case involves municipal authorities, data users, and federal partners, with the objective of guaranteeing integrity and traceability of public datasets. The solution is implemented through blockchain-based notarisation, where cryptographic fingerprints of open data resources are anchored on public blockchains. Nearly 4,000 datasets already include verifiable identifiers, and the approach is being extended nationally through the BLOCKSTEMPEL service in cooperation with the Austrian Federal Computing Centre. The system ensures that any alteration to published data can be detected and verified. It strengthens trust in open government data, reduces disputes over data authenticity, and supports scalable, reusable digital public services across Austria.

column2: Blockchain pilot - Digital food voucher
column4: The City of Vienna sought to replace paper-based food vouchers for employees with a more efficient and transparent system. Stakeholders included city employees, participating vendors, and municipal administrators, with the objective of reducing administrative overhead and fraud. Emerging Technology Implementation: The 2017–2018 pilot issued digital food tokens to employees’ wallets, redeemable at authorised vendors. Transactions were recorded on a blockchain, enabling automatic settlement and auditability. Impact and Results: The pilot reduced manual processing, improved traceability, and demonstrated scalability for up to 20,000 employees, supporting future roll-out decisions.

column2: Austrian Federal Government's Finance Agency, Oesterreichische Kontrollbank
column4: Public debt auctions require high levels of transparency, integrity, and auditability to maintain market confidence and ensure trust in government financial operations. Traditional notarisation and record-keeping processes can be complex and rely on multiple intermediaries, creating potential inefficiencies and risks. This use case addresses these challenges by supporting the Austrian Federal Government’s Finance Agency in notarising a large-scale bond auction, involving the agency, the Oesterreichische Kontrollbank, and market participants, with the objective of strengthening transparency and reliability in sovereign debt issuance. The solution is implemented by recording key steps and outcomes of a bond auction worth approximately $1.3 billion on a distributed ledger, creating an immutable and verifiable record of the process. The system complements existing financial and settlement infrastructures, providing cryptographic proof of integrity and timing without disrupting established market operations. The initiative enhances trust and auditability in public financial management, demonstrates the feasibility of applying distributed ledger technology to capital markets, and supports more transparent and efficient government finance processes.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: BeSure - Demonstrability Service
column4: Belgian public administrations needed a reliable way to prove that digital documents had been sent and received via eBoxes without relying on central intermediaries. Stakeholders included citizens, public bodies, and legal authorities. Emerging Technology Implementation: From 2017 to 2019, a blockchain-based proof service generated immutable timestamps and receipt proofs linked to document exchanges. Impact and Results: The pilot enhanced legal certainty, security, and trust while meeting strict privacy requirements and reducing dependency on central systems.

column2: FlowBikes - Supporting cities and schools to promote sustainable mobility
column4: Traffic congestion and road safety risks around schools are often driven by car-based commuting, affecting children’s safety and urban sustainability. FlowBikes addresses this challenge by encouraging active mobility among schoolchildren. The use case involves local governments, schools, families, and private partners, with the objective of promoting walking and cycling to school while improving safety. The solution is implemented through a digital reward system that tracks children’s journeys along predefined safe routes and issues tokens for active travel. These tokens can be redeemed at local shops or community events, creating positive reinforcement for sustainable behaviour. The system integrates route guidance, participation tracking, and reward management on a blockchain infrastructure. The initiative reduces car traffic near schools, improves safety, supports healthier lifestyles, and strengthens local economies. It provides a scalable model for behaviour-based mobility policies.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: i-Voting in Estonia
column4: Ensuring high participation and trust in democratic processes is a core challenge for modern societies. Estonia addressed this by enabling citizens to vote securely online. This use case involves the national electoral authorities, voters, and technology providers, with the objective of increasing accessibility and participation in elections. The system allows voters to cast ballots online during a pre-voting period using national digital identity tools. Voter identity is verified for eligibility, then cryptographically separated from the ballot to ensure anonymity before counting. The solution integrates with national electoral infrastructure and has been used in local, parliamentary, and European elections. It increases voter turnout, reduces logistical costs, and enables participation from anywhere in the world. Estonia’s i-Voting system demonstrates a mature, scalable model for secure digital democracy.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: e-Health Records in Estonia
column4: Healthcare delivery requires timely access to accurate patient information across providers, yet fragmented systems hinder continuity of care. Estonia addressed this by creating a nationwide electronic health record system. The use case involves healthcare providers, patients, and national authorities, with the objective of enabling secure, unified access to medical records. The system aggregates data from multiple provider systems and presents it in a standardised format via a national patient portal. Integrity and access logs are secured using blockchain-based mechanisms to ensure records cannot be altered without detection. The solution improves clinical decision-making, reduces duplication of tests, and empowers patients with access to their data. It enhances efficiency, safety, and trust in national healthcare services.

column2: Blockchain-based Keyless Signature Infrastructure (KSI)
column4: Governments must protect critical systems and data against both external attacks and internal manipulation. Estonia developed KSI to ensure data integrity across government networks. This use case involves public institutions and infrastructure operators, with the objective of guaranteeing that digital records remain authentic and untampered. KSI uses a distributed ledger to cryptographically seal data and system logs, enabling mathematical proof of integrity without revealing content. The system integrates with existing IT infrastructures and operates at national scale. It prevents undetected data manipulation, strengthens cybersecurity resilience, and provides long-term trust in digital government systems, delivering strong business and societal value through reliable, verifiable data integrity.

column2: FLORA – The federal asylum blockchain infrastructure
column4: European asylum procedures involve multiple national authorities, high volumes of documentation, and frequent data handovers, which can lead to long processing times, errors, and inconsistencies. Germany’s Federal Office for Migration and Refugees, together with research institutions and technology partners, sought to modernise asylum case management and improve cross-border coordination. The objective of FLORA is to support faster, more reliable asylum procedures by enabling secure information sharing across organisations and countries, while respecting national responsibilities. The solution is implemented through a blockchain-based infrastructure where participating authorities deploy their process logic and share verified case data on a distributed ledger. Each step of the asylum process is recorded immutably, enabling traceability, reducing duplication, and ensuring data integrity across organisational boundaries. The system is designed to be federal and nation-agnostic, supporting a European ecosystem rather than a single central database. FLORA reduces error rates, shortens processing times, and improves transparency between stakeholders. It strengthens trust, supports scalability across Member States, and delivers long-term value by enabling more efficient and coordinated asylum management in Europe.
column8: Federal office for Migration and Refugees
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Securing the Internet of Things
column4: The rapid growth of the Internet of Things has introduced challenges related to security, identity management, and trust between connected devices, particularly as systems scale across sectors and jurisdictions. Physical devices require reliable, non-forgeable digital identities to ensure secure communication and interoperability. This use case addresses these challenges through a partnership between the Isle of Man government and a technology provider, involving public authorities, developers, and IoT stakeholders, with the objective of strengthening the security and manageability of connected devices. The solution is implemented using a distributed ledger to assign and manage unique digital identities for physical devices, enabling secure registration, authentication, and lifecycle tracking. The ledger maintains a trusted record of device identities and interactions, supporting scalable and interoperable IoT applications across different use cases. The approach improves trust, security, and resilience in IoT ecosystems, reduces the risk of identity spoofing and tampering, and provides a foundation for the safe deployment of large-scale connected services in both public and private sectors.

column2: Digital Currency Business Register
column4: The Isle of Man Government's Department of Economic Development will create a stand-alone register of Island-based digital currency businesses using the protocol of this local Blockchain service.

column2: Traceability of Italian food products
column4: A collaboration agreement has been signed between Poligrafico and Zecca of the Italian State and CSQA Certificazioni to carry out a project that strengthens the traceability of Italian PDO (protected denomination of origin) and PGI (Protected Geographical Indication) products and offers effective communication to consumers. The agreement is part of the framework of activities for the enhancement of quality supply chains, in order to make the professional skills and technological solutions necessary for the protection and promotion of the specialties of our country available to sector operators and citizens.

column2: Customs 4.0: innovation in the Customs Management
column4: Customs administrations face increasing pressure to manage complex supply chains, ensure regulatory compliance, and provide transparency to businesses and consumers while transitioning from paper-based processes to fully digital operations. In Italy, the Customs Agency (Agenzia delle Entrate) has pursued a progressive transformation towards an eCustoms and Customs 4.0 model, seeking to improve traceability, control, and trust across goods movements and production chains. This use case addresses these objectives through a pilot project focused on the production of Genepì, a traditional herbal liqueur from Piedmont, involving the Customs Agency, producers, consumers, and the technology partner Sogei, with the aim of demonstrating how innovative digital tools can enhance customs management, product traceability, and consumer information. The solution is implemented by digitally replicating all stages of the Genepì production and bottling process on a distributed ledger built on the Ethereum protocol, using open-source components. Data related to origin, processing, and bottling is securely recorded and made accessible through a QR code on the product, allowing consumers and authorities to verify information, while the architecture is designed to be extended to include transport and logistics phases in the future. The project improves transparency, data integrity, and trust along the supply chain, reduces administrative complexity, and strengthens the Customs Agency’s ability to monitor and certify goods, while offering consumers greater visibility into product provenance and providing a scalable model for innovation in customs and trade management.
column8: Agenzia delle dogane e dei Monopoli

column2: Food fraud and falsification for Made in Italy products
column4: Food counterfeiting poses serious risks to consumers and causes significant economic losses for producers, particularly in high-value sectors such as Italian wine. Public authorities and industry stakeholders require reliable tools to guarantee product authenticity across complex supply chains. This use case addresses these challenges by protecting the integrity of Made in Italy wines, involving the Ministry of Agriculture, public agencies, producers, and consumers. The objective is to ensure traceability and prevent fraud using authoritative public data. The solution is implemented through a blockchain-based traceability platform developed by Almaviva, integrating official data from the National Agricultural Information System (SIAN). Supply chain events are recorded on a public blockchain and linked to physical products via technologies such as NFC tags, allowing consumers and authorities to verify provenance and history. By leveraging ministerial datasets already held by public administration, the system ensures accuracy and trustworthiness. The platform improves consumer confidence, reduces counterfeiting, and protects brand value. It enhances transparency, supports enforcement, and delivers economic benefits by safeguarding Italy’s agri-food excellence.
column8: Ministry of Agricultural, Food and Forestry Policies
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair
