column2: Payments on Madrid's buses with blockchain
column4: Madrid’s public transport system involves multiple operators and services, leading to fragmented onboarding, duplicated user accounts and inconsistent payment experiences. In partnership with Banco Santander and Vottun, the city sought to simplify access to public mobility while strengthening data security and interoperability across services. The objective of this pilot is to provide citizens with a single registration and payment interface covering buses, taxis and electric vehicle charging, reducing friction for users and operational complexity for providers. The solution is built around a blockchain-enabled certification layer that securely records user credentials and transactions, allowing a single digital identity and payment profile to be reused across transport services. Transaction records are cryptographically secured, while backend systems integrate with existing ticketing and payment infrastructures. The result is a unified mobility app that enables seamless payments and service access with enhanced data protection. Expected impacts include improved user experience, reduced onboarding costs, better interoperability between transport services and increased trust in digital payments, supporting Madrid’s transition towards integrated smart mobility services.

column2: Enabling the transparent exchange of medical records
column4: This use case addresses the fragmentation and limited patient control of medical records within healthcare systems. Patients and healthcare providers often struggle with data silos, delayed access to records, and unclear consent management. The Proof of Concept, conducted with Groves Medical Group in London, involves patients, doctors, and healthcare administrators. Its objective is to enable patients to securely manage, share, and monetise access to their medical records, while supporting flexible care delivery such as remote consultations. The solution is delivered through a blockchain-based health data platform where patients create digital wallets to manage permissions to their medical records. Health data references and access rights are recorded immutably, while clinical data remains securely stored off-chain. The platform integrates video consultation services and enables payments using digital tokens, incentivising the use of telemedicine services. The Proof of Concept enhances patient empowerment, data transparency, and trust between patients and clinicians. It improves access to care, supports new service models, and provides a scalable foundation for secure health data exchange, with positive implications for efficiency, user experience, and continuity of care.

column2: Validation of judicial expenses incurred during investigations
column4: Law enforcement agencies face complex administrative requirements when validating operational expenses, particularly in sensitive investigations where transparency must be balanced with confidentiality. The French Gendarmerie’s cybercrime division (C3N), together with defence and judicial authorities, launched this pilot to improve traceability and auditability of judicial expenses without exposing operational details. The objective is to simplify expense validation, ensure compliance, and enable secure oversight of funds used during investigations. The system uses smart contracts deployed on a public blockchain to manage authorised expense validation workflows. Only approved actors can interact with the contracts, which record transactions immutably and enable auditing of fund usage while abstracting sensitive operational information. Integration with internal financial systems allows expenses to be justified and reviewed efficiently. The pilot has reduced administrative burden, improved financial traceability, and strengthened accountability while preserving operational secrecy. It demonstrates how distributed ledger systems can support trusted financial governance in high-security public sector contexts.

column2: Blockchain-Based Training Certificates
column4: Employers and institutions often struggle to verify the authenticity of training certificates, while issuing bodies face increasing administrative workloads and fraud risks. The Chamber of Industry and Commerce of Munich and Upper Bavaria, in cooperation with the Bavarian Digital Ministry, launched this pilot to modernise vocational certificate issuance and verification. The objective is to enable instant, reliable validation of qualifications while simplifying processes for graduates, employers and certifying authorities. Certificates are issued digitally, with cryptographic hash values anchored on a blockchain. Graduates share a PDF certificate and a verification key, which employers can upload to a web application to instantly confirm authenticity against the ledger. The solution integrates with existing certification workflows without exposing personal data. The pilot improves trust in credentials, reduces verification time from days to seconds, lowers administrative costs, and enhances labour market efficiency. It provides a scalable foundation for broader adoption of digital credentials across education and training systems.
column8: The Chamber of Industry and Commerce of Munich and Upper Bavaria

column2: Copyright Management
column4: Copyright management requires handling vast volumes of metadata, rights information and transactions, often across fragmented systems that lack transparency and efficiency. The Italian Society of Authors and Publishers (SIAE), together with Algorand, launched this pilot to modernise copyright registration, rights tracking and remuneration processes. The objective is to create a transparent, secure and scalable ecosystem that better serves creators, publishers and rights holders while reducing disputes and administrative costs. The platform uses a public blockchain based on a pure Proof-of-Stake protocol to record rights metadata and transactions immutably. Smart contracts manage ownership, licensing events and revenue distribution, while system integrations connect existing rights management databases to the ledger. The pilot enhances traceability, improves data integrity and supports high transaction throughput. Expected impacts include faster rights registration, improved transparency for creators, reduced reconciliation costs, and a future-proof infrastructure for digital copyright management at national scale.
column8: Italian Society of Authors and Publishers (SIAE)

column2: Finding Waste Heat Spots
column4: Large amounts of industrial waste heat remain unused in cities, despite growing demand for sustainable district heating solutions. The HotCity’s pilot, backed by the Austrian government and deployed in Vienna and Graz, addresses this inefficiency by engaging citizens in identifying waste heat sources. Stakeholders include municipalities, energy providers, industrial operators and residents. The objective is to crowdsource reliable data on waste heat availability and integrate it into district heating planning, improving energy efficiency and reducing emissions. The pilot combines a digital platform with a blockchain-based incentive mechanism that records submissions and rewards contributors with tokens for validated data. Submitted location data is verified and aggregated to support urban energy planning. The approach improves data coverage at low cost while ensuring transparency and trust in contributions. The pilot demonstrates increased citizen engagement, better identification of usable waste heat, and potential reductions in CO₂ emissions by integrating industrial surplus heat into existing heating networks.
column8: Austrian Federal Ministry for Climate Action, Environment, Energy, Mobility, Innovation, and Technology
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Health Certificates To Address Lockdowns
column4: During the COVID-19 pandemic, governments, employers and event organisers required reliable ways to verify individuals’ health status in order to manage access to workplaces, transport and public spaces while limiting lockdowns. Existing paper-based certificates were difficult to verify at scale, easy to falsify and slow to distribute. Health n Go, developed by Swiss company TIXnGO, repurposed an existing digital ticketing infrastructure to address this challenge by enabling the secure issuance and presentation of health certificates. Stakeholders included healthcare providers, public authorities, organisations managing access control, and citizens needing proof of health status. The objective was to provide a scalable, secure and privacy-preserving mechanism for distributing trusted health certificates. The solution uses distributed ledger technology to issue each certificate with a unique, encrypted and traceable digital identity anchored on a blockchain. Certificates are stored in a mobile wallet on the user’s smartphone and can be presented via a QR code, which is scanned and verified against the ledger without revealing unnecessary personal data. The pilot enabled rapid deployment of digital health passes, reduced fraud risk, improved verification speed, and supported safer reopening strategies. It demonstrated the feasibility of decentralised digital certification in crisis response contexts.

column2: Medical Data Management
column4: VeChain co-developed a blockchain-based medical data management platform in partnership with digital healthcare solution firm I-Dante. The platform, dubbed “The E-NewHealthLife”, is being deployed at the Mediterranean Hospital of Cyprus. The first phase of the platform’s rollout will see it used in the emergency department of the Cyprus hospital, allowing patients to identify themselves, check waiting lists, and autonomously manage their medical records from their mobile phone.

column2: Tax Payments with bitcoins in the City of Zermatt
column4: Switzerland's canton system allows municipalities flexibility in payment methods, enabling innovative approaches to public finance that could position localities as attractive destinations for cryptocurrency businesses and residents. Zermatt became the second Swiss municipality after Zug to accept Bitcoin for tax payments, allowing residents to settle obligations up to CHF 250 (approximately €230) using cryptocurrency. The system operates through partnership with Bitcoin Suisse AG, which provides instant conversion to Swiss Francs, eliminating volatility risk for the municipal treasury whilst offering convenience for crypto-holding residents. All transactions maintain full regulatory compliance including anti-money laundering requirements. The initiative reflects Switzerland's broader approach of integrating cryptocurrency into mainstream financial services whilst maintaining regulatory oversight.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Blockchain-based union card
column4: The blockchain-based union card initiative aims to address challenges related to membership verification, data integrity, and secure communication between trade unions and their members. Traditional membership systems are vulnerable to duplication, outdated records, and privacy risks. The Italian trade union Fim Cisl, its members, and union administrators are the primary stakeholders. The objective is to ensure unique, tamper-proof membership registration while enabling trusted, ongoing digital engagement between the union and its members. The solution consists of a tokenised digital union card built on the Ethereum blockchain. Membership credentials are issued as digital tokens managed through mobile applications and wallets. Transactions and updates are recorded immutably, ensuring that each membership is uniquely registered and verifiable, while personal data is protected through cryptographic mechanisms. The initiative provides certified and transparent membership records, reduces the risk of fraud or data manipulation, and strengthens privacy protection. It also creates a secure digital channel for sharing information, announcements, and services, improving member engagement and enabling scalable, trusted union operations.

column2: Traceability of meat and milk
column4: Lombardy Region launched its own blockchain for the traceability of agri-food products in order to meet the needs of consumers and offer a new communication tool to companies. Consumers who find meat and milk products in supermarkets can trace the history of the package simply by scanning the QRcode printed on the label. This way they can have access to information pages containing public administration and companies data. For example, for a liter of milk it will be possible to know the results of the inspections of the regional health inspectors at the farms (use of drugs, health of the animals, hygiene of the structures, etc.) and at the processing plants. Not to mention the controls of third-party companies regarding, for example, animal welfare.
column8: Regione Lombardia per la trasformazione digitale
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain

column2: Digital baccalaureate diplomas
column4: This use case addresses the need for secure, verifiable, and easily shareable educational credentials. Paper-based diplomas are vulnerable to loss, forgery, and costly verification processes. The project involves Villa Flaminia International High Schools, students, and external stakeholders such as universities and employers. Its objective is to issue tamper-proof digital baccalaureate diplomas that certify both the final qualification and the student’s broader educational pathway. The solution issues digital diplomas certified on the public Ethereum platform. Key milestones of the student’s training process, including courses and subjects completed, are notarised and anchored to the ledger. Credentials can be shared digitally, with third parties able to verify authenticity without relying on intermediaries. The initiative improves trust and efficiency in credential verification, reduces administrative costs, and enhances student mobility. It delivers a better user experience for graduates and institutions while providing a scalable, reliable foundation for lifelong digital education records.

column2: Kickstarting the Financial Lives of Refugees in Finland
column4: Asylum seekers in Finland faced significant barriers accessing financial services, as traditional banking requires identity documentation that refugees often cannot provide, forcing reliance on cash and excluding them from the formal economy. The Finnish Immigration Service partnered with MONI, a Finnish fintech startup, to provide asylum seekers with prepaid Mastercards linked to blockchain-based digital identities, replacing cash-based support systems. Several thousand participants received cards enabling participation in the formal economy whilst building transaction histories that could support future creditworthiness assessments. The blockchain component provides identity verification without requiring traditional documentation, enabling financial inclusion for individuals whose identity records may be incomplete or inaccessible. MIT Technology Review recognised the initiative as a pioneering example of blockchain technology addressing humanitarian challenges.

column2: Online services for managing personnel salary accounts
column4: Managing personnel salary accounts across public administrations requires the secure handling and exchange of highly sensitive personal and financial data, while complying with strict legal, privacy, and sovereignty constraints. In Italy, the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the Ministry of Interior must cooperate to process salary information, yet fragmented private cloud infrastructures and rigid data-sharing mechanisms limit efficiency and transparency. The aim of this use case is to enable seamless inter-ministerial collaboration, optimise the use of existing IT resources, and ensure that sensitive data remains protected and under institutional control throughout its lifecycle. The solution is delivered through the SUNFISH platform, which provides Federation-as-a-Service capabilities to securely interconnect heterogeneous private cloud environments. A blockchain-empowered service ledger governs service registration, access control, and accountability across the federation, while sensitive data processing is executed within authorised cloud domains. This approach enables secure resource sharing without centralising data. The demonstrator confirms improved infrastructure utilisation, reduced operational complexity, and secure cross-ministry data exchange, offering a scalable and compliant model for handling sensitive public sector information.
column11: Transfers of a general character between different levels of government.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Use of Public Cloud PaaS to host SaaS and/or to integrate with commercial SaaS solutions
column4: Modernising fiscal services requires public administrations to exploit the scalability of public cloud platforms while preserving strict compliance with secrecy, privacy, and data protection regulations. In Malta, the Ministry for Finance and the Taxation Department must collect, validate, and process data from taxpayers, employers, banks, and SMEs, yet traditional systems constrain integration and responsiveness. The objective of this use case is to simplify interactions between citizens and government, improve service agility, and securely integrate governmental applications with external service providers. The solution relies on the SUNFISH platform to federate public cloud Platform-as-a-Service environments hosting government applications with commercial Software-as-a-Service solutions, such as payroll systems. Secure federation mechanisms enforce policies, control data flows, and ensure regulatory compliance across environments. The results include enhanced scalability, improved interoperability, reduced operational overhead, and faster deployment of digital services, demonstrating how public cloud adoption can strengthen efficiency and service quality in the public sector.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Organised Crime Unit: Secure Cloud Storage for Data
column4: Handling cybercrime evidence demands strict control over data locality, access rights, and confidentiality, while enabling effective collaboration across law enforcement agencies. The South East Regional Cyber Crime Unit must store sensitive investigation data on its own premises but also grant regulated access to other Regional Cyber Crime Units and national bodies during active cases. Conventional sharing models risk exposing sensitive information or breaching jurisdictional constraints. The goal of this use case is to support secure, privacy-preserving cooperation without centralising or duplicating data. The SUNFISH platform provides Secure Multiparty Computation services based on advanced cryptographic techniques, allowing authorised searches and operations to be performed on encrypted data without revealing its content. Data remains local, while access policies are enforced across federated cloud environments. The Proof of Concept demonstrates privacy-preserving cross-cloud data sharing, improved investigative collaboration, and stronger protection of sensitive evidence, establishing a robust and scalable framework for secure law enforcement operations.
column8: South East Regional Cyber Crime Unit (SEROCU)
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Corporate Registry in commercial court
column4: French commercial court clerks uses a blockchain-based platform to record changes in companies’ legal status within the country. The blockchain network was jointly developed by IBM and the National Council of Clerks (NCC). The blockchain platform is built on the Hyperledger Fabric framework and aims to increase transparency and efficiency in legal transactions associated with the lifecycle of companies in the registry. Specifically, the solution will be used to record and share data related to the exchanges of regulatory information related to companies' difficulties, as well as the changes of status of the company registered on the French territory. That includes such data as change of corporate name, registration court office, establishment of branch offices, and business dissolution. In a Proof of Concept, NCC reportedly managed to shorten the time needed to update the registry from several days to a single day.

column2: Verifiable and tamper-proof university degree
column4: Private universities in Italy sought differentiation through technological innovation in credential management, recognising that blockchain verification could provide competitive advantages in demonstrating institutional credibility to employers. Link Campus University in Rome implemented blockchain-certified academic degrees, storing cryptographic hashes on a distributed ledger to enable instant verification by employers and other institutions worldwide. The system allows graduates to share their credentials digitally with verifiable proof of authenticity, eliminating requirements for physical document copies or institutional confirmation letters. This digital sharing mechanism accelerates hiring processes whilst reducing administrative costs for both the university and credential verifiers. The initiative demonstrates private sector leadership in educational technology adoption within the Italian higher education system.

column2: Smart Working management with blockchain in the Municipality of Bari
column4: Public administrations faced organisational and accountability challenges when adopting remote and flexible working arrangements, particularly in tracking activities, authorisations and compliance with internal rules. The Municipality of Bari launched this pilot within the H2020 CITADEL project to explore new ways of managing smart working for municipal employees. Stakeholders included municipal managers, employees participating in agile working, and IT service providers. The objective was to test a transparent and trustworthy digital system to manage work authorisations, activities and reporting while supporting organisational flexibility. The solution involved developing a prototype based on distributed ledger technology to record work agreements, task assignments and activity logs in a tamper-resistant manner. Smart contracts formalised working arrangements and ensured that records could not be altered unilaterally. The system integrated with existing administrative tools while providing a shared, auditable record of smart working activities. The pilot improved transparency, reduced administrative disputes, and increased trust between employees and management. It also demonstrated how public administrations can adopt flexible working models while maintaining accountability, offering a replicable approach for digital transformation in public sector workforce management.

column2: Verification of the authenticity of university degrees
column4: Universities and employers face growing challenges in verifying academic qualifications due to increasing diploma fraud and time-consuming manual validation processes. The University of St. Gallen introduced a pilot to enable near-instant verification of degree authenticity, benefiting graduates, employers and academic institutions. The objective was to reduce verification times from days to seconds while strengthening trust in academic credentials. The solution was developed with Swiss startup BlockFactory and relies on issuing digital diplomas whose cryptographic fingerprints are anchored on a public blockchain. Graduates receive a digital certificate that can be shared electronically, while verifiers upload the certificate to a validation interface that checks its integrity against the blockchain record. The system integrates with the university’s degree management processes without exposing sensitive personal data on-chain. The pilot significantly reduced administrative workload, accelerated recruitment and admissions processes, and enhanced protection against fraudulent diplomas. It demonstrated a scalable and cost-effective approach to digital credential verification that supports international mobility and trust in higher education qualifications.

column2: Flexible energy demand through blockchain
column4: Creating more efficient and sustainable energy systems requires mechanisms that align consumption with local production while rewarding flexible demand. In Bologna, citizens and companies participating in a local energy community face limited incentives to adapt energy usage patterns, despite growing pressure to reduce costs and emissions. The GECO project brings together public bodies, research institutions, and local partners with the objective of enabling flexible energy demand and strengthening community-based energy models. The solution uses a blockchain-based infrastructure to record and validate energy production, consumption, and exchanges among participants, ensuring transparency and trust in shared data. Reduced tariffs and incentives are managed through trusted digital records accessible to community members. The pilot demonstrates improved coordination between producers and consumers, greater transparency in energy flows, lower energy costs, and a replicable model for smart, decentralised energy communities supporting sustainability objectives.
column8: Agency for Energy and Sustainable Development (AESS)
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Enhancing intermodal freight movements in Marseille
column4: Intermodal freight transport along major logistics corridors involves numerous actors, documents and handovers, often resulting in data silos, delays and limited visibility across the supply chain. The Marseille Fos Port Authority launched a proof of concept to improve coordination and data sharing along the Mediterranean–Rhône–Saône corridor. Stakeholders included port authorities, logistics operators, customs, shipping companies and inland transport providers. The objective was to enhance efficiency, transparency and trust in intermodal freight movements. The solution uses a blockchain-based data-sharing platform that allows authorised users to access protected logistics information, such as customs documentation, container status, weight, origin and destination, through controlled permissions. Data entries are recorded immutably, ensuring traceability across transport modes. The platform integrates with existing port and logistics systems, acting as a shared reference layer rather than replacing operational tools. The pilot improved information availability, reduced duplication of documents, and facilitated smoother coordination between actors. It demonstrated the potential for distributed ledgers to support resilient and efficient logistics ecosystems in complex port environments.

column2: University certificates verification in many Spanish Universities
column4: Spanish universities have faced increasing risks of academic credential falsification and administrative burdens in verifying qualifications for employers and international institutions. Three universities—the University of Murcia, the Polytechnic University of Cartagena and the San Antonio Catholic University—launched a joint pilot to test a shared digital standard for certificate verification. Stakeholders included universities, students, employers and accreditation bodies. The objective was to minimise diploma fraud while simplifying verification processes across institutions. The solution is based on a distributed ledger infrastructure where academic certificates are issued digitally and registered as immutable records. Graduates can share their credentials electronically, and verifiers can confirm authenticity against the ledger without contacting the issuing university. The system supports interoperability between institutions through a common certification standard. The pilot reduced verification times, lowered administrative costs, and strengthened trust in academic qualifications. It also demonstrated how multiple universities can collaborate on a shared digital infrastructure, paving the way for scalable national or regional credential verification systems.
column8: University of Murcia, Polytechnic University of Cartagena (UPCT), and the San Antonio Catholic University

column2: Verification of COVID-19 testing with blockchain in Austria
column4: Ensuring the authenticity of health credentials became a critical challenge during the COVID-19 pandemic, as public authorities faced fraud, misinformation, and growing concerns over data privacy. In Austria, the QualiSig project addresses these issues by enabling citizens to securely verify COVID-19 testing and related health information while retaining full control over their personal data. The initiative involves public authorities and leverages the national digital identity framework to support trusted interactions. The solution combines encrypted communications, decentralised data storage, and qualified digital signatures applied via mobile devices. Verifiable records are anchored on the Ardor blockchain using the Ignis tokenisation system, ensuring integrity and tamper resistance. The prototypes demonstrate improved fraud prevention, enhanced trust in digital health information, and strong privacy guarantees, delivering reliable, user-centric verification mechanisms for critical public health processes.
column8: Federal Ministry for Digital and Economic Affairs

column2: Loans and insurance services requests simplification
column4: Public administration employees in Italy rely on complex processes to request loans and insurance services involving salary withholdings, requiring extensive coordination between the Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF), financial institutions and employees. These processes were slow, fragmented and prone to reconciliation errors. CreditoNet 2.0 was launched to modernise and simplify these interactions. Stakeholders include MEF, public employees, banks, insurance companies and payroll service providers. The objective is to streamline requests, improve data security and accelerate reconciliation. The solution uses SIAChain, a blockchain network involving major Italian banks, to create a shared, trusted infrastructure for exchanging and validating loan and insurance data. Requests, approvals and payroll withholdings are recorded on the ledger, enabling automated checks and reducing manual intervention. Integration with existing MEF and financial systems ensures continuity of operations. The pilot improved processing speed, reduced errors and reconciliation times, enhanced data security, and enabled a more transparent and efficient service model. It illustrates how distributed ledger infrastructures can modernise complex financial workflows in public administration.
column8: Direzione dei Sistemi Informativi e dell’Innovazione (DSII), Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF)

column2: Company Passport
column4: As of 1 August 2021, European citizens are supposed to be able to set up a company entirely digitally. With that in mind, the Royal Dutch Association of Civil-Law Notaries (KNB) asked the Dutch Blockchain Coalition to help think about the role of blockchain in the digital process for founding a company. Together with representatives of the KNB, the chambers of commerce, ABN AMRO, Pwc and the Tax and Customs Administration, the concept of an ‘enterprise passport’ has been worked out.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Carrière Wallet
column4: Career Wallets are apps that let individuals manage their own digitally validated diplomas and other proofs of qualifications, certificates of good conduct and so forth, letting them be presented e.g. to employers when they start work. Such wallets give the employees greater control over their own details and credentials, as well as a secure way of storing such data digitally. Various practical tests are already underway. About forty large employers are collaborating on this with Rabobank and Randstad. The UWV (Employee Insurance Administration Agency) is running an ongoing practical test with the Municipality of Rotterdam, and the Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations (BZK) is currently developing a wallet for the European Blockchain Infrastructure allowing e.g. verified credentials for diplomas to be shared.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: uNLock the world
column4: In February 2020, various well-known organisations from the commercial and scientific sectors joined forces in uNLock. This initiative led to a digital solution being created that lets members of the public themselves share coronavirus test results in a trusted and digitally secure way, giving them access to “bubbles”– controlled environments where the risk of coronavirus infection is very low. The digital proof of a negative test result is the final element that lets an event venue create a controlled bubble.

column2: My pension rights transfer
column4: The Mijnwaardeoverdacht.nl platform (My pension rights transfer) is the first blockchain application internationally in the pensions domain. The prototype was developed jointly with several large pension providers and a team of platform developers at APG, under the name Hyfen. You can use this platform to transfer pensions rights much more quickly and simply – and safely. This application searches for pensions for you and immediately gives you the option of looking at a proposal for transferring the value. All the main elements are covered in the proposal, letting you make a proper comparison between the two pensions arrangements. The platform has been developed with a modular structure that allows the administrative systems of pensions sector parties to be connected together. After a technology assessment was carried out, a combination of proven technology (such as databases and cloud infrastructure) combined with distributed ledger technology (DLT) was chosen for implementing the pension rights transfer platform. DLT is essentially a kind of records system that the information can be stored in. In this case, DLT is only part of the overall solution and is used specifically where it can add value, namely in creating a shared understanding for the parties.

column2: Electronic bill of lading (eBL)
column4: Singapore and the Netherlands have collaborated since 2019 to explore electronic bills of lading (eBL) on the Europe-Far East trade lane where Singapore and Rotterdam are two major transhipment ports. This trial demonstrated that an eBL issued by one platform could be verified and processed by another digital trade platform. Before we can start making extensive use of this new option, countries will need to adapt their legislation to accept an electronic bill of lading (eBL) as legally valid in addition to conventional hard copies.
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Energy trading platform
column4: Local electricity markets face increasing complexity due to decentralised renewable generation, fluctuating demand, and grid constraints that traditional market mechanisms struggle to manage in real time. In Germany, energy producers, consumers, grid operators, and market authorities require more flexible trading models to balance local surpluses and bottlenecks efficiently while maintaining system stability. The BEST project, funded by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy and led by the Reiner Lemoine Institute, aims to design a decentralised electricity market framework that enables local energy trading and optimised grid usage. The objective is to support real-time balancing of supply and demand at local level, reducing congestion and improving the integration of renewable energy sources. The solution is implemented through a blockchain-based electricity market provider system (SMBS) that records transactions, matches local buyers and sellers, and manages market rules in a decentralised manner. Smart contracts support automated settlement and coordination between participants, while grid and metering data feeds enable near real-time operation. The project demonstrates improved market transparency, faster settlement, and more efficient use of local energy resources. Expected impacts include reduced grid congestion, lower balancing costs, enhanced renewable integration, and a scalable model for future decentralised energy markets.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Privacy engine
column4: Citizens interacting with Antwerp's digital services lacked control over their personal data stored across multiple government databases, whilst GDPR requirements demanded new approaches to consent management and data portability. Digipolis Antwerp launched the Blockchain on the Move project in partnership with Jolocom (Berlin), V-ICT-OR, and the Flemish Government under the Programme for Innovation Procurement to develop Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) infrastructure. The system enables citizens to manage their own identity data and control which government agencies can access specific information, with initial applications focusing on change of address processes. The initiative forms part of Digipolis's broader ACPaaS (Antwerp City Platform as a Service) ecosystem and includes GDPR management tools and a Business Rules Engine for access rights. By returning data control to citizens, the project demonstrates how blockchain technology can support privacy-by-design principles in public services.

column2: Digital talent profile
column4: Children’s talents and personal interests are often recorded informally and remain fragmented across schools, youth organisations and associations, limiting the ability of educators and supervisors to nurture strengths consistently over time. The City of Antwerp introduced the Digital Talent Profile to address this challenge by creating a trusted way to collect, represent and share children’s talents beyond organisational boundaries. Stakeholders include children, parents, educators, youth organisations and municipal services. The objective is to empower children by giving them ownership over how their talents are documented and shared, while enabling supervisors to better support personal development through a holistic and portable profile. The solution is implemented using a decentralised digital identity approach based on distributed ledger technology. Individual talent statements are captured as claims, supported by proofs and attestations issued by trusted actors, each recorded with a unique and immutable timestamp. These records are managed through digital wallets that allow children, with appropriate guardianship, to control consent, scope and duration of data sharing. The pilot improves trust, data portability and privacy, reduces duplication of assessments, and enhances collaboration across youth services. It delivers qualitative benefits in empowerment and personalisation and provides a scalable foundation for citizen-centric identity services.
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: European Social Security Pass
column4: Cross-border mobility within the European Union creates significant challenges for the verification of social security coverage, as citizens moving between Member States must rely on fragmented, paper-based, or slow administrative procedures. Public authorities, employers, and mobile citizens face delays, errors, and uncertainty in proving entitlement to social security rights. The European Social Security Pass pilot addresses these issues by exploring a digital solution for secure cross-border verification of social security status and identity. Launched in 2021, the initiative involves around a dozen Member States and aims to assess technical, organisational, and legal feasibility ahead of possible large-scale deployment. The solution is developed using the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI), the EU-wide platform designed to support trusted cross-border public services. Social security credentials and identity attributes are issued and verified through decentralised mechanisms, enabling citizens to manage their data while authorities can reliably validate entitlements across borders. Interoperability with national systems and compliance with EU data protection rules are built into the design. The pilot demonstrates faster verification processes, reduced administrative burden, improved fraud prevention, and greater user control. It lays the groundwork for a more efficient, secure, and citizen-centric approach to social security coordination across Europe.

column2: BIMChain: the blockchain application for managing BIM files
column4: Building Information Modelling projects involve multiple actors exchanging complex digital files throughout design, construction and operation phases, often leading to disputes over file ownership, versioning and responsibilities. Italferr developed BIMChain to address transparency and trust issues in collaborative BIM environments. Stakeholders include infrastructure designers, engineers, contractors, clients and asset managers. The objective is to ensure traceability, accountability and trust in the lifecycle management of BIM files while supporting collaborative workflows. BIMChain integrates distributed ledger technology into BIM processes to create a shared environment governed by smart contracts. Each BIM file and modification is associated with a verifiable digital identity, recording who performed which action, with what role and at what time. Smart contracts regulate access rights, responsibilities and exchange conditions between parties, while metadata and transaction logs are anchored immutably to the ledger. The solution integrates with existing BIM tools without duplicating large files on-chain. The pilot improves collaboration efficiency, reduces disputes, enhances auditability and supports compliance in complex infrastructure projects. It demonstrates how blockchain can strengthen governance and trust in digital construction ecosystems.

column2: Diploma Notarisation
column4: Educational institutions and employers require reliable mechanisms to verify academic diplomas, while issuing bodies must protect against falsification and disputes over authenticity. ICE, the Italian Trade Agency, launched a pilot to certify diplomas issued for the 52nd CORCE “Fausto De Franceschi” Master programme in internationalisation. Stakeholders include graduates, ICE, academic partners and employers. The objective is to guarantee authenticity, date certainty and immutability of issued diplomas, while simplifying verification processes. The solution uses distributed ledger technology to notarise diploma data by anchoring cryptographic representations of certificates on a shared ledger. Each notarised record provides proof of existence, authorship and integrity without exposing personal data publicly. Verification can be performed independently by authorised parties by comparing diploma data against the ledger record. The pilot reduces verification time, strengthens trust in qualifications, and lowers administrative overhead for issuing and verifying entities. It also provides graduates with portable, verifiable credentials, demonstrating the potential of blockchain-based notarisation for education and professional certification.
column8: ICE - Agenzia per la promozione all'estero e l'internazionalizzazione delle imprese italiane

column2: A blockchain platform for public administration
column4: Public administrations and trade unions often rely on paper-based or fragmented digital procedures for approvals, negotiations and certifications, limiting transparency and slowing decision-making. Blockchain Italia developed a blockchain-based platform for the Federazione Lavoratori Pubblici e Funzioni Pubbliche (FLP) to support secure and auditable administrative processes. Stakeholders include trade unions, public employees, employers and administrative bodies. The objective is to enable legally robust, transparent and remote execution of administrative and contractual procedures. The platform applies distributed ledger technology to notarise decisions and procedural steps, including budget approvals, remote trade union conciliation, supplementary collective agreements and diploma notarisation. Each action is recorded immutably, creating a shared and verifiable process history accessible to authorised parties. Integration with existing organisational workflows allows gradual adoption without disrupting operations. The pilot improves procedural transparency, reduces delays, enables remote participation and strengthens trust among parties. It illustrates how blockchain platforms can modernise governance processes in public administration and labour relations.
column8: Federazione Lavoratori Pubblici e Funzioni Pubbliche

column2: Secure Digital Identities
column4: Digital services across sectors require secure and interoperable identity solutions, yet fragmented identity systems create barriers to cross-sector and cross-border services. The IDunion cooperative was established to develop a shared self-sovereign identity ecosystem in Germany, supported by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy. Stakeholders include technology providers, public authorities, businesses and citizens. The objective is to establish a production-ready identity network supporting people, organisations and devices across multiple domains such as education, mobility, e-health and industry. The solution is based on self-sovereign identity principles using distributed ledger infrastructure to anchor decentralised identifiers and verifiable credentials. Identity wallets allow users to store and present credentials issued by trusted parties, while a test network supports interoperability and governance across partners. The pilot improves identity security, user control and interoperability, reduces dependency on central identity providers and enables new digital services. With a functioning test network and multiple applications, IDunion provides a scalable foundation for trusted digital identity ecosystems.
column8: German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: Swiss Ledger: Private, institutional and non-profit blockchain network
column4: Swiss institutions required secure and transparent methods for managing digital assets and credentials that met the country's high standards for financial services and data protection. Swiss Ledger was established as a private, institutional, and non-profit blockchain infrastructure providing distributed ledger services to Swiss organisations across sectors including finance, government, and academia. The platform enables secure credential management, asset tokenisation, and data integrity verification whilst maintaining compliance with Swiss regulatory requirements. Operating since 2021, Swiss Ledger provides a permissioned blockchain environment where participating organisations can issue and verify digital credentials, track assets, and maintain auditable records. The infrastructure supports Switzerland's broader digital transformation objectives whilst preserving the privacy and security standards that characterise Swiss financial and institutional services.

column2: National Green Pass
column4: The San Marino Digital Covid Certificate covers vaccination against Sars-Cov2, past infection or negative test result. The certificate issued has a validity of 1 week starting 15 days after the first vaccination dose or 9 months from the completed vaccination cycle. The certificate is issued upon personal request, in a paper or digital format, by the ISS or facilities authorized by the San Marino health authorities. The San Marino Digital Covid Certificate, using a public key for issuance and validation, has been set up as a national infrastructure at San Marino Innovation S.p.A. This ensures the integrity, authenticity and control of the data contained therein. The entity temporarily performs the functions of Country Signing Certification Authority (CSCA) and is in charge of issuing the Root Certificate for the generation of public and private keys necessary for signing the digital certificates. According to the Ministry of Health and Social Security, the extremely efficient development is owed fully to the multi-layered, fruitful collaboration between San Marino’s public entities and private partners DNV and VeChain.

column2: PlanetWatch: monitoring air quality
column4: Traditional air quality monitoring relies on expensive governmental stations providing limited geographic coverage and delayed data publication, leaving many pollution hotspots undetected and populations without timely warnings. PlanetWatch, a French startup and CERN spin-off, developed a global air quality monitoring network using the Algorand blockchain to create the first immutable ledger for environmental data. The platform incentivises individuals and organisations to deploy low-cost sensors through PLANET token rewards, with over 25,000 sensors deployed globally by late 2021. Initial deployments in Milan and Taranto, Italy, demonstrated the system's capability to provide dense, real-time monitoring complementing official stations at a fraction of the cost. Data collected undergoes validation through CERN's C2MON acquisition framework before blockchain recording, ensuring reliability. The Algorand Foundation supported the initiative through grants, recognising its environmental and public health potential.

column2: Blockchain service infrastructure
column4: Italy lacked a national blockchain infrastructure for public services, limiting the country's ability to participate in European initiatives and deploy distributed ledger solutions for citizen services at scale. IBSI (Italian Blockchain Service Infrastructure) was launched in January 2021 by a consortium including AgID, Poste Italiane, INAIL, INPS, Sogei, Politecnico di Milano, University of Cagliari, and others to create Italy's first national blockchain network for public services. The project aims to develop applications supporting digital certificates, Made in Italy supply chain traceability, and sustainable energy models using Distributed Ledger Technology. IBSI operates in synergy with the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI), with Italy receiving €149,928 through the Blockchain4Italy project for EBSI node deployment. The infrastructure implements the Once Only principle, enabling citizens and businesses to provide data once for reuse across public administrations.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Pharmaledger: blockchain enabled healthcare
column4: The healthcare sector faces persistent challenges related to fragmented data, complex supply chains, lengthy drug development processes, and limited trust between stakeholders. Pharmaceutical companies, regulators, healthcare providers, and patients require secure and interoperable systems to exchange information while ensuring transparency, compliance, and data protection. PharmaLedger addresses these needs through a large-scale collaborative initiative funded under the Innovative Medicines Initiative, bringing together pharmaceutical industry leaders, public bodies, research institutions, SMEs, and patient organisations. The project aims to enable a trusted digital platform supporting healthcare processes across three core areas: pharmaceutical supply chains, clinical trials, and health data management. The platform is implemented using distributed ledger technologies to ensure data integrity, traceability, and controlled access among authorised participants. Smart contracts and decentralised governance models support shared processes, while sensitive data is managed in compliance with regulatory and ethical requirements. PharmaLedger demonstrates how shared digital infrastructure can reduce inefficiencies, improve collaboration, and shorten development timelines. The expected impact includes reduced healthcare costs, improved drug traceability, faster and more transparent clinical trials, and enhanced trust across the healthcare ecosystem, providing a scalable foundation for digital transformation in the sector.
column8: Innovative Medicines Initiative (IMI) and the European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations (EFPIA)
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Public procurement: streamlined and faster digital procedures
column4: Public procurement processes are often characterised by complex documentation, fragmented qualification checks and slow administrative procedures, increasing costs and delaying projects. This pilot, proposed by UnionSOA and IBM, aims to fully digitise qualification and documentation management in public procurement. Stakeholders include contracting authorities, suppliers, qualification bodies and oversight institutions. The objective is to simplify administrative processes, ensure legal certainty and improve transparency across the procurement chain. The solution uses a blockchain platform deployed on IBM Cloud, structured around notarisation and notification services. Qualification documents and dossiers are notarised on the ledger, providing proof of existence, originality and provenance, while each procedural step is recorded immutably to ensure traceability. Authorised actors can access validated information without repeated submissions. The pilot reduces processing times, lowers administrative burden, improves auditability and supports faster, more reliable procurement procedures. It demonstrates how blockchain can enable end-to-end digital transformation in public procurement systems.
column8: Associazione Nazionale Società Organismi di Attestazione - Unionsoa

column2: Ecocoin: encouraging virtuous behavior
column4: Encouraging citizens to adopt sustainable behaviours such as recycling and low-emission mobility requires mechanisms that can reliably measure actions and translate them into tangible rewards. Municipalities often struggle to track these activities transparently and at scale, while citizens lack incentives that are trusted and easy to use. The Ecocoin pilot was created to address this gap by assigning concrete value to virtuous environmental actions within a local circular economy. Stakeholders include citizens, municipal waste collectors, local authorities and affiliated commercial partners. The objective is to promote sustainable behaviour while strengthening local economic circuits. The solution combines interconnected sensors used by waste collectors to measure recycling activities with a blockchain-based ledger that records verified actions immutably. Citizens create a digital profile with an associated wallet; when waste is delivered and measured, tokens are generated automatically and credited to the wallet. These tokens can then be spent within participating local networks. The pilot improves transparency, prevents manipulation of data, and builds trust in incentive mechanisms. It delivers qualitative benefits in citizen engagement, supports measurable environmental outcomes, and offers a scalable model for municipalities seeking to reward sustainability while reinforcing local economies.

column2: Blockchain for emergency services
column4: Emergency services require rapid, secure access to physical spaces to save lives and protect property, yet traditional key management and access authorisation processes often cause delays. In the Polish city of Olsztyn, police, fire, and ambulance services faced operational inefficiencies when responding to incidents requiring immediate building access. The objective of this use case is to improve response times and operational effectiveness by enabling authorised emergency crews to access buildings without manual key handovers or administrative delays. The city conducted a pilot using a blockchain-based access management solution known as SmartKey. The system connects smart contracts deployed on the Ethereum network with physical IoT devices installed in buildings and emergency vehicles. Access rights are managed digitally and activated automatically during emergencies, allowing authorised responders to unlock doors securely and auditable. The trial demonstrates faster emergency response, reduced operational friction, and improved coordination between services. Qualitative benefits include enhanced safety for citizens and responders, greater accountability through tamper-proof access logs, and a scalable model that can be extended to other cities and critical infrastructure environments.
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: Blockchain-based service to register digital acts at the Court of Cremona
column4: Judicial systems rely on secure, reliable, and timely registration of legal documents, yet many courts still depend on paper-based or fragmented digital processes that hinder efficiency and continuity, particularly during emergencies. At the Court of Cremona, the Gip-Gup section faced the risk of operational disruption during the COVID-19 lockdown, threatening delays in hearings and case backlogs. The objective of this use case was to ensure continuity of judicial activities while strengthening the integrity and traceability of digital legal documents. In collaboration with Stampiz, the court implemented a service to register digital acts such as judgments, files, copies, and certificates. The solution anchors cryptographic proofs of documents on a blockchain while keeping the files stored securely on the court’s internal servers, ensuring anonymity and preventing unauthorised access. Registration is fully digital and does not require moving files outside the court’s infrastructure. The initiative enabled uninterrupted hearings during the pandemic, accelerated recovery of postponed cases, and improved reliability of registry operations. It demonstrates increased efficiency, stronger evidential integrity, and a robust, scalable approach to digital justice administration.

column2: Digital credentials for graduates at University of Lille
column4: France's largest public university faced substantial administrative burden from diploma verification requests whilst graduates experienced delays obtaining proof of qualifications for employment or further study. The University of Lille partnered with BCdiploma to implement blockchain-based digital credentials through the Dem-Attest project, issuing bilingual (French/English) certificates that are tamper-proof and valid for life. Since July 2021, the university has issued over 80,000 blockchain certificates covering 700 diploma types at approximately 20,000 per year. The system uses the Avalanche blockchain with an estimated energy footprint of just 0.025g CO2 per credential. The University of Lille leads the Fr.EBSI project integrating with the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure and participates in EBSI-VECTOR for W3C Verifiable Credentials deployment. Online verification has been accessed over 132,000 times, demonstrating strong adoption by employers and institutions.

column2: EnergieKnip, Energy Wallet
column4: European student mobility programmes like Erasmus faced administrative friction from credential verification requirements, with universities unable to efficiently validate transcripts and qualifications from partner institutions across borders. The EBSI multi-University pilot, launched through the Early Adopters Programme in 2021, brought together 11 universities from 11 countries and 2 European university alliances to test cross-border Verifiable Credentials for education. Six scenarios were developed including Erasmus transcript validation, cross-border PhD applications, European student card verification, and diploma recognition for employment. The pilot demonstrated end-to-end exchange of W3C-standard credentials via EBSI-compliant digital wallets, with institutions including KU Leuven and Italian universities testing the Belgium-Italy corridor. The EBSI-VECTOR project (2023-2025) expanded these pilots across 52 partners in 20 countries, advancing toward production deployment of decentralised educational credentials.

column2: Data Valorisation Services
column4: Smart cities generate vast volumes of data through IoT platforms, yet much of this data remains underused due to limited trust, unclear ownership, and fragmented access mechanisms. In Santander, municipal datasets produced by smart city infrastructures offer significant potential value for public administrations, businesses, and citizens, but require a trusted framework to enable secure sharing and reuse. This use case, developed within the EU-funded TOKEN project, aims to unlock the economic and social value of municipal data while ensuring transparency, accountability, and data governance. Stakeholders include the municipality, data providers, service developers, and data consumers, all seeking reliable access to high-quality urban data. The solution introduces a blockchain-based data marketplace where datasets generated by IoT platforms are registered, described, and exchanged under clear usage conditions. Distributed ledgers record data transactions, access rights, and usage policies, while smart contracts govern permissions and potential monetisation schemes. Data itself remains off-chain, ensuring scalability and compliance. The results include improved trust in data sharing, new opportunities for data-driven services, and more efficient reuse of public data assets. The initiative demonstrates how cities can foster innovation ecosystems while maintaining control, transparency, and public value creation.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: The red button: debt pause button
column4: Individuals facing problem debt often experience escalating stress due to continuous collection actions, while municipalities require time to assess and implement sustainable debt restructuring solutions. In Amsterdam, the “Red Button” pilot was introduced as an innovative instrument in debt counselling to temporarily pause debt collection, giving debtors space to stabilise their situation. Stakeholders include indebted citizens, municipal debt counselling services, courts and collection agencies such as the Central Judicial Collection Agency. The objective is to reduce pressure on vulnerable individuals while enabling more effective and durable financial solutions. The mechanism allows a temporary, legally recognised suspension of collection activities during counselling periods. Digital coordination between municipal services and enforcement bodies ensures that the pause is applied consistently and transparently. The pilot demonstrated improved cooperation between institutions, reduced stress for debtors, and better engagement in counselling processes. Qualitative results suggest higher chances of successful debt restructuring and more humane public debt management. The approach offers a replicable policy tool for municipalities aiming to balance enforcement with social support.
column8: The municipalities of Amsterdam, The Hague and Eindhoven

column2: Self Sovereign Attendancy
column4: Attendance registration at events, classes and conferences is commonly handled through paper lists that expose personal data, are vulnerable to manipulation and fail to meet data protection requirements. The Self-Sovereign Attendance pilot addresses this operational and compliance challenge by rethinking how attendance is recorded and verified. Stakeholders include event organisers, educational institutions, attendees and auditing bodies. The objective is to provide a secure, privacy-preserving and fraud-resistant alternative to traditional attendance lists. The solution enables participants to check in through a decentralised application built on a distributed ledger infrastructure. Attendance records are cryptographically secured, immutable and accessible only to authorised parties, ensuring that personal data is protected while proof of attendance remains verifiable. The proof of concept demonstrated reliable check-in, resistance to data tampering and improved compliance with data protection rules. The pilot enhances trust and fairness for both organisers and participants and provides a scalable foundation for attendance management across education and events.

column2: Generating Health Certificates
column4: Local authorities are required to verify compliance with health regulations while protecting sensitive personal information, particularly in contexts involving vulnerable groups. In Oldenburg, health organisations faced challenges with fake or invalid registration certificates required under the German Prostitutes Protection Act. The pilot aimed to ensure authenticity and integrity of health certificates while preserving privacy. Stakeholders include municipal health authorities, registered individuals, and regulatory bodies. The objective was to provide a reliable way to prove that health certificates are valid and up to date without exposing personal data. The solution uses a distributed ledger to issue and verify certificates, ensuring that each record is traceable to its source and cannot be altered. A privacy-preserving design allows verification of certificate validity without revealing underlying personal details. The pilot improved trust in certificates, reduced fraud risk and simplified verification procedures. It demonstrated a scalable and secure approach to regulatory compliance in sensitive public health contexts.

column2: Healthy on the Blockchain
column4: Employers seeking to promote healthier lifestyles among staff often face resistance due to concerns about data privacy and misuse of personal health information. The municipality of Roeselare and HOWEST University launched this pilot to encourage preventive health and physical activity among municipal employees while safeguarding trust. Stakeholders include employees, health coaches, the municipality and academic partners. The objective is to stimulate healthy behaviour without granting employers access to sensitive personal data. Wearable devices track physical activity, and progress is visualised through an application that connects trainees with personal coaches. Blockchain technology is used to secure health data and control access, ensuring that employees retain ownership of their information. The pilot increased participation in wellness programmes, improved trust in data handling, and reduced privacy concerns. It delivers qualitative benefits in employee wellbeing and organisational culture and provides a replicable model for privacy-preserving workplace health initiatives.

column2: Blockchain-based Mobiliti App
column4: Business parks often lack efficient transport connections, limiting access for students and job seekers and increasing reliance on private cars. The Mobiliti pilot was developed to address accessibility challenges in the Deinze business park by enabling trusted ride sharing. Stakeholders include students, employers, local authorities and employment agencies. The objective is to improve accessibility while promoting sustainable mobility. The application provides a ride-sharing platform where participants can coordinate trips securely. Blockchain technology underpins identity and transaction trust, ensuring that participants can share rides with confidence while protecting personal data. The pilot improved mobility options, reduced barriers to employment and education, and supported more sustainable commuting. It demonstrates how decentralised trust mechanisms can enable collaborative mobility services in local economic zones.
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: Blockchain for Maritime Ports
column4: Maritime ports involve complex logistics chains where fragmented information systems hinder efficiency, transparency and coordination between authorities and businesses. This pilot investigates the feasibility of using blockchain technology to improve freight transport and government-to-business services in ports. Stakeholders include port authorities, customs, logistics operators and researchers. The objective is to assess whether distributed ledgers can enhance data sharing and service quality in port logistics. The study explores shared ledgers for tracking freight documentation and logistics events, enabling authorised access to consistent and tamper-proof data. Results indicate potential improvements in transparency, reduced administrative friction and better coordination across stakeholders. The pilot provides a knowledge base for future operational deployments in smart port ecosystems.
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain

column2: Transparent management of public accounts
column4: Public administrations face growing pressure to ensure transparency, accountability, and citizen participation in the management of public finances. In the municipality of Katerini, traditional procurement and budgeting processes limited visibility into public spending and constrained meaningful civic engagement. This use case, part of the EU-funded TOKEN project, seeks to enhance trust between citizens and local government by making public accounts more transparent and participatory. The objective is to provide clear insight into procurement processes and enable citizens to actively contribute to decisions on public spending. The solution integrates blockchain technology into existing smart city initiatives to create a tamper-proof system for recording procurement activities and managing participatory budgeting through electronic voting. Transactions related to public spending and voting outcomes are immutably recorded, ensuring traceability and auditability. Citizen participation is supported through secure digital identities and transparent voting mechanisms. The impact includes increased trust in public institutions, reduced risk of fraud or manipulation, and improved civic engagement. The use case demonstrates how transparent digital infrastructures can strengthen democratic processes, improve governance quality, and create more accountable and inclusive local administrations.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: ONCE project: Sovereign Data and Self Sovereign Identity
column4: Digital public services often require citizens to repeatedly register and share personal data across different administrations and sectors, leading to inefficiencies and loss of user control. The ONCE project addresses these challenges by enabling citizens to manage their digital identity data directly on their smartphones. Initially deployed in the German state of Hesse and selected regions in Bavaria and North Rhine-Westphalia, the initiative involves public authorities, municipalities, and private sector partners. Its objective is to simplify online registration and authentication across domains such as e-government, mobility, and tourism, while strengthening data sovereignty and trust. The solution combines self-sovereign identity principles with existing identity infrastructures, building on OPTIMOS 2.0 and mobile identity wallets. Identity credentials are issued, stored, and shared under user control, with verification supported by decentralised trust mechanisms. The system integrates with public and private services without centralised identity databases. The project demonstrates reduced administrative burden, faster onboarding to digital services, and enhanced privacy protection. It provides a scalable and interoperable model for secure digital identities, supporting cross-sector use and reinforcing citizen-centric data governance.
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: Cross-border erasmus exchange scenario
column4: European student mobility programmes like Erasmus faced administrative friction from credential verification requirements, with universities unable to efficiently validate transcripts and qualifications from partner institutions across borders. The EBSI multi-University pilot, launched through the Early Adopters Programme in 2021, brought together 11 universities from 11 countries and 2 European university alliances to test cross-border Verifiable Credentials for education. Six scenarios were developed including Erasmus transcript validation, cross-border PhD applications, European student card verification, and diploma recognition for employment. The pilot demonstrated end-to-end exchange of W3C-standard credentials via EBSI-compliant digital wallets, with institutions including KU Leuven and Italian universities testing the Belgium-Italy corridor. The EBSI-VECTOR project (2023-2025) expanded these pilots across 52 partners in 20 countries, advancing toward production deployment of decentralised educational credentials.

column2: European Student eCard with blockchain
column4: Students across Europe face fragmented systems for proving their status and accessing benefits when interacting with public services in different regions. This pilot, led by the Open Government of Catalonia in collaboration with municipalities, universities and technology providers, aims to improve interoperability of student credentials. Stakeholders include students, city councils, universities and service providers. The objective is to enable students to access municipal discounts using a trusted digital identity. The solution leverages a blockchain-based digital identity wallet aligned with European infrastructure standards, allowing students to present verified credentials securely. The pilot enabled students to obtain discounts in multiple Catalan municipalities while maintaining control over their data. It demonstrated interoperability, compliance with European identity frameworks and improved user experience, laying the groundwork for scalable cross-border student services.
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: EQPR - CoE Refugee Passport
column4: Refugees often face significant barriers to education and employment due to the lack of formal documentation proving their qualifications. The European Qualification Passport for Refugees (EQPR), developed by the Council of Europe, addresses this challenge by providing a structured and trusted assessment of refugees’ educational background, even when evidence is incomplete. The objective is to support further studies, labour market access, and social integration across Europe. Implemented since 2017 in 15 countries, the EQPR involves refugees, higher education institutions, employers, and public authorities. The digital pilot leverages the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure to issue and verify EQPR credentials securely. The entire workflow has been digitalised, with qualification assessments issued as verifiable credentials through a blockchain-based diploma platform. Holders can store and share their EQPR via a digital wallet, enabling instant verification by universities and employers across borders. The initiative significantly improves accessibility, trust, and efficiency in qualification recognition. It reduces administrative delays, empowers refugees with portable credentials, and supports inclusive education and employment pathways across Europe.

column2: Cross-border micro-credentials
column4: Student mobility within Europe is hindered by the lack of interoperable systems for recognising short learning experiences such as micro-credentials. Universities often rely on manual processes, and achievements obtained abroad are difficult to transfer or verify. Tampere University and Kaunas University of Technology face these challenges within the ECIU university network, particularly for micro-modules and challenge-based learning. The objective of this use case is to enable trusted, transferable, and learner-controlled micro-credentials across borders. The solution is implemented through the EBSI Early Adopters pilot, using self-sovereign identity and verifiable credentials. Universities issue micro-credentials aligned with shared semantic standards, while students store them in digital wallets and share them as needed. The EBSI infrastructure ensures authenticity, legal validity, and compliance with GDPR and eIDAS. The impact includes reduced administrative workload, improved student mobility, and better recognition of skills and achievements. The use case demonstrates a scalable European model for micro-credential exchange, supporting lifelong learning and flexible education pathways.
column8: The European Consortium of Innovative Universities (ECIU)
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: Bachelor/Master Diploma Credential
column4: Cross-border recognition of higher education diplomas remains complex, slowing student mobility and increasing administrative costs for institutions and graduates. This use case aims to simplify the issuance and verification of Bachelor, Master, and PhD diplomas across Europe. Involving Romania, Greece, and France, the initiative supports the issuance of Europass diploma credentials that are valid and verifiable across borders. The objective is to provide students, universities, and employers with a trusted and efficient mechanism to exchange academic qualifications. The solution leverages the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure to issue digital diplomas as verifiable credentials. Academic institutions anchor diploma proofs on the shared European infrastructure, while graduates receive credentials they can store and share through digital wallets. Verification can be performed instantly by authorised parties without intermediaries. The results include faster verification processes, reduced fraud risk, and improved interoperability between national education systems. This use case strengthens trust in academic credentials, enhances student mobility, and lays the foundation for a harmonised European digital education ecosystem.

column2: Neurokõne - provide more accessible e-services
column4: Estonian-language e-services remained inaccessible to visually impaired citizens and others who rely on screen readers, as existing text-to-speech solutions for Estonian lacked natural-sounding voices and adequate quality for extended use. The University of Tartu's Natural Language Processing research group developed Neurokõne, a neural network-based text-to-speech synthesis system trained on 92.4 hours of speech data from six speakers. Released as open-source software (MIT licence), the system integrates with Android and iOS accessibility features, enabling Estonian speech synthesis across government e-services and applications. The solution significantly outperforms existing alternatives including Google's Estonian speech synthesis in evaluation tests. Neurokõne forms part of Estonia's national language technology programme ensuring digital services remain accessible to all citizens regardless of ability, supporting the country's digital-first government approach.
