column2: WellCo - Wellbeing and health virtual coach
column4: Supporting citizens in maintaining long-term physical, cognitive, mental, and social wellbeing is a growing societal challenge, particularly in ageing populations where lifestyle-related risks accumulate over time. Individuals often lack personalised guidance and sustained motivation to adopt healthier behaviours, while health and social care systems face increasing pressure to deliver preventive, user-centred support at scale. The WellCo project, funded under Horizon 2020, addresses this challenge by empowering users to make informed and sustainable lifestyle choices across seven wellbeing domains, including physical activity, nutrition, cognitive stimulation, and social participation. Stakeholders include citizens, healthcare and wellbeing service providers, and public authorities seeking preventive, cost-effective interventions that adapt to individual needs. The objective is to provide continuous, personalised guidance and follow-up that supports behavioural change and long-term wellbeing. The solution is implemented through a digital platform featuring a virtual coaching system that delivers tailored interventions based on individual profiles, preferences, and behavioural patterns. Personalisation is achieved through data-driven models that analyse user interactions and contextual information to recommend goals, activities, and social engagement opportunities. The system integrates mobile interfaces, social networking features, and health-related data sources. The project demonstrates improved user engagement, more effective behaviour change support, and scalable delivery of personalised wellbeing services, contributing to better quality of life and reduced long-term pressure on health and care systems.
column8: HI IBERIA INGENIERIA Y PROYECTOS SL and Fondazione Bruno Kessler
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Artificial Intelligence in the Sorting of Municipal Waste as an Enabler of the Circular Economy
column4: Municipal waste management systems face persistent challenges in increasing recycling rates, improving material purity, and reducing the volume of waste sent to landfill. Manual sorting processes are labour-intensive, costly, and expose workers to health and safety risks, while also limiting the efficiency of material recovery. The “ZRR for municipal waste” research project addresses these challenges by modernising waste sorting operations to better support circular economy objectives. Stakeholders include municipal authorities, waste management operators, workers, and recycling industries seeking higher-quality secondary raw materials. The objective is to increase recovery rates, capture valuable materials from rejected waste streams, and improve working conditions within treatment plants. The solution introduces robotic sorting systems equipped with advanced perception and decision-making capabilities, integrated into existing waste treatment facilities. These systems analyse waste streams in real time to identify and separate materials with high precision, complementing or replacing manual sorting. Operational data is used to optimise performance under real plant conditions. Results from pilot deployments, including tests in Barcelona, show higher recycling yields, improved purity of recovered materials, and reduced landfill disposal. The initiative delivers environmental benefits, operational efficiency gains, and safer working environments, demonstrating the potential for scalable, technology-enabled circular waste management.
column40: V. Wilts, H., Garcia, B.R., Garlito, R.G., Gómez, L.S., Prieto, E.G. Artificial Intelligence in the Sorting of Municipal Waste as an Enabler of the Circular Economy. Resources 2021, 10, 28

column2: Prevention of Diabetic Foot Syndrome
column4: Team FAIDEN from Leiden University presented the idea of a user-friendly, AI-driven software that can be used by diabetic patients at home. It allows the patients to monitor and detect early signs of severe foot problems. Diabetic Foot Syndrome can have severe consequences if not detected early, and is a major cause for lower limb amputation in Europe and worldwide.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Smart enforcement: automated detection and handling of prohibited transit in Vojkovice
column4: The municipality of Vojkovice faces a challenge in enforcing traffic laws, particularly those related to prohibited truck transit. The lack of an efficient monitoring and penalization system has led to increased safety risks and road damage, negatively impacting residents' quality of life. This project presents an opportunity to address these issues and improve the municipality's ability to enforce traffic laws, resulting in safer roads and a more livable environment for the community. By implementing the automated traffic violation detection and connected handling system, the municipality of Vojkovice can achieve significant benefits. The system will provide real-time monitoring of traffic violations, enabling prompt action and ensuring fair and accurate penalization. This will improve road safety, reduced damage and traffic flow.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: La Paula i la Rita - the chatbot assistants in the authentication processes in Catalunya
column4: Catalonia's Open Administration Consortium (AOC) receives over 91,000 annual support requests, with citizens struggling to navigate complex e-government procedures for digital identification and certificate management. The AOC deployed La Paula (January 2021) and La Rita (April 2021) as AI-powered chatbot assistants supporting the VALid identification service and idCAT Certificate respectively, developed in partnership with 1MillionBot. The chatbots use natural language processing to interpret citizen queries and provide instant responses 24/7, achieving over 95% accuracy rate. In 2022, Paula supported 62,588 people with 127,454 messages whilst Rita assisted 43,520 people through 100,606 messages. The AOC has pioneered algorithmic transparency in Spain, publishing detailed documentation of chatbot operation, governance, and limitations. In May 2024, the AOC upgraded to generative AI chatbots, serving 11,575 monthly users with 88% success rate and 90% cost savings per query.
column8: AOC Consorci Administració Oberta de Catalunya

column2: Process Mining pilot for the support service and case management of the Diputació de Girona
column4: Public sector support and case management services often appear straightforward but can hide significant operational complexity that affects efficiency, response times, and resource allocation. At the Diputació de Girona, the user support service managed by the AOC faced challenges in understanding process variability and identifying improvement opportunities based on traditional reporting methods. The objective of this pilot was to gain a clear, evidence-based view of how support processes actually function in practice and to identify optimisation scenarios that could improve service delivery. Stakeholders include support staff, service managers, and public sector users relying on timely and effective assistance. The solution applies process mining techniques to system event logs generated by support and records management platforms. By reconstructing real process flows from operational data, the approach reveals hidden process variants, bottlenecks, and rework loops. The pilot uncovered more than 3,400 distinct process variants within workflows previously assumed to be linear. Simulation capabilities allow decision-makers to test “what-if” scenarios, such as changing escalation paths between support levels. The results indicate potential savings of up to 16,000 processing hours per year, alongside improved transparency and data-driven decision-making. The pilot demonstrates how process intelligence can significantly enhance efficiency, service quality, and organisational learning in public administration.

column2: Automatic classification of citizens' issues or solecituds
column4: Public administrations receive large volumes of citizen requests through digital channels, often submitted as generic applications that require manual review and routing. This leads to delays, inconsistent handling, and increased administrative workload. The AOC initiative addresses the need to streamline the intake and management of citizen submissions received via platforms such as EACAT and e-TRAM. Stakeholders include civil servants responsible for case handling, administrative managers, and citizens expecting timely responses. The objective is to improve efficiency and consistency by automatically classifying incoming requests and directing them to the appropriate services. The solution implements predictive classification models trained on historical application data to analyse the content and metadata of submissions. Incoming requests are automatically categorised based on learned patterns, enabling faster routing and prioritisation. The system integrates with existing document and case management platforms, supporting human oversight and continuous improvement. The pilot demonstrates reduced manual effort, faster response times, and more consistent case handling. It also improves scalability as request volumes grow, while freeing staff to focus on higher-value tasks. Overall, the initiative enhances administrative efficiency and user experience, supporting more responsive and effective digital public services.
column8: AOC Consorci Administració Oberta de Catalunya

column2: Lithuanian solution for systematised forecast of the regional labour market demand
column4: Regional authorities often lack timely, evidence-based insights into how labour market demand evolves, making it difficult to plan vocational education, reskilling programmes and economic development policies. In Lithuania, the Panevėžys Development Agency initiated this pilot to address fragmentation of labour market data and the absence of long-term, region-specific forecasts. Stakeholders include regional development agencies, vocational training providers, educational institutions, employers and job seekers. The objective is to provide a comprehensive and forward-looking view of labour market dynamics to better align skills supply with future demand. The solution automatically collects and aggregates publicly available data sources related to employment, education, demographics and economic activity. Advanced forecasting models are applied to identify trends, skill shortages and emerging occupations over extended time horizons. The system integrates these outputs into a regional labour market model that supports scenario analysis and policy planning. The pilot was co-created with Neurotechnology, a Lithuanian technology company acting as a startup partner. The impact includes improved strategic planning for vocational guidance, more targeted training and retraining services, and better-informed regional economic policies. Qualitatively, the solution reduces manual analytical effort, improves forecast accuracy and provides a scalable decision-support tool that can be replicated in other regions.

column2: Lithuania Solution for greening the city of Vilnius using satellite data and AI
column4: Urban greenery plays a critical role in environmental quality and citizens’ wellbeing, yet monitoring vegetation health across an entire city is resource-intensive and often relies on manual inspections. The City of Vilnius launched this pilot to improve oversight of urban green areas and enable early identification of issues such as drought stress, lack of maintenance or illegal felling. Stakeholders include municipal environmental departments, urban planners, maintenance contractors and citizens. The objective is to reduce administrative burden while improving the accuracy and timeliness of greenery management decisions. The prototype continuously monitors the city’s green infrastructure using a combination of radar and optical satellite imagery. Analytical algorithms process time-series data to detect changes in vegetation condition, identify problem areas and flag anomalies requiring intervention. Outputs are visualised through a monitoring interface that supports planning of watering, mowing, replacement and enforcement activities. The pilot significantly reduces the need for on-site inspections, improves detection of illegal or unauthorised actions, and enables proactive maintenance. It delivers qualitative gains in efficiency, environmental protection and decision-making quality, while providing a scalable model for data-driven urban environmental management that can be extended to other cities.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Lithuania Innovative solution for the assessment of the impact and damage of hydrometeorological phenomena
column4: Extreme weather events and hazardous natural phenomena pose increasing risks to populations, infrastructure and economic activity, yet information about their impact is often fragmented across agencies and data sources. This Lithuanian pilot was initiated to strengthen national capabilities in monitoring, forecasting and impact assessment of hydrometeorological events. Stakeholders include public authorities, emergency services, infrastructure operators, businesses and citizens. The objective is to improve situational awareness and deliver more accurate and timely alerts to support preparedness and response. The solution integrates data from multiple sources, including meteorological observations, environmental sensors and historical records, into a unified processing pipeline. Predictive and analytical models are applied to monitor conditions, forecast hazardous events and assess potential impacts and damages across sectors. The system supports the generation of targeted alerts and risk assessments tailored to specific regions and user groups. The pilot improves the performance of climate monitoring and forecasting, enhances coordination between institutions, and supports more effective risk communication. Qualitative impacts include faster response times, better-informed decision-making and increased resilience of communities and economic actors. The approach is designed to scale nationally and to evolve with additional data sources and use cases.

column2: Servizz.Gov Chatbox Pilot in Malta
column4: The Maltese government has recently implemented artificial intelligence (AI) technology to improve its public services. One of the most notable applications of AI in the public sector is the use of chatbots for customer service in government agencies.  In this context, the servizz.gov’s Chatbot offers horizontal collaboration with Ministries and economies of scale through delivery of autonomous digital interaction. Powered by a rule-based mechanism with an FAQ source and machine learning incremental process, user ‘intents’ are captured to train the model and improve bot answers through iteration and feedback. servizz.gov will continue developing its current chat infrastructure into a fully-fledged citizen chatbot, extending its knowledge base but also capable of integrating with third party systems.

column2: AI tool to spot undeclared swimming pools in France
column4: This is an AI computer vision system developed by Capgemini. The French tax office has identified 20,356 residential swimming pools that had previously gone undeclared, leading to 10 million euros in additional tax revenue opened, helping the government tax other undeclared architectural features such as annexes or verandas. The software automatically recognizes pools in aerial photographs (by looking for blue rectangles, for instance) and compares the results to records in real estate and tax databases. If it finds that a relevant address doesn't have a pool registered, the owner is in violation of tax law.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: VeRa 1.0, the artificial intelligence that tracks tax evaders
column4: Italy loses over €99 billion annually to tax evasion, with €30 billion from VAT alone, whilst traditional enforcement methods struggled to identify non-compliant taxpayers among millions of returns. The Agenzia delle Entrate developed VeRa (Verifica dei Rapporti finanziari), an AI-powered algorithm that cross-references taxpayer filings with data from the Financial Relations Archive, property registries, bank accounts, and electronic payments to identify compliance risks. In 2022, VeRa flagged one million high-risk cases and helped prevent over €6.8 billion in fraud. The system assigns risk scores enabling targeted audits rather than random checks, whilst EU-funded projects through Sogei introduce network analysis, machine learning, and data visualisation for complex evasion schemes. In 2023, the agency recovered €18.1 billion, increasing to projected €19.6 billion by 2025. A complementary AI chatbot launched in 2023 analyses VAT returns in real-time, flagging irregularities for immediate investigation.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: "Donate a Speech": Estonia crowdsources speech data for the preservation of the Estonian language
column4: Estonia’s Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications (MKM) and the State Information System Agency launched the “Donate a Speech” campaign, with the goal of preserving the Estonian language and developing language technologies to bolster the delivery of public services for Estonian speakers. By creating an open database of 4,000 hours of spoken language, the nation aims to support companies, public sector institutions and research institutions in creating services and products based on speech technology. Speech technology can be used to record meetings, convert interviews into written form, and generate automatic subtitles to media. The campaign aims to promote the uptake of language technologies in public sector information systems as well as the private sector, including speech recognition technology, real-time subtitling solutions, and text to speech software. This will improve access to services and provide better ways for Estonians to interact with public and private sector services.
column8: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications (MKM)

column2: Applying AI to increase quality in the patent grant process at the European Patent Office
column4: Patent examination at the European Patent Office involves assessing millions of applications against complex technical and legal criteria, with AI-related inventions presenting particular challenges due to rapidly evolving technology and novel patentability questions. The EPO has implemented AI and machine learning tools to enhance patent examination quality, assist examiners with prior art searches, and improve consistency in assessing technical character and inventive step for computer-implemented inventions. The 2024 Guidelines for Examination introduced specific provisions for AI inventions, requiring disclosure of training datasets when necessary to reproduce claimed technical effects and establishing standards for plausibility of AI-related claims. These updates followed decisions from the Boards of Appeal (including T 0161/18) addressing sufficiency of disclosure for machine learning inventions. The system supports examiners whilst maintaining human decision-making authority, processing applications across all 39 member states of the European Patent Organisation.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Advanced Technology for National Topographic Map Updating - Increasing spatial data accuracy with AI in Finland
column4: Maintaining accurate and up-to-date national topographic maps is essential for land management, infrastructure planning, environmental monitoring, and public safety. Traditional map updating methods are time-consuming and struggle to keep pace with rapid changes in the built and natural environment. In Finland, the National Land Survey sought to improve the accuracy and timeliness of spatial data while reducing manual effort. The objective of the ATMU project was to enhance object detection and change recognition for buildings, roads, and watercourses at national scale. Stakeholders include mapping authorities, planners, and public agencies relying on high-quality geospatial data. The solution applies advanced image analysis techniques to aerial and satellite imagery, using deep neural models trained for object detection and change recognition tasks. Different model architectures are applied to specific features, supported by transfer and multitask learning approaches. By the end of the project, building detection achieved an accuracy of 97.9%, and 96% of building and road changes were correctly identified. The project delivers significant improvements in data accuracy, update frequency, and operational efficiency, providing a scalable foundation for modern, data-driven national mapping services.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Mapping mangroves with Serverless and Deep Learning
column4: Global mangrove mapping relied on infrequently updated datasets that could not capture the dynamic changes affecting these critical coastal ecosystems, limiting understanding of carbon sequestration, biodiversity, and natural coastal protection. The UK Hydrographic Office (UKHO) developed the ADMIRALTY Global Mangrove Data Set using deep learning applied to European Space Agency Sentinel-2 satellite imagery, creating the world's highest resolution, most up-to-date global view of mangrove forests. The neural network was trained on over 7,000 coastal images and achieves geo-generalisability across diverse global locations through collaboration between UKHO's Data Science, Data Engineering, and Remote Sensing teams. The system automatically updates every six months, processing new cloud-free satellite imagery to detect mangrove extent changes. This serverless architecture enables scalable analysis supporting marine navigation, coastal sustainability planning, and climate change research on these vital carbon sinks that sequester 3-5 times more carbon than inland tropical forests.

column2: SensusQ: transforming information into knowledge with the help of automation, integration, and interoperability
column4: Military intelligence operations struggle with data overload from multiple sources including UAVs, cyber events, open-source intelligence, and human reports, requiring rapid integration and analysis to support tactical decision-making. SensusQ, an Estonian DefenceTech startup founded by combat veterans Marko Kaseleht and Villiko Nurmoja, developed an AI-powered intelligence management platform that fuses data from diverse sensors and sources into a unified operational picture. The system has been deployed by NATO forces and Ukraine, where it has demonstrated 200% improvement in response times and significant increases in data accuracy. In 2024, SensusQ secured €3.8M funding from US-based New North Ventures and in 2025 contributed to the British Army's Project ASGARD digital targeting initiative. The platform supports both the Estonian Defence League and allied nations, featuring NATO-standard military symbols, cross-data search, and standardised reporting whilst maintaining on-premise deployment to ensure sensitive data remains under client control.

column2: The AI algorithm that blew up Italy’s school system
column4: Italy's education system faced the annual challenge of assigning approximately 100,000 teachers to schools across diverse regions, a complex optimization problem that had historically relied on manual processes prone to errors and delays. The Ministry of Education implemented an algorithmic system to automatically assign teachers to schools based on preferences, qualifications, and regional needs, processing massive datasets to generate placements within compressed timeframes. However, the 2020 deployment generated significant controversy when thousands of teachers received assignments to schools far from their preferences or in regions they had not selected, with some relocated hundreds of kilometres from their homes. The algorithm's logic and decision criteria remained opaque, preventing affected teachers from understanding or effectively challenging their placements. The case became a prominent example in debates about algorithmic accountability in public administration, highlighting tensions between efficiency gains and transparency requirements in automated government decision-making.

column2: Signals - Detection of statistically significant clusters in reported infectious diseases
column4: Germany's disease surveillance system required methods to identify unusual patterns in reported infections that might indicate emerging outbreaks, requiring analysis of large volumes of routine surveillance data against expected baselines. The Robert Koch Institute (RKI) developed the Signals system using statistical algorithms to automatically detect statistically significant changes in reported disease incidence that warrant epidemiological investigation. The system processes data from Germany's mandatory notification system, comparing current case counts against historical patterns whilst adjusting for seasonal variations and reporting delays. When anomalies are detected, automated alerts trigger specialist review to determine whether observed increases represent genuine outbreaks requiring public health response. The platform has operated since 2015, providing early warning capabilities that complement traditional epidemiological surveillance methods and supporting Germany's capacity to detect and respond to infectious disease threats.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI to spot counterfeit products at customs
column4: The ITZBund advises the Central Office for Commercial Legal Protection (ZGR) and the General Directorate of Customs ( GZD ) on AI. The goal is to use artificial intelligence to improve the detection of counterfeit products and thus support the fight against trade mark piracy. Trademark rights can be protected with the help of artificial intelligence.The system is based on image recognition processes and allows goods to be checked for counterfeiting. For this purpose, goods are visually recorded and compared with a comprehensive database using artificial intelligence. As a result, the technology assigns the product to a recognised brand and indicates the probability of success. At the same time, the system constantly learns new impressions and expands the database. However, the decision on whether a fake exists and how to proceed is still up to the customs officers.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Chatbot LinA: the chatbot that answers general inquiries in the field of motor vehicle tax and customs law
column4: Germany's customs authority receives high volumes of citizen inquiries about import duties, customs procedures, and international trade regulations, creating significant workload for staff and variable response times for users. The Federal Customs Administration (Zoll) deployed LinA, an AI-powered chatbot that answers general inquiries about customs matters through natural language interaction available 24/7 on the authority's website. The system handles routine questions about duty calculations, prohibited goods, travel allowances, and customs procedures, freeing human agents to address complex cases requiring specialist expertise. LinA is part of Germany's broader Bundesbots initiative developing standardised chatbot infrastructure for federal agencies, sharing technological approaches and ensuring consistent user experience across government services. The platform demonstrates how conversational AI can improve public service accessibility whilst managing increasing demand for digital interaction channels with government authorities.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Assessment of money laundering activities through AI
column4: The Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) evaluates information to carry out suspicious activity reports for risk analysis. This process is automated through the use of AI, so that new information can be added in the pool and repeated evaluations can be made in a more time-efficient way.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Chatbot ViOlA for the homepage of the german Federal Central Tax Office
column4:
Germany's Federal Employment Agency (Bundesagentur für Arbeit) serves millions of citizens seeking employment services, training, and benefits. With 14,000 daily consultations and 95,000 telephone calls, managing routine enquiries whilst ensuring staff availability for complex cases presents a significant operational challenge.
The Bundesagentur für Arbeit deployed ViOlA (Virtual Online Assistant), an AI-powered chatbot on its website to handle common enquiries about job searches, unemployment benefits, vocational training, and family allowances. The chatbot uses natural language processing to understand user questions and provide relevant information or direct users to appropriate resources. ViOlA operates alongside other digital services including video consultations (Mein Videotermin) that connect over 1,000 locations. The agency's digital transformation runs on a containerised infrastructure with approximately 600 services and 24,000 containers managed through Rancher Prime and Kubernetes, migrated at the end of 2021. The chatbot reduces pressure on telephone services and local offices by addressing frequently asked questions automatically, allowing the agency's 100,000 employees across 156 employment agencies and 302 job centres to focus on personalised advice and complex benefit determinations. The approach supports the agency's mission to remain close to customers whilst managing increasing service demands.

column2: X-PIDER - an electonic investigative tool in the field of e-commerce used by the German tax authorities
column4:
Tax authorities face growing challenges detecting undeclared economic activity as commerce increasingly moves online. Traditional audit methods cannot efficiently monitor the vast volume of internet commercial activity, creating opportunities for tax evasion through e-commerce platforms and social media.

The German Federal Central Tax Office (Bundeszentralamt für Steuern) deployed X-PIDER, an AI-powered web crawling and scraping system to automatically identify taxable business activity online. The system operates as an internet crawler connected to a knowledge management component, systematically following links across websites, e-commerce platforms, and social media to detect indicators of commercial activity. X-PIDER can identify internet pages suggesting business transactions and collects taxpayer-related data for cross-referencing with tax records. The system can be deployed anywhere on the internet and autonomously navigates web content to build comprehensive profiles of online commercial presence.

X-PIDER supports the regional tax authorities by providing centralised intelligence on potential tax non-compliance. The system complements other AI initiatives in German tax administration including the Steuerchatbot virtual assistant (extended nationally and to English and French in 2024), nudging tools that adapt communication based on taxpayer risk profiles, and automated verification systems for processing tax returns. These tools collectively strengthen enforcement whilst reducing manual investigative workload.

column2: Environmental monitoring with AI at the German Environment Agency
column4: Environmental authorities face growing challenges in analysing large volumes of heterogeneous data across climate, air quality, energy and resource use, often stored in fragmented and hard-to-access systems. The German Environment Agency (UBA) established its AI Application Lab to improve the operational use of complex environmental data and support evidence-based environmental policy. Stakeholders include UBA departments, federal and regional authorities, researchers and policy makers. The objective is to enable cross-disciplinary and cross-scale analysis of environmental data while ensuring that digital solutions themselves are energy- and resource-efficient. The Lab develops and evaluates data-driven applications that integrate diverse datasets such as environmental measurements, administrative records and scientific data. Advanced analytical pipelines and modelling approaches are used to structure, process and visualise complex information, making it accessible across domains. Particular emphasis is placed on sustainable system design, including efficient computing architectures and responsible data practices. The initiative improves analytical depth, reduces manual effort in data preparation, and enhances the usability of environmental data for decision-making. Qualitatively, it strengthens institutional capacity, improves transparency and supports scalable, reproducible analysis across environmental policy areas.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: AI warns german businesses that the documents uploaded to apply for import tax exemptions might be wrong
column4: German businesses, in the case of an application to limit the Renewable Energy Sources (EEG) surcharge as part of the special equalization regulation, must submit all relevant documents before the material deadline. If this deadline is missed, the electricity-intensive companies face the irremediable rejection of the application and thus the payment of the full EEG surcharge. In order to minimize this risk for companies, BAFA has fundamentally modernized the application process this year: With the help of artificial intelligence, the uploaded application documents are automatically searched for errors. If it is determined with a certain probability that the wrong documents may have been uploaded, the application portal issues a warning message.

column2: Artificial intelligence helps predict groundwater levels
column4: Climate change and increasing water demand are placing growing pressure on groundwater resources, making reliable prediction of groundwater levels critical for sustainable water management. Public authorities and planners require accurate forecasts to support drinking water supply, agricultural irrigation, infrastructure protection, and land-use planning. The Federal Institute for Geosciences and Natural Resources and the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology address this need through a joint initiative focused on nationwide groundwater level prediction. Stakeholders include water authorities, municipalities, farmers, and infrastructure operators. The objective is to improve decision-making by providing timely and reliable groundwater forecasts that support risk assessment and adaptation strategies. The solution uses artificial neural network models trained on historical groundwater measurements, meteorological data, and hydrological variables. These models capture complex, non-linear relationships influencing groundwater behaviour and generate predictive insights at regional and national scales. The results support more accurate identification of drought-prone areas, zones at risk of subsidence, and regions vulnerable to high groundwater levels. The initiative enhances preparedness, supports sustainable resource management, and enables evidence-based adaptation to climate impacts, delivering significant long-term environmental and socio-economic benefits.

column2: ITERATOR – Automated Detection of Pores in Rocks
column4: Understanding the physical properties of rocks is essential for applications such as geothermal energy, fluid storage, and geological disposal, where porosity plays a decisive role. Traditionally, analysing pore frequency, size, shape, and distribution from microscopic images has been a time-consuming and manual task, limiting consistency and scalability. The ITERATOR project addresses this challenge by supporting geoscientists in the systematic analysis of rock microstructures. Stakeholders include geological research institutions and applied geoscience bodies that require reliable parameters to assess heat flow, fluid flow, and mechanical behaviour of rocks. The objective is to improve the accuracy, reproducibility, and efficiency of porosity analysis while enabling deeper insights into the relationships between mineral phases, stress regimes, and pore orientation. The solution applies advanced image processing techniques to scanning electron microscopy images to semi-automatically detect and characterise pores. Algorithms identify pore boundaries and compute quantitative descriptors, including size distribution and shape metrics, which are then linked to geological and petrophysical models. The results significantly reduce manual effort and increase analytical consistency. The project enables more detailed and data-driven interpretation of rock properties, supporting improved modelling, better parameter estimation, and more robust decision-making in geoscientific applications.

column2: MACRO – Cross-Scale Regionalisation of Hydrogeological Information
column4: Hydrogeological assessments often rely on data collected at different spatial scales, making it difficult to derive coherent regional models that support water management and planning. Local measurements provide high accuracy but limited coverage, while regional datasets lack detail and consistency. The MACRO project addresses this gap by developing methods to integrate and regionalise hydrogeological information across scales. Stakeholders include geological surveys, water authorities, and planners who require harmonised, spatially consistent groundwater information for sustainable resource management. The objective is to establish a methodological foundation that allows reliable upscaling of hydrogeological parameters while preserving local characteristics. The project develops and tests data-driven modelling approaches using selected hydrogeological use cases. Diverse datasets, such as geological maps, monitoring data, and environmental variables, are combined to generate regionally consistent representations of subsurface properties. These methods are designed to support cross-scale learning and validation, ensuring robustness across different spatial resolutions. The project delivers improved regional groundwater models, reduced uncertainty in parameter estimation, and enhanced transferability of local knowledge to larger areas. This contributes to more informed decision-making, better water resource planning, and a scalable framework for nationwide hydrogeological assessments.

column2: EnMAP - Environmental Mapping and Analysis Program
column4:
Traditional satellite imagery captures only a limited portion of the electromagnetic spectrum, restricting scientists' ability to detect subtle environmental changes in vegetation health, water quality, soil composition, and mineral deposits essential for climate monitoring and resource management.

EnMAP (Environmental Mapping and Analysis Program) is Germany's first hyperspectral satellite mission, launched in April 2022 aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9. The satellite carries a dual-spectrometer instrument capturing 230 spectral bands from 420 to 2450 nanometres at 30-metre ground resolution, enabling detection of biochemical and geochemical signatures invisible to conventional sensors. Operating in sun-synchronous orbit at 653 kilometres altitude, EnMAP records up to 5,000 kilometres of imagery daily with 4-day revisit capability. The German Research Centre for Geosciences (GFZ) leads scientific coordination whilst DLR manages operations. In 2024, the SpectralEarth dataset was released, comprising 538,974 image patches for training AI foundation models in hyperspectral analysis.

EnMAP provides unprecedented environmental monitoring capability for agriculture, forestry, water management, and geology applications. The mission supports Copernicus services and contributes to climate protection initiatives by enabling precise characterisation of ecosystem changes. Data is freely available to the global scientific community through DLR's ground segment.
column8: Federal Ministry of Economics and Climate Protection (BMWi)
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: G@ZIELT: Safe shopping on the internet
column4: Consumers in Germany are increasingly purchasing food and cosmetics online, with a rising trend in online purchases of various items including kitchen utensils, toys, clothing, and tobacco products. The Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) hosts a central unit called G@ZIELT, jointly managed by federal states, to regulate internet trade in products covered by the Food and Feed Code and tobacco. The G@ZIELT unit searches the internet to identify risky food offers, misleading labels, and unregistered businesses, reporting findings to appropriate authorities.

column2: Automated image recognition for determining the type of wood
column4: Ensuring the legal origin of timber products is a key requirement under the European Timber Trade Regulation, which obliges market participants to accurately document the wood species used in traded goods. Manual identification of wood species is labour-intensive, requires specialist expertise, and is difficult to apply at scale for inspections and compliance checks. This use case addresses the need for efficient and reliable verification methods that support regulatory enforcement. Stakeholders include regulatory authorities, inspection bodies, and timber traders responsible for due diligence. The objective is to enable large-scale verification of declared wood species and reduce the risk of illegal timber entering the market. The solution involves the development of automated image recognition systems that analyse macroscopic and microscopic wood images. By learning distinguishing visual patterns across species, the system supports consistent classification and comparison against declared information. Integrated into inspection workflows, it enables rapid screening of products and supports expert decision-making. The results include faster and more scalable compliance checks, reduced dependence on scarce specialist resources, and improved detection of mislabelled timber. The initiative strengthens enforcement of timber regulations, supports sustainable forestry, and enhances transparency in the wood supply chain.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Monitoring biological diversity in agricultural landscapes with remote sensing
column4: Maintaining and enhancing biodiversity in agricultural landscapes is a central objective of agri-environment-climate measures under the EU Common Agricultural Policy. Policymakers and environmental agencies require consistent, large-scale monitoring tools to assess habitat quality, land-use intensity, and landscape structure over time. Traditional field surveys are accurate but costly and limited in spatial coverage. This project addresses the need for a nationwide, spatially explicit monitoring approach that captures biodiversity-relevant habitat features across agricultural landscapes. Stakeholders include agricultural authorities, environmental agencies, and policymakers responsible for evaluating and designing biodiversity measures. The objective is to establish a robust indicator framework that reflects landscape and field-level characteristics relevant to different taxonomic groups. The solution uses Earth observation data from the Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 satellites within the Copernicus programme to model habitat features and land-use intensity at large scales. Spatial indicators are derived to describe landscape structure, elements, and management intensity in cropland and grassland areas. The results provide consistent, repeatable monitoring of biodiversity-related patterns at national level. The project supports evidence-based policy evaluation, improved targeting of agri-environment measures, and long-term tracking of biodiversity trends.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Language skills in the digital age
column4: Public administrations operating in international contexts require fast and reliable translation capabilities, yet traditional human-only workflows struggle to keep pace with growing volumes of multilingual content. The Federal Language Office initiated this pilot to modernise language services and support staff working with foreign-language materials. Stakeholders include translators, analysts, defence and diplomatic personnel, and administrative units relying on timely translations. The objective is to improve efficiency while maintaining acceptable quality standards for official use. The pilot applies automated translation systems trained on extensive existing linguistic corpora held by the institution. These systems learn from previously translated documents and continuously improve translation accuracy across relevant language pairs. The solution is integrated into existing translation workflows, allowing human experts to review, correct and refine outputs rather than translating from scratch. The impact includes faster turnaround times, reduced workload for routine translations and better prioritisation of expert human resources. Qualitatively, the project improves operational responsiveness and supports knowledge sharing in multilingual environments, while laying the groundwork for scalable language services across government.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: AI is used in many components of numerical weather and climate forecasting
column4: The  German Weather Service (DWD), together with the LMU in Munich, has already developed an AI-supported process , a so-called neural network. This simplifies the use of satellite images for numerical weather forecasting. The AI ​​thus provides another piece of the puzzle for the overall picture of the weather. Even extreme weather conditions can be predicted at least a little bit better.

column2: AI-based Integrated identity management for asylum procedures, improving plausability, data quality and security aspects
column4: Asylum procedures require accurate registration and verification of personal identity under challenging conditions, often involving missing documents, language barriers, and high case volumes. Manual processes increase the risk of data inconsistencies, duplicate records, and security vulnerabilities. This use case supports the Federal Office responsible for asylum procedures by improving the quality, plausibility, and security of identity data. Stakeholders include caseworkers, asylum authorities, and applicants whose cases depend on accurate and fair identity assessment. The objective is to streamline registration processes while strengthening data integrity and preventing fraud. The solution integrates multiple automated support tools into a single identity management system. These include automated transcription of names from non-Latin scripts, dialect analysis to support origin verification, biometric image checks to prevent duplicate files, and extraction of relevant information from mobile devices when documents are unavailable. The system supports caseworkers with structured, evidence-based inputs while maintaining oversight and control. The results include improved data quality, reduced duplication, enhanced security, and more efficient case handling. This leads to faster, fairer asylum procedures and increased trust in administrative processes.

column2: Chatbot for the 2021 Federal Elections
column4: Elections require well-informed citizens who understand voting procedures, the electoral system, and key democratic principles. During the 2021 Federal Elections, the Federal Agency for Civic Education sought to improve access to reliable information and support voter engagement, particularly among digitally active audiences. Traditional information channels can be difficult to navigate or overwhelming, especially for first-time voters. The objective of this use case was to provide accessible, structured, and neutral information about the election process and related political topics. The solution involved deploying an interactive chatbot that delivered curated content on voting procedures, electoral system structure, and practical considerations before election day. Information was organised into thematic dialogues, complemented by quizzes that allowed users to test their knowledge. The chatbot was accessible through digital channels commonly used by citizens, enabling on-demand interaction. The initiative improved accessibility to civic information, encouraged active learning, and supported informed participation in the electoral process. Qualitative results include increased user engagement, clearer understanding of democratic procedures, and a scalable communication tool for future civic education initiatives.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Sat4GWR_IF-Bund – Remote sensing & AI for the register census
column4: Maintaining an accurate and up-to-date register of buildings and dwellings is essential for population statistics, spatial planning, and evidence-based policymaking. Traditional register maintenance relies heavily on administrative updates and manual checks, which can lead to inconsistencies, delays, and incomplete coverage. The Sat4GWR_IF-Bund innovation project addresses these challenges by supporting the German register census with independent, area-wide verification of building data. Stakeholders include the Federal Statistical Office, public administrations, and policymakers who depend on reliable building and housing information. The objective is to enhance data quality assurance, complement register-based census methods, and support the long-term maintenance of building registers. The solution applies neural network–based image analysis to satellite imagery and aerial photographs to automatically detect buildings and derive characteristics such as building size and usage. These outputs are compared with existing register data to identify discrepancies and support validation workflows. Remote sensing data is used as an auxiliary, non-intrusive source that scales nationwide. The project improves completeness, consistency, and reliability of census data, reduces manual verification effort, and establishes a scalable approach to register maintenance, strengthening the statistical foundation for public decision-making.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Machine learning increases biotechnological protein production
column4: Biotechnological production of proteins, such as those used in vaccines and therapeutics, is complex and sensitive to numerous biological and process variables, making yield optimisation challenging. Researchers at the Paul Ehrlich Institute initiated this project to improve predictability and efficiency in protein synthesis within host organisms. Stakeholders include biomedical researchers, biomanufacturers and public health institutions. The objective is to enhance production yields and process reliability to support biomedical innovation. The project develops mathematical and computational models that integrate experimental data from biotechnological production processes. These models capture relationships between process parameters, biological responses and output yields, enabling more precise predictions and optimisation strategies. By embedding these models into research and development workflows, researchers can test scenarios digitally before applying changes in laboratory or production settings. The results include improved yield forecasts, reduced trial-and-error experimentation and broader applicability across different biotechnological use cases. Qualitatively, the project accelerates research cycles, reduces costs and supports scalable protein production critical for vaccines and advanced therapies.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: KISTRA (use of artificial intelligence for the early detection of criminal offenses) - research project
column4: Online hate crime poses growing societal risks, yet early detection and prevention raise complex legal, ethical and operational challenges for security authorities. The KISTRA research project was initiated to explore how digital tools could support earlier identification of hate-related offences while respecting fundamental rights. Stakeholders include law enforcement agencies, legal experts, social scientists, technology researchers and civil society. The objective is not operational deployment, but the development of scientifically grounded insights, safeguards and prototypes for responsible use. The project combines socio-scientific, legal and ethical analysis with the development of technical demonstrators. Analytical methods are applied to publicly available online content to explore patterns associated with hate crime, while governance frameworks define acceptable use conditions. The results include software demonstrators, ethical guidelines and legal assessments that inform future policy choices. The impact lies in improved understanding of risks and opportunities, clearer boundary conditions for technology use, and a foundation for transparent, accountable approaches to digital crime prevention.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Automated border control via Easypass Registered Traveler Program - biometric face recognition system
column4: International airport border control in Germany faced growing passenger volumes requiring faster processing whilst maintaining security standards and compliance with Schengen external border regulations. The Federal Police implemented EasyPASS, an automated border control system using biometric facial recognition developed by Bundesdruckerei, secunet, and Cognitec to verify travellers' identities against their e-passport chip photos. The system is deployed at Frankfurt, Munich, Hamburg, Hannover, Düsseldorf, Stuttgart, Berlin Brandenburg, and Cologne/Bonn airports, available to EU/EEA citizens aged 12+ and registered third-country nationals from the USA, Hong Kong, South Korea, Taiwan, and the UK. Travellers place their e-passport on the document reader, then proceed through gates where cameras capture facial images for 1:1 comparison against stored biometric data, with information also checked against Schengen databases. Data is stored for 48 hours (extendable to 30 days for police investigations), and the system strictly limits facial recognition to identity verification purposes under the Passport Act.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: InA– The Chatbot at the Office of Integration
column4: The Office of Integration of the Ministry for Social Affairs, Health, Family, Youth, and Senior Citizens integrated a chat bot into its website in February 2020. This chat bot is being further developed in a follow-on project. Using the chat bot, people can call up general information, arrange for callbacks, and determine time windows for contact purposes. It is also possible to use the chat bot to submit an initial application for accompanying assistance.

column2: KI4LSA – Artificial Intelligence for Adaptive Traffic Signal Control
column4: Increasing urban traffic congestion leads to longer travel times, higher fuel consumption, and increased noise and air pollution. Optimising traffic signal systems is a critical lever for improving traffic flow in dense city environments, yet conventional control strategies struggle to adapt to dynamic traffic conditions. The KI4LSA project addresses this challenge by exploring adaptive control of traffic lights in inner-city areas. Stakeholders include transport authorities, municipalities, and road users seeking smoother traffic flow and reduced environmental impact. The objective is to optimise traffic signal behaviour in real time to minimise congestion and unnecessary stopping. The solution uses reinforcement learning, a data-driven control approach trained in detailed traffic simulations. Traffic signal controllers learn optimal strategies by observing traffic states and evaluating outcomes such as queue length and waiting time. After training, the learned control policies are evaluated against conventional approaches. The project demonstrates improved traffic flow, reduced stop-and-go behaviour, and lower emissions resulting from fewer accelerations and idling periods. The results highlight the potential of adaptive signal control to improve urban mobility efficiency and environmental performance, while providing a transferable framework for future smart traffic management systems.
column8: Municipalities of Bielefeld, Gütersloh, Lemgo, Neuss, Rheine and Soest
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Pilot project to support the future development of photos with AI
column4: Municipal archives often hold vast photographic collections that are difficult to index and search due to limited metadata and manual cataloguing processes. The Heilbronn City Archive launched this pilot to improve access to its digital photo collections and enrich historical documentation. Stakeholders include archivists, researchers, local historians and the general public. The objective is to increase indexing depth and accelerate the availability of detailed information about archived images. The pilot trains image analysis systems to recognise approximately 1,500 individuals and around 200 distinctive buildings using existing labelled photographs. The resulting metadata is integrated into the HEUSS online archive database, enabling faster and more detailed tagging of images. The impact includes significantly reduced manual indexing effort, richer search functionality and improved public access to cultural heritage materials. Qualitatively, the project enhances the long-term value of archival assets and demonstrates scalable approaches to digital heritage management.
column40: Bendszusn, R. (2022). Make-or-buy in the area of Artificial Intelligence: Insights from AI use cases in the German public sector (master's thesis). Hertie School, Berlin.

column2: Intelligent layout of areas for optimal school districts design
column4: School districts should be designed in such a way that schools have as even a capacity as possible and the distances to school for children are short. At the same time, it is important to achieve a social mix among the students. In order to achieve these goals, school office employees in the Berlin district of Tempelhof-Schoeneberg have invested a lot of time. Streets had to be walked through one by one, information was recorded in Excel spreadsheets. This work is now done by an algorithm that automatically outputs a first draft of the cut based on the available data from the State Statistical Office.

column2: AI Beach Capacity Monitoring
column4: The City Council has contracted the services of the company DatActions to implement the DasEyes application, whose function is to sectorize the beaches in multiple virtual areas in order to detect the number of people in each sector. On weekends, when there is more confluence and traffic on the beach, municipal agents are provided with tools to monitor capacity; these agents patrol to make sure there are no problems and ensure order in the areas. In addition, to make it easier for them, Artificial Intelligence methods make it possible to predict when the beaches will be filled. Depending on the data, the agents open and close the different sections to the public in such a way that there will always be a safe capacity in all areas.

column2: AI to support decision-making processes in social services
column4: The municipal social services deal with an average of 50,000 initial visits every year ranging through a variety of issues (from financial or addiction problems to gender-based violence) and are handled by a team of over 700 professionals. An algorithm provides the staff with digital support in the decision-making process. Having been trained with a corpus of 300,000 interviews, it suggests the appropriate resources for each case. It's a recommendation algorithm, hence, the algorithm is an aid for the decision-making process, the decision is ultimately made by the professional handling the case. The algorithm was audited by an external third party.

column2: Robotic Automation Plan
column4: The Robot Automation Plan has created a new service for robotic process automation. The aim behind the project is to provide the City Council with innovative solutions, such as increasing the quality of citizen help and information services and reducing human error in processes that are repetitive and with little added value, increasing the Council’s production capacity, for example in processing licences for café terraces, and increasing the guarantee and transparency of processes and ensuring coherency in the data processed. The purpose of this initiative is to free up the time that workers currently spend on manual, repetitive tasks that are of little added value, so they can devote their time to providing real value to the organisation and reduce the time it takes to respond to requests.

column2: ELASTIC – Autonomous Transport Network for Urban Tramways
column4: Urban public transport systems require precise situational awareness to ensure safety, reliability, and efficient operation, particularly as cities move towards higher levels of automation. The ELASTIC project addresses this need by developing a software architecture for advanced mobility systems, with a focus on autonomous and assisted transport networks. The pilot, deployed on Florence’s tramway network, involved transport operators, city authorities, and technology providers. The objective was to enhance real-time monitoring of tram operations, infrastructure, and surrounding environments without compromising passenger privacy. The solution integrates multiple onboard and roadside sensors, including cameras, radar, lidar, and inertial measurement units, installed on tram vehicles and at stops. Sensor data is processed in real time using perception and data fusion techniques to extract information on tram positioning, obstacle detection, and movement of vehicles and pedestrians. Visual data is analysed on the fly and immediately discarded, ensuring that no personal data is retained. The pilot demonstrates improved operational awareness, enhanced safety, and better incident prevention. It provides a scalable and privacy-preserving foundation for future autonomous public transport systems in urban environments.

column2: Nantes Social Water Pricing
column4:
Access to affordable water is a fundamental right, yet low-income households often struggle with utility costs. Traditional means-tested assistance programmes require complex application processes and frequently miss eligible beneficiaries who are unaware of available support.

Nantes Métropole developed an open-source algorithm to determine eligibility for its social water tariff (Tarification Solidaire de l'Eau), guaranteeing that no household spends more than 3% of income on water for reasonable consumption of 30 cubic metres per person annually. The algorithm calculates eligibility based on household income and family composition using data from the Caisse d'Allocations Familiales (CAF). For CAF beneficiaries with family quotient below the threshold, assistance is granted automatically. Other residents can apply at their town hall, where agents enter information into Deltas software developed in-house by Nantes Métropole. The algorithm processes data in real time, providing immediate eligibility determination. The source code is published under the CeCILL-B open-source licence.

In 2024, 5,866 households benefited from the social water tariff, receiving €407,153 in total assistance. Since January 2025, reformed Agence de l'Eau charges have been integrated into the pricing structure. The open-source approach supports transparency in public decision-making and enables other municipalities to adapt the algorithm for their own social pricing programmes.

column2: Nantes Solidarity Transport Pricing
column4:
Public transport affordability is crucial for social inclusion, yet fixed-price ticketing can exclude low-income residents from mobility services essential for employment, healthcare access, and social participation.

Nantes Métropole implemented an algorithmic system for solidarity transport pricing (Tarification Solidaire Transports) on its Naolib public transport network. The algorithm determines eligibility for free travel or discounts up to 50% on unlimited annual passes based on household income and family composition. All households with a family quotient of 900 or below qualify for solidarity pricing. Users apply at their local town hall, where agents collect supporting documents and enter information into Deltas, software developed by Nantes Métropole. The algorithm processes data in real time, immediately notifying applicants of their entitlement level. Benefits apply to all household members regardless of age. Since May 2023, the eligibility threshold was raised from 600 to 900 family quotient, significantly expanding coverage.

The solidarity pricing programme extends beyond metropolitan residents to include commuters from surrounding areas who travel within Nantes Métropole. This ensures transport affordability for the most vulnerable populations whilst supporting labour market access and social cohesion across the metropolitan region.

column2: Carbon Intelligent City - Paris
column4: Cities face increasing pressure to accurately measure, understand, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions across all sectors, from buildings and transport to industry and energy production. In Paris, fragmented data sources and limited temporal resolution make it difficult to link emissions to concrete activities and evaluate the impact of climate policies. The Carbon Intelligent City pilot addresses this gap by providing a comprehensive, multi-scale emissions monitoring system. Stakeholders include city managers, policymakers, businesses, NGOs, and citizens seeking transparent and actionable climate information. The objective is to support evidence-based climate action through detailed, science-based emissions tracking at city, district, and street level. The solution combines diverse datasets within a unified platform, using advanced analytics to extract activity signals from defined areas of interest and infer associated CO₂ emissions. Satellite imagery and other data sources are analysed to detect operational activity, such as industrial site usage, and translate these signals into emissions estimates. The pilot improves transparency, enables continuous monitoring, and supports scenario analysis and impact assessment. It strengthens the city’s capacity to manage emissions strategically and engage stakeholders in collective climate action.

column2: Carbon Intelligent City - Copenhagen
column4: Achieving climate neutrality requires cities to move beyond aggregated emissions inventories towards dynamic, activity-based monitoring of greenhouse gas emissions. In Copenhagen, the need for precise, spatially resolved emissions data is critical to guide urban planning and track progress towards ambitious climate targets. This pilot within the AI4Cities project applies the Carbon Intelligent City approach to the Danish context. Stakeholders include municipal authorities, urban planners, businesses, and civil society organisations. The objective is to provide a reliable, transparent emissions information system that supports informed decision-making at multiple urban scales. The solution integrates heterogeneous datasets into a single analytical platform, where automated methods extract activity patterns from areas of interest and infer corresponding emissions. By linking observed activity to emissions factors, the system generates timely and comparable emissions estimates across sectors such as transport, buildings, and industry. The pilot improves the granularity and responsiveness of emissions monitoring, supports evaluation of policy interventions, and enhances public transparency. It demonstrates how data-driven emissions intelligence can strengthen climate governance and accelerate urban decarbonisation strategies.

column2: IX3 - Mobility Pilot Project in Paris
column4: Urban traffic inefficiencies contribute significantly to greenhouse gas emissions, particularly through frequent stopping and re-acceleration at traffic lights. The IX3 mobility pilot in Paris addresses this issue by optimising traffic signal management to smooth vehicle flows. Stakeholders include city transport authorities, road users, and residents affected by congestion and air pollution. The objective is to reduce unnecessary stops, improve driving continuity, and lower traffic-related emissions at city scale. The solution combines real-time machine vision sensors with traffic light controllers to observe traffic conditions and dynamically adjust signal logic. Visual data is processed locally to extract anonymous traffic metrics, such as vehicle counts and flow patterns, without storing personal identifiers or retaining footage beyond quality assurance. The system continuously adapts signal timing to actual traffic demand. Pilots and earlier deployments show that city-wide adoption can reduce traffic-related emissions by around 2%, alongside improved travel times and driving comfort. The project demonstrates a privacy-preserving, scalable approach to intelligent traffic management with measurable environmental and mobility benefits.

column2: IX3 - Mobility Pilot Project in Helsinki
column4: Urban traffic congestion in Helsinki contributes to unnecessary fuel consumption, emissions, and reduced travel comfort, particularly due to frequent stopping and waiting at red lights. Traditional traffic signal control systems are often rule-based and insufficiently responsive to real-time traffic conditions. This pilot, part of the AI4Cities project, addresses the need for more adaptive and efficient traffic management that supports the city’s environmental and mobility goals. Stakeholders include the City of Helsinki, transport authorities, technology providers, and road users. The objective is to reduce stop-and-go traffic, improve flow continuity, and lower traffic-related emissions without requiring major infrastructure changes. The solution integrates real-time machine vision sensors with existing traffic light controllers, enabling continuous observation of traffic conditions such as vehicle presence and flow intensity. These data feed optimisation logic that dynamically adjusts signal timing to actual demand. The system is developed in collaboration with MarshallAI and Dynniq, combining expertise in computer vision and traffic management. Only anonymous traffic data is processed, and video footage is not retained beyond quality assurance. The pilot builds on earlier deployments showing that city-wide adoption can reduce traffic-related emissions by around 2%, while also improving travel times, driving comfort, and overall traffic efficiency in a scalable and privacy-preserving manner.

column2: BerDiBa Project: Berlin Digital Railway Operation
column4: Belin Digital Railway Operations (BerDIBA) project aims to develop automated rail driving. Over a 4 year period, a consortium of 12 partners will work to develop and test technologies to equip automated trains with AI able to detect obstacles and to analyse the state of rail infrastructure. As part of the established technology and innovation funding programme ProFIT, the state of Berlin is funding application-oriented research in the field of digital rail operations as another innovative component of the large-scale Siemensstadt project. The aim is to research and test new technologies for automated rail travel under real operating conditions, e.g. artificial intelligence for detecting obstacles in the track, hazards to passengers on the train and monitoring changes in the rail infrastructure. The basis for this are high-performance sensor systems for environment perception on and in the train.

column2: Signalen - AI Categorisation of Public Space Reports
column4: Municipalities receive large volumes of reports from residents concerning issues in public space, such as waste, infrastructure damage or nuisance, which must be routed quickly to the correct department. Manual triage is time-consuming and can delay resolution. Signalen was developed as a shared, open-source system by and for Dutch municipalities to streamline the intake and handling of such reports. Stakeholders include local authorities, civil servants responsible for service delivery and residents submitting reports. The objective is to improve response times, reduce administrative workload and ensure consistent handling across municipalities. The system automatically categorises incoming reports based on their content and routes them to the appropriate municipal unit. It uses language-based classification models trained on historical report data, relying primarily on keyword recognition and probabilistic categorisation. The platform is API-driven and integrates with existing municipal case management systems. Personal data is optional and excluded from automated processing, ensuring privacy by design. Signalen is currently used by municipalities serving over two million residents and processes more than 600,000 reports annually. The system has improved operational efficiency, reduced misrouting, and increased transparency for citizens, while its open-source nature supports scalability, reuse and long-term sustainability across the Netherlands.
column8: City of Amsterdam and other dutch municipalities

column2: Energy Optimisation of Buildings
column4: Municipal buildings represent a major share of urban energy consumption, yet optimising energy use while maintaining indoor comfort is operationally complex. Copenhagen Solutions Lab and Copenhagen City Properties initiated this pilot to reduce energy consumption, emissions and costs across municipal buildings. Stakeholders include facility managers, city authorities, energy providers and building occupants. The objective is to support energy-efficient operations without compromising indoor climate quality. The project integrates building management system data, energy consumption records, indoor climate measurements and external data such as weather and grid conditions. Predictive optimisation tools generate operational recommendations for heating, ventilation and electricity use, accounting for fluctuating demand and supply constraints. The impact includes reduced heating and electricity bills, lower CO₂ emissions and improved operational insight for facility managers. Qualitatively, the pilot enhances comfort, supports climate targets and provides a replicable model for data-driven energy management at city scale.

column2: HOLONI – Blockchain-Based Local Green Energy Exchange
column4: Cities seeking to accelerate the energy transition face challenges in efficiently exchanging locally produced renewable energy and rewarding prosumers for their contributions. In many urban contexts, surplus solar energy is underutilised due to fragmented systems and limited incentives. The HOLONI project, part of AI4Cities, addresses this challenge by enabling reliable and transparent exchange of local green energy within urban communities. Stakeholders include municipalities, solar prosumers, energy retailers, and local businesses aiming to increase renewable self-consumption and community-based energy sharing. The objective is to automate incentive mechanisms and improve redistribution of locally generated energy surpluses. The solution uses distributed ledger technologies to record energy exchanges and automatically execute result-based reward schemes. Transactions related to energy production, consumption, and incentives are immutably logged, ensuring transparency, traceability, and trust among participants. Smart contracts enable automated settlement and reward distribution based on predefined sustainability criteria. The pilot demonstrates improved efficiency in local energy exchange, increased engagement of prosumers, and fair redistribution of benefits within the community. It provides a scalable and trustworthy foundation for decentralised energy markets, supporting urban decarbonisation and strengthening local energy ecosystems.

column2: HOLONI – AI for Urban Solar Potential Assessment
column4: Unlocking the full potential of urban solar energy requires accurate assessment of production capacity and surplus at city scale, yet municipalities often lack tools to analyse this potential dynamically. This HOLONI pilot within AI4Cities focuses on enabling data-driven planning and coordination of local solar energy generation. Stakeholders include city administrations, sustainable brands, energy retailers, and prosumers interested in collective climate impact. The objective is to predict solar generation and surplus across the urban fabric and use these insights to support local energy exchange and new community-based business models.
