The solution applies predictive analytics to diverse urban datasets, including building characteristics, solar exposure, and consumption patterns, to estimate current and future solar surplus potential. These insights support planning of incentive schemes, energy sharing strategies, and commercial relationships between stakeholders. The system integrates with local energy platforms to align predictions with operational decision-making. The pilot enables municipalities to better understand city-wide renewable potential, supports efficient matching of supply and demand, and strengthens community engagement around sustainability goals. It demonstrates how predictive intelligence can drive more effective local energy systems and accelerate the transition to clean, collective urban energy models.

column2: SPIKE – Energy Pilot Project in Copenhagen
column4: Buildings account for a significant share of urban energy consumption, yet many existing facilities lack the flexibility to optimise energy use without costly renovations. The SPIKE pilot in Copenhagen addresses this challenge by enabling real-time optimisation of building energy consumption while maintaining occupant comfort. Stakeholders include building owners, occupants, energy communities, and city authorities pursuing energy efficiency and decarbonisation targets. The objective is to reduce operational energy use and integrate buildings into flexible, community-based energy systems. The solution deploys a scalable plug-and-play kit of sensors and controllers that interfaces with existing HVAC systems. Real-time data is used to fine-tune energy usage dynamically, without structural changes. An integrated platform orchestrates energy loads and renewable production under an “Energy as a Service” model, enabling coordination at community level. The pilot achieves on average 20% operational energy savings and over 90% in-comfort time for occupants. It also demonstrates the potential to aggregate flexible buildings into a virtual power plant at urban scale, supporting grid stability, renewable integration, and cost-effective energy management.

column2: Spike - Energy Pilot Project in Amsterdam
column4: In Amsterdam, achieving climate goals requires scalable solutions that reduce building energy consumption while enabling participation in local energy communities. Many buildings operate inefficiently due to static control systems and limited integration with renewable energy sources. This SPIKE pilot, part of AI4Cities, aims to demonstrate how existing buildings can become active components of a flexible urban energy system. Stakeholders include municipalities, building managers, occupants, and energy providers. The objective is to optimise energy use in real time, improve comfort, and support local energy balancing without major retrofits. The solution uses a plug-and-play hardware kit combined with a unified digital platform that continuously adjusts HVAC operation based on real-time conditions. The platform supports “Energy as a Service” by orchestrating demand and renewable production across multiple buildings. Results mirror those achieved in other pilots, with average energy savings of around 20% and high comfort levels for occupants. The project shows how buildings can be aggregated into virtual power plants, enabling scalable energy flexibility, reduced emissions, and new value chains within urban energy communities.

column2: LAIban - A chatbot for children
column4: Children in municipal preschools frequently ask practical questions about daily routines, activities and schedules, which can place additional demands on staff. The City of Helsingborg introduced LAIban to support children’s independence while complementing the role of educators. Stakeholders include children, preschool staff, municipal administrators and parents. The objective is to provide accessible, age-appropriate information while fostering children’s confidence and inclusion. LAIban operates as a conversational assistant deployed across all 80 public preschools in Helsingborg. It responds to common questions using structured content and language models trained on predefined educational and administrative data. The system supports multiple languages, reflecting the demographic diversity of its users, and adapts responses accordingly. Children were actively involved in the design and iterative improvement of the assistant, influencing both content and functionality. LAIban also explains its own role and limitations to children, supporting transparency and digital literacy. The solution remains in active use and has improved children’s autonomy, reduced repetitive questions for staff and enhanced inclusiveness through multilingual support. Its participatory design approach has strengthened trust and usability, while its controlled scope ensures reliability and age-appropriate interaction.

column2: AI Safety Perception Patterns
column4: Perceived safety is a critical factor influencing how citizens use and experience urban spaces, yet it is difficult to measure systematically over time. The AI Safety Perception Patterns project, developed by Senseable Stockholm Lab in collaboration with the City of Stockholm, addresses this challenge by creating a reproducible, data-driven approach to mapping perceived safety. Stakeholders include urban planners, architects, policymakers, and residents concerned with inclusive and secure public environments. The objective is to support long-term monitoring of perceived safety and evaluate the impact of urban interventions. The solution combines multiple data sources, including web-based surveys and urban data, to model safety perception across the city. Analytical methods identify patterns and generate spatial representations that are integrated into the city’s GIS platform. The approach is designed to be fully replicable, using the same code and methods year after year, enabling longitudinal analysis. The results provide planners with actionable insights into where and why safety perceptions vary, supporting evidence-based urban design. The project delivers a scalable framework for monitoring social sustainability and assessing the effectiveness of policy and design interventions over time.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AVENUE – Mobility Pilot Project in Tallinn
column4: Urban authorities increasingly need to align shared mobility policies with ambitious greenhouse gas reduction targets, yet lack tools to quantify the climate impact of different regulatory strategies. In Tallinn, policymakers face complex choices around fleet caps, pricing, and incentives for shared mobility services, with limited visibility of their emissions outcomes. The AVENUE pilot addresses this challenge by providing a decision support capability to assess and monitor the carbon footprint effects of shared mobility policies. Stakeholders include city leaders, transport authorities, and mobility operators. The objective is to enable data-driven, timely decisions that support climate mitigation while maintaining effective mobility systems. The solution combines large-scale geolocated mobility data from anonymised mobile network records and operator-shared datasets with urban data sources. Demand prediction models simulate modal choice behaviour and assess how regulatory measures influence shared mobility usage. Scenario analysis evaluates resulting emissions impacts. The pilot improves policymakers’ ability to compare strategies, anticipate outcomes, and track progress over time. It supports more effective regulation of shared mobility, enhanced accountability of climate policies, and measurable reductions in transport-related emissions at city scale.

column2: AVENUE – Mobility Pilot Project in Stavanger
column4: Designing shared mobility policies that effectively contribute to greenhouse gas reduction is a growing challenge for medium-sized European cities. In Stavanger, authorities require robust evidence to understand how regulatory choices affect travel behaviour and emissions. The AVENUE pilot responds by offering a data-driven framework to analyse and monitor the environmental impact of shared mobility strategies. Stakeholders include municipal decision-makers, transport planners, and mobility providers. The objective is to support climate-aligned policy design through continuous measurement and scenario-based evaluation. The solution integrates anonymised geolocated mobility data and operator-provided information to model demand and modal shift patterns. Predictive models simulate how changes in policy parameters influence the uptake of shared mobility services and the resulting emissions footprint. These insights enable comparison of alternative regulatory approaches and ongoing monitoring of their effectiveness. The pilot strengthens strategic planning, reduces uncertainty in policy decisions, and enhances the city’s capacity to implement evidence-based mobility measures that contribute to decarbonisation and sustainable urban transport systems.

column2: MPAT – Mobility Policy Auto Tuner Pilot in Tallinn
column4: Cities aiming to decarbonise transport must identify where shared micro-mobility can most effectively replace car trips, yet traditional planning tools offer limited insight into spatial and temporal dynamics. The MPAT pilot in Tallinn addresses this gap by enabling targeted optimisation of mobility policies to maximise emissions reductions. Stakeholders include city transport authorities and policymakers seeking to accelerate low-carbon mobility adoption. The objective is to identify priority areas for policy interventions such as subsidies or fleet adjustments and to quantify their climate impact. The solution analyses travel patterns to determine where shared electric bicycles, scooters, or mopeds are most likely to displace internal combustion vehicle trips. Forecasting models anticipate growth in micro-mobility demand and evolving mode shift patterns. Policy scenarios are evaluated based on their projected CO₂ savings and monitored over time. The pilot demonstrates improved precision in policy targeting, greater transparency in emissions outcomes, and enhanced ability to adapt measures dynamically. It provides a practical tool for embedding climate objectives directly into mobility policy design and evaluation.

column2: MPAT – Mobility Policy Auto Tuner Pilot in Amsterdam
column4: Amsterdam’s ambition to reduce transport emissions requires sophisticated tools to understand and influence modal shift towards low-carbon mobility. The MPAT pilot supports this goal by providing a data-driven engine to optimise shared micro-mobility policies. Stakeholders include municipal authorities and urban planners responsible for balancing accessibility, sustainability, and public space management. The objective is to maximise the emissions reduction potential of shared electric mobility while anticipating future demand. The solution combines spatial analysis of travel behaviour with predictive modelling to identify locations where micro-mobility can most effectively replace car journeys. Policy levers such as incentives, parking regulations, and fleet size adjustments are evaluated based on their projected climate benefits. Continuous monitoring allows policymakers to track performance and refine interventions. The pilot enhances strategic planning, supports transparent assessment of policy impacts, and contributes to measurable CO₂ reductions. It demonstrates how predictive analytics can guide more effective and adaptive urban mobility policies aligned with climate targets.

column2: Milli, AI Chatbot for Mental Health
column4: Adolescents often face barriers to accessing mental health information and support, including stigma, lack of anonymity and limited service availability. Milli was developed in Finland to provide a confidential entry point for young people seeking information about mental health concerns. Stakeholders include adolescents, healthcare providers, psychologists and public health institutions. The objective is to improve early access to reliable information and guide users towards appropriate support when needed. Milli operates as a conversational assistant that responds to anonymous user queries and provides tailored information based on the user’s age group. It processes text inputs to identify themes and risk indicators, enabling differentiated responses and escalation in urgent situations. If signs of self-harm or crisis are detected, the system directs users to emergency services or trusted adults. Identifying information is automatically deleted, and users can request explanations about data use and system functioning. Milli currently handles between 500 and 1,200 queries per week. Reported impacts include improved accessibility to mental health information, reduced barriers to help-seeking and strengthened trust through transparency, privacy safeguards and user-centred design. Ongoing development focuses on fairness, accessibility and inclusive language.

column2: Intelligent Wellness Platform Experiment
column4: The City of Espoo sought to improve preventive social, healthcare and education services by better understanding citizens’ needs before problems escalated into costly or irreversible social exclusion. Traditional service planning relied on siloed data and reactive interventions, limiting the city’s ability to act early and in a targeted way. In collaboration with Tieto (now Tietoevry), Espoo launched an experiment to test whether large-scale data analysis could support more timely, citizen-centric welfare services. Stakeholders included municipal social and healthcare departments, education services, policymakers and the city’s residents. The objective was to assess whether predictive insights could inform service planning and resource allocation while respecting strict data protection requirements. The solution integrated longitudinal data from social services, healthcare and education covering the years 2002–2016, representing around 520,000 residents and over 37 million service interactions. Data were anonymised at source, encrypted in transit and processed in a secure analytical environment. Predictive models were used to identify risk patterns associated with social exclusion and future service needs, with results fed into planning processes rather than individual-level decisions. The experiment demonstrated that data-driven insights could support earlier interventions, more efficient service planning and new preventive service pathways. Qualitatively, it showed strong potential for improving wellbeing outcomes while reducing long-term social and healthcare costs.

column2: Gallery Visitor's AI Analysis
column4: The Hugh Lane Gallery, working with Dublin City Council’s Smart City team, set out to identify technological solutions that would provide accurate, non-intrusive analytics, measuring how many visitors attend the Gallery and how they move throughout the Gallery. This vital information can assist in exhibition curation; optimising and enhancing the experience of visitors, improving and understanding space utilisation, as well as providing important health and safety information. Working with technology provider Arm, an innovative, privacy-enhanced technology was identified, that gathers and analyses anonymised data from a system of connected cameras within the Gallery. The use of ‘Heat Mapping’ which is a data visualisation technique will assist greater e-scooter safety, by flagging footpath riding incidents indicating problematic junctions or inadequate cycling infrastructure.

column2: AI-controlled Smart Traffic Junctions in Manchester
column4: The rapid growth of walking and cycling requires traffic management systems that can dynamically prioritise different road users while maintaining safety and efficiency. In Greater Manchester, traditional signal control struggled to adapt to changing travel patterns, particularly during increased active travel uptake. Transport for Greater Manchester implemented AI-controlled smart junctions to address this challenge. Stakeholders include transport authorities, pedestrians, cyclists, and motorists. The objective is to improve junction performance, support active travel, and optimise traffic flow. The solution uses sensors with embedded intelligence to anonymously detect and classify road users in real time. Traffic signals are dynamically adjusted to prioritise pedestrians or cyclists when demand increases. Deployed initially at selected sites and later scaled to 20 junctions, the system operates without collecting personal data. The project improves safety, reduces waiting times for active modes, and enhances network efficiency. It demonstrates a scalable approach to adaptive traffic management that supports sustainable mobility and more inclusive urban streets.

column2: Behaviour Analytics to Measure Impact of Social Distancing in Transport
column4: During the COVID-19 pandemic, transport authorities faced unprecedented challenges in ensuring passenger safety while maintaining essential mobility services. Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) needed timely insights into how travellers moved through interchanges and where social distancing was difficult to maintain. Existing CCTV systems provided visual coverage but limited analytical value. TfGM partnered with startup Humanising Autonomy to pilot a behaviour analytics solution at key transport hubs, including Bolton and Wythenshawe interchanges and Wigan bus station. The objective was to support rapid, evidence-based decisions on temporary infrastructure changes and operational measures. The implementation analysed live and recorded CCTV footage through a cloud-based platform that extracted anonymised behavioural patterns, such as crowd density, group formation and directional movement. The system generated dashboards highlighting hotspots where distancing compliance was challenging, without identifying individuals. Insights were integrated into TfGM’s operational decision-making processes. The pilot enabled faster identification of problem areas, informed layout adjustments and supported safer passenger flows. Qualitatively, it improved situational awareness, reduced reliance on manual observation and demonstrated how existing infrastructure could be leveraged to enhance public safety during emergencies.

column2: Gavius Virtual Assistant
column4: Municipal social services often struggle to provide timely, clear, and personalised information on social benefits, leading to underutilisation of available aid and administrative overload. The City Council of Gavà led the Gavius project to improve citizens’ access to social assistance by simplifying how benefits are discovered, requested, and managed. Stakeholders included social services staff, municipal managers, citizens—particularly vulnerable groups—and seven public and private partners. The objective was to reduce bureaucratic barriers, accelerate benefit processing, and improve the quality of interaction between the municipality and residents. The solution was implemented as a virtual assistant accessible via mobile devices, designed to guide users through available social aids, eligibility criteria, application processes, and follow-up. The system integrates municipal databases, service workflows, and rule-based decision logic to provide tailored responses and automate parts of case handling. In addition to the citizen-facing assistant, separate virtual assistants were developed for municipal staff and managers to support internal processes. The project was funded through ERDF under the Urban Innovative Actions programme and formally concluded in February 2023. Reported outcomes include improved accessibility to social benefits, reduced administrative burden, and faster processing times, demonstrating the feasibility of conversational digital services in local welfare administration.

column2: Rimini Chatbot
column4: Citizens seeking information about municipal services often rely on email, phone calls or in-person visits, creating pressure on administrative staff and limiting accessibility outside office hours. The City of Rimini introduced a digital assistant to provide continuous support to residents navigating municipal services. Stakeholders include citizens, municipal departments and customer service staff. The objective is to improve access to information, increase citizen autonomy and reduce the workload on traditional service channels. The chatbot is embedded in the city’s institutional portal and interacts with users through natural language queries. It is trained on administrative service descriptions, procedures and FAQs, and continuously improves its responses based on user interactions. The system integrates with municipal content management systems to ensure up-to-date information. The assistant is available 24/7 and complements existing communication channels rather than replacing them. The solution remains in active use and has improved response times, reduced routine enquiries handled by staff and enhanced user satisfaction. Its conversational design supports intuitive interaction, while continuous learning contributes to scalability and long-term service quality.

column2: AI Algorithm used toclassify unemployed people in Poland
column4: The Polish government implemented an AI algorithm aimed at profiling individuals without jobs and establishing an automated system to assist in determining the support they could receive, utilizing their personal data and responses from job center interviews. However, this initiative was abandoned in December 2019 due to its deficiency in transparency and violations of data protection rights.

column2: Hello Charly - AI chatbot assisting students making decisions
column4: Young people often struggle to navigate complex education and career choices, particularly when information is fragmented or difficult to personalise. Hello Charly was developed to provide accessible guidance to students aged 14–24 through familiar digital channels. Stakeholders include students, educational institutions, career advisors and public authorities. The objective is to support informed decision-making while ensuring anonymity and ease of access. The service engages users through conversational interactions, asking structured questions to understand interests, preferences and aspirations. It draws on a large, curated database covering more than 850 professions and 110,000 training pathways. Users can explore options, compare alternatives and build personalised scorecards without registering or sharing personal data. Hello Charly is available via web and popular social media platforms. Since its launch in 2016, it has been used by over 220,000 people in France. Reported impacts include improved engagement with guidance services, greater clarity for students and reduced reliance on traditional counselling resources. Its anonymity-first design and broad coverage have supported scalability and sustained uptake.

column2: PARCOURSUP | Screening of university applicants in France
column4: France introduced Parcoursup to manage the allocation of university places following reforms that allowed institutions to select applicants. The system addresses the challenge of matching student preferences with available study programmes at national scale. Stakeholders include prospective students, universities, the Ministry of Higher Education and admissions staff. The objective is to coordinate applications, manage capacity constraints and standardise admissions procedures. Parcoursup collects student preferences, academic records and programme criteria, and applies algorithmic sorting to prioritise applications. While human decision-making remains central at institutional level, the system supports ranking and matching processes. Parcoursup has faced criticism regarding transparency and perceived fairness, particularly due to limited disclosure of sorting logic. Despite ongoing public debate, the system remains in use nationwide. Impacts include faster processing of large application volumes, centralised coordination and reduced administrative fragmentation, alongside continued scrutiny concerning accountability and explainability.

column2: DELIA | Algorithm to predict location and perpetrator of crime
column4: Law enforcement agencies in Milan sought more effective ways to prevent and investigate serial crimes, particularly commercial and bank robberies, which had significant social and economic impacts. Traditional policing methods struggled to identify patterns across large volumes of crime data over time. The City of Milan’s Questura began long-term field trials of predictive crime analysis tools developed by startup KeyCrime, with the objective of supporting investigative work and targeted prevention strategies. Stakeholders included police analysts, operational units, judicial authorities and academic evaluators. The solution analysed historical crime records, spatial and temporal patterns, and offence characteristics to identify links between incidents and likely future scenarios. Over successive trial phases, the system evolved into the DELIA platform, improving usability, analytical performance and pattern-linking capabilities. Results were reviewed by the Italian Ministry of Justice and independently evaluated by the University of Essex to ensure objectivity. The impact was significant: between 2008 and 2019, commercial robberies in Milan decreased by around 58%, while bank robberies in the wider province fell by approximately 89% between 2009 and 2019. The project demonstrated strong operational value in crime prevention and investigation.

column2: Resource Allocation System
column4: The Resource Allocation System (RAS) is just a tool used to generate an estimated budget for community care. Staff will use their judgement as to the amount of money estimated and the suitability of this budget to meet care and support needs. The Social Care professional, the person assessed and their nominated representatives will determine a support plan to meet the outcomes relevant to the identified needs. Support planning is the process which determines how the needs recorded in the assessment will be met for the individual in question. As part of this process, the estimated budget acts as guidance with regard to the likely order of budget that the local authority would expect to spend. The Council take into consideration the application of a strengths based approach and meet needs from the person’s own network and community. All records can be challenged reviewed.

column2: 80 councils in the UK use AI to identify possible frauds in welfare claims
column4: Local authorities in the UK are responsible for administering welfare benefits while preventing fraud and error. To manage limited investigative resources, around 80 councils introduced automated risk screening tools to flag claims for further review. Stakeholders include councils, welfare claimants, investigators and technology providers. The objective is to prioritise checks and reduce erroneous payments. The system analyses administrative data associated with claims and assigns risk indicators that inform follow-up actions by caseworkers. Human review remains mandatory before any decision is taken. Investigations revealed that certain variables, including age, influenced risk assessments, raising concerns about bias and proportionality. As a result, the use of such systems has come under legal, regulatory and public scrutiny. While tools continue to be used, councils have been required to review practices, improve governance and ensure compliance with equality and data protection standards. Impacts include efficiency gains in case selection, alongside heightened awareness of the need for transparency, oversight and safeguards in automated welfare administration.

column2: RADAR-rechts | Assessing the risk of violence by far-right extremists
column4: Following a series of fatal right-wing extremist attacks in Germany in 2019 and 2020, federal and state law enforcement authorities faced increasing pressure to strengthen the prevention of politically motivated violent crime. To support a more systematic and person-centred approach, the Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA) developed RADAR-rechts, a structured risk assessment instrument designed to prioritise individuals known from the right-wing extremist spectrum who may pose an acute threat of committing life-threatening violence. The tool supports police forces nationwide by enabling consistent prioritisation of so-called dangerous and relevant persons and identifying cases requiring more detailed individual risk analysis. RADAR-rechts is based on a scientifically grounded, rule-based analytical framework that evaluates standardised behavioural, situational and criminological indicators drawn from police data and professional assessments. The system integrates with existing police workflows while preserving human responsibility for final decisions. Since becoming operational in May 2022, RADAR-rechts has improved the consistency and transparency of risk management across Germany, allowing law enforcement agencies to allocate preventive and investigative resources more effectively. Its introduction represents a significant step towards standardised, evidence-informed approaches in the person-oriented prevention of far-right extremist violence.

column2: VicomTTS - Quality synthetic voices
column4: VicomTTS was developed to address the limited availability of high-quality digital services in the Basque language, which can hinder its everyday use in public, educational and personal contexts. The service provides citizens, learners and institutions with a reliable tool for converting written Basque text into clear, natural-sounding speech, supporting linguistic inclusion and accessibility. Built as a digital public service, VicomTTS uses neural speech synthesis models implemented in Python and executed via the PyTorch framework, trained on Basque-language datasets to ensure accurate pronunciation and intelligible prosody. The system is deployed as an online service designed for low-latency performance and high reliability. While initial uptake was lower than anticipated, the service has steadily gained popularity and is now processing approximately 5,000 requests per day. Performance metrics indicate that around 50% of requests are handled in under one second, with a reported success rate of 99.7%. VicomTTS demonstrates how advanced speech technologies can contribute to the preservation and digital normalisation of minority languages while delivering a scalable and dependable public-facing service.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Enhancing road safety through automatic identification of traffic violations
column4: Road safety is a paramount concern due to the prevalence of traffic violations such as the absence of seat belts and distracted driving caused by mobile phone usage. These violations not only pose a risk to drivers and passengers but also contribute significantly to road accidents and human error-related sanctions. This presents an opportunity to develop a solution that can effectively identify and discourage these violations, thereby enhancing road safety and reducing the associated risks.. The goal is to improve road safety by identifying and deterring two common violations: the absence of seat belts and the use of mobile phones while driving. The story highlights the potential benefits of implementing a solution that utilizes Artificial Intelligence (AI) for automated violation detection and the optimization of the sanctioning process.

column2: Fraud detection in family allowances (Groeipakket)
column4: The Flemish government provides a family allowance, known as Groeipakket, to every child residing in Flanders. This support program comprises customized family benefits and financial allowances based on the specific circumstances of each child. The total annual allocation for 1.6 million children amounts to 4 billion euros. The benefits system operates through an automated rights assignment approach, by which additional allowances are determined by the parent's income. However, instances of fraud exceeding 500,000 euros were detected in 2022. Fraud exceeding 500,000 euros was detected in 2022, highlighting the need for an AI application capable of identifying fraud indicators and promptly notifying grant outsourcers. Implementing such a system would yield several anticipated benefits, including enhanced fraud detection effectiveness, improved efficiency for file managers and social inspections, and the potential for a budget surplus for the agency.

column2: Proactive administrative procedures
column4: Public administrations often place a heavy administrative burden on citizens, requiring repeated data entry and proactive applications for benefits, which disadvantages those with limited bureaucratic capacity. Spanish authorities explored proactive administrative procedures to ensure that public resources reach those most in need, focusing initially on university scholarships. Stakeholders included students, universities, regional administrations and social services. The objective was to reduce administrative friction while complying with legal requirements that still require explicit citizen consent for procedures. The pilot integrated existing administrative datasets to pre-fill applications and notify eligible students of potential entitlements, minimising the steps required from citizens. Rather than fully automated decision-making, the system supported a “near-proactive” approach, prompting users to confirm intent and data accuracy. The impact to date has been limited due to low uptake and insufficient communication, but the pilot demonstrates potential reductions in administrative workload, fewer missed application deadlines and fairer access to benefits. Lessons learned are informing future iterations and broader diffusion strategies.

column2: Trombia Free experiment in Helsinki
column4: Maintaining cleanliness in large, complex environments such as airport parking areas and service zones is labour-intensive and costly, particularly in Nordic climates with year-round maintenance needs. Helsinki Airport tested the Trombia Free autonomous street sweeper to assess whether automated cleaning could improve efficiency and reliability. Stakeholders included airport operators, facility managers and technology provider startup Trombia Technologies. The objective was to evaluate performance in semi-closed environments combining narrow indoor spaces and busy outdoor areas. The vehicle used sensor-based navigation and Trombia’s patented air-knife cleaning technology, enabling effective dust removal without water, even in sub-zero temperatures. During the pilot, Trombia Free covered between 5,000 and 15,000 m² per hour, outperforming traditional suction sweepers. The results showed improved cleaning efficiency, reduced manual labour requirements and suitability for continuous, year-round operations. Qualitatively, the pilot demonstrated how autonomous maintenance vehicles could lower operational costs and improve service quality in large infrastructure environments.

column2: Swedish police used face recognition until 2019 at the Skavsta airport
column4: As part of Operation Rimfrost, aimed at combating organised crime, the Swedish Police tested facial recognition tools to identify suspects across images from crime scenes and CCTV footage. The objective was to enhance investigative capabilities by quickly identifying recurring individuals across large image datasets. Stakeholders included law enforcement units, oversight authorities and the public. The police used the Clearview AI application during pilot deployments, including at Skavsta Airport. The system compared images against a large external database to suggest potential matches for investigative follow-up. In 2021, the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection ruled that the use of the system violated the Swedish Criminal Data Act, citing unlawful processing of personal data. As a result, the pilot was terminated and all personal data were required to be deleted. The case had significant impact by clarifying legal boundaries for biometric technologies, reinforcing data protection obligations and influencing future governance of digital tools in law enforcement.

column2: Use of smart video surveillance by the local police
column4: Since 2019, local police forces in the Belgian cities of Kortrijk, Kuurne and Lendelede have used smart video surveillance systems to support investigations, public safety and policy analysis. The system assists officers in efficiently reviewing large volumes of video footage, for example by enabling the search for individuals based on attributes such as clothing or movement patterns, reconstructing escape routes, or locating missing persons. It also supports live alerting in predefined situations, such as detecting prohibited vehicle movements near schools, and produces aggregated statistical insights for policing and urban policy. The system stores and categorises objects appearing in video footage, allowing investigators to filter and analyse recordings more effectively. Facial recognition capabilities exist but are subject to strict legal constraints and may only be used in exceptional cases with judicial authorisation. The deployment has improved investigative efficiency and situational awareness, while also raising ongoing discussions about proportionality, oversight and data protection in the use of advanced surveillance technologies by law enforcement.

column2: Automated Facial Recognition technology within the UK's South Wales Police Department
column4: Over the course of 2020, the UK witnessed the first high profile application of the Law Enforcement Directive 15 concerning the use of face recognition technologies in public spaces by the police. Seen as an important precedent on a hotly debated topic, the verdict was greeted with a great deal of attention from civil society actors and legal scholars all over Europe and beyond. The case was brought to court by Ed Bridges, a 37 years old man from Cardiff, who claimed his face was scanned without his consent both during Christmas shopping in 2017, and at a peaceful anti-arms protest a year later. The court initially upheld the use of Automated Facial Recognition technology (“AFR”) by South Wales Police, declaring it lawful and proportionate. But the decision was appealed by Liberty, a civil rights group, and the Court of Appeal of England and Wales decided to overturn the High Court’s dismissal and ruled it unlawful on August 11, 2020.

column2: Face recognition tool for the Slovenian police
column4: The Slovenian police began using facial comparison software as early as 2014 to support criminal investigations involving images from open sources such as social media and online platforms. The tool, known as Face Trace, was used to assist investigators in identifying suspects by comparing facial features across image datasets. However, for several years the use of this technology lacked a clear legal basis and was only formally regulated in 2019, five years after its initial deployment. The system is intended to generate investigative leads rather than definitive identifications, with results requiring verification through traditional investigative methods. Following legal scrutiny and public debate, the use of facial recognition by the Slovenian police has since been significantly constrained, with stricter requirements for authorisation, documentation and oversight. This case has become emblematic of broader European concerns regarding the lawful, transparent and proportionate use of biometric technologies in policing.

column2: Face recognition system at Brussels Airport
column4: In July 2015, Brussels Airport installed ‘e-gates’ with face recognition technology that checks the identity of EU-citizens from outside the Schengen zone. It electronically checks to see if identity papers are real and if a passenger is flagged. In addition, the face recognition software compares the picture on the passport chip in the identity card with the digital picture the software renders at the e-gate (De Morgen, 2019). In early 2020, the airport scrapped the e-gates, which cost €2.4 million, because they were constantly defective, according to the police (The Bulletin, 2020).

column2: SPIRIT - Scalable privacy preserving intelligence analysis for resolving identities
column4: SPIRIT project (“Scalable privacy preserving intelligence analysis for resolving identities”). Aims to use tools such as face extraction and matching, to correlate information from social media data, and to continuously initiate complex associative searches across all sources relevant to criminal investigation. Trial runs will be organized, including in Greece.

column2: Automated surveillance through audio processing
column4: In Saint-Étienne (pop. 175,000), the city planned to deploy microphones in order to automatically detect suspicious sounds. The project was to be implemented in a poor neighborhood, in coordination with CCTV cameras and an autonomous drone equipped with a camera. The plan was to register all “suspicious” sounds, including things like gunshots but also electric drills, sprays, and whistles. It is currently on hold because the French data protection authority considered it illegal.

column2: ALICEM - face recognition for administrative procedures
column4: Government program (“Alicem”) that allows identification via face recognition for administrative procedures. Citizens register their face’s biometric characteristics using a smartphone app. They can then use the app to go through administrative procedures that currently require them to physically visit a government agency. After Bloomberg reported about the scheme in October 2019 (Fouquet, 2019), the public outcry led to the government delaying the launch of the app. Now, it is expected to launch in 2020, but no official launch date has been made public.

column2: BOM - Biometrics on the Move
column4: European border authorities face constant pressure to improve passenger flow efficiency while maintaining high security standards at external borders. Long queues, manual document checks and fragmented procedures can negatively affect both traveller experience and border operations. To explore alternatives, Frontex, together with the Portuguese Immigration and Border Service and Lisbon Airport Authority (ANA), launched the voluntary “Biometrics on the Move” (BOM) pilot at Humberto Delgado International Airport in October 2019. The objective was to test whether travellers could cross borders more smoothly without repeatedly presenting physical documents, while still complying with border control requirements. The pilot relied on biometric enrolment of participating passengers, who consented to the use of their facial image and fingerprints. These biometric identifiers were captured once and then used at subsequent checkpoints, where automated gates matched live biometric scans against enrolled templates. The system integrated with existing border control infrastructure and operated alongside traditional procedures. The pilot demonstrated the technical feasibility of near-seamless border crossing, significantly reducing interaction time at checkpoints and improving passenger experience. While limited in scope and duration, BOM provided valuable insights into operational performance, user acceptance and data protection considerations, informing future EU-level discussions on biometric border management.

column2: Machine vision AI solution for better traffic management in Tallinn
column4: To improve evidence-based transport planning, the City of Tallinn launched a pilot project in 2018 to monitor traffic flows using automated video analysis at selected intersections. The initiative, developed in cooperation with the private company Sifr OÜ, aimed to provide accurate data on the number and types of vehicles entering and leaving the city, supporting decisions on parking management, road construction and congestion mitigation. Cameras installed at three intersections capture traffic footage, which is analysed to count and classify vehicles such as cars, buses, trucks and motorcycles. The system was trained using locally collected data to reflect real traffic conditions. While the pilot was limited in scale, it demonstrated the feasibility of continuous, automated traffic monitoring and provided city authorities with more detailed and timely insights than manual counting methods. The project also laid the groundwork for potential future extensions, including pedestrian counting, although no large-scale expansion has been documented.
column8: Public Transport and Traffic Management Department

column2: System replacing final examinations in a pattern following previous years
column4: Automated decision-making system deployed by the Office of Qualifications and Examinations Regulation (Ofqual) on the basis of an data-based assessment to replace A-level examinations as wells as General Certificates of Secondary Education i 2020 and replacing International Baccalaureate (IB) final examinations in 2020 in such a way that “the distribution of grades follows a similar pattern to that in other years”. As exams did not take place this summer, students’ grades were instead based on evidence of their likely performance in the exams had they gone ahead. In the absence of any formal assessments delivered by exam boards, evidence relating to students’ expected performance in qualifications this year was required. The algorithm was scrapped after students' protests.
column8: Office of Qualifications and Examinations Regulation (Ofqual)

column2: System called ARNO for submitting and processing application in Estonia
column4: The City of Tartu uses the ARNO system to manage applications and placements for municipal nursery schools, kindergartens and general education schools in a centralised and transparent manner. The system addresses the administrative complexity of allocating limited places fairly while taking into account parental preferences, proximity and eligibility rules. Parents apply for childcare and school places through a single digital interface, while municipal education authorities use the system to manage queues and generate annual placement decisions. ARNO integrates population registry data, address information and parental input to automatically determine placements, with options for families to request changes if necessary. Developed by the Estonian company OÜ Piksel, the system has become a core component of Tartu’s education administration, reducing manual workload and improving clarity for families. By standardising decision rules and making the process traceable, ARNO has increased trust in allocation outcomes and improved service efficiency.

column2: ETIAS (EU Travel Information and Authorisation System)
column4: The Schengen Area faces the challenge of facilitating legitimate travel while managing security, migration, and public health risks associated with visa-exempt travellers. ETIAS was designed to address this by introducing a pre-travel authorisation requirement for non-EU nationals who do not need a visa to enter Schengen States. Stakeholders include EU border authorities, national security agencies, travellers, and the EU Agency eu-LISA, which is responsible for system development and operation. The objective is to strengthen external border management while reducing refusals at border crossings and improving traveller predictability. ETIAS operates as a centralised IT system that collects traveller application data and automatically checks it against multiple EU information systems, including security, migration, and health-related databases. Risk rules and screening criteria are applied to identify applications requiring further manual assessment by national authorities. Most applications are expected to be processed within minutes, while a minority may take up to 30 days. Although initially planned for earlier deployment, ETIAS has been postponed and is now expected to become operational in 2026, with transitional measures foreseen. Once active, ETIAS is expected to streamline border checks, enhance security cooperation, and improve the overall efficiency and reliability of Schengen border management.

column2: European Criminal Records Information System – Third Country Nationals (ECRIS-TCN)
column4: The system provides judges, prosecutors and other relevant authorities with easy access to comprehensive information on an individual’s criminal history, no matter in which Member State that person was convicted in the past. Efficient exchange of criminal records information is instrumental in combating cross-border crime. ECRIS-TCN will contribute to implementing the principle of mutual recognition of sentences and judicial decisions in a common area of justice and security where people move freely, such as the Schengen Area. The ECRIS-TCN System is scheduled to be ready in conjunction with the roll-out of the components required to implement interoperability.

column2: CENTAUR - Greek camps for asylum seekers surveillance systems
column4: The Greek Ministry of Migration and Asylum has implemented the Greece Integrated Maritime Surveillance System (GIMSS) in refugee camps to enhance security and prevent illegal activities. GIMSS utilizes drones, thermal cameras, and motion sensors for real-time monitoring and data collection. The collected data is processed using advanced algorithms to generate actionable intelligence. However, concerns have been raised about the lack of transparency and public consultation during the system's deployment. Privacy and human rights issues arise, with potential violations of refugees' rights and their freedom of movement and privacy.

column2: BorderSens - Border detection of illicit drugs and precursors by highly accurate electrosensors
column4: Customs and border authorities require fast, reliable tools to detect illicit drugs, precursors and cutting agents during inspections, often under time pressure and with limited laboratory access. Existing methods can be slow, costly or prone to false positives, creating operational inefficiencies. The EU-funded BorderSens project aimed to address this challenge by developing a portable, low-cost and user-friendly device for on-site drug detection. Stakeholders included border guards, customs officers, forensic experts and research institutions. The objective was to enable accurate identification of a wide range of substances within seconds, supporting frontline decision-making. The solution combined electrochemical sensing with selective nano-Molecularly Imprinted Polymers (nanoMIPs) and advanced nanomaterials to capture and enrich chemical fingerprints. These signals were processed through analytical models to distinguish drugs, precursors and adulterants with high precision. The prototype targeted results within 45 seconds, with reduced rates of false positives and negatives. The project demonstrated strong potential for improving border inspection efficiency, lowering operational costs and enhancing reliability compared to traditional field tests, while supporting safer and more effective enforcement activities.

column2: Project RAPID - Automatic Identification of Passengers Holding Traveling Documents
column4: The Portuguese Immigration and Border Service invested 4.2M euros + VAT in 2019 to update Project RAPID, an automated system for controlling passengers crossing Schengen's external borders. RAPID combines the operations of reading and verifying electronic passports with innovative biometric assessment features. The system checks the authenticity of electronic passports and validates the data stored in the chip. It also compares the passenger's photo stored in the chip with their physical appearance on-site, automatically opening the border gate when the images match. The newly purchased devices will be installed in the airports of Lisbon, Porto, Faro, and Madeira, enhancing border control capabilities.

column2: Automated decision making system to determine the risk represented by visa applicants
column4: In August 2020, the UK government's Home Office abandoned an ADM system called the "Streaming tool" that assessed the risk of visa applicants. The algorithm faced legal challenges from the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants (JCWI) and Foxglove, as it was believed to perpetuate racism and bias in the visa system. The algorithm suffered from "feedback loop" issues, where past bias and discrimination influenced future outcomes. It prioritized applicants from predominantly white countries and subjected people from other nations to a more rigorous review process. In response to the criticism, the Home Office announced plans to redesign the ADM system, explicitly addressing concerns about unconscious bias and the use of nationality in the new system.

column2: EU Exit: ID document check - applying for settled status in the UK after Brexit
column4: The government implemented the registration system in the form of a smartphone app “EU Exit: ID document check” app. Applicants are required to complete five steps: firstly, they take an image of the personal details page of either their passport or identity card. Then, they must scan this document, via a smartphone, by using the NFC feature. The smartphone then reads the contents of the electronic chip stored inside modern identity documents. The applicant must then take a video and a photo of their face. Finally, she or he must answer an online questionnaire. If the applicant submits their National Identification number, automated processing of tax and other benefits is carried out. This algorithm-based verification process is done against records held by the tax authority (HMRC) and the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP). This is not a mandatory step, but official documentation says that applicants who submit their NI number will not have to provide further evidence.

column2: Pro-Kid used for risk assessment by the Dutch police
column4: The Dutch police have used an automated risk assessment tool called ProKid since 2011 to evaluate the risk of (re)offending among children and young people. The tool is an actuarial risk assessment system that assigns weights to specific information to estimate the risk of future violent and property offenses. However, it does not actually predict the likelihood of criminality but rather the likelihood of a child being registered on the police system in relation to a crime. ProKid utilizes police data from criminal fact and criminal opinion databases to formulate its risk assessments. Being assessed as 'at risk' by ProKid can have significant consequences for children and their families, potentially leading to separation and involvement with youth care and child abuse protection services. The assessments have been found to be incorrect and erroneous in a significant number of cases. A Ministry of Security and Justice evaluation revealed that one-third of the assessed children had their risk levels mis-assigned due to errors or reports based on irrelevant incidents. ProKid profiles serve as the initial step in a series of automated risk assessments that can have serious implications in the criminal justice system. Despite its name, ProKid has raised concerns and controversy.

column2: “Top 600” and “Top400” used for automated risk modelling
column4: The Amsterdam Municipality, police, and social services use the "Top 600" and "Top 400" systems for automated risk modeling and profiling. The Top 600 focuses on identifying the 600 young people most at risk of committing "High Impact Crime" based on criteria such as past arrests for serious offenses, bankruptcy proceedings, and convictions. Being on the Top 600 list leads to consequences like increased penalties, active prosecution for minor offenses, and asset confiscation. Those listed face regular police surveillance, home checks, limited participation in activities, potential residence permit revocation, and employment difficulties. The Top 400 targets children under 16 to prevent involvement in crime and uses the ProKid risk assessment model with broader criteria, including non-criminal indicators and mere suspicion of involvement. These systems have significant impacts, including ongoing surveillance, restricted activities, and potential negative consequences for employment and residence permits.

column2: The Sensing Project for risk assessment and profiling by the Roermond police
column4: In Roermond, the Netherlands, the police use an algorithmic system called the Sensing Project for risk assessment and profiling. The project aims to identify and "predict" likely perpetrators of pickpocketing and shoplifting in a local shopping center. The Roermond police developed the algorithm based on their own criteria, focusing on a concept they call "mobile banditry," committed by foreign groups from Eastern European countries. Dutch nationals are excluded from this definition. The Sensing Project algorithm evaluates individuals traveling in Roermond against specific criteria. These criteria include traveling by car with passengers, following certain routes, having specific license plates, or using rental cars associated with previous criminality. The algorithm calculates an overall risk score, and if it's high, the car, driver, and passengers are labeled as "suspicious," generating a "hit" in the system. Police officers have discretion in responding to these notifications. Even if individuals in the car are not considered suspected criminals, their car's details are recorded in the police system. Subsequently, when the car is driven in Roermond again, it receives a higher risk score due to its previous registration in the system, regardless of any evidence or police suspicion of criminality beyond the Sensing Project's risk assessment.

column2: Cassandra – Automated risk assessment system used by the Ukraine Ministry of Justice
column4: Judicial systems often seek tools to support consistent and efficient pre-trial and pre-sentence decision-making, particularly in assessing the likelihood of re-offending. In Ukraine, the Ministry of Justice introduced Cassandra, an automated system used by probation services to support the preparation of reports for judges. Stakeholders include probation officers, judges, defendants and justice authorities. The objective is to provide a structured assessment of re-offending risk to inform sentencing decisions, such as custodial versus non-custodial measures. Probation officers input information about an individual offender through a structured questionnaire covering personal history, social factors and prior behaviour. The system assigns weighted scores to responses and aggregates them into a numerical risk estimate ranging from 0 to 97. These outputs are included in reports submitted to courts. While Cassandra has streamlined report preparation and introduced greater standardisation, limited transparency exists regarding the underlying data, weighting logic and validation. Concerns have been raised about accountability, explainability and potential bias. The case highlights both the efficiency gains of automated assessment tools and the importance of governance, oversight and safeguards in justice contexts.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: AI tool to predict recidivism in prisoners in Switzerland
column4: The Swiss Execution of Penal Sentences and Justice follows a system of levels where inmates are granted increasing freedom based on their imprisonment duration. To prevent recidivism, the Risk-Oriented Sanctioning (ROS) system was introduced, dividing offender work into triage, assessment, planning, and progress stages. The triage stage uses an automated tool called the Fall-Screening-Tool (FaST) to classify cases into classes A, B, and C based on risk factors. Class C indicates a risk of violent or sexual delinquency, while class B signifies a general risk of further delinquency. Class A has no assessment needs. The FaST tool uses criminal records and statistical risk factors to determine classifications. However, it is not a risk analysis but a way to identify cases requiring further assessment. The triage classification guides decision-making and determines the offender's problem profile for sentence execution planning. ADM applications are not used in other stages of ROS, and FaST is only utilized during the triage stage.
column8: Swiss Execution of Penal Sentences and Justice

column2: Gangs Violence Matrix (GVM) to predict crime in London
column4: This predictive policing tool is used since 2012 by the Metropolitan Police in order to identify and risk-assess gang members across London involved in gang violence, and to identify those at risk of victimisation. The GVM measures the harm that individuals pose by scoring them based on evidence of them committing violence and weapons offences, as well as police intelligence relating to them having access to weapons, or them being involved in, or at risk from, gang violence: individuals are thus graded as red, amber or green, denoting the level of risk (for victims) or harm (for offenders) they present. The database encompasses both adults and minors. Amnesty International criticised the Metropolitan Police for refusing to divulge information about the precise criteria used to assign automated 'harm scores' to individual on the matrix.

column2: Harm Assessment Risk Tool (HART) in Durham
column4: Preliminary research into a two-year pilot programme run by Durham Constabulary, found the Harm Assessment Risk Tool (Hart) was slightly better overall at predicting reoffending rates than human custody sergeants. As part of the study, overseen by Cambridge University affiliate Dr Geoffrey Barnes, both the machine-learning program and human custody officers assessed whether offenders brought into custody were at high, medium or low risk of reoffending. Hart successfully predicted that 89.8% of those who were actually brought back into custody for a serious offence within two years had been at high risk of reoffending, compared to an 81.2% success rate for custody officers. Of those predicted to be at low risk of reoffending, Hart got it right 78.8% of the time, compared to 66.5% accurate forecasting by humans. The study was based on individuals who were brought into custody in County Durham and Darlington between September 2016 and October 2017, then followed up whether they reoffended and returned to custody within two years.

column2: STIR - an algorithm to fight VAT fraud in Poland
column4: The STIR system in Poland aims to automatically identify and label suspicious transactions and fraudulent entrepreneurs to predict VAT fraud. It gathers financial information from banks and operates under the State Clearance Chamber. The system uses automated decision-making tools to provide risk indicators to tax authorities, allowing them to block suspicious accounts without informing the bank or account owner. The freezing of an account can last up to 72 hours initially, with the possibility of extension up to three months. The algorithm used by STIR remains undisclosed, but criteria such as unusual bank account activity and transfers to high-risk fraud countries are considered. The law prohibits the disclosure or unauthorized use of algorithms or risk indicators, with potential imprisonment of up to five years for those who violate this provision.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Gladsaxe Model - profiling families for early detection of vulnerable children
column4: The Gladsaxe-model was a points-based profiling system designed to detect children in vulnerable families. It utilized risk indicators such as parental mental health, unemployment, missed medical and dental appointments, and divorce. Initially a pilot project, it faced heavy criticism and was put on hold. However, the municipality of Gladsaxe continued working on the algorithm by incorporating additional data. Permission to proceed was denied by data protection authorities, leading to the project's termination without explanation. In 2020, a new research project at the University of Århus aimed to develop an algorithmic tool for identifying vulnerable children, but faced criticism for following a similar approach as the Gladsaxe Model.

column2: Offender Assessment System (OASys)
column4: The Offender Assessment System (OASys) is a national risk/need assessment tool with algorithmic components used across probation areas and prison establishments in England and Wales. Originally trialled as a paper-based system, OASys rapidly became a digital tool, and has gathered a range of digital components. OASys was designed by the Ministry of Justice, and is today managed in close connection to the Ministry of Justice Data Science Hub. Its stated aims are to assess how likely an offender is to reoffend, identify and classify offending-related needs, assess risk of serious harm, risks to the individual and other risks, assist with management of risk of serious harm, link the assessment to the sentence plan, indicate the need for further specialist assessments, and measure change during the offender’s sentence. OASys assessments are carried out at several points in the justice system: for a pre-sentence report; at the start of a sentence in prison or in the community; at regular review periods; at key decision points, such as when an offender is up for parole; and on termination of the sentence.

column2: National Data Analytics Solution (NDAS)
column4: National Data Analytics Solution (NDAS) is used for risk assessment and profiling by West Midlands Police, UK Home Office & other police forces. NDAS uses machine-learning and predictive analytics to conduct “behavioural analysis” and “predictive modelling” in order to create and provide individual predictions and profiles about people and their likely future actions. These are intended to inform and influence pre-emptive policing interventions, using data from police intelligence reports on individuals and ‘events’, stop and search data, drug use data and custody information. Stop and search data use was criticised because of its discriminatory nature: for instance, in 2019/20, Black people were four and a half times more likely than white people to be stopped and searched in the West Midlands Police area of England.

column2: Machine-learning to detect tax fraud in France
column4: The French finance ministry introduced a legal amendment in the 2020 budget law allowing them to scrape data from social networks, classified advertising websites, and auction platforms to detect tax fraud. This includes comparing a person's lifestyle on platforms like Instagram with their tax returns using machine learning algorithms. The plan faced criticism from the French data protection authority, but the Constitutional Court ultimately deemed the scheme legal after several MPs appealed against it.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Profiling system for the unemployed
column4: To claim unemployment benefits, a profile is created for newly unemployed individuals using a nationwide tool. This profile is automatically generated and includes data such as age, origin, unemployment history, previous jobs, education, and the cause of unemployment, including health indications. The purpose of this profile is to estimate the likelihood of long-term unemployment and provide decision support to officials handling the case. It is important to note that this profile is not used as an automated decision-making tool but rather as a tool to support decision-making processes.

column2: Automatic proactive social service
column4: In 2019, Estonia's Social Insurance Board (SKA) introduced an automatic proactive service that eliminates the need for parents to apply for services. Leveraging the existing citizen information collected at birth, the SKA activates family benefits automatically. Once a child's birth is registered and they are named, the Social Security Agency notifies the parents via email. After confirming receipt, the parents can start receiving family benefits. This unique solution, developed by Nortal, uses an automated algorithmic system and has resulted in organizational process improvements within the SKA. The goal is to transition towards application-free services, making it the first event-based service of its kind in Estonia.

column2: AuroraAI – Life-Event Management for Public Services
column4: Citizens often struggle to navigate complex public services during major life events, such as moving, retirement, or job loss, leading to unmet needs and administrative inefficiencies. AuroraAI addresses this challenge by rethinking service delivery around life events rather than institutional structures. Stakeholders include citizens, public authorities, and service providers across sectors. The objective is to proactively guide individuals towards relevant services based on their personal circumstances, reducing friction and improving outcomes. The solution integrates data from multiple public sector domains to identify patterns associated with life events and anticipate service needs. Personalised recommendations are generated to suggest appropriate public and private services at the right time. The system supports proactive outreach while respecting governance and oversight requirements. The initiative demonstrates improved accessibility of services, more coordinated public support, and enhanced user experience. It provides a vision for citizen-centric service ecosystems, where timely guidance and personalised pathways help individuals navigate life transitions more effectively.

column2: Automated benefit processes at the Social Insurance Institution of Finland
column4: Kela utilizes automation for routine tasks that don't require individual consideration. Automated decisions are made without human involvement but only in cases where the issues are indisputable and can be resolved by applying relevant legislation and known facts. Machine learning or statistical modeling is not used for automated decision-making. Automation improves customer service delivery by speeding up processes, allowing Kela to allocate freed-up resources for customer advice and support. Automated decisions clearly indicate their automatic nature, provide information sources, and include instructions for appeals. Kela makes automated decisions in various contexts, such as benefit rate adjustments, student financial aid awards, cancellations, and reviews based on updated information. They also automate decisions related to government guarantees for student loans and certain benefit cancellations for non-compliance or lack of response. Proposed decisions on student financial aid benefits may also be automated, becoming final if not reviewed by the recipient.

column2: System to control social welfare recipients
column4: The Udbetaling Danmark (Payout Denmark, UDK), the Public Authority responsible for payments under the welfare state system, automatically controls the lawfulness of payments by collecting a wide variety of datasets including civil registers, housing registers, or the tax authorities also of housemates and family member of beneficiaries and analysing them. Data analysis is based upon a set of criteria that are being developed constantly, including “modular criteria” where data analysis is run on different questions, and all such sub-results are, then, included in the overall conclusion. The organization, which is run by the agency for the digitization of the ministry of finance, was set up to centralize the payment of welfare benefits overseen by municipalities: pensions and benefits related to housing, family, disability, and maternity leave. The agency was criticised because Its fraud control algorithms can access the personal data of millions of citizens, not all of whom receive welfare payments.
column8: Agency for the digitalisation (Ministry of Finance)

column2: Pensioners prove they are alive via face or voice recognition
column4: Each year, thousands of Portuguese pensioners living abroad must prove they are alive to continue receiving benefits, traditionally requiring in-person visits to consulates or paper-based processes. This creates administrative burdens for both pensioners and public authorities, particularly for elderly individuals with mobility constraints. Caixa Geral de Aposentações launched a pilot to simplify this process using remote identity verification, initially in Macau. Stakeholders include pensioners abroad, welfare administrators and consular services. The objective was to reduce costs, improve convenience and maintain trust in pension disbursement. The system allows pensioners to submit proof of life remotely using biometric verification, based on facial or voice recognition through a digital interface. Captured biometric samples are matched against previously registered references to confirm identity, after which confirmation is transmitted securely to the pension authority. The pilot showed clear efficiency gains and was positively received by digitally confident users. However, feedback also highlighted usability challenges for some elderly participants unfamiliar with digital tools. The project demonstrated both the administrative benefits of remote verification and the need for inclusive design and alternative channels.

column2: PHM Hydro – Performance and Health Monitoring for Hydropower Plants
column4: Hydropower operators depend on the reliable performance of turbines, generators, and auxiliary equipment to ensure safe, continuous, and profitable electricity production. Unplanned downtime or undetected efficiency losses can lead to significant economic impacts and operational risks. The PHM Hydro initiative addresses the need for systematic, scalable monitoring of equipment condition and performance across multiple components and power plants. Stakeholders include hydropower plant operators, maintenance teams, and energy companies seeking to optimise asset management and extend equipment lifetime. The objective is to establish a unified architecture that supports early fault detection, performance optimisation, and predictive maintenance. The solution combines physics-based modelling with data-driven analytics to analyse sensor data from critical components. Algorithms are deployed within a cloud and fog computing architecture, enabling both local processing and centralised analysis. This flexible design supports scalability across plants and assets while integrating new analytical methods over time. The approach improves visibility into equipment health, reduces unplanned outages, and supports condition-based maintenance strategies. The project delivers increased operational efficiency, lower maintenance costs, and enhanced reliability of hydropower generation, contributing to a more resilient and sustainable energy system.
column8: Royal Norwegian Ministry of Trade, Industry and Fisheries

column2: Heart failure risk prediction by Ahus
column4: The Ahus project will develop an algorithm that will be able to predict the risk of heart failure. The tool is based, among other things, on ECG measurements and is intended to be used as decision support in the clinic.

column2: Robotised baggage handling (Roba) in airports project
column4: Airports face increasing operational pressure to handle growing passenger volumes while maintaining safety, efficiency and cost control in baggage handling operations. Manual packing of luggage into trolleys is labour-intensive, ergonomically challenging and difficult to scale. Avinor initiated the RoBa (Robotised Baggage Handling) project to research and pilot automated methods for packing luggage and managing trolley transport at Oslo Gardermoen Airport. Stakeholders include airport operators, ground handling services, technology providers and passengers. The objective is to improve robustness, throughput and working conditions. The project explores robotic manipulation systems capable of handling luggage of varying sizes and weights, integrated with automated transport and intermediate storage systems. Sensor-based perception and control systems enable robots to place baggage efficiently into trolleys while adapting to operational variability. Results from the research phase are being implemented in a pilot facility. Expected impacts include reduced manual labour, improved safety, more predictable operations and scalability to other airports, supporting long-term efficiency gains in baggage handling.

column2: FLAIT – AI-Assisted Airport Safety Reporting through Human–AI Teaming
column4: Airport aprons are complex, high-risk environments where aircraft, vehicles, and personnel operate in close proximity, leading to frequent safety incidents and costly damage. Despite the scale of the risk, safety reporting in many airports remains manual, time-consuming, and prone to underreporting. The FLAIT project addresses this challenge by supporting safety reporting through human–technology collaboration. Stakeholders include airport operators, ground handling staff, and safety managers responsible for reducing incidents and improving situational awareness. The objective is to increase the volume, quality, and timeliness of safety reports without adding burden to personnel. The solution introduces the FLAIT Assistant, a decision support system that supports apron staff in documenting incidents more efficiently. The system automates parts of the reporting workflow, structures incident information, and supports consistency, while keeping humans in control of validation and decision-making. By lowering reporting barriers, the approach encourages more comprehensive incident capture. The project improves safety data availability, strengthens risk analysis, and supports proactive safety management. It contributes to reduced ground damage, improved operational safety, and a stronger safety culture at airports through effective human–technology teaming.

column2: IARI - AI-based image recognition for railway infrastructure inspection
column4: Ensuring the safety and reliability of railway infrastructure requires frequent inspections of tracks, overhead lines, and supporting structures. Traditional manual inspections are labour-intensive, costly, and often require track closures, disrupting services and limiting inspection frequency. The IARI project addresses these challenges by modernising inspection processes to improve efficiency and coverage. Stakeholders include infrastructure managers, maintenance teams, and railway operators seeking safer and more cost-effective inspection methods. The objective is to automate selected inspection tasks while improving understanding of infrastructure condition. The solution uses image recognition techniques applied to data captured by cameras mounted on trains and drones, complemented by infrared and depth sensors. Algorithms are trained on datasets of known defects to identify faults such as wear, cracks, or misalignments.
