Image-based analysis is combined with classical methods to improve robustness and interpretability. The system supports continuous data collection without service disruption. The project reduces the need for manual inspections, improves safety for inspection personnel, and enables earlier detection of defects. It delivers cost savings, improved asset management, and more resilient railway operations through data-driven infrastructure monitoring.

column2: AI decision support in medical emergency calls
column4: The project AISMEC is divided in three parts: First, a detailed mapping of what contact stroke patients in Helse Bergen was made with the healthcare system before they reach the hospital and end up in the Stroke Register. Then they use these patients' 113 calls and structured hospital data (Norwegian Patient Register) to develop and test (on historical data) an AI model. Finally, the model will be put into use live in AMK Bergen to investigate whether the accuracy for stroke is better with the use of AI, compared to the current system.

column2: AI for improved diagnostics in endometrial cancer
column4: Accurate diagnosis and personalised treatment of endometrial cancer require deeper integration of imaging, molecular, and clinical information. While medical imaging reveals structural and functional patterns associated with disease progression, these biomarkers are not yet fully embedded in clinical decision-making. This initiative addresses the need to translate imaging-derived insights into actionable diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. Stakeholders include clinicians, researchers, and patients seeking more precise diagnostics and tailored treatments. The objective is to improve diagnostic accuracy and identify molecular targets by linking imaging phenotypes to underlying disease mechanisms. The solution analyses imaging data to extract quantitative biomarkers associated with tumour characteristics and progression. These biomarkers are correlated with molecular and clinical data to support stratification of patients and inform treatment pathways. The approach supports integration into clinical workflows and research settings. The project improves understanding of disease heterogeneity, supports more informed treatment selection, and lays the groundwork for personalised oncology. It contributes to earlier and more accurate diagnosis, improved treatment outcomes, and more efficient use of healthcare resources in gynaecological oncology.

column2: CoSeM - Computational sepsis mining and modelling
column4: Sepsis is a life-threatening condition requiring rapid detection and intervention, yet early signs are often subtle and distributed across unstructured clinical notes and structured patient data. Clinicians face challenges in continuously monitoring complex patient trajectories in real time. The CoSeM project addresses this by enhancing clinical decision support for infection and sepsis management. Stakeholders include healthcare professionals, hospitals, and patients in acute care settings. The objective is to support early detection, personalised risk assessment, and timely intervention. The solution integrates structured clinical data with temporal interpretation of free-text clinical notes to reconstruct individual patient histories. These data feed models that monitor infection progression and sepsis risk in real time. Outputs are presented through a clinical dashboard that extends existing electronic health records with process monitoring and alerts. The project improves situational awareness, supports individualised care, and reduces delayed interventions. Indirectly, it contributes to more appropriate treatment decisions and reduced antimicrobial resistance. CoSeM delivers improved patient safety, better clinical outcomes, and enhanced use of healthcare data for real-time decision support.

column2: Fraxinus - An open source, software only, navigation system for bronchoscopy
column4: Bronchoscopy is a critical procedure for diagnosing lung cancer, yet navigating the complex airway structure to reach small peripheral lesions remains challenging. Limited visibility and anatomical complexity result in low diagnostic success rates for non-visible tumours, increasing the need for repeat procedures or more invasive diagnostics. The Fraxinus project addresses this challenge by improving navigation support during bronchoscopy. Stakeholders include pulmonologists, hospitals, and patients requiring accurate and minimally invasive diagnostics. The objective is to increase biopsy success rates and reduce procedure complexity. The solution provides a software-only, open-source navigation system that guides clinicians through the airway tree using preoperative imaging and real-time procedural data. By correlating planned pathways with live bronchoscope positioning, the system supports more precise targeting of lesions. The approach integrates into existing clinical workflows without requiring proprietary hardware. Fraxinus improves diagnostic accuracy, reduces procedure time, and increases confidence in sampling difficult-to-reach lesions. It supports wider adoption of navigated bronchoscopy, leading to earlier diagnosis, improved patient outcomes, and more efficient use of clinical resources.

column2: IDDEAS - Individual Digital Decision Assist System for Child and Adolescent Mental Health
column4: Children and adolescents with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder represent a significant proportion of mental health cases in Norway, with long-term consequences if diagnosis and treatment are delayed or inadequate. Clinicians face challenges in providing consistent, evidence-based care tailored to each patient’s evolving needs, particularly across prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up. The IDDEAS project addresses this gap by supporting specialist healthcare services with structured, personalised decision support. Stakeholders include healthcare professionals, patients, families, and the wider health system. The objective is to improve clinical outcomes and service quality through early intervention, continuous monitoring, and optimised treatment pathways. The solution is a knowledge-based decision support system that adapts clinical data, guidelines, and patient-reported information to individual needs. It provides clinicians with evidence-based recommendations and supports personalised assessment and follow-up workflows. The system is designed to integrate into existing healthcare processes and support prevention and early diagnosis as well as long-term management. The expected impact includes improved diagnostic accuracy, more targeted interventions, better continuity of care, and reduced burden on families and services. IDDEAS contributes to more effective, equitable, and sustainable mental healthcare for young people.

column2: Segmenting and characterising lymph nodes from thoracic CT scans using machine learning
column4: Accurate staging of lung cancer depends on reliably identifying whether the disease has spread to nearby lymph nodes and understanding their anatomical relationships. Manual interpretation of thoracic CT scans is complex and time-consuming, particularly in the mediastinal region where structures are densely packed. This project addresses the need for precise, reproducible tools to support clinicians in cancer diagnosis and treatment planning. Stakeholders include radiologists, oncologists, and patients requiring accurate assessment. The objective is to improve detection and localisation of potentially malignant lymph nodes while reducing manual workload. The solution introduces a newly annotated dataset covering fifteen mediastinal anatomical structures and lymph nodes of varying sizes. A two-dimensional semantic segmentation and instance detection pipeline is developed to automatically identify and delineate these structures in CT images. The approach enables clear spatial understanding of lymph nodes in relation to airways and surrounding anatomy. The results improve diagnostic confidence, support more accurate staging, and facilitate treatment planning. By enhancing consistency and efficiency, the project contributes to better clinical decision-making and more personalised lung cancer care.

column2: AI for cancer treatment
column4: Radiotherapy planning requires precise delineation of tumours and surrounding organs at risk, a process that traditionally involves extensive manual work by oncologists. Drawing anatomical structures across hundreds of CT slices is time-consuming and can limit the time available for clinical decision-making and patient interaction. This use case addresses the need to streamline treatment planning while maintaining accuracy and safety. Stakeholders include oncologists, hospitals, and cancer patients. The objective is to reduce manual workload and enable clinicians to focus on treatment quality and patient care. The solution automates the identification and outlining of organs such as the heart and lungs within radiotherapy planning systems. Automated segmentation supports faster preparation of treatment plans and reduces variability between clinicians. The impact includes significant time savings, increased capacity to treat more patients, and improved consistency in treatment planning. Clinicians can dedicate more time to evaluating individual cases and refining therapeutic strategies. The initiative enhances efficiency in oncology departments and supports high-quality, patient-centred cancer treatment.

column2: AI in image-guided neurosurgery
column4: Brain tumour treatment requires careful consideration of tumour characteristics, progression, and response to therapy, yet predicting outcomes remains complex. Clinicians must interpret large volumes of imaging and clinical data to guide personalised treatment decisions. This project addresses the need to integrate imaging biomarkers with clinical knowledge to support prognosis and therapy planning. Stakeholders include neurosurgeons, oncologists, researchers, and patients. The objective is to improve understanding of disease progression and enable more personalised treatment strategies. The solution develops predictive models trained on imaging, clinical, and outcome data from the Central Norwegian Brain Tumour Registry and Biobank, complemented by national and international datasets. Automated image analysis extracts relevant features, which are combined with historical patient data to predict survival and treatment response. The project improves insight into tumour behaviour, supports individualised decision-making, and advances research into personalised neuro-oncology. It contributes to more informed treatment choices, improved patient outcomes, and better use of clinical data in neurosurgical practice.

column2: Personalised decision support for patients with chronic pain
column4: Chronic pain affects a diverse patient population with varying causes, responses to treatment, and side effects, making it difficult to identify optimal therapies. Existing decision support tools are largely generic and fail to account for individual patient differences. This project addresses the need for personalised, evidence-based treatment guidance for chronic pain management. Stakeholders include patients, clinicians, and healthcare institutions. The objective is to support shared decision-making by providing tailored treatment options with transparent probabilities of outcomes and side effects. The solution collects patient-reported data via a mobile application and combines it with electronic health record data from multiple institutions. Privacy is ensured through secret sharing and distributed computing techniques, allowing data to contribute to analysis without being centrally shared. Personalised decision trees are generated in real time to support clinical consultations. The impact includes more informed treatment choices, improved patient engagement, and better alignment between therapies and individual needs. The project supports more effective and patient-centred chronic pain management.

column2: ClinCode: Computerised clinical ICD-10 coding
column4: Accurate ICD-10 coding is essential for clinical documentation, billing, and healthcare planning, yet the complexity of the coding system often leads to incomplete or incorrect entries. Clinicians face time constraints and administrative burden, which can compromise coding quality. The ClinCode project addresses this challenge by supporting clinicians with computer-assisted coding. Stakeholders include healthcare professionals, hospital administrators, and health authorities. The objective is to improve coding accuracy and completeness without increasing clinicians’ workload. The solution analyses existing electronic patient records, including free-text notes and structured data, to learn coding patterns from previously coded cases. Using natural language processing and deep learning techniques, the system suggests relevant ICD-10 codes during clinical documentation. The impact includes higher coding quality, improved billing accuracy, and better data for planning and research. ClinCode enhances efficiency, reduces administrative burden, and supports more reliable use of clinical data across healthcare systems.

column2: IM-PACT - Identification of multimorbid patients with modifiable risk profiles using artificial intelligence
column4: Patients with multiple chronic conditions often experience fragmented care, delayed interventions, and limited involvement in decision-making, leading to poorer outcomes and inefficient use of health resources. In Northern Norway, healthcare providers face the challenge of coordinating hospital and municipal services for complex patient groups with diverse and evolving needs. The IM-PACT project addresses this gap by supporting a patient-centred, integrated care model that identifies individuals with multimorbidity and modifiable risk profiles. Stakeholders include patients, carers, clinicians, and healthcare organisations involved in the PAtient Centered-Team (PACT) model. The objective is to enable proactive, timely, and personalised care by identifying patients who can benefit most from targeted interventions. The solution analyses large-scale datasets from hospitals and municipal care services to identify patterns of risk and opportunity for intervention. Data-driven models support stratification of patients and provide insights to multidisciplinary care teams, while incorporating patient and carer feedback into care planning. The project improves coordination across care levels, supports shared decision-making, and enables earlier, more tailored interventions. IM-PACT contributes to improved clinical outcomes, better patient experiences, and more sustainable delivery of integrated healthcare services.

column2: Predicting the risk of postoperative wound infections with AI
column4: The main goal is to develop an AI-based tool that can indicate the probability of a postoperative wound infection occurring in the individual patient, so that risk-reducing measures can be taken and the burden of disease for the patient is reduced. The project's team will evaluate the validity of four non-AI-based risk tools to see which best predict the risk of postoperative wound infection. The variables, such as previous illnesses and age, will be used to develop algorithms for an AI-based risk tool, which we assume will predict the risk of postoperative wound infection better than the non-AI-based risk tools.

column2: Prediction of relapse in glioblastoma
column4: Glioblastoma is an aggressive brain tumour with a poor prognosis, and recurrence after surgery is common, often occurring in tissue surrounding the original tumour site. Surgeons and oncologists face difficult decisions about how much tissue to remove and how to plan subsequent treatment, given the risks of neurological damage. This project addresses the need for better tools to anticipate tumour relapse and guide personalised treatment strategies. Stakeholders include neurosurgeons, oncologists, radiologists, and patients with glioblastoma. The objective is to identify areas at high risk of tumour regrowth using post-operative imaging. The solution analyses magnetic resonance and positron emission tomography images of peripheral brain regions surrounding the resection site. Predictive models identify patterns associated with future tumour formation, enabling risk mapping of surrounding tissue. The results support more informed surgical planning and treatment decisions, potentially improving local tumour control. The project contributes to personalised neuro-oncology, with the potential to improve survival outcomes and optimise treatment strategies for patients with glioblastoma.

column2: WARIFA - Watching the risk factors to prevent chronic conditions
column4: Preventing chronic diseases requires early identification of risk factors and sustained support for lifestyle change, yet individuals often lack timely, personalised feedback about their health risks. The WARIFA project addresses this challenge by empowering citizens and patients with tools for early risk detection and prevention. Stakeholders include individuals, healthcare providers, and public health authorities across Europe. The objective is to support healthier lifestyles and early intervention through personalised risk notification and guidance. The solution combines user-generated data with additional health-related information to assess individual risk profiles. A mobile application delivers tailored risk alerts and personalised recommendations for lifestyle changes and health education, aligned with each individual’s risk level. Users at increased risk are encouraged to engage with healthcare services for further assessment. The project motivates preventive behaviour, supports early detection of chronic conditions, and reduces long-term disease burden. WARIFA demonstrates how digital prevention tools can strengthen public health, improve individual awareness, and support more sustainable healthcare systems.

column2: Phenotyping for Genetic Analysis Requisitioning
column4: Accurate requisitioning of genetic tests for hereditary conditions depends on clear and structured descriptions of patient phenotypes, yet clinicians often document symptoms in free text, making the process time-consuming and error-prone. This use case addresses the need to simplify and improve genetic test requests while increasing diagnostic precision. Stakeholders include clinicians, genetic laboratories, and patients undergoing genetic evaluation. The objective is to support faster, more accurate selection of genetic analyses. The solution interprets clinical text and converts it into structured phenotype descriptions aligned with the Human Phenotype Ontology. By assisting clinicians in selecting appropriate phenotype terms during requisition, the system improves form completion and data quality. The approach supports consistent documentation and reduces administrative burden. Developed as a proposal for a national cloud-based service, the solution enhances diagnostic workflows, increases the likelihood of selecting the correct genetic test, and accelerates time to diagnosis. It contributes to improved patient outcomes and more efficient use of genetic testing resources.

column2: Automatic Classification of Skin Lesions in Digital Pathology
column4: The incidence of malignant melanoma has risen sharply in recent decades, placing growing pressure on pathology departments to examine increasing numbers of skin biopsies with limited specialist resources. Traditional diagnosis relies on expert pathologists manually reviewing whole-slide histological images to identify subtle morphological features, a process that is time-consuming and subject to inter-observer variability. The central challenge is efficiently identifying rare malignant melanomas among a large volume of benign or less aggressive lesions. This use case, developed within the Western Norway Health Region’s centre for applied digitisation, aims to support pathologists in delivering faster, more consistent, and more accurate diagnoses. The solution introduces a computer-aided diagnostic system for whole-slide images that automatically analyses tissue morphology and cytology to assist in lesion classification. Digital slides are processed using image analysis techniques to highlight patterns associated with malignancy and prioritise cases for expert review. The system is designed to integrate into existing pathology workflows. The expected impact includes reduced examination time per case, lower diagnostic variability, and improved detection accuracy for malignant melanoma. This supports better patient outcomes, improved use of specialist expertise, and more sustainable pathology services under increasing diagnostic demand.

column2: CLARIFY – Cloud Artificial Intelligence for Pathology
column4: Digital pathology offers major opportunities to improve diagnostic quality and accessibility, yet widespread adoption is limited by the complexity of image interpretation and the need for scalable infrastructure. The CLARIFY programme addresses these challenges by bridging engineering and medicine to advance automated digital diagnostics in pathology. The initiative brings together academic, clinical, and industrial partners to strengthen expertise at the intersection of clinical pathology, cloud computing, and advanced analytics. Its objective is to create a robust, automated digital diagnostic environment that supports pathologists in interpreting whole-slide images regardless of location. The solution focuses on developing cloud-based platforms and algorithms that enable large-scale storage, processing, and analysis of high-resolution pathology images. Automated analysis supports tasks such as tissue characterisation and diagnostic decision support, while cloud-oriented architectures ensure accessibility, scalability, and collaboration across institutions. Alongside technical development, the programme trains early-stage researchers to ensure long-term capacity building. The impact includes improved diagnostic efficiency, wider access to specialist expertise, and accelerated adoption of digital pathology. CLARIFY contributes to more consistent, scalable, and accessible pathology services, while strengthening Europe’s research and skills base in digital health technologies.

column2: Automated Measurement of Proliferation in Breast Cancer
column4: Accurate assessment of tumour proliferation is critical for prognosis and treatment decisions in breast cancer, yet current methods are either subjective or labour-intensive. Markers such as Ki-67 and mitotic counts are widely used but lack standardised, reproducible assessment across laboratories, leading to potential over- or undertreatment. This project addresses the need for objective, validated, and efficient measurement of proliferation indicators in breast cancer pathology. Stakeholders include pathologists, oncologists, and patients whose treatment decisions depend on reliable prognostic information. The objective is to improve consistency and accuracy in assessing proliferation indices such as the Mitotic Activity Index, Ki-67, and PHH3. The solution develops and validates a digital image analysis system that automatically identifies and quantifies relevant cellular features in histological slides. By training models on annotated data, the system supports standardised calculations aligned with clinical guidelines. Integration into pathology workflows reduces manual counting and variability. The project improves diagnostic precision, reduces workload for pathologists, and supports more informed therapeutic decisions. It contributes to higher-quality breast cancer care and more consistent application of prognostic biomarkers in clinical practice.

column2: Automated Quantitative Analysis of Endometrioid Intraepithelial Neoplasia (EIN)
column4: Endometrioid intraepithelial neoplasia is a premalignant condition with a significant risk of progression to endometrial cancer, making accurate diagnosis and risk stratification increasingly important. Although quantitative morphometric methods such as the D-score provide reproducible risk assessment, their adoption has been limited due to cost and time constraints, leading many laboratories to rely on less precise diagnostic criteria. This project addresses the need for faster and more standardised evaluation of EIN. Stakeholders include pathologists and clinicians managing patients at risk of endometrial cancer. The objective is to enable widespread use of quantitative, evidence-based EIN scoring. The solution develops an automated application that performs morphometric analysis on digital pathology images to calculate EIN scores rapidly and reproducibly. The system standardises assessment across laboratories and aligns diagnostics with contemporary classification systems. The impact includes reduced analysis time, improved diagnostic consistency, and better identification of high-risk patients. By supporting accurate and efficient risk assessment, the project enhances clinical decision-making, facilitates earlier intervention, and contributes to improved prevention of endometrial cancer.

column2: Pathology services in the Western Norway Health Region – a center for applied digitization: Optimization of the pathology workflow
column4: Pathology departments face growing workloads due to increasing sample volumes and analytical complexity, while staffing levels remain relatively stable. Inefficient workflows risk longer turnaround times and increased strain on personnel. This use case addresses the need for systematic optimisation of pathology operations across the Western Norway Health Region. Stakeholders include pathology managers, laboratory staff, and healthcare providers dependent on timely diagnostic results. The objective is to improve efficiency and resilience by optimising resource allocation and workflow planning. The solution leverages detailed operational data from the Unilab-700 system, which captures granular information about each step in the pathology process. An optimisation model is developed to generate both long-term capacity plans and short-term, adaptive schedules for daily operations. The system supports scenario analysis and informed decision-making across departments. The impact includes improved turnaround times, better utilisation of staff and equipment, and increased transparency in workload management. This initiative supports sustainable pathology services by enabling data-driven operational planning and continuous improvement.

column2: Ki-67 in neuroendocrine tumours of the gastrointestinal tract
column4: Ki-67 is a critical biomarker for grading and treatment planning in neuroendocrine tumours of the gastrointestinal tract, yet accurate assessment requires counting a large number of cells, making manual evaluation impractical in routine settings. As a result, visual estimates or reduced counts are often used, compromising precision and reproducibility. This project addresses the need for accurate, standardised Ki-67 measurement in gastrointestinal neuroendocrine tumours. Stakeholders include pathologists, oncologists, and patients whose treatment decisions depend on reliable tumour grading. The objective is to improve accuracy and consistency in Ki-67 index calculation. The solution develops a digital image analysis system that automatically identifies tumour cells and calculates the Ki-67 index based on large cell populations in histological slides. The system is trained and validated on representative datasets and designed for integration into clinical workflows. The impact includes improved grading accuracy, reduced observer variability, and lower manual workload. This supports better treatment stratification, more reliable prognostic assessment, and improved quality of care for patients with neuroendocrine tumours.

column2: MIM - Machine learning in the Mammography programme
column4: Breast cancer screening programmes generate very large volumes of mammograms, the majority of which show no signs of disease, yet all images must be carefully reviewed by specialists. This creates significant workload pressure and limits the capacity of screening services. The MIM project addresses the need to improve efficiency and effectiveness in mammography screening while maintaining high diagnostic quality. Stakeholders include radiologists, screening programme operators, and women participating in national screening initiatives. The objective is to support early detection of breast cancer by enabling more efficient triage of screening images and allowing clinicians to focus attention on higher-risk cases. The solution develops deep learning models trained on large datasets combining mammograms, screening metadata, and confirmed breast cancer diagnoses. By learning complex visual patterns associated with disease and normal findings, the system can identify mammograms that are highly likely to be free of cancer. These predictions can be used to prioritise or streamline review processes within screening workflows. The expected impact includes reduced reading workload, improved use of specialist expertise, and increased screening capacity. Ultimately, the project supports more sustainable screening services while maintaining early detection and patient safety.

column2: Naturinngrep i inngrepsfri natur
column4:
The Norwegian Environment Agency (MDIR) is responsible for maintaining a map of intervention-free nature in Norway, which is defined as areas where there is at least 1 km to the nearest nature intervention. NR developed methods for the detection of construction roads and forest roads from Sentinel-2 satellite images with 10 m spatial resolution.

The method is based on the deep neural network U-Net. By training U-Net on wind power development areas, the method found all the wind power development areas in Åfjord municipality, Trøndelag. Several of these development areas resulted in a reduction of intervention-free nature. By training U-Net on known roads, the method found an illegally built road in Melhus municipality, Trøndelag.

column2: Chatbot Frida
column4: Chatbot that uses AI technology to answer questions from citizens. The technology is not developed by NAV, it is delivered by boost.ai

column2: RE-SIKTET- Conversation and documentation quality in medical telephone assessment assisted by artificial intelligence
column4: Emergency medical call centres play a critical role in patient safety, yet telephone assessments are complex, time-pressured, and highly dependent on operator experience. Important questions may be missed, documentation quality can vary, and subtle indicators of serious conditions may be overlooked. The RE-SIKTET project addresses these challenges by supporting operators during live medical calls. Stakeholders include emergency call centre staff, healthcare services, and patients relying on accurate triage. The objective is to improve conversation quality, documentation, and clinical assessment without disrupting the natural flow of dialogue. The solution provides an interactive web interface that dynamically suggests relevant questions based on the conversation context, predicted severity, and known medical patterns. Selected questions and responses are automatically documented, creating structured and standardised records. Over time, the system generates large, high-quality datasets describing medical assessment interviews. The project evaluates the effect of this support on call quality and documentation. The impact includes more consistent assessments, reduced risk of misinterpretation, improved documentation, and better decision support for operators. It also creates a strong foundation for further research into communication, triage, and patient safety.
column42: Automated reasoning and Automated Decision Making (ADM)

column2: CONWIND: Forskning på smarte driftskontrollteknologier for havvindparker
column4: Offshore wind energy has significant potential but faces operational challenges that limit efficiency and profitability. Larger turbines reduce costs per unit of energy, yet they also create complex wake effects and turbulent wind fields that increase mechanical stress and reduce energy output. The CONWIND project addresses the need for smarter operational control of offshore wind farms. Stakeholders include energy producers, turbine operators, and grid operators seeking to maximise output and reduce maintenance costs. The objective is to improve wind farm performance through more accurate prediction and control of wind conditions under uncertainty. The solution develops advanced control algorithms that combine wind field modelling, sensor data, and predictive methods to optimise turbine operation across the farm. These control strategies account for wake interactions and uncertainty in environmental conditions. Even modest improvements in control performance can yield significant gains at scale. The project demonstrates that a control system increasing profitability by just over 2% would have major economic impact. CONWIND contributes to lower operating costs, higher energy production, and increased competitiveness of offshore wind, supporting the transition to renewable energy.

column2: Research grant for case management AI
column4: The Research Council of Norway (RCN) processes large volumes of grant applications, requiring efficient classification and routing to appropriate expert panels. Manual handling is time-consuming and risks inconsistencies, particularly as interdisciplinary research grows. To address this, RCN explored digital support tools to optimise case management while keeping human oversight central. Stakeholders include case officers, expert evaluators, applicants and research institutions. The objective was to reduce administrative workload and improve consistency in thematic classification. The solution applies thematic modelling to analyse submitted proposals and predict characteristics such as academic disciplines, research topics and sector relevance. These predicted tags are presented to case officers, who review and decide on final classifications. The system integrates with existing grant management workflows and supports, rather than replaces, human judgement. Early results indicate improved efficiency, faster processing times and more consistent tagging across applications. The pilot demonstrates how decision support tools can enhance public sector workflows while preserving accountability and professional control.
column42: Automated reasoning and Automated Decision Making (ADM)

column2: AdaptAl - Adaptive control of aluminium production
column4: Aluminium production involves complex, tightly coupled processes that are sensitive to raw material quality, chemical variations, and operational disturbances. Without adaptive control, these variations can exceed tolerance limits, leading to increased costs, waste, and quality issues. The AdaptAl project addresses this challenge by extending digital process optimisation into real-time adaptive control. Stakeholders include aluminium producers, industrial operators, and downstream manufacturing sectors such as automotive and furniture. The objective is to stabilise and optimise production across the aluminium value chain under varying conditions. The solution builds on Hydro’s PRO3 digital twin, which simulates and optimises aluminium production processes. AdaptAl integrates sensor data, virtual models, and optimisation software to enable adaptive control that continuously adjusts process parameters in response to observed deviations. The system is demonstrated in extrusion and forming processes across multiple industrial markets. The impact includes improved process stability, reduced production costs, higher product quality, and increased flexibility. AdaptAl strengthens industrial competitiveness and demonstrates how digital twins and adaptive control can deliver measurable value in energy-intensive manufacturing.

column2: Norsk Hydro's AI tool to detect hackers
column4: Large industrial operators face significant risks from cyberattacks targeting operational technology and production infrastructure, as demonstrated by the 2019 ransomware attack against Norsk Hydro. As a partly state-owned aluminium producer operating complex, globally distributed industrial systems, Hydro needed a way to strengthen cyber resilience beyond traditional perimeter security. The objective of this use case is to continuously identify vulnerabilities and early warning signs of malicious activity across industrial control systems, reducing the likelihood of large-scale operational disruption. The solution monitors industrial equipment and network behaviour to detect deviations from normal operating patterns over time. It applies machine learning–based anomaly detection techniques trained on historical operational data to identify subtle changes that may indicate cyber intrusion or system misuse. The tool integrates with Hydro’s existing industrial monitoring and security infrastructure, enabling continuous assessment without interrupting production processes. The pilot improved Hydro’s ability to detect security weaknesses earlier and prioritise mitigation actions, strengthening situational awareness across plants. Qualitatively, it reduced reliance on reactive incident response and improved confidence in industrial cybersecurity governance. The project demonstrates the business value of predictive security monitoring in critical infrastructure, particularly for large industrial actors with high availability requirements.

column2: Unsupervised learning to detect customs declarations
column4: Foreign operators are reported in the customs declaration as a free text field. This creates challenges for further analysis by both customs officers and data analysts. The data analytics department has developed an unsupervised algorithm to group a list of names. It is based on cluster analysis in a network and splitting cluster.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Automatisert saksbehandling med arkivmessige hensyn for kunstig intelligens
column4: The Norwegian Maritime Authority has initiated a major reorganisation of how the organisation will conduct case processing and associated information management in the future. When the Norwegian Maritime Directorate issues certificates in the future, it will be in an automated solution. Together with the National Archives and the Directorate of Digitisation, they will now look at how they can ensure that the documentation is taken care of and can be accessed. The new solutions will also be responsible for data collection. It is important that correct documentation, both incoming and outgoing, process and rules are ensured in an appropriate manner.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair
column42: Automated reasoning and Automated Decision Making (ADM)

column2: NVE Copernicus 2
column4: Norsk Regnesentral develops algorithms that retrieve information about water and snow for emergency warnings related to avalanches and floods.
column8: Norwegian Water Resources and Energy Directorate

column2: AI-Mind
column4: Dementia places a growing burden on healthcare systems, and early identification of individuals at risk remains a critical unmet need. The AI-Mind project addresses this challenge by focusing on patients with mild cognitive impairment, a group in which approximately half may progress to dementia. Stakeholders include clinical researchers, neurologists, patients, healthcare providers, and academic institutions coordinated by Oslo Metropolitan University. The objective is to enable earlier and more reliable risk stratification, supporting timely intervention and improved care planning. The project centres on the analysis of electrophysiological brain signals collected through EEG examinations, which can reveal disturbances in brain networks associated with early synaptic dysfunction. The AI-Mind Connector processes EEG data to detect network-level patterns indicative of elevated dementia risk, integrating clinical, demographic, and neurophysiological information into a unified assessment framework. The system is designed to support clinicians rather than replace diagnostic judgement. Funded with approximately EUR 14 million and subject to European ethical review, the project remains in the research and validation phase. Expected impacts include improved diagnostic accuracy, better targeting of preventive measures, and reduced long-term healthcare costs through earlier intervention.

column2: Optical Character Recognition (OCR)
column4: The National Archives of Norway has undertaken multiple projects focused on automated detection of metadata, yielding highly promising outcomes. To improve optimal character recognition (OCR) results, thorough preprocessing of files was performed. The team successfully captured a wide range of metadata types, including letter headings, sender and receiver names, and corresponding dates. Notably, in one project, they employed metadata categorization to extract concepts such as locations, individuals, organizations, and derived concepts from the documents. Additionally, leveraging trained models, the National Archives of Norway identified case files containing sensitive information by using training data that encompassed typical sensitive content.

column2: AI to detect incorrect health reimbursement payments
column4: Public health reimbursement systems must balance timely payments with robust controls to prevent incorrect or fraudulent claims. In Norway, Helfo initiated a collaborative innovation project to improve the accuracy of health reimbursement payments and ensure responsible use of public funds. Stakeholders include Helfo, healthcare providers, researchers from Skatteforsk at the Norwegian University of Life Sciences, the Norwegian Institute of Public Health, and the University of Agder. The objective is to detect erroneous claims earlier, reduce manual audits, and simplify compliance for legitimate providers. The project develops data-driven control systems that analyse large volumes of reimbursement claims, historical payment data, and provider behaviour patterns. These systems generate risk indicators that support pre-payment checks and post-payment reviews, while remaining integrated with existing reimbursement workflows. Emphasis is placed on uncovering blind spots in current controls and improving reporting efficiency. The initiative is still under development, with pilots used to test models and operational integration. Anticipated benefits include reduced incorrect payments, more efficient use of inspection resources, and increased trust among healthcare providers by focusing controls where risks are highest.

column2: SSmart On-Line health assessment of cable terminations
column4: Failures in high-voltage cable terminations pose a significant risk to electricity supply security, particularly in transmission networks. The SmartACT project addresses this issue by developing a continuous, non-intrusive monitoring system for 420 kV oil-filled cable terminations, which are among the most failure-prone components. Stakeholders include the Norwegian transmission system operator Statnett, industrial partners, and research institutions such as SINTEF and NTNU. The objective is to enable early detection of degradation and prevent costly supply interruptions. The project develops long-life sensors installed inside cable terminations to continuously measure temperature, humidity, pressure, and partial discharges. Sensor data is analysed through a digital assessment framework that combines physical modelling with data-driven evaluation methods. The monitoring system is designed to integrate with grid operation and maintenance systems, enabling condition-based maintenance strategies. SmartACT remains in the research and testing phase under the ENERGIX programme. Expected impacts include improved grid reliability, reduced unplanned outages, lower maintenance costs, and enhanced decision-making for critical energy infrastructure.

column2: NewbornTime
column4: Accurate documentation of events during newborn resuscitation and early care is essential for clinical quality, training, and patient safety, yet manual recording is often incomplete or imprecise. The NewbornTime project aims to generate an objective and detailed timeline of events surrounding newborn care immediately after birth. Stakeholders include clinicians, neonatal researchers, hospitals, and healthcare quality organisations. The objective is to improve clinical documentation, support performance analysis, and enhance learning in neonatal care. The project combines infrared thermal video and optical video recordings captured in delivery rooms and resuscitation areas. Using advanced modelling techniques, the system estimates the precise time of birth from thermal signals and recognises key clinical activities from video streams. These events are automatically assembled into a structured timeline that can be reviewed by clinicians and researchers. The system is designed to integrate with hospital workflows while respecting strict data protection and ethical requirements. NewbornTime remains in the research phase, with validation studies ongoing. Potential impacts include more accurate clinical records, improved training and audit capabilities, and better insights into time-critical newborn interventions.

column2: Automated case management of family immigration with a professional background
column4: Norway’s Directorate of Immigration (UDI) faced growing backlogs in citizenship and family immigration applications, driven by rising volumes and fully manual case handling. Processing times regularly exceeded one year, placing strain on applicants, caseworkers and administrative resources. The objective of this use case was to streamline case management by automating routine assessments, while preserving legal certainty and human oversight. The system focuses on applications from individuals with professional backgrounds where documentation and eligibility criteria are relatively standardised. The solution uses rule-based automation combined with data-driven classification models to pre-process applications, extract relevant information and assess whether cases meet predefined conditions for simplified handling. Structured data from application forms and supporting documents is analysed and routed accordingly, while complex or ambiguous cases are flagged for manual review. The system integrates with UDI’s existing case management platforms and national registers. The impact has been a substantial reduction in average processing time for eligible cases, freeing case officers to focus on more complex applications. Administrative efficiency improved, backlogs decreased and applicants benefited from faster, more predictable outcomes, demonstrating how automation can enhance public service delivery at scale.

column2: Ada-bot helps process applications for family reunification
column4: The robot "Ada" at the Directorate of Immigration is programmed to make individual decisions based on laws and other legal sources. Ada collects documents for family reunification cases. This can make the whole process up to five times faster.

column2: KartAi - Automate and streamline case management in construction cases
column4: Accurate and up-to-date property and land-use data are essential for urban planning, construction permitting, and public administration. Manual processing of construction cases and maintenance of cadastral and mapping databases is time-consuming and prone to inconsistencies. KartAi addresses the need to improve data quality and streamline administrative workflows related to construction and property management. Stakeholders include municipalities, planning authorities, surveyors, and citizens interacting with land administration systems. The objective is to automate and support case management while improving the quality of the property register and the Central Common Map Database. The solution applies machine learning to analyse geospatial data, documents, and historical records to detect inconsistencies, support classification, and assist decision-making in construction cases. Automated analysis supports faster updates to cadastral records and improved alignment between maps and legal property information. The impact includes reduced processing time, higher data accuracy, and more transparent case handling. KartAi contributes to more efficient public administration, better land-use decisions, and improved trust in core spatial data infrastructures.

column2: AI in Social Services
column4: The Social Services of the City of Barcelona deal with an average of 50,000 visits every year. These relate to a variety of issues ranging from financial or addiction problems to gender-based violence and are handled by a team of over 700 professionals. People arriving at the centre are welcomed in private booths. The social worker records the conversation and, at the end, writes up the problem and the service to which it has been referred. Now, an algorithm will provide the staff with digital support in the decision-making process. The algorithm, which has been trained with a corpus of 300,000 interviews, will suggest the appropriate solution and resources for each case. The algorithm works as a supporting tool in the decision-making process, but the decision is still ultimately made by the professional handling the case.

column2: Helsinki chatbot
column4: Helsinki chatbot is a 24-hour customer service channel of the City of Helsinki information website, aimed at improving the accessibility of customer service and the customer experience. The service provides relevant information to each customer’s specific questions faster than by searching for the information on the website. In the initial phase, the chatbot will implemented within the Sports and Recreation related pages. For instance, the chatbot will answer questions related to sports facilities and services. The service will work based on the trained rule-based discussion paths and the question-answer pairs determined by specialists from Helsinki City Information and thematic experts.

column2: E-vignette: toll road payment controlled by AI
column4: The e-vignette is the new online way of paying the motorway toll for travelling through slovenian highways, using just the personal e-mail and the registration number of the vehicle. The correct use of the e-vignette is controlled by the Motorway Company in the Republic of Slovenia (DARS), the Financial Administration of the Republic of Slovenia and the Police. The toll road operator controls whether travellers have bought the e-vignette via cameras on the motorway network, at control points, and by using toll control vehicles. After the system has checked the vehicle for which the e-vignette is purchased, this information is erased from the system immediately upon establishing tolling compliance, and no traces are left in the system regarding the use of the toll road. If non-compliance is detected, the system stores the information until the completion of the minor offence proceedings.
column8: Financial Administration of the Republic of Slovenia

column2: Algorithmic Transparency Standard
column4: Public administrations increasingly rely on algorithmic systems, creating a growing need for transparency, accountability and public understanding. Cities lacked a shared, practical framework for explaining how such systems are used, for what purpose, and with what safeguards. The Algorithmic Transparency Standard was developed to address this gap by providing a common structure for documenting algorithmic use across local governments. The initiative involves municipalities, civic technology organisations and researchers, with the objective of enabling meaningful transparency for citizens while supporting internal governance. The standard defines a shared set of information categories, such as system purpose, data sources, decision impacts, oversight mechanisms and risks. These are published in open, machine-readable formats (CSV and Excel), allowing comparison across systems and jurisdictions. The standard can be integrated into existing registers or disclosure portals without requiring new technical infrastructure. Its impact lies in improved comparability, easier public scrutiny and stronger internal documentation practices. Cities using the standard benefit from clearer governance processes and reduced duplication of effort, while citizens gain accessible insight into algorithmic decision-making. The initiative supports trust, accountability and responsible digital transformation in public administration.
column8: City of Amsterdam, Barcelona, Brussels, Eindhoven, Mannheim, Rotterdam and Sofia
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: BEE Pilot - Building Energy Efficiency
column4: Buildings play a crucial role in the energy transition, both as major energy consumers and as flexible assets that can support renewable integration. The BEE pilot addresses the challenge of reducing emissions while maintaining comfort for building occupants. Stakeholders include building owners, facility managers, energy providers, and city authorities. The objective is to use buildings as flexible energy resources that shift consumption towards periods of high renewable availability. The solution integrates with existing building management systems to collect data on energy use and indoor conditions, combined with external data such as weather forecasts and grid information. Daily forecasts of grid mix, building usage, and flexibility potential are used to generate optimised control schedules for heating, cooling, ventilation, water heating, and electric vehicle charging. These schedules proactively minimise energy use and maximise renewable consumption while preserving comfort. The pilot demonstrates reduced emissions, improved energy efficiency, and better alignment between buildings and the energy system. BEE shows how intelligent building control can support decarbonisation at city scale while delivering operational and comfort benefits.

column2: Public Eye: AI crowd monitoring system in Amsterdam
column4: Public Eye maps the crowds in a few places in Amsterdam (e.g., Arena Boulevard, Marineterrein and Dam Square). There are cameras that are linked to a server of the municipality. An algorithm on the server analyses through the images how many people are present in the location or how many biking parks are filled. Then the information, but not the images, are shown on the publicly available platform for citizens' access. The information about the number of people is also forwarded to municipal employees, who can use the count to better regulate traffic. The ambition is to realize this for all locations.

column2: AI for poverty detection
column4: The City of Amsterdam receives a report from health insurers, housing associations, energy suppliers and Waternet, amongst others, if they have a customer who has payment arrears with them. The system forwards the report to a social worker from the municipality, who then contacts the citizen within 14 days and makes an initial analysis within 28 days to offer information and make detailed agreements. The social worker reports to the central reporting point which agreements have been made, so that a creditor knows what the next steps are for the payment arrears. The linking of the creditors' report with the data of the Poverty Reduction Department is automated, but a human action is required to start the link. From the moment the report has been forwarded to the emergency services in the correct district, the process is no longer automated. The social service provider visits the reported citizen if the citizen does not make contact himself.

column2: Autonomous Boats Deployment
column4: Amsterdam’s dense canal network presents both opportunities and challenges for urban mobility, logistics and environmental monitoring. Traditional boats require human operators, limiting scalability and increasing operational costs. The Roboat project was launched by MIT and the AMS Institute, together with the City of Amsterdam and Waternet, to explore autonomous vessels as a new urban infrastructure layer. The objective is to deploy self-driving boats capable of performing multiple functions, including passenger transport, waste collection and water quality monitoring. Roboats are equipped with sensor suites such as cameras, lidar and GPS, enabling perception, navigation and collision avoidance in complex canal environments. Onboard systems process real-time data to plan routes and coordinate with other vessels, while shore-based platforms monitor fleet operations. The pilots demonstrated safe autonomous navigation, modular vessel design and flexible deployment across use cases. Impacts include reduced labour requirements, improved efficiency in waste collection and new data streams for environmental management. The project highlights the potential of autonomous waterborne transport to complement urban mobility systems, reduce congestion and support sustainable city operations.

column2: Using AI to measure accessibility
column4: Ensuring equal access to public space for people with disabilities requires detailed, up-to-date information on urban accessibility. Historically, such data was fragmented, outdated or manually collected. The City of Amsterdam launched the “Amsterdam for All” initiative to systematically measure accessibility barriers across the city and support evidence-based interventions. Stakeholders include city planners, accessibility experts and residents with mobility impairments. The objective is to identify obstacles such as blocked pavements, missing curb ramps or inaccessible crossings. The project analyses street-level imagery and spatial data to detect urban features relevant to pedestrian accessibility. Predictive models identify the presence and condition of sidewalks, crossings and obstacles, producing city-wide accessibility maps. These outputs integrate into municipal planning tools and dashboards used by city officials. The impact includes improved visibility of accessibility issues, better prioritisation of infrastructure investments and reduced reliance on manual surveys. Qualitatively, the project strengthens inclusive urban design and supports Amsterdam’s commitment to universal accessibility through data-driven decision-making.

column2: Automatically Assessing and Improving the Readability of Municipal Communication
column4: According to recent research, 16% of the people between 16 and 65 in Amsterdam have low literacy skills. This hinders societal participation in tasks such as voting, paying taxes, reissuing documents, or applying for social benefits. Thus, as part of "Amsterdam for All" project, the City of Amsterdam set on a mission to research the use of AI for measuring and improving the readability of municipal communication, using an innovative methodology for contextualized lexical simplification, which means substituting complex words within the context of a sentence with more accessible ones.

column2: AI for green roof management
column4: Although the City of Amsterdam has mapped green roof coverage across the city before, there is lack of comprehensive data. For example, in the previous study, if less than 30% of a building roof was covered in green, the entire roof area would be categorised as a green roof. This meant that up to two-thirds of the data collected was unreliable and, likely, inaccurate. To resolve this, and to achieve an accurate estimation of green roof area coverage across the City of Amsterdam, Green City Watch built a proof-of-concept using Very High Resolution (VHR) satellite imagery. VHR imagery enables a pixel resolution of ±30 cm, so we can quantify the exact roof composition, pixel by pixel.

column2: 3D Point Cloud
column4: The City of Amsterdam uses mobile LiDAR-scanners - a type of technology that allows to gather a 3D representation of the environment through the visual representation of "point clouds". This technology allows to detect the exact location of objects and to capture geometric properties such as their width and height. The goal of this project is to automatically locate and classify various assets such as trees, street lights, traffic signs, and other street furniture in street level point clouds; this offers valuable information about the streets of Amsterdam.

column2: The Hague Digital Twin
column4: The Municipality of The Hague is developing a Living Lab on the coast of Scheveningen to learn how digitisation and technological solutions can contribute to solutions for social, economic and societal challenges facing the city. One of these technological solutions is the online Digital Twin dashboard where (live) data is visualised and can be analysed. As a first step, pedestrian and bicycle data is examined to, among other things, support the management of peak traffic volumes. The solution includes real-time location of buses, trams and trains, information about the cycle network, cycle paths and bicycle parking facilities and accessibility analyses of hotspots.

column2: Digital Twin Technology to Improve Operations
column4: The Port of Rotterdam piloted  the development of an Internet of Things cloud-based platform in partnership with IBM, Esri Cisco and Axians to create the port's digital twin. This platform would allow users to access a variety of hydrological and metereological data - for examples, water levels, salinity, wind speeds, visibility and currents - that has been collected with the aid of sensors. The platform is intended to help the port reduce waiting times and optimise mooring, loading and departure windows. or example, the platform could be used to predict the best time for mooring and departure, with the guarantee of maximum capacity utilisation, based on the recorded water level, currents and wind conditions.

column2: Chatbot for rental apartment search
column4: The rental apartment search chatbot is a 24-hour customer service channel of the City of Helsinki housing services aimed at improving the accessibility of customer service. The service provides relevant information to each customer’s specific questions realted to finding housing in the city, faster than by searching for the information on the website. The chatbot responds based on the trained rule-based discussion paths and the question-answer pairs determined by rental apartment search experts. The service also increases efficiency by enabling the automation of frequently asked questions.

column2: Proof of Business
column4: The Swedish Companies Registration Office has tested proof a concept on a digital wallet that enables entrepreneurs, customers, clients and partners to quickly and securely share or exchange verified evidence and certificates with each other. The proof of concept presented how blockchain technology can be used to collect and manage verified and quality-assured company information in a simple way using Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT).
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Participa.gov – Digital Public Participation Platform
column4:
Instruments for public participation make it possible to encourage collaborative discussion between decision-makers and stakeholders, creating the ideal environment for obtaining more thoughtful and equitable decisions, avoiding institutional suspicion, increasing the likelihood of consensus, and consequently increasing the success of the policies themselves. During the 2019 Simplex program, a national flagship initiative based on citizen engagement and co-creation for the simplification and modernization of the public sector, the idea came up and AMA delivered.

Participa.GOV is exactly that – a one-stop-shop platform where citizens can present their proposals and decide through their votes on all relevant initiatives for their lives – From the national budget to a GovTech contest or their children’s school initiatives, everything is possible at Participa.gov. Simply put, public entities launch a challenge, based on a set of rules predefined at the back office, and citizens present their proposals and then decide through their voting. A school might want its students to vote on the launch a social responsibility project, or the central government might launch a national participatory budgeting, or we can even have the NHS wanting to launch a restricted and exclusive participatory, process, available only to its workers – all of it is possible and works under the same rules. And better yet? The look and feel of the front office will always be the same.

When developing participa.gov we took into account two major factors:

1. The need to be customizable, because each entity has its own idiosyncrasies and target audiences and because each initiative is different.
2. The need to be safe, secure, and transparent because we know that the biggest challenge of any participatory system is related to trust factors.

The Participa.gov platform was therefore built using highly secure eID mechanisms and blockchain technology, ensuring security, auditing, and transparency in the process from start to finish. Because data is protected by cryptography and thus immutable, blockchain and distributed ledger technologies make it possible to overcome corruption and ensure there is no intervention from malicious outside players. Secure digital identity mechanisms add an extra layer of security and, in the case of the Portuguese eID ecosystem, it allows the participants' scope to be defined based on the attributes available in the Portuguese digital identity: age, address, professional attributes, and so on.

The Participa.gov portal was created with the belief that digital technologies, when combined with public participation tools, can play an important role in connecting citizens to democracy. And, in this new era of digital democracy, we want Participa.gov to become the single national platform for all public participatory processes, involving more citizens in public decision-making every day.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Reducing Friction in Trade (RFIT) in the UK
column4: The Reducing Friction in Trade (RFIT) project was initiated as a proof of concept to establish how blockchain technology can be used to seamlessly integrate supply chain data with HM Revenue & Customs and the Food Standards Agency’s systems. The project intends to do so by guaranteeing the timeliness and provenance of critical data and avoiding the need for discrete declarations. In this case, Chainvine Limited - a private sector provider - had shown the "intelligent Wine" application,which helps users (in this case, importers and exporters) trace health, wealth, and happiness of a good digitally. In doing so, the application reduces the cost of trust in the supply chain ecosystem. The proof of concept collected upstream data from wine producers that is relevant to Port Health Authorities and Customs and Border agencies, distributing it to authorised parties in a secure manner within a Blockchain.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain

column2: Harnessing the potential of blockchain technology for due diligence and sustainability in cotton value chains
column4: Global cotton value chains are complex and fragmented, making it difficult for governments and companies to ensure traceability, compliance, and sustainability from production to retail. Risks such as forced labour, environmental harm, and lack of transparency undermine due diligence efforts and consumer trust. The UNECE pilot project addresses these challenges by supporting traceability and accountability across the cotton supply chain, involving farmers, processors, manufacturers, brands, regulators, and consumers. The objective is to provide practical tools that enable reliable verification of origin, production practices, and sustainability claims, aligned with international policy and trade frameworks. The solution implements a distributed ledger–based traceability system that records key events and data points along the value chain, from field to shelf. Standardised data models developed under the UN/CEFACT framework ensure interoperability and consistency, while secure digital records enable verifiable information sharing among authorised actors. The pilot improves transparency, supports regulatory compliance, and strengthens due diligence processes. It enables more responsible sourcing, reduces information asymmetries, and enhances trust between stakeholders. Overall, the initiative contributes to more sustainable cotton value chains and provides a scalable reference model for traceability in the garment and footwear sector.
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain

column2: LBChain - Blockchain sandbox
column4: As the central bank and financial market supervisory authority, the Bank of Lithuania is primarily responsible for the regulation and supervision of market participants in accordance with applicable legal acts and requirements. Development of a fintech-conducive regulatory and supervisory ecosystem as well as fostering innovation in the financial system is one of the Bank of Lithuania’s strategic directions. Together with other state authorities, the Bank of Lithuania contributes to the development of new growth opportunities for fintech market participants by creating and maintaining a supportive environment, stimulating competitiveness and innovations in the field of finance. LBChain is a blockchain-based technological sandbox which combines regulatory and technological infrastructures and allows start-ups to test their business solutions in a controlled environment. The platform will enable start-ups to gain new knowledge, carry out blockchain-oriented research, test and adapt blockchain-based services and offer state of-the-art innovations to their customers.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Transforming International Remittance for Serbian Diaspora using Blockchain and Digital Identity
column4: There is clear demand to reduce remittance costs to encourage micro and macro economic development, in Serbia and globally. The solution is a product of a collaborative partnership between AID:Tech and United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) Serbia. It is one that encompasses the end-to-end remittance journey. The blockchain digital assets translate to the real-world entitlements, such as utilities and merchandize items, while they can be easily created and their movement from one person to another made traceable. At the same time, blockchain transactions allow participating individuals to maintain their privacy. The solution will be made available to Serbian Diaspora as senders, who wish to remit to friends and family in Serbia as recipients. The solution launched in Septermber 2018 in the city administration of Nis.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Blockchain of Frequencies
column4: With the fast growing development of new digital services and the explosion of connected objects, frequencies are becoming a crucial asset in the new economy. The spectrum being finite, frequencies are a scarce resource. The performance of their mode of management is therefore essential to guarantee both their availability and their quality. To avoid the tragedy of commons, Agence Nationale des Fréquences (ANFR )has developed, on a Blockchain, a solution for booking frequencies in the general authorization bands and in particular, in the Programme Making and Special Events (PMSE) bands. This solution aims at preventing signal interference between the numerous wireless microphones that are likely to use the same frequencies during major special events, such as sport, cultural or political events (Roland-Garros, Ryder Cup, …).

column2: DiBiHo - Digital Credentials for Higher Education Institutions
column4: Higher education institutions face growing demand for secure, interoperable, and internationally recognised academic credentials, as student mobility and digital service delivery increase. Paper-based certificates and fragmented digital solutions create administrative burdens, slow verification, and limit cross-border recognition. The DiBiHo project addresses this challenge by exploring a trusted digital infrastructure for academic credentials that can be used nationally and internationally. Stakeholders include German higher education institutions, students, employers, and credential evaluators. The objective is to define a vendor-independent, interoperable standard for issuing, storing, presenting, and verifying digital academic credentials. The solution investigates distributed infrastructure approaches that allow institutions to issue tamper-resistant credentials while enabling learners to control and share them securely. Common standards and governance principles are defined to ensure long-term interoperability, legal validity, and alignment with European and international frameworks. The project improves efficiency in credential management, reduces verification costs, and enhances trust in academic records. It supports student mobility, lifelong learning, and digital transformation in higher education, while providing a foundation for scalable, future-proof credential ecosystems independent of specific technology providers.

column2: Competency certificates in Small Private Online Courses (SPOCs)
column4: The objective of the project was to generate accreditations through the use of blockchain technology generated through the Learning Management System (LMS) platform- OPEN edX - and that were already issued in digital format by the University.

column2: Intelligent management of Aid to Citizens and companies
column4: During the COVID-19 pandemic, the Andalusian administration was required to rapidly distribute large-scale financial aid to citizens and businesses to protect employment and economic stability. The volume and complexity of aid schemes made manual processing inefficient, error-prone, and difficult to scale. This use case addresses the need to accelerate aid delivery while ensuring compliance, transparency, and accountability. Stakeholders include public administrators, beneficiaries, and oversight bodies. The objective is to automate complex administrative workflows to process aid applications quickly and reliably. The solution applies robotic process automation to handle repetitive, rule-based tasks such as data validation, eligibility checks, and application processing. Automated workflows integrate with existing administrative systems, while human oversight is retained for exception handling and decision-making. Since implementation, the system has processed more than 300,000 aid applications, significantly reducing processing times and administrative workload. The initiative improves service delivery, ensures timely support to citizens and companies, and strengthens institutional resilience during crises. It demonstrates how intelligent automation can enhance public sector efficiency, scalability, and responsiveness in high-pressure policy contexts.

column2: Monitoring structure health of bridges with InSAR
column4: Using radar satellite images, theoretical displacements down to the millimeter level can be determined by measuring phase differences between successive images. This is done based on an INSAR (Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar) analysis.The project aims at monitoring bridge deformations based on this technique. The main goal of the project is to investigate whether INSAR can be deployed in the daily management of bridges and to investigate which types of satellite images and analysis are best suited for this purpose. The second objective of this innovation is to detect problems faster based on the analysis carried out and to use topographic measurements more efficiently on bridges that may pose a risk. Ideally, anomalies in the deformations can be automatically detected for this.
column8: Flemish Ministry of Mobility and Public Works (MOW)

column2: Hello Lamp Post
column4: The Environment Agency has a responsibility to protect communities from flood and coastal risks. In the past, the agency has struggled to scale their public engagement and reach their diverse audiences, while also retaining a local relevance. Hello Lamp Post was brought on to provide an interactive, live 24/7 conversational channel to educate and inform the public on flood safety, in high-risk locations around the South West of England and Newcastle.

column2: ViEWS - The Political Violence Early-Warning System
column4: The Violence Early-Warning System (ViEWS) is a publicly available data-driven forecasting system that generates monthly predictions of conflict fatalities up to 36 months ahead – throughout Africa and the Middle East. The project launched in 2017 to help policy-makers and practitioners plan anticipatory action and humanitarian interventions with a transparent and evidence-based approach. It is based at Uppsala University and Peace Research Institute Oslo.

column2: Citibeats – Ethical AI for Social Understanding
column4: Citibeats is a AI platform that searches and analyzes large amounts of text provided by citizens - the application structures this data using natural language processing (NPL) and machine learning (ML) techniques. The solution identifies social trends, leading indicators, and insights for governments, and organizations thanks to the analysis of open data.

column2: Madrid Mobility 360 (multimodal planner for urban commuting)
column4: Madrid Mobility 360 is a mobile application that allows citizens to move around the city and calculate the most efficient route between all modes of transport in the capital  (public transportation, carsharing and bicycles). It includes a powerful route planner, which estimates current and future journey times, and provides the real-time capacity on specific buses. The app allows payment for various methods of transport and reports the CO2 emissions generated.
column8: Empresa Municipal de Transported del Ayuntamiento de Madrid

column2: Transforming Public Healthcare and Education Using AI-powered Mixed Reality Technology
column4: Public healthcare in Serbia is transformed through innovative use of mixed reality technology powered by Artificial Intelligence, which potentially increases efficiency and quality of healthcare, minimizes risks and efforts, and optimizes procedures. Through remote collaboration, doctors have the same insight into patient’s condition without the need of physical presence, enabling joint real time inputs and medical interventions with experts from anywhere, as well as remote education for medical students.
column8: Faculty of Medicine, University of Belgrade

column2: My First Salary - a project to address youth unemployment
column4: The programme My First Salary addresses youth unemployment by providing a salary subsidy grant for the first employment of high school or university graduates bellow 30. It is implemented through a state-of-the-art tech platform that automates the application process, approval and match-making.
