column4: The Tallaght-based facility is the first of its kind in Europe and builds on South Dublin's relationship with Amazon Web Services (AWS). Students and teachers visit AWS Think Big Space for free workshops on virtual reality and robotics. These sessions and monthly events allow participants to engage with technologies that will become more commonplace in the future. AWS Think Big Space also offers cloud computing courses and AWS certifications for adult learners.

column2: Cornwall Council brings Newquay Futures development project to life with a VR experience
column4: The Newquay Futures project proposes active travel infrastructure and new community spaces for the coastal town. Cornwall Council and Digital Urban incorporated these proposals into a virtual reality version of Newquay. This VR experience includes 360-degree images, city sounds, and virtual neighbors viewed from a cyclist's perspective. Cornwall residents provide feedback after their VR experiences at three community events.

column2: Eskilstuna Kommun develoeped virtual reality training tool for its Jobbcirkus youth employment program
column4: The city's Jobbcirkus team worked with Combitech AB and Reality Labs on the 360-degree VR program. Local students in grades 9 simulate post-secondary career and education decisions in a low-stress virtual environment. Each user learns about industries with talent shortages and experiences potential work environments. Eskilstuna plans an expansion of the technology into virtual site visits as well as broader use by residents facing career decisions.

column2: Virtual reality technology programme used to tackle youth violence in Greater Manchester
column4: Local officials and Round Midnight started with a virtual reality training pilot across 31 schools. The VR program placed 1,395 students into a simulated situation with multiple potential outcomes. Participants discussed the roots of violent behavior and smart decision-making over a 12-week curriculum. Virtual_Decisions was expanded to all schools with Year 5 through 8 students as Greater Manchester seeks reductions in youth violence.

column2: Municipality of Härnösand is building a training center that utilizes realistic virtual reality environments
column4: The Municipality of Härnösand will be the first in Norrland to build a training center based entirely on virtual reality. The municipality's Learning Center is partnering with Technichus Science Center to develop this VR studio learning environment. The VR studio will utilize virtual reality technology to create realistic training environments for social interactions and conflict management with sensors that detect a participant's movement, body language, tone, word choice, etc.

column2: Municipality of Horn created the “360° Horn” website for visitors to experience the city in virtual reality
column4: The city of Horn partnered with the Kunsthaus Horn event venue to create the “360° Horn” website for visitors to experience the city in virtual reality and promote tourism. The 360° Horn website consists of a large number of high-quality images with a 360° viewing angle which allows the user to experience the city virtually via computer, smartphone, or VR glasses.

column2: Turku Municipality produces Save Turku augmented reality game for resident engagement with local history
column4: City officials and area developer CTRL Reality Oy honored Turku's 800 years of history with an interactive game. The free mobile app uses augmented reality for a guided adventure through Turku. The objective of Save Turku is to recover the city's founding document with help from local actors. Individuals and teams are asked to carefully observe historical attractions in their search for clues starting from the Old Great Square.

column2: London Borough of Redbridge addresses root causes of domestic violence with VR training
column4: Addressing domestic violence requires behavioural change interventions that help perpetrators understand the impact of their actions, yet traditional programmes often struggle to foster empathy and reflection. In Redbridge, local authorities sought innovative approaches to strengthen existing intervention programmes. The objective of this pilot is to enhance offender awareness by enabling participants to experience domestic violence scenarios from a child’s perspective. The implementation introduces immersive virtual reality training capsules integrated into an eight-week behavioural programme. Participants are exposed to realistic scenarios designed to evoke emotional understanding and reflection, supported by structured facilitation. Academic researchers assess participant responses and attitudinal changes. Expected impacts include deeper emotional engagement, improved programme effectiveness and stronger evidence for behaviour change. Qualitative results suggest increased empathy, improved self-reflection and potential reductions in repeat offending, supporting future scaling of immersive interventions.

column2: Karlskrona generates digital twin to show the city's present and future in three dimensions
column4: Municipal planning increasingly requires integrated spatial analysis and effective citizen engagement to manage complex urban environments. In Karlskrona, fragmented datasets limited the ability to simulate future development scenarios. The objective of this project is to create a comprehensive digital representation of the city to support planning, infrastructure management and public engagement. The implementation combines aerial imagery, building plans, drone data, IoT inputs and departmental datasets into a three-dimensional digital twin. The model supports internal analysis, such as infrastructure planning, and immersive engagement through AR and VR events for residents. Expected impacts include more informed decision-making, improved coordination across departments and enhanced transparency. Qualitative outcomes include stronger public participation, better scenario testing and streamlined municipal processes.

column2: Skanderborg Municipality uses virtual reality to help people with anxiety
column4: People with anxiety often struggle to engage with traditional therapeutic approaches due to fear of real-world exposure. In Skanderborg, social and employment services explored new welfare technologies to support gradual treatment. The objective of this pilot is to provide a safe, controlled environment for exposure-based therapy. The implementation uses virtual reality to recreate social situations that users can experience progressively, guided by professionals. The approach allows customisation to individual needs and integration into existing care pathways. Expected impacts include improved treatment engagement, reduced anxiety symptoms and enhanced therapeutic outcomes. Qualitative benefits include increased confidence, better coping strategies and a scalable model for mental health support.

column2: Municipality of Skövde  provides funds to purchase driving simulators to improve training and reduce emissions
column4: The Municipality of Skövde granted funds to vocational teachers at Vehicle Transport and Gymnasium Skövde the building and construction program to help them purchase eight driving simulators. The new technology will be used to partially replace traditional driving instruction when teaching machine, vehicle, and truck drivers. The municipality funded the driving simulators purchase to increase access to practical exercises, expand student capacities, and reduce their overall environmental impact.

column2: City of Helsinki evaluates sensory room and virtual reality therapy at Kustaankartano Senior Centre
column4: Older adults with memory disorders often experience anxiety, stress, and reduced quality of life, placing pressure on care staff and increasing reliance on medication. The City of Helsinki sought innovative, non-pharmacological approaches to improve wellbeing in residential care settings. This pilot, developed at the Kustaankartano Senior Centre, addresses the need for calming, engaging therapeutic environments for residents with cognitive impairment. Stakeholders include residents, care staff, healthcare providers, and city authorities. The objective is to reduce stress, improve mood, and support care delivery through immersive and sensory-based interventions. The solution combines a sensory room featuring natural elements with interactive digital installations and virtual reality experiences. Residents can engage with calming nature simulations or creative activities that stimulate senses and memories. These interventions are integrated into daily care routines and tailored to individual needs. The pilot demonstrates reduced stress levels among residents, lower staff workload, and decreased use of antidepressant medication. It improves quality of life, supports staff efficiency, and provides evidence for scalable, technology-enabled therapies in elder care.

column2: Greater London Authority places city planning into virtual reality with Square Mile VR project
column4: Urban planning in London involves balancing growth, density and heritage across multiple boroughs, while ensuring transparency and public trust in decision-making. The Greater London Authority (GLA), borough planning departments, developers and citizens require clear ways to understand how new developments affect their surroundings. The objective of this use case is to improve planning decisions, pre-application discussions and public consultations by providing accurate, city-wide visual context for proposed developments. The implementation relies on a highly detailed three-dimensional digital model of London, covering the full 1,619 km² of the capital with accuracy down to 15 cm. The platform integrates geospatial data, building information and planning datasets, allowing users to visualise massing, height, density and sightlines in an interactive environment, including immersive virtual reality. It integrates with planning workflows used by boroughs and developers. The results include faster and more informed planning decisions, improved quality of submissions and clearer communication with citizens. Over 80% of London boroughs use the platform, reporting improved transparency, reduced planning friction and better understanding of impacts, delivering time and cost savings while increasing confidence in the city’s long-term development strategy.

column2: Trafford Council  mixes virtual reality with online engagement for Urmston Active Neighbourhood
column4: Trafford Council started with a pre-COVID listening session that generated more than 3,400 comments on walking and cycling plans for Urmston. Residents now use the council's virtual room to find data, suggested solutions, and maps in a format similar to the in-person session. Project leaders conduct webchats with up to 20 participants in each session for additional feedback. Engaged residents can help those without computers understand the planning process as project ambassadors.

column2: Fingal County Council, IE shows Balbriggan residents the future with a 3D Virtual Reality plan
column4: Urban regeneration projects often struggle with public engagement, as long-term plans can be difficult for residents to visualise. Fingal County Council addressed this challenge in Balbriggan by creating a 3D virtual reality experience showcasing six years of planned development. Stakeholders include local residents, planners and county operations staff. The objective is to improve transparency, community involvement and understanding of how regeneration initiatives will affect daily life. The VR environment presents planned infrastructure such as smart public furniture, maker spaces, micro-mobility hubs and harbour upgrades. Users explore the town’s future layout using VR headsets maintained at the Our Balbriggan Hub, with staff available to answer questions. The system integrates urban planning data and architectural models into an immersive experience. The impact has been improved public understanding, more informed feedback and stronger community buy-in. The project demonstrates how immersive technologies can enhance participatory planning, reduce consultation barriers and support smarter, citizen-centred urban development.

column2: Fingal County Council and Cork county council partnered with AVIVA Driving School to launch "Your Life - Your Choice" VR
column4: Fingal County Council and Cork county council partnered with AVIVA Driving School to launch the "Your Life - Your Choice" virtual reality experience at Castleknock Community College. This road safety programme uses virtual reality headsets to place viewers into a the passenger seat of a car during a simulated traffic accident. The simulation showcases the causes of the accident including distractions and speeding alongside the serious consequences. The event will target young drivers between the ages of 16 and 25.
column8: Fingal County Council and Cork county council

column2: Pothole ROBOT hits the roads in Hertfordshire
column4: Local highway authorities face persistent challenges from potholes, which damage vehicles, increase accident risk and generate high maintenance costs through labour-intensive repair processes. Hertfordshire County Council sought a solution to improve repair speed, consistency and worker safety while minimising disruption to road users. The objective of this use case is to modernise road maintenance by automating pothole detection and repair. The implementation deploys a robotic system equipped with sensors, cameras and automated repair mechanisms capable of identifying surface depressions, assessing their size and filling them without manual intervention. The system combines computer vision, depth measurement and automated material application, integrating with maintenance planning processes to log repairs and prioritise intervention areas. The reported impact shows that repairs can be completed up to 70% faster than traditional methods, with fewer road closures and reduced exposure of workers to live traffic. Qualitative outcomes include improved road condition reliability, lower operational costs over time, enhanced worker safety and increased public satisfaction with road maintenance services.

column2: Chatbot alicante
column4: Municipal administrations routinely handle large volumes of repetitive citizen enquiries related to local services, opening hours and administrative procedures, which places pressure on staff and slows response times. The City of Alicante aimed to improve accessibility to information while optimising internal resources. The objective of this use case is to provide fast, consistent answers to general enquiries through digital channels. The implementation introduces a conversational interface embedded in the municipal website, connected to curated knowledge bases containing validated service information. The system retrieves relevant answers and directs users to appropriate online resources or offices when needed, integrating with existing content management systems. The impact includes reduced call centre workload, improved response times and increased availability of information outside office hours. Qualitative benefits include enhanced citizen experience, greater service consistency and more efficient allocation of municipal staff to complex cases.
column8: Suma Gestión Tributaria. Diputación de Alicante

column2: Office in the metaverse through mobile phone
column4: Accessing municipal services can be difficult for citizens facing mobility constraints, limited opening hours or low familiarity with traditional digital portals. Alicante sought to explore new interaction models that improve accessibility and engagement. The objective of this use case is to provide an immersive, user-friendly digital environment for interacting with public services. The implementation delivers a virtual office accessible via mobile devices, where citizens create avatars and navigate a three-dimensional municipal space to consult services and obtain guidance. The environment integrates service information, interaction tools and navigation support within a simulated public office setting. The impact includes improved accessibility, increased citizen engagement and a more inclusive service channel. Qualitative results suggest higher user interest, improved understanding of available services and a novel complement to traditional e-government platforms.
column8: Suma Gestión Tributaria. Diputación de Alicante

column2: Innovation in Local Government: digital transformation and GeoAI for Data Management.
column4: Local governments often face revenue losses due to incomplete or inconsistent property data spread across multiple administrative databases. Alicante sought to improve tax fairness and revenue collection. The objective of this use case is to identify buildings that are not correctly registered for taxation. The implementation integrates geospatial analysis with data-sharing across cadastral, planning and utility databases. By cross-referencing spatial footprints with administrative records, discrepancies are detected and flagged for review. The impact includes increased tax revenue, improved data accuracy and more efficient enforcement processes. Qualitative benefits include fairer taxation, improved inter-agency cooperation and stronger financial sustainability for local government.
column8: Suma Gestión Tributaria. Diputación de Alicante
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: AI within the Flanders Investment and Trade organisation
column4: Flanders Investment and Trade (FIT) is the trade promotion organisation (TPO) of Flanders, a region of Belgium. TPOs are facilitative agencies that promote and stimulate trade by providing information, linkages, technical advice, marketing and policy advocacy. Answering ‘trade cases’ is one of the core business activities of FIT. A ‘trade case’ is a question from a Flemish company about a foreign market, that concerns the services of FIT, for example inquiries about the size or customs of a local market, potential foreign business partners, trade regulations or barriers, subsidies or market opportunities. The existing digital tools (before the AI adoption) for answering trade cases consisted of Microsoft Dynamics as a CRM software, used for receiving, assigning and answering incoming questions, and Microsoft SharePoint as a collaborative document management and storage system, used for storing relevant information on local markets, partners and regulations, and for storing documents drawn up for previous cases. The studied algorithm is aimed at partly automating the process of answering trade cases. Using natural language processing, trained on a large dataset of past trade questions and answers, the algorithm was designed to retrieve past answers to frequently-asked routine questions. It acts as an AI-powered search engine, not just comparing individual words, but interpreting the entire body of the question to find the most relevant answer. The AI suggestions were integrated into the CRM system as an extra tab on the CRM screen displaying the information on the incoming question.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: AI-enable accessibility check tool to make EU-compliant federal websites
column4: The FPS (Federal Public Service) Policy and Support (BOSA), established in 2017, assists the government and supports federal organisations in various fields including IT, HR, organisational management, integrity policy and public contracts. In 2016, a new EU directive mandated that all government websites need to be accessible. An AI-driven open-source mode integrated into BOSA’s accessibility checker to reduce human errors in evaluating the degree of accessibility of public organisations' websites. It can be used by any public agency.
column8: Federal Public Service Policy ans Support (FPS BOSA)

column2: The Flemish public employment service reinvents orientation tests with AI
column4: VDAB, the Flemish public employment service, worked together with Radix, a Belgium-based company providing AI tools, to update and streamline its orientation test by making use of machine learning. Orient 2.0, a digital and AI-supported tool that creates a personalised orientation test and uses machine learning to predict professional interests.

column2: Brussels Airport Company revolutionize the airport experience using AI - Check-in project
column4: As part of the Airport Operations Plan (AOP), Radix’s AI-powered solutions superpower airport operations managers with accurate forecasts for critical processes in the airport. In turn, the airport operations managers communicate these forecasts back to all Brussels Airport Company (BAC) partners to ensure the right workforce capacity, maintaining a smooth passenger experience while keeping costs efficient. In this project, BAC aimed to predict the number of passengers arriving at check-in every 30 minutes. By analyzing data from previous passenger surveys, it  was built an ML model that takes into account different behaviors for different types of flights.

column2: Brussels Airport Company revolutionize the airport experience using AI - Person Reduced Mobility Project
column4: As part of the Airport Operations Plan (AOP), Radix’s AI-powered solutions superpower airport operations managers with accurate forecasts for critical processes in the airport. In turn, the airport operations managers communicate these forecasts back to all Brussels Airport Company (BAC) partners to ensure the right workforce capacity, maintaining a smooth passenger experience while keeping costs efficient. Every day, hundreds of passengers with reduced mobility need assistance navigating the airport, which requires enough staff from BAC partners to assist them from arrival to departure. Providing this kind of end-to-end guidance is quite labor-intensive. On top of that the service is provided for free, to be able to do that the airport needs to go about it in an efficient way. This means having a good idea of the amount of PRMs to foresee the right amount of staff and avoid breaking the bank.

column2: Brussels Airport Company revolutionize the airport experience using AI - Baggage project
column4: As part of the Airport Operations Plan (AOP), Radix’s AI-powered solutions superpower airport operations managers with accurate forecasts for critical processes in the airport. In turn, the airport operations managers communicate these forecasts back to all Brussels Airport Company (BAC) partners to ensure the right workforce capacity, maintaining a smooth passenger experience while keeping costs efficient. In this project, BAC aimed to accurately predict the number of bags per flight, especially for transfer flights where time is critical. In January 2023, the average number of departing bags processed daily was 17150, of which 5796 were “transfer” bags. The airport can avoid bottlenecks and improve its performance by making a more accurate bag forecast.

column2: Analyzing Public Sentiment for Statistics Flanders with AI
column4: Statistics Flanders is the network of Flemish government agencies that develop, produce and publish official statistics. Their goal is to make reliable statistics while utilizing various data sources. They decided to experiment with publicly available data to see how Artificial Intelligence can contribute to producing reliable statistics, for example leveraging on social media data that allows to acquire insights on public sentiment.For example, they wanted to understand how much discomfort was sparked by COVID-19 and how it changed over time and how the Ukrainian war made Flemish citizens feel. The pilot regards consists of an AI-powered dashboard displaying public sentiment on various relevant topics (e.g., Ukrainian War, COVID-19), based on analyzing up to 50k Flemish tweets per month.

column2: AI-powered speed measurement by Munipical police in Prague
column4: The automatic system of speed measurement in Prague consists of over 60 devices measuring the speed. More than 40 of the devices are section measurements (calculating the speed of cars between two points - using induction loops on the road) and the other 20 devices measure the speed directly (radar-based cameras). In two systems, photos and plate recognition are done automatically. There are around 300,000 fines taken every year using the data perceived. All decisions regarding fines are reviewed by humans (namely, a member of the Municipal police).

column2: Algorithm based quality control of presidential candidates
column4: The target of this algorithm are presidential candidates and their supporters. Thanks to  analyses of the 2013 elections, two candidates were excluded because of this quality control. The presidential candidates may choose two ways to qualify for the race: they either need to get the support of parliament members (20 representatives or 10 senators) or collect 50,000 signatures of eligible voters. In the second case, a statistical control conducted by the Ministry of Interior is essential to ensure that the signatures are provided by eligible voters. This method is like standard statistical quality control, even if it is not well designed to ensure the required results. However, an algorithm is used to randomly select two batches of 8,500 signatures.

column2: Algorithm to detect fraud at the National Tax Authority in Hungary
column4: Tax administrations face increasing challenges in detecting complex and evolving forms of tax fraud while ensuring compliance with strict data protection and transparency requirements. The Hungarian National Tax Authority (NAV) manages extensive datasets related to declarations, transactions and compliance behaviour, yet traditional analytical approaches limit the ability to uncover hidden patterns at scale. To address this, NAV established a dedicated working group in April 2022 to explore advanced, data-driven approaches to fraud detection within a clearly defined legal and organisational framework. The objective of this use case is to improve the effectiveness of fraud prevention and risk analysis while safeguarding taxpayer rights and data confidentiality. The implementation focuses on developing a semantic data asset methodology that enables structured use of anonymised tax data for advanced analytical research. This includes the design of algorithms for pattern detection, as well as robust validation and testing methodologies to assess reliability and bias. Strong governance mechanisms ensure that data protection rules define the operational boundaries of the work. The expected impact includes improved identification of fraudulent behaviour, more targeted inspections and better allocation of investigative resources. Qualitative outcomes include enhanced analytical capacity, stronger evidence-based decision-making and a scalable methodological foundation for future tax compliance initiatives.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Blockchain-enhanced INPS disability card
column4: People with disabilities often face repeated verification processes when accessing services, while authorities must ensure eligibility, prevent fraud and protect personal data. INPS aimed to strengthen the European Disability Card system. The objective of this use case is to improve reliability, transparency and accessibility of disability certification. The implementation supports the physical card with a secure distributed digital infrastructure that records eligibility attributes and access rights in a tamper-resistant manner. Service providers can verify entitlements quickly without accessing unnecessary personal data. The impact includes reduced fraud risk, faster service access and improved user experience. Qualitative benefits include greater trust, better interoperability across services and improved protection of sensitive information.

column2: Blockchain-enabled "building bonus" credits in Italy
column4: Italy’s “110% superbonus” policy generated complex administrative flows for managing construction tax credits, creating risks of fraud and inefficiency. The objective of this proof of concept is to improve transparency and traceability of bonus payments to construction firms. The implementation tokenises building credits on a distributed ledger, enabling secure tracking of issuance, transfer and redemption. The impact includes improved oversight, reduced fraud risk and more efficient credit circulation. Qualitative outcomes include stronger confidence in public incentive schemes and improved administrative control.

column2: Welfare ticket - a blockchian-based platform for the gig economy environment
column4: Labour platforms and gig workers often operate outside traditional social security frameworks, creating gaps in insurance coverage and administrative complexity for short-term or task-based work. In Italy, a concept known as the “Welfare Ticket” was developed to address these challenges by enabling temporary, portable social security coverage for gig economy workers. Stakeholders included employers, platform workers, and social security institutions, with the objective of simplifying compliance while ensuring that workers remained insured during the execution of specific tasks. The proposed solution centred on a shared digital register where employers could issue a time-bound social security ticket linked to a specific job. The platform design relied on distributed ledger technology to record the issuance, validity period, and redemption of welfare tickets in an immutable and auditable manner. Each transaction would be visible to authorised parties, reducing disputes and administrative reconciliation. Although the concept was developed through an enterprise design thinking process and documented by INPS, it did not progress to operational deployment. Nevertheless, the initiative provided a structured blueprint for how decentralised registers could support more flexible labour markets, influencing later discussions on portable benefits and digital welfare infrastructure.

column2: Blockchain-based virtual large-scale storage for photovoltaics system operators
column4: As feed-in tariffs are phased out, photovoltaic system operators face declining revenues, while consumers seek affordable green electricity. Traditional energy markets offer limited support for local energy exchange and self-consumption. The BloGPV project addresses this challenge by enabling local trading of solar electricity among neighbours through a virtual storage concept. Stakeholders include PV system operators, electricity consumers, and local energy communities. The objective is to maintain the economic viability of solar installations and strengthen local renewable energy use. The solution creates a distributed virtual storage network that connects producers and consumers, allowing surplus electricity to be traded locally. Automated transaction mechanisms record energy exchanges and settlements within the network, ensuring transparency and reliability. By coordinating production and consumption, the system reduces reliance on centralised grids and supports local balancing. The impact includes improved returns for solar producers, more attractive green electricity for consumers, and increased local self-sufficiency. BloGPV contributes to decentralised energy systems and offers a replicable model for community-based renewable energy trading.

column2: Norway steps into the metaverse with decentraland tax office
column4: Public administrations increasingly seek new ways to reach citizens and businesses, particularly younger and digitally native audiences, while improving accessibility to information and services. In Norway, authorities identified emerging digital environments as potential new channels for public engagement. The objective of this use case is to explore whether immersive virtual spaces can complement traditional service channels for providing tax-related information and guidance. The implementation involves establishing a virtual tax office within Decentraland, a decentralised digital platform, through a partnership between Brønnøysund Register Centre, the national tax authority Skatteetaten and external advisors. The virtual office replicates elements of a physical service point, allowing users to access information, attend events and interact with guidance resources in a three-dimensional environment. The pilot focuses on experimentation, user experience and organisational learning rather than transactional services. Expected impacts include insights into citizen engagement in virtual environments, expanded outreach to new user groups and lessons on integrating public services into emerging digital ecosystems. Qualitative benefits include increased visibility of public institutions, innovation capacity building and evidence to inform future digital service strategies.
column8: Norway’s central register and Norway's tax authority

column2: The City of Helsinki is investigating the benefits of generative artificial intelligence in various work tasks
column4: Municipal administrations face increasing pressure to deliver high-quality services while managing growing volumes of documents, correspondence and internal coordination tasks. In the City of Helsinki, employees across departments spend significant time on routine knowledge work, limiting their capacity for strategic analysis, collaboration and citizen-facing activities. The objective of this use case is to assess how everyday work processes can be streamlined and supported in a controlled and responsible manner, while maintaining public sector values such as reliability, transparency and staff wellbeing. The implementation consists of a structured experimentation phase within the existing Microsoft 365 work environment, where selected employees test integrated digital assistance functions. These include support for drafting and reviewing documents, summarising lengthy materials, managing email overload and organising information across files and meetings. Usage is monitored to understand task suitability, workflow integration and organisational implications rather than to automate decisions. The expected impact includes reduced administrative workload, time savings in routine tasks and improved consistency in document handling. Qualitative results focus on improved employee experience, better focus on high-value work and evidence-based insights to guide future adoption decisions, ensuring that productivity gains align with ethical, organisational and operational requirements.

column2: Traceability Platform for Alcoholic Production Certification
column4: Producers of high-quality regional spirits face increasing regulatory and market demands for traceability, authenticity, and proof of origin. Certification processes are often fragmented across authorities and perceived as administrative burdens rather than value drivers. This project addresses the need to transform certification into a strategic asset for “Made in Italy” products, starting with Genepì del Piemonte. Stakeholders include producers, certification bodies, public administrations, and consumers. The objective is to ensure transparent tracking of production batches while enhancing product value and consumer trust. The solution implements a platform-based certification system that records production and certification data across the supply chain. Information is securely shared among authorised administrations, creating a one-stop framework for compliance and verification. Batch-level traceability enables producers to demonstrate authenticity and quality to markets and regulators. The impact includes reduced administrative complexity, improved transparency, and stronger brand protection for regional products. The initiative supports innovation in agri-food certification and provides a scalable model for traceability across other protected productions.
column8: Agenzia delle Dogane e dei Monopoli (ADM)
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain

column2: BLOCKCERTS: Blockchain-based university degrees at Bicocca and Padua University in Italy
column4: The University of Milan Bicocca has introduced a blockchain system to guarantee students the validity and integrity of official documents and certificates on the web. Developed together with the Cineca inter-university consortium, the new technology allows documents to be issued certified digital documents, ensuring that they cannot be manipulated or falsified. Companies and businesses and entrepreneurs will only need to click to check their validity, without having to apply to universities.

column2: App ACI Space - blockchain-based notarisation
column4: ACI, in cooperation with EY, has developed a smartphone app that allows end users to have their vehicle's data certified and notarise it virtually through a private blockchain platform and Ethereum. The aim is to allow citizens to consult and check the lifecycle history of a car simply by entering the number plate into a smartphone app.

column2: "LLMoin" pilot project to provide insight into Hamburg's medium-term LLM strategy
column4: Public administrations face growing demands for efficiency in text-heavy tasks such as summarising documents, drafting responses and conducting research. Hamburg’s local government launched the LLMoin pilot to explore how large language models could support administrative work while meeting strict data protection and organisational requirements. Around 100 employees across departments participate, with the objective of assessing acceptance, usefulness and governance needs. The pilot uses the Luminous language model developed and hosted in Germany by startup Aleph Alpha, with a custom front-end and data processing layer built specifically for Hamburg. The system supports tasks such as text summarisation, drafting and information retrieval, operating within controlled environments. Outputs are reviewed by users, ensuring human responsibility for decisions. Early results show productivity gains for participating staff and valuable insights into training needs, technical integration and ethical considerations. The pilot informs Hamburg’s medium-term strategy for language technologies in government, highlighting both efficiency potential and the importance of sovereign, transparent deployment models in the public sector.

column2: Dublin City Council accelerates the potential of drones across Ireland
column4: Local authorities increasingly recognise the potential of unmanned aerial vehicles to support public services, yet fragmented regulation, limited operational experience and lack of shared standards have slowed adoption. Dublin City Council (DCC), through its Smart Dublin programme, launched a coordinated initiative to explore how drones could be safely and effectively used across Ireland for civil defence, emergency response, public safety and environmental monitoring. Stakeholders include local and national government agencies, emergency services, regulators and technology providers. The objective is to build institutional knowledge, test real-world applications and inform policymaking through evidence rather than isolated trials. The programme establishes a cross-agency network to identify priority use cases, assess operational constraints and share best practices observed internationally. Drone deployments are tested in controlled pilots, integrating aerial data collection with existing emergency management and environmental monitoring systems. Outputs include geospatial imagery, situational awareness data and operational insights rather than standalone services. The initiative has accelerated learning across the public sector, reduced duplication of effort and positioned Ireland to adopt drones more strategically. Its impact lies in improved readiness for emergency scenarios, more efficient data collection and a clearer regulatory and governance framework for scaling drone use nationwide.

column2: AI-powered “Gestore Emendamenti” (GEM) at the Italian Senate
column4: The Italian Senate utilizes an AI system, “Gestore Emendamenti” (GEM), to manage amendments. The system employs text clustering algorithms to quickly identify groups of similarly worded amendments in order to schedule their simultaneous voting. While the system produces near-instant results, staff members review, approve, modify, and integrate the machine-generated analysis to ensure that no decisions are made without human oversight.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Artificial Intelligence and Mobility - AIAMO
column4: The main aim of the project is to establish data sovereignty in transport by networking mobility services, simplifying data collection for environmentally oriented and multimodal traffic management. Trusted AI-powered applications will deliver research results through a networked integration zone, with a focus on facilitating market access for SMEs and numerous cities. The project is coordinated by ITS Germany - Federal Association of Economics and Science for Transport Technologies and Intelligent Mobility in Berlin. ITS Germany is also committed to expand its project throughout Europe. That is why a webinar on the "AIAMO goes Europe" initiative, whose target are other national ITSs, took place in June 2024.
column8: Federal Ministry for Digital and Transport (BMDV)
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Open Mobility Electrical Infrastructure - OMEI
column4: The expansion of electromobility requires coordinated planning of charging infrastructure, integration of renewable energy, and efficient management of energy flows across regions. Fragmented data and incompatible systems currently limit optimisation of charging networks and energy use. This use case addresses these challenges by developing an open data and service framework to support planning, operation, and optimisation of fast and bidirectional charging infrastructure across Europe. The project involves public authorities, infrastructure operators, energy providers, and mobility stakeholders, with the objective of enabling a decarbonised and economically viable energy supply for electric mobility. The solution is implemented through a digital platform that collects and integrates charging, user, energy, and traffic data from demonstration sites, including fast-charging stations with hybrid energy storage and bidirectional charging capabilities. Advanced analytics and simulation models are used to optimise operating strategies, economic performance, and vehicle-to-home and vehicle-to-grid concepts, while distributed ledger technology is examined for secure and transparent energy trading and billing. The project supports improved infrastructure planning, better utilisation of renewable energy, and scalable data sharing across borders. It contributes to reduced emissions, more efficient charging operations, and a robust foundation for future European mobility and energy data spaces.
column8: University of Applied Sciences Energy Technology Center (TZE)
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: National Initiative for AI-based Transformation in the Data Economy - NITD
column4: The aim of the National Initiative for AI-based Transformation into the Data Economy is to promote and scale the data economy in Germany and Europe. In doing so, the NITD assumes a networking and implementation-oriented role and thus creates a basis for marketable and common good-oriented AI applications that focus on people. The project focuses on three main areas: (1) The NITD develops technical and organisational foundations for the successful networking of data rooms and implements these in use cases. (2) Harmonized quality and testing standards for AI applications will be designed and made visible and tangible in a quality and innovation center. (3) As part of the project, a funding concept will be designed that builds on existing successful innovation funding and meaningfully complements it with a focus on AI applications.
column8: acatech - German Academy of Engineering Sciences
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Automation of Hamburg's on-demand offering with integration into public transport - AHOI project
column4: The core goal of the AHOI project is to analyse, design and implement previously unresolved tasks relating to the operation of a large and mixed fleet of manually controlled vehicles and vehicles with autonomous driving functions in on-demand operation as well as the development of an associated control center. Particular attention is paid to accessibility along the entire travel chain as well as the application and further development of the newly created legal framework. In addition, key results should be obtained about user structures and patterns as well as the effects on mobility behavior. This is done iteratively in three operational phases: (1) Operating a manually controlled on-demand fleet with passengers; (2) Test integration of vehicles with autonomous driving functions including technical supervision into the operation of the existing on-demand fleet (initially without passengers); (3) Operation of a mixed on-demand fleet with passengers including technical supervision and integration of the data ecosystem.
column8: Free and Hanseatic City of Hamburg, Authority for Transport and Mobility Transition (FHH)

column2: Establishment of a U-Space real laboratory in Hamburg - U-Space real laboratory
column4: The project served as a lighthouse project for the implementation of European U-space regulation. It showed how drones can be safely and fairly integrated into the airspace as air traffic participants. In a real-world laboratory, which has been specially set up for this purpose, the interaction of individual airspace actors as well as the associated technical, procedural and operational implementation was demonstrated and even the participation of third parties will be enabled. The project served as a model for further implementations of U-Spaces in Germany and Europe.

column2: Trustworthiness platform for AI solutions and data rooms - TrustKI
column4: The aim of the project is to create a trustworthiness platform including an index and reputation system with the aim of enabling users to build trust in manufacturing companies as well as in their AI solutions or data rooms in order to actually use them. Manufacturing companies are given the opportunity to document the trustworthiness of their organisation as well as that of their AI solutions and data rooms – and to do so in an industry-specific cooperative manner with the relevant stakeholders of the domain.

column2: Alpine mobility data room Inntal - ALMODA
column4: Mountain regions face complex mobility challenges due to terrain, weather variability, cross-border traffic, and safety requirements. Local communities in the Alpine Inntal region require accurate, real-time information to support safe, efficient, and sustainable mobility. This use case addresses these needs by establishing a cross-border data space that supports real-time communication, navigation, and surveillance services for mobility users and authorities. The project involves regional administrations, mobility service providers, and infrastructure operators, with the objective of enabling Mobility 4.0 through a shared and trusted digital situation picture. The solution is implemented through a multimodal data platform that aggregates high-precision digital elevation models, static and dynamic geographic protection zones, navigation and communication data, and high-resolution local weather information. Data is authenticated, validated, and combined using advanced analytical methods to create real-time situational awareness, which is then distributed securely to users and systems across borders. The platform supports multiple mobility use cases tailored to local needs, including safety, routing, and traffic management. The project improves reliability and safety of mobility in complex Alpine environments, enhances cross-border coordination, and enables scalable data-driven services. It delivers clear value by supporting informed decision-making, reducing risk, and laying the groundwork for integrated, resilient mobility services in sensitive regions.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Digital test field Air Cargo - DTAC
column4: The goal of the DTAC is to increase the efficiency and performance of the air freight transport chain through increased networking and digitalisation of processes. Specifically, the aim is to improve the exchange of data between stakeholders in the air freight transport chain, as well as to automate and autonomize dispatching, handling and transport, as well as the development of AI-supported optimization and forecasts. The plan is to develop and test solutions for air freight in a neutral, cross-site manner together with the respective stakeholders.
column8: Fraunhofer Institute for Material Flow and Logistics IML

column2: AI and discrete loading optimization models to increase utilization in KV - KiBa
column4: The aim of the project is to solve this assignment problem in terms of IT technology and to optimize it with the help of suitable calculation methods and methods from the field of artificial intelligence (AI). The aim is to ensure that every request for loading a loading unit receives a proposal for an optimal placement of the loading unit on a wagon set within the shortest possible time. However, this decision must be made before all the information is available about what other loading units for the same start/destination routes will arrive at the CT terminal over time.
column8: Kombiverkehr German Society for Combined Freight Transport mbH & Co. KG

column2: Networked LiDAR bus for safe autonomous use in shared spaces - LiDARshared
column4: The main aim of the project is to collect valuable urban traffic data using buses equipped with high-performance LiDAR sensors, particularly in busy city centers and main arteries. This data is crucial for smart city applications, providing insights for optimizing traffic flow and enhancing urban traffic beyond public transport, including adjustments to traffic light phases, speed limits, one-way rules, and identifying areas of risk for cyclists or pedestrians. The project involves collaboration with stakeholders in Kiel to gather necessary data for urban traffic optimization.

column2: Feasibility study for the harmonization of truck parking space occupancy through networked model-predictive control systems based on V2X technologies - PAHA
column4: The fuel and rest areas (T+R facilities) on highly frequented federal highways are characterized by overcrowding, especially in the evening and at night, despite extensive investments in additional infrastructure. On the basis of different reference scenarios and an interface analysis, a forecast model was developed using the derived available input variables, which predicts the expected occupancy rate of a T+R facility in terms of time and space in order to determine optimal rest areas for each vehicle. In addition, approaches were developed for a control system for improved utilization of free parking capacities, which provides an individual recommendation of the best possible rest area with regard to static and dynamic input variables as a starting point. In addition, it was possible to determine requirements for existing systems with large-scale infrastructure for occupancy detection in order to enable these systems to be expanded with the developed forecasting and control methods as well as existing V2X technologies.
column8: Erfurt University of Applied Sciences, Faculty of Economics, Logistics, Transport

column2: Smart Urban Logistics through Electrification Collaboration and Automation - SULEICA
column4: Electrification and automation – these are the keywords of the current mobility change. They offer the potential to completely rethink the transport processes of tomorrow. To make this a success, SULEICA designed, implemented and validated an approach for emission-free, traffic-reducing and efficient urban logistics to create liveable urban spaces. In addition to carrying out pilot operations with (partially) automated logistics vehicles that can communicate and interact with people, the focus was on integrating the system into the urban transport infrastructure and involving citizens. Key project results are a road-approved test vehicle (Ducktrain) and the integration of findings from pilot operations into the next generation of vehicles, as well as a roadmap for future vehicle registration, taking into account increasing automation. Furthermore, it could be shown that the Duck offers a lot of potential for the urban traffic flow as well as the users, i.e. logistics companies, who can improve their logistics processes ecologically and ecologically. In acceptance studies, it could be shown that the Ducks are perceived positively for urban traffic and it could be determined which factors, e.g. in the use of the infrastructure or the length of the Ducktrain, influence the perception. The results of the SULEICA project form an excellent starting point for the more in-depth research of individual aspects.

column2: Autonomous shuttles & Co in the digital test field for city traffic - Shuttles &Co
column4: The testing of highly automated fleet vehicles showed the limits of the available technology, but provided valuable experience on the requirements for future regular operation. The methods for perception and prediction impressively demonstrated the positive effect of cooperation between vehicles, such as improved localization and increased road safety. Further developed cooperative methods of traffic control and traffic information based on vehicle-2-everything (V2X) communication and the advantages of V2X technology could be demonstrated to the stakeholders in the state of Berlin. In the process, new data and exchange platforms were integrated. With the recognition and updating of map objects, the self-updating of the map provided by the Berlin administration could be further developed.
column8: State of Berlin, represented by the Senate Department for the Environment, Transport and Climate Protection

column2: AI and M2M -based optimization of safety and comfort for people with mobility restrictions in non-motorized individual transport in the ERFURTS location
column4: The creation of a barrier-free mobility infrastructure supports the development of an inclusive society that ensures mobility for all people. The situation for people with mobility restrictions, especially for people with disabilities in the transport system, is often not optimal. There is a high potential for conflicts or, in the worst case, accidents when encounters with (motorized) private and public transport (ÖV). Particularly relevant here is pedestrian traffic at junctions controlled by traffic lights (LSA) and the transition from pedestrian traffic to public (local) transport at stops. The aim of the project was to increase road safety and comfort for people with limited mobility at traffic control intersections and at the transitions from non-motorized private transport to public transport. This is to be achieved through automatic detection, the automatic intelligent adjustment of LSA controls and the information of drivers on roads and public transport. In the future, this information can also be transmitted to fully automated vehicles via appropriate vehicle-to-everything ( V2X ) interfaces. On the one hand, the project contributed to an inclusive society and at the same time advanced the development of a networked and automated transport system.

column2: Smart control center for automated transport robots and buses in the city of Lauenburg/Elbe - TaBuLa-LOGplus
column4: The aim of the project was to implement a prototypical control centre as a basis for the efficient use of combined passenger and goods transport. This was done on the basis of the test track already implemented in the TaBuLa and TaBu-La-LOG projects, self-developed transport robots and a conventional regular-service bus. In addition, AI-based methods were used to optimize coordination and networking. Furthermore, the degree of automation of transport robots increased by technical developments.

column2: Shuttle model region Upper Franconia II - SMO-II
column4: Public transport providers in rural and semi-rural regions face growing pressure to offer flexible, reliable, and cost-efficient mobility services despite low passenger density and challenging operating conditions. In Upper Franconia, authorities and operators sought to modernise shuttle services by reducing dependence on onboard staff, improving passenger assistance, and enabling safe operation even in difficult weather, while complying with national regulations on autonomous driving. This use case focuses on the SMO-II project, involving regional transport authorities, operators, and control centres, with the objective of holistically automating the mobility chain and enabling demand-responsive shuttle services. The solution is implemented through a highly automated transport system that integrates vehicle automation, sensor technology, passenger assistance systems, and remote operations. Shuttles are designed to operate at Level 4 autonomy in defined environments, with real-time monitoring and manoeuvre release managed from an existing control centre. Digital systems cover vehicle interiors, door areas, and passenger support functions, while advanced perception and control technologies enable safe operation without accompanying personnel. The project improves operational efficiency by reducing staffing requirements, increases service availability through on-demand operation, and enhances safety and reliability under varied conditions. It supports scalable deployment of autonomous shuttles in non-urban regions and contributes to more inclusive, sustainable public transport offerings.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI-based system for connected mobility - KIS'M
column4: The state of Berlin is faced with the challenge of offering affordable and reliable local public transport (ÖPNV) even in rapidly growing residential areas. To solve the problem, KIS'M pursues a number of goals. This includes the experimental implementation of driverless on-demand transport, the development of safe and reliable processes for technical supervision, at virtual stops and in vehicles. With better networking and cooperation between automated vehicles as well as with traffic control, a higher level of traffic safety is to be achieved even in a mixed system, especially through realistic autonomous driving maneuvers. Artificial intelligence is to be used to derive more up-to-date and accurate map and traffic information from the data recorded in traffic. In addition, KIS'M intends to use the experience gained to derive a strategy for the further use of driverless vehicles in the state of Berlin.
column8: State of Berlin, represented by the Senate Department for the Environment, Mobility, Consumer and Climate Protection

column2: AI -supported data analysis and simulation of rescue services - AI-Rescue
column4: The rescue service in Germany is very heterogeneous; in terms of the degree of digitalisation, it fell significantly behind in comparison to other European countries in recent years. The optimal functioning of the rescue chain depends on many dynamic and static influencing factors. The focus of the feasibility study was the development of a simulation as a demonstrator for the use of AI in rescue services. A spatial reference was established: real data about the frequency and duration of missions was collected, evaluated, and used to parameterize the model. The simulation model then replicated operational decisions using AI strategies.

column2: Weather-independent and highly automated ridesharing service in Kelheim (Germany)
column4: The aim of the KelRide project is a highly automated ride-sharing service in the Kelheim district using artificial intelligence. A mixed fleet of highly automated and conventional shuttles integrated into public transport will transport passengers in the operating area. The main development goal of this project is a powerful overall system that functions to a significantly improved extent in "bad weather" conditions and thus allows year-round real operation in German weather conditions for the first time. The desired real operation should serve as a blueprint for corresponding services in other municipalities.

column2: Adaptive maps and AI -based infrastructure monitoring - AK-KII
column4: Highly automated mobility is impossible without the use of highly accurate maps. A vehicle can only be moved safely, efficiently and sustainably if it has accurate and up-to-date information about the infrastructure to be traveled, usually a road, depending on time and place. An important aspect here is the condition of the infrastructure, its automated monitoring and the reflection of the monitoring results in a reasonable time, for example in different levels of such highly accurate maps. The aim of this preliminary study is to form an interdisciplinary consortium for AI-based infrastructure monitoring for highly automated mobility. As part of the preliminary study, the relevant state of the art is first analysed and it is determined which sensor technologies can be used to derive information about the infrastructure, and which processes already exist and can be networked for this purpose. In addition, scenarios and research questions are defined for specific topics such as the condition recording of bridges, sensor data fusion for different types of roads, as well as evaluation criteria for relevant data or the automated documentation of AI decision-making processes.

column2: AI -based cross-type mobility optimization in non-urban regions - KIMoNo
column4: Regions at the interface between urban and rural areas often suffer from fragmented mobility systems, inefficient logistics, and limited transport alternatives, leading to higher emissions and reduced accessibility. In the Passau district, stakeholders sought to better understand and optimise mobility patterns across different transport modes, taking into account the specific needs of rural populations. This use case addresses these challenges by developing cross-type mobility optimisation strategies that improve sustainability and performance in non-urban regions, involving local authorities, residents, logistics providers, and researchers. The objective is to design mobility solutions that are technically viable, socially accepted, and tailored to local demand. The project is implemented through data-driven analysis of mobility flows across logistics and individual transport, supported by advanced modelling and optimisation techniques. Decentralised infrastructure concepts are evaluated, and digital solutions are developed to coordinate different modes more efficiently. The initiative also establishes the necessary technological infrastructure and actively engages citizens through targeted science communication, public events, and interactive exhibitions to assess acceptance and usability. The project supports more efficient and sustainable mobility patterns, improves alignment between infrastructure and real demand, and strengthens public acceptance of new mobility services, creating long-term value for regional transport planning.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Artificial intelligence in Ingolstadt's transport system - KIVI
column4: Despite increasing digitalisation and networking, current transport systems are largely characterized by incomplete information about all road users. Pedestrians and cyclists in particular often cannot be adequately taken into account in traffic control and traffic safety functions. In KIVI, data-driven AI methods were developed to improve traffic control and safety. For this purpose, a high-definition test field is set up across several nodes, equipped with local sensors for the detection of all road users. This stationary view was supplemented and AI-based fused with additional mobile sensors (cars, buses, bicycles and pedestrians equipped with 5G smartphones) in order to generate as comprehensive an up-to-date digital image of the local and network-wide traffic situation as possible and to be able to make a forecast of the multimodal network-wide traffic situation. Based on this, a needs-based green time distribution for all road users as well as a traffic light phase assistant was implemented. A warning function that uses AI processes to recognize the behavior and intention of (non-)motorized road users and specifically warns of dangerous situations visually and acoustically was also developed.

column2: Silicon Economy Logistics Ecosystem - SELE
column4: The project aimed to create an open-source infrastructure and technical components for the platform economy in logistics,facilitating digitisation and automation of supply chain processes. It focused on developing AI algorithms and integrating new technologies to improve digital infrastructure, leading to enhanced industry performance and positive transport outcomes. The core of the project was the implementation of new artificial intelligence algorithms, the integration of new technical possibilities (including from the field of mobile communications) and the targeted use of these developments for new common and cross-company processes and services.
column8: Fraunhofer Institute for Material Flow and Logistics IML, Dortmund
column16: Tracking of goods and assets along the supply chain

column2: Intelligent information technologies for process optimization and automation in inland ports - Binntelligent
column4: In addition to road and rail, inland waterways are also important for freight transport, especially since inland shipping still has free capacity. The project focused on enhancing coordination within the transport chain to boost the performance of inland ports in transport and handling with the support of AI. It involved designing, developing, and testing intelligent information technologies to collect and share decision-relevant data among all stakeholders. Specifically, efforts included improving predictive capabilities for inland port transports through experiments and adapting inland waterway transport to the broader freight transport system using real-world data.
column8: Institute of Shipping Economics and Logistics (ISL)

column2: Autonomously working machines in road construction 4.0 - ROBOT road construction 4.0
column4: The main aim of the project was to enable road construction machines to work autonomously and in a networked manner using modern sensor and IT systems, along with innovative machine technology. This aims to eliminate the need for additional operating personnel during the asphalt paving process, ensuring permanent quality control. This initiative not only enhanced occupational safety but also improved paving quality, increased road asphalt durability, reduced congestion, and enhanced road safety. A 3D model of the road was built  as the starting point for automated road construction.

column2: Smartmail - intelligent handling of mails
column4: Municipal administrations handle large volumes of physical and digital correspondence, and inefficient mail distribution and journaling processes can lead to delays, lack of transparency, and increased administrative costs. In Norddjurs Municipality, improving internal mail handling was identified as a way to accelerate case processing for citizens while ensuring accountability within municipal workflows. This use case addresses these challenges by optimising postal and journaling processes across municipalities, involving administrative staff, caseworkers, and citizens, with the objective of reducing processing times and improving transparency. The solution is implemented through an intelligent mail-handling system that automatically classifies incoming letters and emails, identifies the correct recipient or department, and journals documents immediately within municipal case management systems. The system integrates with existing digital infrastructure and supports both physical and electronic correspondence. Following successful implementation, the solution was scaled to ten municipalities. The approach significantly reduces manual handling, shortens case processing times, and lowers the risk of misrouting or delays. It improves transparency by ensuring timely registration of correspondence and delivers clear efficiency gains for municipalities, while enhancing service quality and responsiveness for citizens.

column2: Decision support for handling municipal document access cases
column4: The purpose of the project was to create the foundation for increased quality, uniformity and faster internal processing time of cases about document access by making available technical models that can improve the handling of document access. Today, municipalities' processing of document access cases is very resource-intensive. By using artificial intelligence for decision support when processing document access cases, the municipality gets the opportunity to free up resources for, for example, more citizen-related tasks. The project developed a so-called technical natural language process model (NLP), which an read and analyse large amounts of text simultaneously. Thereby, the models can be used for file inspections to efficiently search for files and documents and identify information that should be anonymised. The models can thus contribute to higher quality, uniformity and faster internal processing time of cases about access to documents, where compliance with the GDPR is supported by language technology.

column2: Optimised building use with artificial intelligence
column4: The project aimed to reduce energy and CO2 consumption in primary schools by gathering activities through intelligent room allocation using building data, IoT solutions (Internet of Things) and artificial intelligence. Primary schools make up a large part of the municipalities' building stock. The buildings are typically characterized by their old age and a backlog in relation to energy renovation, as well as a low degree of use of the premises. There is therefore great potential in optimizing building use for this particular type of municipal buildings, especially in the afternoon hours and at the weekend, when the buildings are used to varying extents by, for example, leisure organisations. The project is based on the "Smarte Kvadratmeter" project from Syddjurs Municipality and uses mature technology platforms and existing data sets as a basis for spreading intelligent algorithms in the school area. Against this background, the solution was able to deliver recommendations for action which can optimize the use of specific buildings and can thereby lead to energy optimisation. The solution's algorithm was basically developed as open-source with a view to increased dissemination.
column8: GovTech Midtjylland, Favrskov, Syddjurs and Aarhus Municipality

column2: An effective and holistic employment effort through the artificial intelligence technology Process Mining
column4: The purpose of the project was to create the basis for a more efficient, flexible and holistic effort in the area of ​​employment for the benefit of the citizen by using the technologies Process Mining and Predictive Monitoring. In the project, Process Mining and Predictive Monitoring used existing log files from case-bearing systems, financial systems, etc. to map the citizen's journey through the system from start to finish. It included a mapping of the workflows that make up an employment pathway for insured unemployed and citizens in resource pathways. Including IT systems, departments, teams, employees and relevant efforts. Process Mining was used to create a database-based and comprehensive understanding of which internal and external efforts in the field of employment are actually used and how effective the efforts are towards different citizen groups and the work with the holistic effort. Predictive Monitoring used historical data and Machine Learning to predict which activities and processes should be initiated in order to most effectively get the citizen into employment.

column2: Increased continuity in elderly care using artificial intelligence
column4: The project aimed to create transparency and improved the citizens' experience of coherent and safe care for the elderly by increasing the continuity of the employees the citizen met. Familiar faces who knew the individual's needs and wishes were crucial for the citizen's experience of coherence and security in the care, and this positively impacted the quality of care. The project adapted the existing Greenforce solution, which previously proposed optimal visit plans based on various parameters like the maximum number of different employees and scheduled working hours. It further optimized these plans by adding one or two more crucial parameters, like actual time spent on visit. The project also incorporated a feedback loop for the solution, allowing it to be continuously retrained and learn from user input. The solution was implemented in Aalborg and Syddjurs Municipalities, with different organisational setups. This provided insights into various organisational structures, planning approaches, and the city-country divide, informing the future widespread adoption of the solution. Additionally, the project investigated whether implementing the algorithm could have planned greener routes, reducing transportation time between visits and contributing to a greener footprint.
column8: Aalborg Municipality, Syddjurs Municipality, Aalborg University

column2: Artificial intelligence to support the visitation of rehabilitation courses (AIR Project)
column4: Municipal case workers in Denmark faced increasing pressure to assess citizens’ suitability for rehabilitation programmes consistently and efficiently. The AIR pilot in Aalborg aimed to support decision-making while maintaining professional judgement. Stakeholders included municipalities, case workers, healthcare providers, and citizens.Between 2018 and 2021, statistical and predictive models analysed historical rehabilitation data to estimate eligibility, risk of dropout, and expected outcomes. Outputs were presented as decision support within existing case management systems. The pilot improved consistency and efficiency in assessments, supported better allocation of rehabilitation resources, and freed staff time, while keeping humans in control of final decisions.

column2: Artificial intelligence for the prevention of malnutrition and hospitalization of the elderly
column4: The project aimed to prevent emergency hospital stays by identifying citizens at risk of malnutrition, so that treatment can be initiated in a timely manner. Malnutrition among the elderly is a significant predictor of longer hospital stays, readmissions and mortality. The project can thus contributde to citizens first and foremost receiving better treatment, where efforts against malnutrition are effectively initiated. In the long term, this would lead to citizens with greater self-reliance and an increased quality of life, and in the long term fewer municipal services for personal help.
column8: Aarhus University Hospital, Aarhus Municipality,MedTech Innovation Consortium,Regional Hospital Horsens and Skanderborg Municipality

column2: Better diagnostics of pregnant women using artificial intelligence
column4: The project aimed to develop a solution to improve the quality of ultrasound scans of pregnant women in order to increase the ability to predict complications. It is essential to identify any complications as early as possible in order to initiate preventive measures. The project built on a tested prototype, which has shown good results in terms of checking the quality of already completed recordings of ultrasound scans. The intention of the project was to further develop the prototype into a tool that can increase the quality of the individual scan by supporting the clinical staff (midwives, nurses and doctors) during the scan. Specifically, the tool ensured that the images from the scan have the optimal quality while the examination is taking place.
column8: CAMES Rigshospitalet, DTU Compute, Region Zealand, Southern Denmark and North Jutland

column2: Artificial intelligence for predicting exacerbations in multidisease with easy-to-understand explanation of complex results
column4: The project aimed to improve the treatment of citizens with several chronic diseases by developing a tool that can predict disease exacerbations in the citizen. This was done by including information about relevant chronic diseases in conjunction with other information, e.g. about the occurrence of relevant acute epidemic diseases. The project also developed and tested methods to communicate complex results from the algorithms in an easily understandable, trustworthy and ethically sound manner.
column8: TeleCare Nord/Region Nordjylland, North Jutland, Aalborg University, TeleCare Nord

column2: Artificial intelligence to support the visitation of referrals from general practice
column4: The project aimed to support patients' rights and ensure greater uniformity in visitation practice by using artificial intelligence to optimise the handling of referrals of citizens from general practitioners to hospitals. Region Zealand's somatic hospitals receive around 250,000 referrals from the practice sector annually. The visitation workflow is a relatively complex administrative and clinical professional process, where referrals in several stages must be manually handled and searched to the correct medical specialty and local department before further processes for the citizen can be initiated. Concretely, the intention was to develop an intelligent solution that can evaluate incoming referrals from general practitioners and distribute these according to specialty. For two selected specialties, the solution must then associate a patient process with the individual referral and assign the patient to a local department.
column8: Region Zealand, Capital Region of Denmark

column2: Artificial intelligence for treatment support for eye patients
column4: Ophthalmology departments faced growing demand to assess patients with age-related macular degeneration (AMD), a leading cause of blindness in Denmark. The pilot aimed to support treatment decisions and optimise specialist time. Stakeholders included patients, clinicians, and hospital administrators. From 2017 to 2019, decision-support algorithms analysed clinical imaging and patient data to classify patients requiring treatment, observation, or discharge. The system integrated into hospital workflows with a focus on transparency. The pilot enabled greater delegation of routine assessments, reduced pressure on ophthalmologists, and improved efficiency while maintaining trust and clinical oversight.

column2: WARD-HOME – Safe discharge from hospital
