column4: The project aimed to enable citizens' rehabilitation in their own homes by ensuring early detection and intervention in case of disease worsening. This was done by developing intelligent wireless monitoring that can assess the citizen's condition 24 hours a day and alert staff if necessary. The WARD-HOME project built on the advanced clinical support system, WARD, which collects information about the body's vital parameters (heart rate, oxygen saturation, breathing, etc.). Based on this, WARD assesses the patient's condition and alerts the healthcare professional if the need arises.
column8: Capital Region of Denmark (Bispebjerg Hospital and Rigshospitalet) DTU and Region Zealand
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: From A to B: Generalizing the mathematics of artificial neural networks (ANNs) to biological neural networks (BNNs)
column4: Artificial neural networks (ANNs) are at the core of the AI revolution. In the past years, enormous efforts have been made to unravel their mathematical properties, leading to fundamental insights and mathematical guarantees on when and why deep learning works well. This project proposes the development of advanced mathematical tools in nonparametric and high-dimensional statistics to analyze learning in Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs), a specific type of ANNs, as a statistical method. The starting point is a novel interpretation of the local updating of BNN parameters as a specific and non-standard, derivative-free optimization method. Whereas derivative-free optimization is thought to be slow, the conjecture is that it leads to favorable statistical properties in the setting underlying BNNs. If the research is successful, it has the potential to open a new research area in mathematical statistics and provide insights into how the brain learns. It could also lead to recommendations on how to make AI more efficient with less training data and how to train neuromorphic computer chips mimicking BNNs.

column2: Practical, Learning-Based Tools for Finding and Fixing Bugs
column4: Software bugs are a major problem for software developers and users alike, as they cause crashes, security vulnerabilities, and data loss. The LearnBugs ERC project, on which this proposal is based, has developed ground-breaking techniques to automatically find bugs and to propose suitable bug fixes. These techniques are based on artificial intelligence and deep learning, making them particularly powerful for kinds of bugs missed by traditional software developer tools. This Proof of Concept proposal, named BugGPT, aims to make learning-based techniques for finding and fixing software bugs practical and usable by software developers. The project will develop practical tools that enable software developers to automatically find and fix bugs in their code. Overall, BugGPT has the potential to make a significant impact on the software development industry by making learning-based bug finding and fixing practical for software developers. If successful, the project could be the beginning of a commercial product that stirs up the market of software development tools.

column2: Avifauna in archaeoecological networks
column4: AviArch, an innovative project led by experts in biomolecular archaeology, seeks to delve into the historical relationship between humans and birds, shedding light on its deep-rooted complexities. Focusing on the transition to agriculture in Epipalaeolithic/Neolithic southwest Asia and the emergence of urban life in Bronze Age Crete, AviArch employs advanced techniques such as zooarchaeology, palaeoproteomics, and AI to identify avifauna and understand their roles in past human systems. By examining biodiversity hotspots and integrating ecological, morphological, and behavioral data, AviArch aims to provide unprecedented insights into human-avian interactions throughout history. Through network analysis and multispecies theory, the project will elucidate how environmental changes impacted both humans and birds. Ultimately, AviArch aspires to enhance human adaptability, inform avian conservation strategies, and foster a deeper appreciation for these magnificent creatures.

column2: AI-enabled healthcare services during cross-border medical emergencies and regular patient services
column4: Healthcare systems face increasing strain when managing medical emergencies across borders and ensuring continuity of care for mobile populations, while also improving routine patient services. Differences in systems, data availability, and coordination can hinder timely and effective treatment. This use case addresses these challenges through the ESCORT project, which brings together healthcare providers, emergency services, patients, and research organisations across multiple European countries. The objective is to improve patient treatment, experience, and system resilience in both emergency and regular care scenarios, including cross-border situations. The project is implemented through the integration of connected medical devices, wearable technologies, and advanced data analytics to support pre-clinical, clinical, and post-clinical care. Digital services are co-designed with stakeholders and validated in real-life scenarios across six countries, building on prior European research initiatives. The system supports real-time data exchange, decision support, and coordination between providers in emergency medicine and routine care. The project enhances the quality and continuity of care, improves response times in emergencies, and strengthens cross-border cooperation. It delivers scalable digital health services that increase resilience of healthcare systems and improve outcomes and experience for patients across Europe.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: CLARITY - Causative Link between respirAtory syncytial viRus and chronic lung diseases: Identifying Targets for therapY
column4: The CLARITY project seeks to uncover genetic risk factors and molecular mechanisms behind virus-induced asthma, focusing on Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV). By analysing national cohorts from Estonia and Spain and using AI techniques, the project aims to identify how RSV disrupts cellular networks, leading to asthma. CLARITY aims to develop personalised prevention strategies and identify drug-like compounds to reverse RSV-induced effects, potentially benefiting other non-communicable diseases triggered by viral infections.

column2: WISDOM - Well-being improvement through the Integration of healthcare and reSearch Data and models with Out border for chronic iMmune-mediated diseases
column4: Integrating medical and research data, ensuring secure data sharing, and harnessing responsible AI models can help mitigate the impact of chronic immune-mediated diseases (CIMDs), which affect 10 % of Europeans. The EU-funded WISDOM project aims to utilise computational tools to translate complex biological information into practical insights. It addresses barriers to data integration and accessibility by developing computational risk stratification and outcome prediction models and tools for various CIMD use cases. The project seeks to encourage data use and facilitate responsible and critical assessment of AI usage in healthcare.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: ZEBAI - Innovative methodologies for the design of Zero-Emission and cost-effective Buildings enhanced by Artificial Intelligence
column4: The push for greener technologies and the adoption of zero-emission policies have spurred green innovation in various sectors. Buildings and construction represent one of these sectors, where developing technologies and methodologies for cost-effective and environmentally sustainable buildings is crucial. The EU-funded ZEBAI project aims to design zero-emission, cost-efficient buildings using a holistic approach. To achieve this goal, an open-access catalogue of well-characterised materials will be created. Then, an AI-based design methodology will be developed to create designs that enhance energy efficiency, indoor environmental quality and cost-efficiency, and reduce environmental impact. Additionally, the ZEBAI methodology will optimise efficiency and sustainability for various uses and climates, and with climate change resilience in mind.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: FARMWISE - Future Agricultural Resource Management and Water Innovations for a Sustainable Europe
column4: The EU-funded FARMWISE project aims to transform the EU’s agriculture sector by providing farmers and decision-makers with a revolutionary decision support system using AI, remote sensing, and precision agriculture. Furthermore, the project will collaborate with European organisations to increase knowledge sharing and co-creation, develop novel AI-based tools for improved water monitoring and management, and assist farmers and researchers. Finally, to achieve this, FARMWISE will collaborate with researchers and stakeholders in the sector.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: EXTRA-BRAIN - Explainable Trustworthy brain-like AI for Data Intensive Applications
column4: Data-intensive applications increasingly require learning systems that are energy-efficient, adaptable across tasks, and transparent to users, particularly when deployed across edge and cloud environments. Traditional approaches often struggle with high energy consumption, limited generalisation, and lack of explainability, constraining their use in critical or resource-constrained settings. This use case addresses these limitations through the EXTRA-BRAIN project, involving researchers, technology developers, and application partners, with the objective of developing more universal, trustworthy, and efficient learning models inspired by brain function. The project is implemented by designing novel neural network architectures based on principles from computational neuroscience, enabling functional flexibility and improved hardware compatibility. These models are supported by data optimisation pipelines that enhance data quality and reduce annotation effort, alongside explainability frameworks that allow users to understand and trust system outputs. The solutions are tested across diverse use cases along the edge–cloud continuum, addressing different performance and hardware constraints. The project contributes to reduced energy consumption, improved adaptability across tasks, and greater transparency of advanced data-processing systems. It supports wider adoption of trustworthy, efficient learning technologies in demanding real-world applications, delivering long-term value for industry and society.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: IRE - Intelligent Robotic Endoscopes for Improved Healthcare Services
column4: The Intelligent Robotic Endoscopes (IRE) project aims to enhance healthcare services by developing intelligent robotic solutions for endoscopy procedures. By integrating robotics control with learning from human operator data, bio-mechanical modeling, sensory feedback, and soft robotics for training, IRE addresses the challenge of improving colonoscopy success rates, particularly in cancer detection. With colonoscopies becoming increasingly essential for early cancer screening, IRE leverages data, simulation, and learning to enable robots to navigate the complexities of the human anatomy safely. By utilizing a large dataset of real-life colonoscopies and simulated training on biomechanical models, IRE aims to improve navigation and safety in endoscopic procedures. Through digital twins, simulation, and clinician input, IRE accelerates the design and integration of robotic endoscopes, ultimately enhancing autonomy and adaptability in navigating complex environments to avoid risks and optimize outcomes.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: ARISE - Advanced AI and RobotIcS for autonomous task pErformance
column4: The ARISE project aims to advance automation in crucial industries like renewable energy and agriculture by introducing a novel combination of perception and control modules centered around reconfigurable robotic manipulators. Key components include pneumatic-based manipulators with soft end-effectors, advanced perception, localization, and mapping modules, semantic mapping capabilities, knowledge representation frameworks, hierarchical imitation learning, and human-interaction conditioned path and task planning. Additionally, an edge-AI framework will streamline the deployment of machine learning models and computer vision algorithms. ARISE will be validated in real-world scenarios including installation and repair tasks in solar farms and transplanting and harvesting tasks in hydroponic farms, aiming to address labor shortages and enhance competitiveness in these industries
column8: Twi Ellas Astiki Mi Kerdoskopiki Etaireia
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: How Iceland is using GPT-4 to preserve its language
column4: Iceland partnered with OpenAI to investigate how to improve the Icelandic language abilities of its new model, GPT-4, which was officially announced today. This cooperation results from the visit of the President of Iceland and ministers to the United States in Spring 2022 when, among others, they met Sam Altman, the founder and CEO of OpenAI. One of the key goals of this collaboration was to begin building a resource that could serve to promote the preservation of other low-resource languages, helping to ensure all languages and cultures are represented in digital technologies.
column8: Language Planning Department of the Government of Iceland

column2: Better Reykjavik
column4: Better Reykjavik is a collaborative initiative between the Citizens Foundation, Reykjavik City, and its residents, aimed at fostering connection, trust, and policy improvement within the community. Serving as a platform for crowdsourcing solutions to urban challenges, it incorporates various democratic functions such as agenda setting, participatory budgeting, and policymaking. The platform uses artificial intelligence and machine learning. Unique features include a distinct debating system, crowd-sourcing capabilities, submission of multimedia content, and extensive utilization of artificial intelligence to enhance user experience and improve submitted content.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Generative AI Transformation Project with Derby City Council and ICS.AI
column4: Municipal councils face increasing demand to deliver responsive, high-quality customer services while managing budget constraints and rising case complexity. Derby City Council needed to modernise how residents interact with services, reduce pressure on frontline staff, and improve resolution of more nuanced enquiries across departments. This use case addresses these challenges through a long-term transformation of customer service operations, involving the Council, residents, and the technology partner ICS.AI, with the objective of streamlining service delivery, improving accessibility, and enabling staff to focus on higher-value tasks. The solution is implemented through generative language technologies embedded into existing digital assistants, Darcie and Ali, which are extended to function as conversational copilots across customer service workflows. These systems are trained on council service data, policies, and historical interactions to support natural, context-aware dialogue, case triage, and guided resolution, while integrating with customer relationship management and back-office systems. The initiative improves service consistency, reduces handling times, and enables more complex interactions to be resolved digitally. Over the four-year, £7 million programme launched in 2024, the Council expects improved citizen experience, greater operational efficiency, and scalable digital capability that delivers long-term value for public service transformation.

column2: Elements of AI - free online course designed by the Icelandic government
column4: The Elements of AI is a series of free online courses created by MinnaLearn and the University of Helsinki. They want to encourage as broad a group of people as possible to learn what AI is, what can (and can’t) be done with AI, and how to start creating AI methods.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Auditing a City of Vienna AI solution under IEEE CertifAIEd
column4: Public service providers increasingly deploy automated systems that must align with ethical, legal, and societal values. The City of Vienna sought to ensure that its digital services adhere to principles of transparency, accountability, and human-centric design. This pilot addresses the challenge of systematically assessing automated systems used by a large municipal service provider, Wiener Stadtwerke. Stakeholders include public authorities, service users, regulators, and technology developers. The objective is to validate that deployed systems meet recognised standards for responsible and trustworthy use. The solution applies the IEEE CertifAIEd assessment process to a concrete application, evaluating governance, risk management, transparency, and impact on users. Independent auditors analyse documentation, development processes, and operational practices against established criteria. The impact includes increased organisational awareness of ethical and governance requirements, strengthened trust in public digital services, and international recognition of Vienna’s digital humanism strategy. The pilot provides a reference for other cities seeking structured assurance of responsible technology deployment in public services.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: British Standards Institution: EU AI Act Readiness Assessment and Algorithmic Auditing
column4: In April 2023 it was announced BSI is partnering with Citadel AI, a provider of automated AI testing and monitoring tools. Through Citadel AI’s tools, as part of their assessment process, BSI can measure AI compliance against technical standards, supported by in-depth technical analysis including fairness testing, bias detection and robustness testing. Comprehensive analysis and reporting can also be automatically generated to support the assessment process, assuring the safety and reliability of AI systems
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Z-Inspection – Assessment for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
column4: Governments adopting automated systems face complex ethical, legal, and social considerations that cannot be addressed through technical evaluation alone. The Z-Inspection pilot in the Province of Fryslân addresses the need for holistic assessment methods that support responsible adoption across public administrations. Stakeholders include policymakers, civil servants, citizens, and interdisciplinary experts. The objective is to identify risks, ethical dilemmas, and governance gaps early in the lifecycle of automated systems and to build institutional capacity for responsible use. The solution applies the Z-Inspection methodology, bringing together experts from multiple disciplines to analyse the purpose, design, data use, and societal impact of a concrete system. Structured discussions explore conflicts of interest, accountability, and alignment with public values. The outcomes are documented as lessons learned and shared across government. The impact includes improved understanding of responsible deployment practices, increased digital awareness, and a practical framework for assessing future systems. The pilot supports more confident, transparent, and trustworthy use of advanced technologies in the public sector.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Anotherblock
column4: Music rights are traditionally difficult for creators to monetise directly, with complex royalty chains and intermediaries that can reduce transparency for both artists and fans. Anotherblock addresses this by enabling artists and rights-holders to sell defined portions of track-level rights to their community, creating a clearer route for participation and investment in streaming royalties while maintaining a legally enforceable relationship between parties. Stakeholders include creators, collectors, platforms handling royalty reporting, and regulators seeking clarity on how tokenised intellectual property rights should be structured and supervised, with the objective of making rights transfers simpler, auditable, and fairer. The service is implemented by issuing music ownership tokens on Ethereum using the ERC-721 standard, where each token carries a unique identifier and encodes an ownership percentage for a specific song, alongside associated artwork and a linked real-world legal contract defining royalty terms and ownership guarantees. Transactions and ownership history are recorded on-chain, while off-chain legal documentation and royalty flows are aligned to token holdings through platform governance and reporting integrations. The approach can reduce friction and costs in rights distribution, improve transparency of ownership, and create new, engagement-driven revenue options for artists; participation in the European Blockchain Sandbox is used to test compliance pathways and surface regulatory needs for legal certainty in tokenised IP structures.

column2: Dedit.io – Blockchain-Based Notarisation and Custodial Wallet Platform
column4: Organisations and individuals increasingly rely on digital documents for contractual, legal, and administrative processes, yet traditional notarisation, signing, and document management remain fragmented, slow, and costly. Ensuring legal validity, integrity, and long-term trust in digital documents is a persistent challenge, particularly in cross-border contexts. Dedit.io addresses this need by providing a unified platform for secure document notarisation, signing, storage, and sharing. Stakeholders include legal professionals, businesses, public authorities, and end users requiring trusted digital transactions. The objective is to streamline document workflows while ensuring high levels of legal certainty and regulatory compliance. The solution provides a distributed notarisation and validation service supporting multiple protocols and development environments, combined with a custodial wallet for managing digital assets and documents. Advanced electronic signatures are integrated, with ongoing development towards stronger digital identity and signature authentication mechanisms. The platform is designed for interoperability and scalability, allowing adaptation to diverse legal and market requirements. The impact includes faster document processing, reduced administrative overhead, and improved trust in digital transactions. Participation in the European Blockchain Sandbox supports regulatory alignment and positions Dedit.io as a scalable, compliant solution for end-to-end digital document management.

column2: Chunk Works – Universal Security Layer for Distributed Data Protection
column4: As organisations increasingly operate across hybrid and decentralised infrastructures, ensuring data security, availability, and resilience has become more complex. Conventional storage systems face risks from cyberattacks, data loss, and emerging threats such as quantum computing. Chunk Works addresses this challenge by providing a universal security layer that combines strong encryption with resilient data storage. Stakeholders include enterprises, public bodies, and service providers handling sensitive data across cloud, on-premise, and edge environments. The objective is to ensure privacy, integrity, and continuous availability of data without compromising performance or deployment flexibility. The solution breaks data into encrypted fragments using erasure coding, adds redundancy, and distributes these fragments across multiple locations using distributed ledger technology. Post-quantum hybrid encryption protects data against future cryptographic threats, while autonomous recovery mechanisms reconstruct data even if parts are lost or unavailable. The impact includes increased resilience to failures and attacks, reduced risk of data loss, and future-proof security. Chunk Works delivers a scalable, infrastructure-agnostic solution that strengthens trust in digital data management and supports secure operations in increasingly decentralised environments.

column2: Coinfirm – Blockchain Analytics and Investigations for Regulatory Compliance
column4: The growth of crypto-assets and decentralised finance has created new challenges for combating money laundering, terrorist financing, and financial crime. Regulators and financial institutions require sophisticated tools to understand complex transaction flows and assess risk in blockchain ecosystems. Coinfirm addresses this need by providing advanced analytics and investigation capabilities for digital assets. Stakeholders include banks, virtual asset service providers, payment providers, financial intelligence units, regulators, and decentralised platforms. The objective is to support compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing requirements while enabling responsible innovation. The solution aggregates on-chain, off-chain, and shared intelligence data to build detailed models of asset flows and address behaviour. Advanced risk analytics evaluate transaction patterns, identify high-risk entities, and enable real-time monitoring of address clusters. These insights support source-of-funds analysis, transaction screening, and regulatory reporting. The impact includes improved detection of illicit activity, stronger compliance capabilities, and increased transparency in crypto markets. Coinfirm supports safer adoption of digital assets and contributes to the integrity and stability of the evolving financial ecosystem.

column2: Eonpass: stopping counterfeit goods
column4: Counterfeit goods entering the European Union pose risks to consumer safety, brand integrity, and public revenues, while placing significant strain on customs authorities. Eonpass emerged as a proposed solution following a competition organised by the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO), aiming to improve the detection of fraudulent goods at borders. Stakeholders included EUIPO, customs authorities, logistics providers, and private-sector partners such as EY and Jet Air Service. The objective was to create a shared, trusted mechanism for verifying the authenticity of goods across complex international supply chains. The proposed system used distributed ledger technology to record product identifiers, shipment data, and verification events, enabling customs officials to cross-check goods against tamper-resistant records at the point of entry. Open standards were emphasised to allow interoperability between manufacturers, freight operators, and enforcement agencies. While Eonpass was recognised as the strongest conceptual model in the EUIPO competition, public sources indicate that it remained at proof-of-concept level and was not rolled out as an operational customs system. Nonetheless, the project demonstrated how shared digital ledgers could enhance transparency and trust in border processes, informing later policy and technical discussions on anti-counterfeiting measures.

column2: Hydropower for bitcoin mining
column4: Small hydropower plants often struggle with economic viability during periods of low energy demand, leading to underutilised infrastructure. In northern Italy, municipalities sought alternative revenue models. The objective of this initiative is to improve the economic sustainability of hydropower plants by coupling energy production with high-performance computing demand. The implementation involves colocating data centres at hydropower facilities, where computing capacity is sold to external operators. This model avoids direct municipal involvement in digital asset markets while monetising surplus capacity. Expected impacts include improved financial sustainability of renewable infrastructure and reduced waste. Qualitative outcomes include regional innovation, repurposing of abandoned assets and alignment between renewable energy and digital infrastructure.

column2: Bitcoin and Tether as Legal Tender in Lugano
column4: Seeking to position itself as a European hub for digital finance, the City of Lugano announced a partnership with Tether to promote the use of cryptocurrencies for everyday transactions. The initiative aimed to allow residents and businesses to use Bitcoin, Tether, and a local token (LVGA Points) for taxes, public services, and retail payments. Stakeholders included the city administration, local businesses, residents, and technology providers. The objective was to stimulate innovation, attract investment, and modernise payment infrastructure. The initiative relied on blockchain-based payment rails and digital wallets to enable peer-to-peer and merchant transactions without traditional intermediaries. While the announcement framed cryptocurrencies as “de facto legal tender,” subsequent reporting indicates a more limited implementation focused on voluntary acceptance, pilot use cases, and municipal payments rather than universal adoption across all businesses. The initiative increased Lugano’s international visibility and led to selective uptake among merchants, but it did not replicate the mandatory legal tender model seen elsewhere. As such, it represents a partial implementation that illustrates both the opportunities and practical constraints of integrating crypto-assets into local economies.

column2: Central Bank of Norway CBDC Sandbox
column4: The Central Bank of Norway has hit a major milestone in digital currency efforts, releasing the open-source code for the country’s central bank digital currency (CBDC) sandbox. Available on GitHub, the sandbox is designed to offer an interface for interacting with the test network, enabling functions like minting, burning and transferring ERC-20 tokens, the Norges Bank’s official CBDC partner Nahmii said in a blog post.  Nahmii stressed that the current version of the code does not support the major Ethereum wallet MetaMask by design and is only privately accessible by users with appropriate credentials. In addition to deploying the appropriate smart contracts and access controls, the Norges Bank sandbox includes a custom frontend and network monitoring tools like BlockScout and Grafana. The front end also shows a filterable summary of transactions on the network, Nahmii noted.  The Norges Bank mentioned that Norway’s CBDC prototype infrastructure is based on Ethereum technology. The central bank previously made a reference to Ethereum in a CBDC-related blog post in May. The Norges Bank stated that the Ethereum cryptocurrency system is expected to provide a “core infrastructure” for issuance, distribution and destruction of digital central bank money, which is also referred to as DSP. “The prototype will be used to test a number of the important features for DSP,” the bank said.

column2: Digital Product Passport solution for the Circular Economy
column4: Upcoming EU regulations require manufacturers to provide transparent, verifiable information on product origin, composition and environmental impact, creating significant compliance and data management challenges across supply chains. Compellio, a Luxembourg-based startup, developed a Digital Product Passport (DPP) solution to support circular economy objectives and regulatory readiness under the Ecodesign and Battery Regulations. Stakeholders include manufacturers, regulators, consumers and third-party auditors. The objective is to create a trusted, interoperable mechanism for tracking product lifecycle data while ensuring data integrity and regulatory compliance. The solution uses blockchain-based infrastructure to anchor verified product data and Product Circularity Data Sheets (PCDS) in line with the emerging ISO 59040 standard. Third-party verified data is recorded to ensure traceability and immutability, while off-chain systems manage detailed product information and updates. The platform integrates with enterprise systems and supports selective data disclosure. Selected for the EU Blockchain Regulatory Sandbox, the pilot demonstrates compliance-by-design, improved trust across supply chains and reduced reporting friction. It positions DPPs as a scalable tool for transparency, enabling businesses to meet regulatory demands while supporting greener consumer choices and circular value chains.
column51: Compellio (ID to be added in next month's deliverable)

column2: Digital Bonds on public blockchain
column4:
European Investment Bank (EIB) launched a digital bond issuance on a blockchain platform, deploying this distributed ledger technology for the registration and settlement of digital bonds, in collaboration with Goldman Sachs, Santander and Societe Generale.
In a partnership with Banque de France, the payment of the issue monies from the underwriters to the EIB has been represented on the blockchain in the form of CBDC. The EIB believes that the digitalisation of capital markets may bring benefits to market participants in the coming years, including a reduction of intermediaries and fixed costs, better market transparency through an increased capacity to see trading flows and identity asset owners, as well as a much faster settlement speed.

column2: Survcoin
column4: Urban mobility is a major contributor to carbon emissions, particularly commuting by private car, and local authorities are seeking practical ways to influence behaviour while supporting employee wellbeing. In Differdange, the municipality aimed to reduce emissions generated by daily commuting among its approximately 800 employees and encourage healthier, more sustainable travel choices. This use case addresses these objectives by incentivising the use of soft mobility options such as walking, cycling, and public transport, involving municipal employees and local stakeholders. The solution is implemented through a digital reward system based on an alternative local currency, Survcoin, which is issued to participating employees according to their verified reduction in CO₂ emissions. Mobility data is collected to calculate individual contributions, and rewards are credited in Survcoins that can be used within the local ecosystem. The initiative increases awareness of travel-related emissions, encourages behavioural change, and promotes active mobility. It delivers measurable environmental benefits through reduced car usage, supports employee health and wellbeing, and strengthens local engagement, while providing the city with a scalable and transparent mechanism to link climate goals to everyday mobility choices.

column2: AI for Emergency information system
column4: Emergency departments face increasing pressure due to high patient volumes, time-critical decision-making, and limited medical staff, raising the risk of delayed or missed diagnoses. This project proposal aimed to explore a new emergency information system to support faster and more accurate triage and intervention. Stakeholders included hospitals, emergency responders, healthcare professionals, and regional authorities. The objective was to improve the overall rescue process by enhancing early assessment of patients’ conditions before or during emergency intake. The proposed system would analyse facial expressions and vocal characteristics captured during emergency interactions, combining these signals with contextual information to identify potential indicators of distress or medical urgency. Conceptually, the system would integrate real-time data capture with analytical models and clinical workflows, providing decision-support signals to medical staff rather than automated diagnoses. Despite its inclusion in Interreg Europe’s project ideas, the initiative remained at the exploratory and proposal stage, with no evidence of operational deployment. Nonetheless, it highlighted emerging directions in emergency care innovation and contributed to policy-level discussions on the responsible use of advanced analytics in high-risk healthcare settings.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: ARTIMATION Project: Building trust in air traffic management AI
column4:
Artificial intelligence (AI) increasingly simplifies complex tasks and automates mundane ones entirely. In air traffic management (ATM), a lack of transparency around AI techniques such as neural networks and deep learning has serious consequences for trust and implementation.
The ARTIMATION (Transparent Artificial Intelligence and Automation to Air Traffic Management Systems) project, funded under the SESAR Joint Undertaking to modernise Europe’s ATM system, has developed ‘explainable AI’ (XAI) algorithms that reveal the working of ATM’s increasing range of AI solutions.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI Anonymisator for Estonian public sector
column4:
At the end of 2022, a bureaucrat personal anonymization application was completed, the purpose of which is to train bureaucrat appeals even more securely! The data anonymisator has the ability to identify from the text the name entities of personal data, such as the names, personal identification codes and locations of persons, which the application can replace with another value of the same entity class (for example, Tallinn is replaced by the Tartu entity).
The application is intended to be used both in a bureaucrat and for implementation in applications and information systems of other institutions that are faced with the processing or storage of personal data. The anonymisator helps to mitigate the risk in the processing of personal data and is in accordance with the GDPR regulations. The application has the potential to be used by both anonymization and pseudonymization methods according to the needs of the user. Interfacing clients can use the application on the basis of the NER corpuses developed by the University of Tartu as well as additionally train the corpus with institution-specific datasets.

column2: Automatic real-time subtitles
column4:
Autosubtitles are real-time machine-generated captions to increase the accessibility of current information and discussion for the hard of hearing and to make it easier to follow current live broadcasts. Autosubtitles are created in real time by artificial intelligence and, unlike standard subtitles, are not edited by humans. Therefore, there may be errors and inaccuracies in the subtitles, as well as a delay of a couple of seconds. The ability of the program to create captions is dependent on the clarity of speech, speed, background sounds, etc.
Automatic subtitles can be selected using the TV remote control and can be used by all residents of Estonia whose service provider supports it. The autosubtrite project is based on the Quick Writer solution created at Tallinn University of Technology.

column2: Bureaucrat on eID website
column4: Citizens interacting with public authorities often need to consult multiple institutions, each with its own website and processes, leading to fragmented experiences and repeated enquiries. Estonia addressed this challenge by creating a unified conversational access layer across government websites. This use case involves the Information System Authority, multiple public institutions, and citizens, with the objective of providing a single, intuitive way to access public information. The Bureaucrat operates as an interoperable network of chatbots deployed across authority websites, enabling colloquial, natural communication through a shared chat interface. The system routes queries to the relevant institution and retrieves accurate information from official sources, while maintaining a consistent user experience. Integration across agencies reduces duplication and improves coherence of responses. The solution enhances accessibility of public services, reduces navigation complexity for users, and improves efficiency for institutions by sharing a common conversational infrastructure. It delivers scalable digital government interaction and strengthens trust in public service delivery.

column2: Prototype of the entrepreneur's early warning service
column4: Small and medium-sized enterprises often encounter financial distress too late to take corrective action, leading to insolvency risks with wider economic consequences. Estonia developed a prototype early warning service to help entrepreneurs identify potential liquidity and solvency issues at an early stage. Stakeholders include business owners, public authorities and economic policymakers. The objective is preventive support rather than enforcement, enabling informed decision-making before financial difficulties escalate. The prototype continuously monitors publicly available and submitted financial data, comparing company performance against sector-specific benchmarks and economic principles. When deviations from typical patterns are detected, additional analyses assess trends related to cash flow, obligations and sustainability. If risks persist, the system generates an informational alert to the company. The service is advisory only and carries no legal consequences. The prototype demonstrated feasibility and value as a preventive economic tool, supporting business resilience and reducing the likelihood of sudden failures. Its impact lies in empowering entrepreneurs, improving economic stability and enabling public authorities to offer support earlier, while maintaining a non-intrusive, trust-based approach.
column8: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Bureaucrat of the Environment Agency
column4: The Bureaucrat chatbot operates on the website of the Environmental Board and takes on some of the people's inquiries. A bureaucrat is an interoperable network of chatbots on the websites of public authorities, which allows a person to receive information from institutions through colloquial communication through a chat window. He is an opportunity for a person, that is, a user, to use public direct and information services with colloquial communication with the help of virtual assistants.

column2: Remote snow monitoring
column4: Measuring snow cover at weather stations using machine vision allows saving time and money and collecting data to develop new forecast models.

column2: GAP: preparation of Estonian language tests easy and the assessment automatic.
column4: The preparation of Estonian language tests easy and the assessment automatic. The institue uses natural language technology to generate gap tests in Estonian. Teachers can quickly and easily create gap tests by selecting the appropriate text, determining word types, word forms and word difficulty for practicing or testing learning outcomes, and generating a gap test where words that meet the selected criteria are gaps.

column2: Machine translation customer support communication window on the website of Tartu University Hospital
column4: Healthcare providers increasingly serve diverse populations with varying language needs, making effective communication essential for safety and patient satisfaction. Tartu University Hospital sought to improve accessibility of its online customer support for non-Estonian speakers. This use case involves the hospital, patients, and support staff, with the objective of enabling multilingual communication through digital channels. The solution is implemented as an online customer support window combining live chat, email submission, FAQs, and machine translation powered by Askly. Incoming messages are translated in real time, allowing staff to respond in their own language while patients communicate in theirs. The system integrates with existing support workflows and hospital information systems. The solution improves inclusivity, reduces misunderstandings, and enhances response efficiency. It delivers better patient experience, supports equitable access to healthcare information, and enables scalable multilingual support without proportionally increasing staffing costs.

column2: Politsei.ee large-language model LLM trained on data
column4: Citizens often struggle to find accurate and relevant information on public authority websites, particularly when content is complex or legalistic. The Estonian Police and Border Guard Board tested a pilot to improve information retrieval on politsei.ee by developing a tailored language model trained on official legal texts. Stakeholders include citizens, public service users and government web administrators. The objective was to assess whether customised language models could improve searchability and user interaction in Estonian-language public services. The pilot trained a model on State Gazette data and tested it within the Azure OpenAI environment, using a ChatGPT 3.5-based architecture. The system was evaluated on its ability to answer questions and retrieve relevant information. Results showed that performance in Estonian lagged behind English, highlighting language resource gaps and training data limitations. Despite mixed results, the pilot provided valuable insights into localisation challenges, infrastructure needs and language equity in digital public services, informing future investments in Estonian-language models.
column8: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications

column2: RaRa Bureaucrat
column4: The bureaucrat operates on the website of the National Library of Estonia and takes on some of the people's appeals. A bureaucrat is an interoperable network of chatbots on the websites of public authorities, which allows a person to receive information from institutions through colloquial communication through a chat window. He is an opportunity for a person, that is, a user, to use public direct and information services with colloquial communication with the help of virtual assistants.

column2: HIF EE: Information-based decision support
column4:
In their daily work, healthcare professionals come into contact with a number of patients whose symptoms do not always indicate an unequivocal diagnosis. In this case, for example, the doctor or nurse will have to look for information from several sources, spending unnecessary time trying to find the right diagnosis and treatment for the patient. The Information Management Decision Support provides Estonian healthcare professionals with access to the latest scientific literature and enables them to search for up-to-date information and treatment recommendations among both international and domestic treatment guidelines.
Decision support gives the healthcare professional more time to listen to each patient and find the most suitable solution to their health problem. The service relies on databases of treatment instructions, with the help of which it is possible to quickly find answers to a clinical problem during a visit. Decision support also includes a database of drug interactions, an application for adverse drug reactions, and a database of pregnancy and lactation information.

column2: Computer Vision for monitoring the condition of streets and the city environment
column4: Municipalities require timely information on the condition of streets, waste containers and public infrastructure to maintain cleanliness and service quality. Manual inspections are costly and infrequent. This pilot addressed the problem by developing a visual monitoring system for overflowing waste containers, with potential extension to other urban anomalies. Stakeholders include city service departments and residents. The objective was to improve responsiveness and optimise maintenance operations. A computer vision model based on fine-tuned ResNet-32 architecture was trained on a dataset of 300 images of waste containers collected from streets and online sources. The system detects overflowing containers and visual anomalies, with results displayed on a city map via a React-based frontend using Leaflet. Although limited in scale, the pilot demonstrated improved situational awareness, faster intervention by city services and better user experience for residents. It highlighted scalability potential to potholes, signage and broader urban monitoring, offering a cost-effective approach to smarter city maintenance.

column2: Digital Europe Language Tools - eSummary
column4: Public administrations and organisations handle large volumes of documents, making it time-consuming to identify relevant information quickly. The European Commission developed eSummary to support faster understanding of documents across languages. This use case involves EU institutions, public administrations, and professionals, with the objective of improving efficiency in document analysis. The service is implemented as an online summarisation tool that processes common office and PDF formats and produces concise summaries highlighting key points. By connecting to the EU’s eTranslation service, it supports all EU languages and additional ones. The system applies advanced text analysis techniques to identify salient information and generate structured summaries returned as Word documents. The solution reduces reading time, supports quicker decision-making, and improves accessibility of information across language barriers. It delivers productivity gains, supports multilingual workflows, and provides scalable value for document-intensive public sector environments.

column2: Digital Europe Language Tools - eBriefing
column4:
eBriefing is an AI-based service to assist in drafting official briefings for EU Staff, but also for anyone who needs a general briefing on given topics in a similar format. Users can upload a series of documents on the subject in question and get back an AI-generated briefing in the EU Institutions’ preferred format, that is, with a basic summary, the line-to-take, defensive points, etc.  eBriefing output, like all AI-generated output, needs to be checked for accuracy and style before passing it on. eBriefing delivers a first draft to simplify the process, not a final document. Can I use it in any EU language? eBriefing is connected to eTranslation, so you can submit and receive documents in any EU language along with the others eTranslation supports. How does eBriefing know what to include in its draft?
eBriefing uses the power of the latest generation GPT models, but to get a proper briefing it works with the documents, you, the user, upload. These could be older briefings, general information on a topic, information about the upcoming event for which the briefing is needed, etc. Extensive tests have been carried out to develop a prompt that generates the output in the format usually used at the EU institutions, but with a little tweaking, the output can be adapted to other countries’ conventions or just turned into an informal summary of several documents. Especially when used for official purposes, the output needs to be reviewed and checked. What document types can it handle? eBriefing accepts docx, txt or PDF files. Only PDF files containing text will be processed. If they contain a scanned document, eBriefing cannot process them. It returns a Word document.

column2: Digital Europe Language Tools - Speech Transcription
column4: eTranslation Speech Transcription is a website that makes it easy to get a transcription of video or sound files. It currently uses customized Microsoft Azure speech-to-text engines, but will be adding alternatives as it grows.

column2:
Digital Europe Language Tools - Social Media Translator, Anonymization support,
Classification
column4: The eTranslation Social Media Translator is a website that makes it easy to tweet in the EU languages you need to best connect with your audience. With it you can write a tweet in your preferred language and then have in translated. You can then tweet it directly from the interface using your own registered Twitter account. It uses the eTranslation “General Text” engines, which are better for short and sweet comments. The character limit in the original is slightly less than Twitter’s limit to allow for longer translations, along with a statement that the tweet has been machine translated.

column2: Digital Europe Language Tools - Named-entity recognition
column4:
The eTranslation named-entity recognition website provides a user-friendly interface to extract the named-entities (people, places and specific things) from documents in all common formats. It returns an xml file with tagged entities. 
The eTranslation Classification web site provides a user-friendly interface to classify documents in all common formats. It uses the JEX tool to provide Eurovoc descriptors for documents submitted to it. These are returned in a json format. 
The eTranslation anonymization service can be used to replace or hide identifiable information in documents using either entity replacement or obfuscation (' █ ').
The service itself is a version of the MAPA (Multilingual Anonymization for Public Administrations) project funded by the Connecting Europe Facility, a standalone version of which is available on gitlab.

Can I submit PDF files for processing?
You can, but only PDF files containing text will work. If the text inside the PDF file is a scanned image, we will accept it, but you will get an empty response as, for technical reasons, we do not support OCR (optical character recognition) outside the main machine translation system.

column2: Acquedotto 4.0, Hera invests in artificial intelligence
column4: Water utilities face significant challenges in maintaining ageing distribution networks, where undetected leaks can lead to water loss, infrastructure damage, and costly, disruptive repairs. Hera Group sought to improve monitoring of water networks in several municipalities in Emilia-Romagna while minimising the need for invasive inspections and roadworks. This use case addresses these challenges by enabling early detection of leaks and targeted maintenance, involving the utility operator, municipalities, and residents. The Acquedotto 4.0 pilot is implemented by combining low-cost acoustic sensors with data-driven analysis of water flow sounds to identify patterns associated with leaks. Sensors are deployed across the network to continuously capture data, which is analysed to detect anomalies and localise potential failures. The system integrates with existing maintenance and asset management processes to support timely interventions. The project improves reliability of water supply, reduces non-revenue water, and lowers repair costs by avoiding unnecessary excavation. It also limits traffic disruption and environmental impact, demonstrating how digital monitoring can enhance resilience and efficiency of critical urban infrastructure.
column8: Municipalities of Santarcangelo di Romagna, Riccione and Cattolica
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Carboni Zero: minimizing the carbon footprint of the food sector
column4: Food supply chains face increasing pressure to quantify and reduce carbon emissions, yet fragmented tools and lack of trust hinder consistent measurement and reporting. The Carboni Zero project in Catalonia addresses this gap by creating an integrated system to quantify and certify emissions across food production processes. Stakeholders include agri-food companies, technology providers, researchers and local authorities. The objective is transparency with confidentiality, enabling optimisation without exposing sensitive production details. The solution uses blockchain infrastructure to create a shared standard for emissions data while ensuring immutability and traceability. Zero-knowledge proof techniques allow verification of total emissions without disclosing underlying proprietary data. The platform is designed by the Centre Blockchain de Catalunya (CBCat), with participation from food companies Bonpreu, Noel and Llet Nostra, technology partner SEIDOR, and academic expertise from UVic. The pilot supports process optimisation, trusted certification and regulatory readiness, demonstrating how secure digital infrastructures can align sustainability goals with business confidentiality and scalability.

column2: reWatt: renewable energy standard
column4: Energy self-consumption has grown rapidly in Spain, yet regulatory and market structures have limited the ability of households and communities to exchange surplus renewable energy efficiently. The reWatt Peers project, developed by the Centre Blockchain de Catalunya (CBCat) with the support of Badalona City Council, addresses this gap by enabling the economic exchange of surplus self-consumed energy at both individual and collective levels. Stakeholders include energy prosumers, consumers, energy marketers and local authorities. The objective is to foster local renewable energy communities, increase participation in clean energy generation and improve the economic viability of self-consumption within the framework of Spanish regulation (RD 244/2019). The platform uses distributed ledger technology to record energy surplus transactions in a peer-to-peer format, removing the need for central intermediaries in surplus management. Smart contracts manage allocation and settlement of exchanged energy between participants of the same energy marketer, while metering data provides the basis for accurate accounting. The pilot demonstrates increased transparency, trust and efficiency in surplus energy sharing. Qualitatively, it encourages collaboration, local energy resilience and citizen engagement in the energy transition, while laying the groundwork for scalable community-based renewable energy markets.

column2: Blockchain-Based Gaia-X Data Infrastructure
column4: European organisations face growing challenges in sharing and monetising data across borders while preserving data sovereignty, intellectual property protection and compliance with EU values. Pontus-X, developed by startup deltaDAO AG, addresses these challenges by providing a federated Web3 ecosystem aligned with Gaia-X principles. Stakeholders include companies, research organisations and public bodies across sectors such as mobility, aerospace, healthcare and manufacturing. The objective is to enable trusted, sovereign data and service exchange within the European digital single market. Pontus-X combines a distributed ledger–based transaction and logging layer with smart contracts for sovereign data exchange, NFT-based metadata catalogues and identity services built on self-sovereign identity and verifiable credentials. Privacy-preserving techniques such as Compute-to-Data allow analytics without exposing raw data. The platform integrates regulated digital payments through the EUROe stablecoin for real-time settlement. Selected for the EU Blockchain Regulatory Sandbox, Pontus-X demonstrates scalable, interoperable data marketplaces that reduce legal and technical friction. Its impact includes improved trust in cross-organisational collaboration, new data monetisation opportunities and stronger alignment with European data strategy objectives, supporting competitiveness and innovation while safeguarding sovereignty.

column2: Vidameco for edge data integrity and standardization
column4: Industries such as maritime transport generate large volumes of operational data, yet lack of standardisation and data integrity undermines trust, efficiency and regulatory compliance. The Vidameco research project addresses this problem by providing a unified approach to standardised data collection and tamper-proofing at the edge. Stakeholders include vessel operators, certification bodies, regulators and service providers. The objective is to reduce redundancy, prevent manipulation and simplify compliance with regulations such as the EU’s Monitoring, Reporting and Verification (MRV) framework. Vidameco combines onboard hardware with a microservices-based software architecture to collect, structure and validate data at source. Blockchain technology anchors data fingerprints and stakeholder actions, ensuring traceability and immutability across the value chain. Smart contracts automate repetitive verification and reporting processes, while identity binding reduces errors and fraud. Selected for the European Blockchain Sandbox, the pilot demonstrates reduced administrative burden, faster verification cycles and improved trust in certified data. Its impact extends beyond shipping, offering a reusable model for data integrity and standardisation in other sectors, accelerating digitalisation while maintaining regulatory confidence.

column2: Equilibrium Group: Tokenizing real-world assets
column4: Financial markets and corporate structures are increasingly exploring digital representations of real-world assets, but face legal, regulatory, and operational uncertainty, particularly in the European context. Equilibrium Group aims to enable compliant tokenisation of assets such as fiat currency and shares of unlisted companies, while ensuring interoperability, transparency, and regulatory alignment. This use case involves financial institutions, regulators, and market participants, with the objective of creating efficient, legally robust digital asset infrastructures. The solution is implemented through distributed ledger systems that represent real-world assets as digital tokens, including EUROe, a fully regulated electronic money instrument issued under EU law. Tokenisation frameworks are designed to support payments, asset registries, and future marketplaces, while integrating identity and compliance mechanisms such as advanced digital identity and zero-knowledge proof-based verification. Participation in the European Blockchain Regulatory Sandbox allows regulatory questions around issuance, trading, and rights to be explored in a controlled environment. The initiative delivers efficiency gains through faster issuance and settlement, improved interoperability, and reduced operational costs, while contributing to regulatory clarity and laying the foundation for scalable, compliant digital asset markets in Europe.

column2: Eviden battery passport
column4: The transition to electric mobility requires transparent lifecycle information on batteries to support sustainability, safety, repairability and recycling, as mandated by upcoming EU Battery Regulations. Eviden’s Battery Passport initiative addresses this need by providing a shared digital infrastructure for battery data across the automotive ecosystem. Stakeholders include vehicle manufacturers, battery producers, repair centres, recyclers and regulators. The objective is to uniquely identify batteries, enable controlled data sharing and ensure compliance without exposing commercially sensitive information. Each battery is represented by a unique digital passport, with production, performance and usage data securely linked over time. Blockchain technology ensures integrity, traceability and decentralised governance of records, while cloud-based services allow participants to manage their own data. The platform supports selective access rights, enabling downstream actors to retrieve only relevant information. Participation in the European Blockchain Sandbox validates the regulatory relevance of the approach. The pilot enhances transparency, supports circular economy practices and reduces information asymmetries across the value chain, creating long-term value through improved sustainability reporting, compliance readiness and operational efficiency.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Open Customs Blockchain
column4: International trade and customs processes remain burdened by paper-based documentation, fragmented data exchange and limited transparency, resulting in delays, higher costs and unnecessary inspections. The Open Customs Blockchain initiative, led by the Open Logistics Foundation with major logistics companies and research partners, aims to modernise customs data exchange across borders. Stakeholders include logistics providers, customs authorities, IT vendors and exporters/importers. The objective is to streamline customs procedures, improve traceability and accelerate cross-border trade. The solution stores customs-relevant information, such as invoice data and export accompanying documents, in a tamper-proof distributed ledger accessible to authorised parties throughout the supply chain. By ensuring consistent data provenance and reusability, the platform reduces duplication and errors. Integration with existing logistics systems enables incremental adoption. Selected for the European Blockchain Sandbox, the pilot demonstrates potential reductions in transit times, fewer physical inspections and improved trust between public and private actors. The project supports more sustainable, efficient international trade by replacing manual processes with verifiable digital workflows.

column2: Privacy Layer for Digital Pound
column4: Public trust in a potential UK digital pound depends heavily on assurances around privacy, fraud prevention and proportional oversight. To address these concerns, the Bank of England is collaborating with startup Nuggets to explore a privacy and identity layer for a future central bank digital currency. Stakeholders include central banks, payment providers, consumers and regulators. The objective is to design a system that prevents transaction tracking and correlation by default, while still supporting lawful controls against fraud and money laundering. The proposed architecture uses decentralised identity and privacy-enhancing technologies to separate user identity from transaction data. Cryptographic techniques enable selective disclosure, ensuring that personal data is not centrally stored or routinely accessible. The privacy layer is designed to integrate with potential digital pound core infrastructure rather than replace existing payment systems. Although exploratory, the pilot informs the Bank of England’s ongoing consultation and design choices ahead of any issuance decision post-2025. Its impact lies in demonstrating that strong privacy, regulatory compliance and usability can coexist, shaping public confidence and policy discussions around sovereign digital money.

column2: CAMINS Academic credentials
column4: Catalan public universities face growing demand to issue short, modular learning credentials that are easily verifiable across institutions and borders, while giving students greater control over their academic data. The CAMINS initiative brings together five public universities in Catalonia, coordinated by Rovira i Virgili University, with additional universities and shared service bodies acting as observers. The objective is to create a common, decentralised system for issuing, managing and verifying academic microcredentials that is interoperable at European level and aligned with emerging EU standards. The solution is implemented on the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI), enabling credentials to be issued as verifiable digital records anchored to a distributed ledger. Universities exchange student data securely through a shared blockchain platform developed by Seidor and Validated ID, which also provides digital identity, electronic signatures and verifiable credentials. Students receive credentials in a personal digital wallet linked to a recognised digital identity, allowing selective sharing with employers or other institutions. The pilot improves trust, reduces administrative overhead and enables cross-border recognition of learning achievements. It strengthens student data sovereignty while laying the foundation for scalable, EU-wide academic credential ecosystems.

column2: Historical heritage protection
column4: Córdoba City Council manages extensive historical and artistic heritage assets that are vulnerable to theft, forgery, and loss of provenance information. Existing tools did not allow rapid verification of ownership, location, and authenticity. This use case involves the municipality, museums, cultural authorities, and citizens, with the objective of certifying and protecting heritage assets. The solution is implemented through a digital certification system where heritage records are stored on a blockchain-based platform, combined with physical tags incorporating geolocation and motion sensors. Each asset is digitally registered with provenance, authorship, and condition data, enabling real-time monitoring of up to 2,000 items. The system improves traceability, prevents fraud, and supports rapid alerts in case of theft or unauthorised movement. It enhances protection of cultural heritage, supports data-driven asset management, and delivers long-term value through improved security, transparency, and preservation.

column2: Tokenised participatory loans to finance SMEs
column4: Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) often face limited access to flexible financing instruments, while investors encounter high operational costs and fragmented processes when supporting SME growth. To address this challenge, the Spanish stock market operator BME tested a pilot for issuing participatory loans to SMEs using tokenisation. The objective was to improve efficiency, transparency and legal certainty across the lifecycle of SME financing instruments, while exploring the regulatory boundaries of distributed ledger–based markets. The pilot uses Ethereum-based tokenisation to represent participatory loans as digital assets, with smart contracts governing issuance, distribution, settlement and registration. Blockchain records ensure immutability and auditability of transactions, while automated processes reduce manual reconciliation and settlement delays. Although secondary trading was restricted under existing regulation, the pilot anticipated future frameworks such as the EU DLT Pilot Regime and updates to Spanish securities law. The results demonstrate operational efficiencies across issuance and administration, reduced processing times and lower costs for market participants. The initiative positions tokenised financing as a viable complement to traditional SME funding channels, supporting innovation in European capital markets while informing future regulatory developments.

column2: CircularTrust, waste management system
column4:
Minsait and Ecoembes have opted for blockchain technology to optimize their waste management systems. The objective of the Circular Trust project is to increase the transparency and reduce the costs of the recycling process while minimizing the environmental impact.

All players who participate in one way or another in the recycling process will be able to access the system data and improve information transactions. Thus, public administrations, local entities, operators, recyclers and other organizations will have quality information on real-time monitoring of waste selection, collection and recycling.

Proposals like this are now key in the strategies of companies and public administrations, even more so taking into account that around 2.5 billion tons of waste are generated annually in the EU, which requires adequate management to reduce its impact on the environment. Since CircularTrust began operating in 2020, hundreds of daily transactions have been recorded associated with the removal of waste in more than 90 sorting plants in Spain, as well as the transfer of waste to more than 70 approved recycling facilities, with monitoring. of the entire process in real time. During the first half of 2022, an average of 4,725 withdrawals and around 18,000 monthly transactions have been processed, which have been immutably recorded in the blockchain network.

As this project demonstrates, blockchain offers the possibility of continuing to take steps to improve the recycling process, offering greater control of information thanks to the establishment of smart contracts, the generation of documentary evidence or automation or payments, among others. . In this way, this technology not only results in greater transparency, but also in greater efficiency.

column2: Globallogic (Nuggets) - European cross-border payments
column4: Cross-border payments within Europe remain costly and complex due to fragmented identity verification processes, compliance checks and intermediaries. This use case, led by startup Nuggets (part of GlobalLogic), aims to simplify and secure European cross-border payments by combining decentralised identity with distributed ledger infrastructure. Stakeholders include individuals, businesses, payment providers and public authorities operating across EU jurisdictions. The objective is to enable faster, more transparent and compliant remittances while reducing friction in customer onboarding and regulatory checks. The solution uses Self-Sovereign Decentralised Identity (SSDID), allowing users to verify themselves once and reuse trusted credentials across payment services. Blockchain infrastructure supports transaction recording and validation, ensuring traceability and integrity without centralised data silos. Verifiable credentials are used to demonstrate compliance attributes, such as identity or eligibility, without exposing unnecessary personal data. Selected for the European Blockchain Sandbox, the pilot enables structured dialogue with regulators on identity, payments and compliance. The initiative shows potential to reduce transaction costs, improve user experience and enhance trust in cross-border payments, while aligning with European data protection and digital identity principles.

column2: Internet Native Organizations
column4: Decentralised organisations face legal uncertainty in Europe, limiting their ability to operate transparently and engage with public and private institutions. The Internet Native Organization (INO) initiative, led by non-profit startup INO MTÜ in Estonia, seeks to establish a compliant governance framework for decentralised, code-driven organisations. Stakeholders include digital communities, regulators, developers and public administrations interested in new organisational models. The objective is to enable decentralised governance structures that combine legal recognition, accountability and community participation. INO provides a governance architecture where organisational rules are embedded in code and aligned with national legal systems, particularly Estonia’s advanced e-governance environment. Distributed ledger technology is used to record governance actions, membership decisions and organisational changes in a transparent and auditable manner. Smart contracts formalise decision-making processes, while compliance-focused updates ensure alignment with evolving regulation. Selected for the European Blockchain Regulatory Sandbox, INO supports regulatory experimentation around DAOs. Its impact lies in increasing trust, legal clarity and adoption of decentralised governance models, contributing to Europe’s leadership in digital organisational innovation.

column2: unBlock, the web3 Smartcity SuperApp
column4: Residents and visitors typically navigate multiple disconnected channels to discover services, buy tickets, make bookings, and pay local businesses, which increases friction and limits adoption of digital public services. Murcia’s unBlock aims to provide a single mobile entry point for day-to-day city interactions—such as purchasing event tickets, reserving restaurants or hotels, and paying in participating local commerce—while supporting more seamless authentication for digital access. Stakeholders include the City Council, local businesses and cultural venues, citizens and tourists, and European partners working on digital identity interoperability, with the objective of reducing complexity and improving uptake of city services through one consistent experience. The solution is delivered as a mobile “superapp” connected to participating service providers and payment flows, designed to align with European digital identity initiatives (including wallet-based authentication) and selected for dialogue within the European Blockchain Regulatory Sandbox to explore governance, compliance, and interoperability questions. Conceptually, the platform combines secure user onboarding, a service catalogue, and transaction orchestration, with distributed-ledger components used where auditability or verifiable credentials add value (for example, ticketing integrity and traceable vouchers), while core service fulfilment remains integrated with existing provider systems. The expected benefits include faster access to city services, improved user experience through one-click bookings and ticket display, stronger trust in digital transactions, and a scalable model that can be extended to new municipal services and merchants as adoption grows.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: EU’s Digital Identity Wallet
column4: European citizens and businesses face fragmented digital identity systems that hinder access to cross-border public and private services. The EU Digital Identity Wallet initiative addresses this by creating a unified digital identity solution recognised across Member States. Stakeholders include citizens, businesses, banks, public authorities and service providers across the EU and associated countries. The objective is to enable secure, interoperable and user-controlled digital identity for everyday transactions, from healthcare and education to finance and transport. The EUDI Wallet allows users to store and present identity attributes and credentials securely, using distributed ledger technology to support trust and interoperability between national systems. Biometric verification and cryptographic mechanisms ensure secure authentication, while selective disclosure enables privacy-preserving data sharing. The pilot phase involves more than 250 organisations across 25 Member States and partner countries, testing real-life use cases. The initiative improves user experience, reduces duplication in identity verification and lowers costs for service providers. It represents a foundational step towards a trusted European digital identity ecosystem aligned with EU regulation and values.
column16: Authentication of self-sovereign Digital ID services

column2: Tokenised securities with payments through stablecoins
column4: Capital market transactions remain operationally complex, with fragmented settlement processes and delayed payment cycles. This pilot, involving startups 2Tokens and Assetblocks alongside ABN AMRO and Rabobank, explores tokenised securities combined with settlement through euro-denominated stablecoins. Stakeholders include banks, investors, issuers and regulators. The objective is to test how distributed ledger–based securities and programmable money can streamline issuance, trading and settlement while remaining compliant with EU financial regulation. The solution tokenises securities as regulated digital financial instruments and uses stablecoins or tokenised bank deposits for payment.
