Both hosted and non-hosted wallets are supported, enabling flexible access models. Smart contracts coordinate delivery-versus-payment processes, reducing counterparty risk and settlement delays. Selected for the European Blockchain Regulatory Sandbox, the pilot facilitates structured regulatory dialogue on custody, settlement finality and investor protection. The initiative demonstrates efficiency gains, faster settlement and reduced operational risk, while informing future frameworks for digital capital markets in Europe.

column2: Circularity solution for Digital Product Passports
column4: Manufacturers face increasing regulatory and market pressure to demonstrate product sustainability, traceability and circularity. Startup Twinu addresses this challenge by providing a blockchain-based solution for Digital Product Passports that supports upcoming EU regulation. Stakeholders include manufacturers, consumers, recyclers and regulators. The objective is to enable transparent lifecycle tracking of physical products while improving repairability, reuse and recycling. Twinu assigns each product a digital twin recorded on a blockchain, capturing key lifecycle events from production to end-of-life. This digital passport enables secure data sharing, customer engagement and circular business models without centralised control. The solution aligns with EU proposals for Digital Product Passports and was recognised as “Most Sustainable Solution” by Deutsche Telekom and T-Mobile. Selected for the European Blockchain Sandbox, the pilot demonstrates how distributed ledgers can operationalise circular economy principles. The impact includes improved transparency, stronger customer trust and actionable sustainability insights, supporting both regulatory compliance and long-term environmental value creation.

column2: Public Administrations Interoperability
column4: The project leverages the power of the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI) to build the foundations of the digital infrastructure needed for interoperability among local, regional, and national public administrations. The vision is to create a presentation and verification platform that establishes a Mutual Trust agreement among Regional and Local governments to exchange and recognise different types of verifiable attestations of attributes to improve administrative services to citizens, based on the EU SSI Trust Model supported by EBSI. EBSI allows governments (and other institutions endorsed by them) to issue multiple verifiable attestations of citizens’ attributes under their respective administrative political competence, in which citizens are the authentic owners of the verifiable credential defining the attestation. Citizens need to continue using public services when moving across regions. SSI and Verifiable Credentials include citizens as an active element of interoperability between public administration systems. The project is led ed by a consortium of 5 Spanish Public entities representing +11M citizens throughout Spain.

column2: Educational Identification in Europe
column4:
The aim is to create an architecture for general student identification using Educational IDs that other systems can use for more specific use cases in the educational sector, with the end goal of helping students manage their own student identification.  A Web3 presentation and verification infrastructure for student identification credentials brings together digital wallets (used by students and universities to receive, store, and send credentials on the move), verifiable credentials (for formatting, presenting, and exchanging credential data in a machine-friendly way) and blockchain (for delivering a highly secure, resilient, and decentralised way of verifying credential information). 
The project leverages the power of the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI) to build the foundations of the digital infrastructure needed for a seamless cross-border learning experience. The project unites 2 universities and 4 IT providers from Spain, Romania and Ireland to achieve this.
column8: University of Extremadura and Western University of Arad

column2: Cross-border cross-university micro-credentials in Europe
column4:
A consortium led by 4 universities, 4 IT solutions providers and a research centre - from France, Spain, and Romania are pioneering the future of micro-credentials in Europe by developing a presentation and verification platform using eDiplomas to achieve cross-university education mobility to facilitate the cross-border issuance, recognition, and acceptance of educational digital certificates. A challenge facing the current education system is its tendency to overlook the diverse achievements of learners, particularly when it comes to micro-credentials. Despite learners demonstrating a range of valuable skills and interests by completing courses, programmes or trainings, their accomplishments are often unnoticed. This lack of recognition not only hampers learners' ability to showcase their competencies but also limits their opportunities for admission to relevant programs, especially within the framework of European mobility. Micro-credentials, owned by the learner, provide a unique opportunity for individuals to showcase their skills in a portable and shareable format. Whether as standalone achievements or combined into comprehensive bundles, micro-credentials offer a dynamic representation of an individual's capabilities.  When leveraging EBSI, this opportunity transforms into a powerful catalyst for recognising and valuing individual achievements. It will allow life-long learners to ensure that their educational- and professional-gained skills, represented as micro-credentials, can be shared and recognised across borders and institutions. A team of education ministries and universities across Europe envision this project as a way to reshape the learning environment in Europe and demonstrate how learners can benefit from micro-credentials.

EBSI allows the Micro-credentials project to design, build, and operate the next generation of decentralised services.
column8: Universities of Strasbourg, Deusto, Politehnica University of Timisoara and Lideea

column2: Formal Accreditation and Recognition
column4:
This project is represented by 12 universities and 2 organisations from Spain, Switzerland, and Greece. These organisations are working on the future of formal accreditation in Europe by enabling students with degrees to apply seamlessly to education programmes in the same field but at other national or European universities. Web3 facilitates the seamless issuance, presentation, and verification of diploma and transcript of records credentials without data protection risks, reducing student application hurdles and help European educational institutions strengthen the acceptance and popularity of verifiable credential solutions. Leveraging the power of the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI), the project aims to enhance the foundations of the digital infrastructure needed for a seamless cross-institution learning experience. The vision is to create a presentation and verification platform using eDiplomas to achieve cross-university education mobility. The project unites the Greek Ministry of Education, the Greek University Network (GUNet), a Swiss accreditation authority, and the University of Lausanne to achieve this.

EBSI allows the Formal Accreditation and Recognition project to design, build, and operate the next generation of decentralised services.
column8: Greek Ministry of Education, the Greek University Network (GUNet), and the University of Lausanne

column2: Vocational Education & Training
column4:
A consortium of Government ministries, federal and research institutes, public companies, and universities are pioneering the future of vocational education and training in Europe with EBSI by facilitating the issuance, acceptance, and recognition of all types of vocational educational and training certifications across borders.  Vocational education and training (VET) systems in Europe are not centrally governed but rather rely on a well-developed network of VET stakeholders. The social partners that govern these networks are employers and trade unions, structured as chambers, committees, and councils.

However, the way these social partners communicate and exchange information in Europe still faces challenges at administrative and regulatory levels, especially with the issuance, acceptance, and verification of paper or digital vocational education credentials. This is because vocational education qualifications in Europe lack administrative processes and regulatory implementation - including the use of standardised data models. This gap affects all levels of the process, resulting in a high number of steps, prolonged timeframes, shared and ambiguous responsibilities, or a reliance on intermediary third parties. Web3 allows a streamlined digital verification process for learners' affiliations and credentials without a reliance on intermediaries, helping the Europe wide VET system strengthen the acceptance and popularity of vocational verifiable credentials while upholding user information privacy. This is specifically relevant when employers (verifiers) require professional and specific skills from the applicants: a reliable authentication process for vocational skillsets opens up novel career paths for the European vocational workforce. The project is leveraging the power of the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI), to develop a seamless cross-border vocational education experience.
column8: University of Strasbourg, Chamber of Handicrafts Dresden, RISE, Fraunhofer IFAM and GovPart

column2: Employment credentials
column4: A project led by 4 organisations, one university, and a government ministry is helping to shape the future of cross-border hiring processes in Europe by establishing a verification and authentication system for processing employee professional skills and experience. In the labour market, proving professional experience and qualifications to employers remains a complicated process, especially at the European level. Professionals applying to national or cross-border work often have to provide proof of professional skill or experience via physical (paper) documents, digitally signed PDFs or self-written CVs. HR departments can either trust the authenticity of the documents or dedicate time and effort to phone back previous employers for verification. Even in the best case, there is no method to authenticate if the trusted document is really linked to the said individual. This means current processes are time-consuming and fraud-susceptible.
column8: Ministerie van Justitie en Veiligheid, University of Strasbourg

column2: Development of in Silico Methodologies to Predict the Toxicity of Proteins in Food and Feed Risk Assessment
column4: Assessing the safety of novel proteins in food and feed is complex, time-consuming and often reliant on experimental testing. Regulatory authorities seek faster, reliable methods to support risk assessment. The objective of this initiative is to identify computational methodologies capable of predicting protein toxicity and associated mechanisms. The implementation explores sequence-based, structural and machine-learning approaches to identify homology with known toxic proteins and detect molecular signatures linked to toxicity. The work focuses on methodology identification and validation criteria rather than operational deployment. Expected impacts include improved efficiency of risk assessment, reduced reliance on animal testing and stronger scientific consistency. Qualitative benefits include enhanced regulatory decision support and scalable foundations for future safety assessment tools.

column2: AICEBlock
column4: The proposal is about the development of a blockchain-based platform that allows fostering trust in basic applications in Artificial Intelligence through its certification. This platform can be used to support any type of AI-based application, whether at the training or production level. However, its need is more evident in critical areas such as Health or high safety requirements. In addition, in cases of collaborative development, it also gains special relevance – such as in Public Administration, for example. These areas have sensitive and inflexible requirements. By providing a new AI product with a seal that ensures it can be audited, with their individual decisions tracked, while making it easier to comply with standards such as GDPR, or the inclusion of layers of interpretability, AICeBlock increases users' trust in the product.

column2: AI and ML for the Galician Public Employment Service
column4: Service for the design, development and development of a decision-making information and support system with the application of artificial intelligence and machine learning for the Galician Public Employment Service and the integration of visualisation of the new uses in the applications of this service for the Galician Public Employment Service. REACT-EU funded procurement under the ESF Operational Prog Galicia 2014-2020, as part of the EU’s response to COVID-19.
column8: Contracting, Projects and Works Service Dept of Regional Government

column2: SMARTECOMOUNTAINS Project in Andalusia
column4:
IT services: consulting, software development, Internet and support. Deep learning machine and artificial intelligence for LifeWatch ERIC. Deep learning machine and artificial intelligence for LifeWatch. Set out in the specification Andalusia ERDF projects, Operational Framework 2014-2020 POPE, in particular: or SMARTECOMOUNTAINS Project, Ref.: LIFEWATCH-2019-10-UGR-01
Smart EcoMountains is an institutional project of the University of Granada associated with the European Research Infrastructure LifeWatch-ERIC to develop a Thematic Center on Biodiversity, Global Change and Sustainable Development in mountains with international projection, using as a case study the mountain of Sierra Nevada (Southeast of the Iberian Peninsula).
The mission of Smart Ecomountains is: Generate information on biodiversity, ecosystem services and global change in mountain ecosystems, applying a Data Management Plan that ensures that all information collected is reliable, traceable, accessible, interoperable and reusable.
To develop new technological tools and services that facilitate the exchange, localization, access and analysis of data by scientists in order to improve our knowledge of mountain ecosystems and the main global change processes that affect them.
To create educational content and management tools that inform society about the most important global change processes affecting biodiversity and ecosystem services in mountains, supporting managers, decision-makers and citizens in making science-based decisions.

column2: AI4AGRIFOOD platform in Flanders - Precision agriculture and data technology
column4:
With the “AI Platform for Agrofood” project, ILVO wants to simplify and democratise the use of artificial intelligence and data science within the daily job of ILVO researchers. This through purchasing a platform, service or technical solution that allows users with machine learning and programming knowledge as well as without much knowledge to apply machine learning in their research. The challenge lies in providing a user-friendly AI platform, as well as the collaboration and knowledge sharing of researchers with different levels of experience. In this project the focus is on the platform, service or technical solution. Things such as structuring, uniformising and providing pre-existing data, change management and purchase of hardware are outside the focus of this project.
The outcome of the market consultation should enable ILVO to develop, test or purchase an appropriate (innovative) solution for the desired need through a public contract.
The private conversations allow to further explain any technologies, solutions or relevant projects to the project team. No pitch or presentation should be prepared for these conversations as the conversations will be based on questions by the project team.
Who are we looking for as a target audience: for the ‘AI Platform for Agrifood’ project, together with the Institute for Agricultural, Fisheries and Food Research and Addestino Innovation Management, companies, consultants and organisations with knowledge and experience in Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning and platform development, we invite companies, consultants and organisations to participate in a market consultation.
Participants with specific competencies:
— experts in integrating platforms around artificial intelligence, machine learning (especially with low-code environments), knowledge management or knowledge sharing,
— experts in AI and ML,
— experts in platform development (with GUI such as a drag-and-drop interface).
column8: Flanders Research Institute for Agriculture, Fisheries and Food (ILVO)

column2: Artificial intelligence in operational forest management planning
column4: Managing state-owned forests requires balancing economic use, environmental protection, and social value, while operating across large and diverse territories. Metsähallitus needed more precise data to support operational planning and sustainable timber harvesting. This use case involves the Finnish state forestry organisation, planners, and technology providers, with the objective of improving decision-making in forest management. The solution is implemented through advanced data-driven models that generate assortment-level timber yield predictions, which are integrated into operational planning systems. These predictions complement existing forestry data and enable more accurate planning of harvesting, logistics, and conservation activities. The solution improves planning accuracy, supports sustainability goals, and reduces uncertainty in operational decisions. It enhances efficiency, optimises resource use, and delivers long-term value by supporting responsible management of public land and natural resources.

column2: Lille Metropole Smart Sensors
column4: Researching, developing, prototyping, and acquiring smart sensors to qualify public space. The services are divided into two phases: research and development, followed by the acquisition of prototypes. The framework agreement will be implemented through the issuance of purchase orders. Implementations has been made in Lille with the support of the VizioSense startup that developed for the city of Lille and intelligent parking technology sector by integrating advanced artificial intelligence features into its sensors. VizioPark's AI-equipped sensors enable optimized management of parking spaces by providing real-time information on availability and facilitating navigation for drivers

column2: Imatra Street AI project
column4:
StreetAI Innovation Partnership: Cloud-based Platform and Digital User Interface. Developement a cloud-based environment for data storage, analysis, and interfaces managed by cities.  The main goals of the Street AI project are to make information easily understandable and accessible. In order to achieve this, the project creates a real-time view of the current situation of the streets and tests and implements sensors, sensors and artificial intelligence solutions for data collection, analysis and presentation.
The goal is to implement a web-based user interface for real-time street situation presentation and decision-making support. Augmented reality solutions for street maintenance will also be tested. The developed solutions is used to test sensor and information service solutions.  The project supports everyday decision-making, improves the service level of cities and creates significant savings.Real information regarding, for example, traffic volumes, snow cover and ruts on the streets helps target maintenance activities even more efficiently, improves the flow of traffic and reduces the load on the environment.

column2: Robotics and artificial intelligence solutions for Helsinki City Central Library
column4: Robotics and artificial intelligence solutions for Helsinki City Central Library

column2: Central Bohemian Region to test on-demand public transport
column4: Establishing an innovation partnership to design and deliver a system supporting the optimization of the settings of the transport service system in public transport. The system will use multi-source data aggregation, including big data and access for processing.  The partner will develop the system based on the minimum technical conditions specified by the contracting authority.

column2: City of Herne road maintance with AI
column4: The city of Herne in western Germany, in collaboration with Edgital, a subsidiary of contractor Hochtief, has developed a digital solution for maintaining its 380km road network. The initiative utilises artificial intelligence to analyse road conditions and ensure efficient maintenance and rehabilitation. Initially, a database will be integrated into a Building Information Modeling (BIM) system to enable proactive maintenance management. In the future, BIM models enriched with road condition data will contribute to a digital twin of the road infrastructure, enhancing maintenance efficiency. Hochtief manager Bernd Holtwick highlights that the AI-driven solution reduces data costs and improves decision-making, ultimately providing safer roads. Herne's Mayor Frank Dudda emphasises the importance of timely identification of road issues and the integration of diverse data sources for effective planning. The partnership aims to tackle the challenges of road maintenance innovatively.

column2: City of Prague AI Traffic Management System
column4: Creation of a traffic management system using multi-agent and adaptive systems with elements of artificial intelligence. The system aims to manage traffic based on real-time vehicle numbers, traffic intensity, and directionality at transport hubs. It will assess the traffic situation in the entire area and apply a real-time signaling plan for smooth traffic flow. Public transport preference for tram transport will be maintained within the management.

column2: Artificial Intelligence-Enhanced Electricity Transmission Network Planning
AI Platform for to support primary healthcare in Regione Puglia
column4: Detailed design, implementation, commissioning and management of an artificial intelligence platform to support primary healthcare.

column2: Virtual assistant on the eportugal portal for information about the Digital Mobile Key (CMD)
column4: The virtual assistant for information about the Digital Mobile Key (CMD) answers questions about activating, unlocking, changing data, canceling or using the CMD digital signature, among others.

column2: Automated evaluations of applications at NAV
column4: NAV processes a very large number of benefit applications each year, and manual handling of all cases can lead to delays and inconsistent outcomes. This use case addresses the need to improve efficiency while ensuring decisions comply with legislation. It involves NAV, applicants, and public authorities, with the objective of delivering faster and more consistent benefit decisions. The solution is implemented through automated decision systems that check applications against legal criteria for benefits such as child benefit, unemployment benefit, pensions, and parental benefits. The system verifies eligibility, determines benefit levels and duration, and generates formal decisions without human intervention for eligible cases. Integrated with NAV’s case management systems, it ensures transparency and traceability. The solution significantly reduces processing times, frees staff to focus on complex cases, and improves service quality for citizens. It delivers scalable efficiency gains, legal consistency, and sustainable public administration value.

column2: New AI-Powered Cameras Spot Distracted Drivers in the UK
column4: The new type of technology captures footage of passing motorists. The images are processed using artificial intelligence (AI) to analyse whether the motorists could be using a handheld mobile phone or drivers may be without a seat belt. The images are then passed to police for consideration on any action to be taken. Drivers can be fined up to £500 for not wearing a seatbelt in addition to penalty points. While using a mobile phone while driving can result in a fine of up to £1,000 and six penalty points.

column2: AI camera system catches litter louts in Loughborough
column4: A camera that uses artificial intelligence (AI) to spot litter louts led to a reduction in rubbish being dumped on a busy Leicestershire road.The technology was deployed on the town's busy Warwick Way at the end of last year. It was designed to work alongside automatic number plate recognition. As a result of the trial, a total of 28 £150 fines were issued. During this period, there was a 37% decrease in the amount of litter dropped in that area, and 28 fines have been issued and paid or in the process of payment, appeal, or debt collection. Even though the trial has been a success, there are no immediate plans to permanently install litter cams, but further implementation could happen in the future.

column2: NHS AI test spots tiny cancers missed by doctors
column4: An AI tool tested by the NHS successfully identified tiny signs of breast cancer in 11 women which had been missed by human doctors. The tool, called Mia, was piloted alongside NHS clinicians and analysed the mammograms of over 10,000 women. Most of them were cancer-free, but it successfully flagged all of those with symptoms, as well as an extra 11 the doctors did not identify.

column2: French police test AI-powered security cameras ahead of Olympics
column4: France tested Artificial Intelligence-driven video surveillance technology that will be deployed during the Olympic Games, opens new tab at a Depeche Mode concert, calling the exercise a success. French legislation passed in 2023 permits the use of AI video surveillance for a trial period covering the Games to detect abnormal events or human behaviour at large-scale events. The law allows for eight different "events" to be flagged by AI surveillance software during the Games that include: crowd surges; abnormally heavy crowds; abandoned objects; presence or use of weapons; a person on the ground; a fire breaking out; contravention of rules on traffic direction. France's Interior Ministry has created an evaluation committee to keep tabs on civil liberties throughout the trial period.

column2: Dublin City Council and OpenAI Announce Partnership to Show the Potential of AI to Support Europe’s Tourism Industry
column4: Co-development of a Dublin Itinerary Planner Proof of Concept “A Day in Dublin”: Leveraging the advanced capabilities of GPT-4, this innovative tool will showcase how bespoke travel recommendations can make Dublin's rich cultural heritage and vibrant city life more accessible to visitors. This prototype has been designed to show the potential of generative AI to support unique, bespoke experiences for visitors instead of generic to-do lists.

column2: OrQA - Organ Quality Assessment - the technology scans images of organs to look at suitability for kidney and liver transplant
column4: Known as OrQA - Organ Quality Assessment - the technology scans images of organs to look at suitability for kidney and liver transplants. The tool was created by the University of Bradford and Newcastle Upon Tyne Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust.

column2: ChatGPT-powered GOV.UK chatbot on trial
column4: The Government Digital Service has pledged to take a “measured” approach to its work on a proposed GOV.UK chatbot, after public trials of the tool – powered by the technology behind ChatGPT – showed promise but shed light on “issues of accuracy and reliability”. A survey of their experiences found that about 65% of participants were satisfied overall, while 70% said they received useful answers from the technology – which, using large language model (LLM) tech from ChatGPT creator OpenAI – is designed to scout content on GOV.UK and use it provide “a human-like response” to users’ questions.

column2: Barcelona tests with a digital twin if it is a 15-minute city
column4: This is a web platform for analysing the accessibility of public facilities that makes it possible to assess if Barcelona complies with the provision of services or facilities of the so-called 15-minute city model.

column2: UrbanistAI, a generative AI platform for participatory planning and co-design: Helsinki use case
column4: The City of Helsinki organised two events with UrbanistAI with citizens and entrepreneurs to study what functions they would prefer to have in the city areas in question. Generative AI was used to produce numerous alternative visions. The city officials and consultants acted as moderators for the workshop.

column2: City of Bologna digital twin
column4: The City of Bologna is addressing complex urban challenges related to sustainable mobility, energy efficiency, and climate change mitigation, which require coordinated planning across multiple policy domains. City planners, utilities, transport authorities, and citizens all need reliable evidence to support long-term infrastructure investments and behavioural change. The objective of the digital twin initiative is to provide an integrated decision-support environment that allows the city to analyse current conditions and simulate future scenarios before implementing policies or investments. The solution is implemented through a digital replica of the city that integrates data from sensors, administrative systems, and qualitative social data. In mobility, the model combines traffic counts, environmental sensors, and citizen feedback to assess the impact of cycling lanes and tram infrastructure. In the energy domain, it simulates building energy performance to evaluate renovation strategies, incentives, and urban plans aimed at achieving net-zero targets. The digital twin enables scenario testing and cross-sector analysis within a shared platform. As a result, Bologna can design more coherent policies, reduce planning risks, and prioritise investments with higher sustainability impact. The initiative improves transparency, supports data-driven governance, and strengthens the city’s capacity to plan resilient and climate-neutral urban development.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Roboat - an autonomous fleet for Amsterdam
column4: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and the AMS Institute will collaborate to solve complex urban problems for Amsterdam with the development of autonomous “roboats”. MIT has signed an agreement to engage in research collaborations with the Amsterdam Institute for Advanced Metropolitan Solutions (AMS) in the Netherlands. The collaboration’s flagship project, led by researchers from multiple departments at MIT, will be to develop a fleet of autonomous boats for the city’s canals.
column8: Amsterdam Institute for Advanced Metropolitan Solutions (AMS)

column2: Digital twin of the City of Pilsen
column4: The City of Pilsen is developing and implementing the Smart City concept in different areas including mobility, security, business support, environment, ICT and public participation. The digital twin goal is to interrelate transport and noise pollution in a 3D environment in order to support decision making to improve mobility, sustainability and livability of urban areas.

column2: Albania to speed up EU accession using ChatGPT
column4: The aim of the project led by the Albanian government, overseen by Prime Minister Rama, is to utilize ChatGPT, an AI language model developed by Mira Murati and her team at OpenAI, to translate and integrate EU legal measures and provisions into Albanian. This initiative seeks to expedite paperwork processes and facilitate quicker accession to the EU for Albania. By leveraging AI technology, the project aims to streamline the alignment of local legislation with EU rules, eliminating the need for extensive manual translation and legal analysis, thus speeding up the process of EU integration.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: SOCIAbLE - Social Cognitive Interventions following Adverse early Life Experiences
column4: The main aim of the project is to develop a novel psychosocial intervention called SOCIAbLE, targeting the cognitive difficulties associated with childhood trauma. The key people involved include researchers working on the project to establish the feasibility and benefits of this VR-based intervention for individuals with a history of childhood trauma, with the goal of informing future definitive clinical trials.

column2: PERCEIVE - Perceptive Enhanced Realities of Colored collEctions through AI and Virtual Experiences
column4: Cultural heritage institutions face growing interest in digitally preserving and presenting ancient artefacts while addressing gaps in historical understanding, particularly around the original appearance of classical sculptures. Many Greek and Roman statues were originally painted, but their polychromy has been lost over time, limiting public understanding and scholarly interpretation. This use case addresses these challenges by enabling accurate study, reconstruction, and exhibition of coloured cultural heritage, involving researchers, museums, educators, and creative industries. The PERCEIVE project is implemented through advanced image analysis, digital reconstruction techniques, and immersive visualisation technologies that analyse traces of pigment and material data to recreate original colours. These reconstructions are rendered through extended reality and hybrid exhibition formats, supported by a service-based digital architecture and dedicated design toolkits for education and creative reuse. The project improves the accuracy and accessibility of cultural heritage interpretation, enriches visitor experience through immersive storytelling, and supports new educational and creative applications. It delivers lasting value by combining scientific research with engaging digital experiences, strengthening preservation, knowledge transfer, and public appreciation of Europe’s cultural heritage.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: TIMELAPSE - A VR application for speeding up time passage during chemotherapy
column4: The main aim of the TIMELAPSE project, stemming from the ERC Advanced Grant AN-ICON, is to prototype, test, and launch a virtual reality (VR) application that accelerates subjective time passage during chemotherapy, enhancing the mental well-being of oncologic patients. Key people involved include the core research group from AN-ICON, industrial partner Khora specializing in VR applications, and clinical partner Fondazione IRCCS San Gerardo dei Tintori with expertise in cancer treatment.

column2: BRAINmade - Against prostheses abandonment: immersive and low-cost platform for the rehabilitation of upper-limb amputees
column4: The ERC-funded BRAINmade project addresses the high abandonment rate of upper-limb prostheses (up to 44%) by introducing a novel approach. Utilizing virtual reality scenarios controlled by electromyographic (EMG) activity, the four-week motor training, even with non-anthropomorphic prosthetics, shows promising results. The project integrates fully-customized, low-cost EMG sensors, aiming to reduce prosthesis abandonment and positively impact amputees' lives and rehabilitation practices.
column8: Università degli Studi di Roma La Spaienza

column2: KATSIM - Cataract Simulator Diagnostics Device Development Using Computational Holographic Displays
column4: The main aim of the EU-funded KATSIM project is to develop a computational holography approach for simulating vision in patients with cataract, allowing clinicians to predict surgical outcomes and choose the most suitable replacement lens for individual patients. Key people involved in the project include experts in computational holography, vision simulation, and cataract surgery.

column2: AI-OBSERVER - Enhancing Earth Observation capabilities of the Eratosthenes Centre of Excellence on Disaster Risk Reduction through Artificial Intelligence
column4: The EU-funded AI-OBSERVER project aims to enhance the Earth observation capabilities of the ERATOSTHENES Centre of Excellence (ECoE) by integrating artificial intelligence (AI) for multi-hazard monitoring and assessment in Cyprus. This collaboration involves twinning with the German Research Centre for Artificial Intelligence and the University of Rome Tor Vergata, with commercialization activities led by CELLOCK Ltd, a Cypriot SME. The project aims to elevate scientific excellence, innovation capacity, and research management skills at ECoE. AI-OBSERVER project aims to significantly strengthen and stimulate the scientific excellence and innovation capacity, as well as the research management and administrative skills of the ECoE, through several capacity building activities on AI for EO applications in the Disaster Risk Reduction thematic area, upgrading and modernising its existing department of Resilient Society, as well as its research management and administration departments, and assisting the ECoE to reach its long-term objective of raised excellence on AI for EO on environmental hazards.

column2: RadioVal - International Clinical Validation of Radiomics Artificial Intelligence for Breast Cancer Treatment Planning
column4: Radiomics has the potential to support more personalised cancer treatment, but its clinical adoption is limited by the lack of large-scale validation, consistency across centres, and evidence of real-world usability. In breast cancer care, clinicians need reliable tools to predict how patients will respond to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in order to avoid unnecessary treatment and optimise outcomes. The RadioVal project addresses this challenge by bringing together hospitals, clinicians, researchers, and technology developers across eight clinical sites in Europe and beyond, with the objective of validating radiomics-based decision support in routine breast cancer treatment planning. The project implements advanced radiomics pipelines that extract quantitative features from medical imaging data, combined with machine learning models trained to predict treatment response. These tools are evaluated using harmonised imaging datasets and clinical data, and assessed across multiple dimensions, including technical robustness, algorithmic fairness, clinical usability, and acceptance by healthcare professionals, with integration into existing clinical workflows. The project strengthens confidence in data-driven imaging tools, supports more precise treatment decisions, and aims to reduce over-treatment while improving patient outcomes. By providing robust clinical validation and usability evidence, RadioVal contributes to increased trust, scalability, and responsible adoption of advanced imaging analytics in oncology.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Optimize risk prediction after myocardial infarction through artificial intelligence and multidimensional evaluation
column4: This project aims to enhance risk prediction in myocardial infarction (MI) surgery by integrating innovative data from wearable devices, biomarkers, and non-invasive imaging through artificial intelligence. Enrolling a large cohort of MI patients, the study will collect comprehensive data to create a multidimensional dataset, leveraging AI computation to identify novel "computational biomarkers" for risk. The goal is to improve current risk prediction standards, aiding clinicians and patients in informed treatment decisions.
column8: Fundacion para la Investigacion de Malaga en Biomedicina y salud

column2: HYBRIDS - Hybrid Intelligence to monitor, promote and analyse transformations in good democracy practices
column4: The Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions project HYBRIDS addresses the pervasive issues of fake news, disinformation, and hate speech online, which contribute to societal tensions and extremism. With a focus on countering this digital phenomenon, HYBRIDS integrates social and human sciences knowledge into natural language processing tools and deep learning algorithms. The project aims to develop hybrid intelligence systems that combine machine and human intelligence, addressing the limitations of current artificial intelligence methods and providing researchers with effective tools to combat disinformation.

column2: TITAN - AI for Citizen Intelligent Coaching against Disinformation
column4: The EU-funded TITAN project aims to combat disinformation by developing an engaging ecosystem that empowers citizens to question, investigate, and understand the truthfulness of statements through an AI-driven, intuitive, and personalised 'question-and-response' interaction system, focusing on logical interpretation and critical assessment, with personalized intelligent coaching schemes

column2: TOLIFE - Combining Artificial Intelligence and smart sensing TOward better management and improved quality of LIFE in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
column4: The EU-funded TOLIFE project aims to improve personalized treatment for Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) by developing an AI solution using unobtrusive sensors to predict exacerbations, assess health outcomes, and enable clinicians to offer early, personalized interventions, enhancing the quality of life for patients.

column2: ARISTOTELES - Applying ARtificial Intelligence to Define clinical trajectorieS for personalized predicTiOn and early deTEctiOn of comorbidiTy and muLtimorbidiTy pattErnS
column4: Patients with atrial fibrillation and other complex conditions often experience fragmented care due to coexisting diseases, delayed diagnosis, and lack of personalised risk assessment, leading to avoidable hospitalisations and increased mortality. Healthcare systems require better tools to manage comorbidities holistically and support timely clinical interventions. The ARISTOTELES project addresses these challenges by involving hospitals, clinicians, researchers, and patients across multiple countries to improve the management of atrial fibrillation and multimorbidity. Its objective is to support personalised prediction, early detection, and coordinated care pathways that improve outcomes and patient adherence. The project establishes a multinational health data platform that integrates heterogeneous patient data, including clinical records, diagnostics, and longitudinal health information. Advanced analytical models are developed to identify disease trajectories, predict risks, and support personalised treatment strategies in an ethical and privacy-aware manner, with a focus on integration into clinical practice. The approach enables earlier diagnosis, better treatment targeting, and improved coordination of care. It aims to reduce disease progression, hospital admissions, and mortality, while enhancing patient engagement and adherence. ARISTOTELES delivers long-term value by supporting more proactive, efficient, and patient-centred management of complex chronic diseases.

column2: MELISSA - Mobile Artificial Intelligence Solution for Diabetes Adaptive Care
column4: The MELISSA project aims to deliver a clinically validated, cost-efficient AI-based digital diabetes management solution, independent of glucose monitoring devices, incorporating advanced AI approaches, reinforcement learning for insulin adjustments, and tools for assessing complications, to support both healthcare providers and insulin-treated patients with personalized treatment and care recommendations.

column2: ARTILLERY - Artificial Intelligence for early detection of non-communicable disease risk in people with breast cancer
column4: The ultimate goal of the EU-funded ARTILLERY consortium is to improve the long-term health outcome of breast cancer survivors via optimal use of routine imaging for the detection of chronic conditions. The objective is to develop AI systems for accurate risk prediction of common chronic conditions: cardiovascular disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, unfavourable body composition, and osteoporosis. These AI systems will provide healthcare professionals with quantitative parameters of patients’ chronic disease risks, leading to prompt treatment and risk-reducing strategies.

column2: CINDERELLA - Clinical Validation of an AI-based approach to improve the shared decision-making process and outcomes in Breast Cancer Patients proposed for Locoregional treatment
column4: Treatment of breast cancer usually involves surgery that varies depending on the individual tumour, the size and shape of the breasts or personal wishes. The EU-funded CINDERELLA project proposes to create an easy-to-use application based on AI that uses a clinical database and performs picture and biometric matching to create images that simulate to the patient the outcome of surgery. The platform called CINDERELLA APProach will reinforce the role of the patient in clinical decision-making.

column2: AI4AGRI - Romanian Excellence Center on Artificial Intelligence in Earth Observation Data for Agriculture
column4: Romania has significant agricultural potential and could play a significant role in the strategic autonomy, food security and sustainability of Europe. However, agricultural capacity is underexploited due to lack of technology and funds, soil fragmentation and erosion, and desertification. AI-based systems and applications, fostering Earth observation (EO) data (mainly the freely available data from the Copernicus EU programme), offer incredible possibilities. The EU-funded AI4AGRI project will create a dedicated research centre for AI on EO data for the agricultural sector by twinning with two research institutes in AI and EO from France and Italy. The research centre will provide training to young scientists and maps of vegetation status for farmers, and develop administrative and management skills for R&I.

column2: APOLLO - A Proactive Approach to the Recovery and Recycling of Photovoltaic Modules
column4: The growing volume of end-of-life photovoltaic modules presents a sustainability challenge, as current recycling processes recover limited materials and fail to support a truly circular solar economy. To meet environmental and economic goals, the photovoltaic sector needs better traceability, higher-quality material recovery, and stronger links between production, reuse, and recycling. The APOLLO project addresses these challenges by involving manufacturers, recyclers, technology providers, and market actors to establish a circular framework for photovoltaic modules. Its objective is to maximise material recovery, enable reuse and remanufacturing, and reduce environmental impact across the full product lifecycle. The solution is implemented through blockchain-based Digital Product Passports that store secure, trustworthy information on materials, composition, hazards, and lifecycle history of photovoltaic modules. These passports support automated access to data needed for recycling and reuse and are linked to an online marketplace for recovered and refurbished components. The approach improves transparency, traceability, and coordination across the value chain, increases the economic viability of recycling, and supports regulatory compliance. APOLLO enables higher-value material recovery, reduces waste, and creates scalable foundations for a circular photovoltaic industry.

column2: Swarmchestrate - Application-level Swarm-based Orchestration Across the Cloud-to-Edge Continuum
column4: Collecting and analysing large amounts of data in the Cloud-to-Edge computing continuum raises novel challenges. Processing all this data centrally in cloud data centres is not feasible anymore as transferring large amounts of data to the cloud is time-consuming, expensive, degrade performance and may raise security concerns. Therefore, novel distributed computing paradigms, such as edge and fog computing emerged to support processing data closer to its origin. Application microservices are managed in a dynamic Orchestration Space by decentralised Orchestration Agents, governed by distributed intelligence that provides matchmaking between application requirements and resources, and supports the dynamic self-organisation of Swarms. Knowledge and trust, essential for the operation of the Orchestration Space, will be managed through blockchain-based trusted solutions using methods of Self-Sovereign Identities (SSI) and Distributed Identifiers (DID). End-to-end security of the overall system will be assured by utilising state-of-the-art cryptographic algorithms and privacy preserving data analytics.

column2: Delivery of digital entitlements via Digital Identity for Syrian Refugees in Lebanon
column4: This project saw AID:Tech deliver Digital Identity and subsequently, international aid to Syrian refugees in Tripoli, Lebanon in December 2015. Using  Blockchain technology, AID:Tech worked alongside the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) and delivered identity in the form of 500 smart cards to 100 Syrian families. Each smart card also contained records of aid entitlement, which beneficiaries were able to redeem via our local merchant partners. AID:Tech was also able to provide transparency of the overall journey via a dedicated platform for our agency partner, IFRC.

column2: BUILDCHAIN - BUILDing knowledge book in the blockCHAIN distributed ledger
column4: Several EU directives address sustainability, resilience and energy efficiency in the building sector. Thus, it is necessary to provide a marketplace where various actors can share their offers, including their quality certificates and credentials, and where it would be possible to trace all activities related to the overall life cycle of buildings. The EU-funded BUILDCHAIN project will develop the Digital Building LogBook (DBL) to be used by municipalities to manage and administer their set of buildings. The DBL will integrate existing and new data, tools and functionalities by employing the Decentralised Knowledge Graph (DKG) open-source blockchain-based solution. The software will include specific building-related ontologies tracing and continuously updating the life cycle of buildings.

column2: OPENVERSE - OPEN and co-created metaVERSe for Europe
column4: Europe is advocating for the development of open, inclusive and ethical virtual worlds that emphasise the technological sovereignty of the EU industry. This vision aims to deliver benefits such as enhanced accessibility, increased freedom and control, reduced entry barriers and a more equitable distribution of resources. Furthermore, it places a premium on interoperability, privacy, security and data ownership to promote innovation, creativity and social benefits. The EU-funded OPENVERSE project is dedicated to creating a knowledge base on European virtual worlds. To that end, it will establish a community of stakeholders, test user co-creation and extended reality, explore challenges, and generate standards, technology roadmaps and recommendations.

column2: MetaCities Excellence Hub in South-Eastern Europe
column4: Cities and regions in South-Eastern Europe face shared challenges related to sustainable mobility, green urban development, and digital transformation, but often lack the coordinated innovation ecosystems needed to scale advanced solutions. The METACITIES project addresses this gap by bringing together place-based innovation ecosystems in Cyprus, Greece, and Bulgaria to collaboratively shape future cities and regions. The objective is to strengthen regional capacity, foster cross-border collaboration, and develop practical solutions for smart mobility and green urban planning. The project establishes an excellence hub that promotes advanced digital technologies, including next-generation communication networks, digital twins, and large-scale data analytics, supported by artificial intelligence. These technologies are applied to urban use cases such as mobility optimisation, environmental monitoring, and sustainable spatial planning, with shared experimentation, knowledge exchange, and skills development across regions. The hub supports testing and validation of solutions in real-world contexts and connects public authorities, researchers, industry, and citizens. METACITIES enhances innovation capacity, accelerates adoption of advanced urban technologies, and supports more sustainable, data-driven planning. It delivers long-term value by strengthening regional ecosystems and enabling scalable, future-ready solutions for European cities.

column2: National Quantum Computing Centre
column4: The NQCC is the UK's national lab for quantum computing. It works with businesses, government and the research community to deliver quantum computing capabilities for the UK.

column2: CityGML-based district energy simulation
column4: The aim of the project is to utilize the public CityGML data model to propose energy-positive solutions for urban blocks through simulation techniques, led by VTT. By leveraging up-to-date data provided by CityGML, the project aims to streamline the collection of input data and enhance simulations with the latest building information, primarily geometry. This enables the study of various building renovation scenarios and alternative energy supply solutions to achieve energy positivity. The project, previously part of EU MODER projects and now integrated into SPARCS, employs simulators like Apros simulation software interfaced with CityGML to model the energy systems of buildings and districts. The main beneficiaries of this innovation are energy and urban sustainability planners, building consultants, and stakeholders involved in building renovation and sustainability programs, who can use open building data to propose or evaluate energy efficiency solutions and improvement measures at the district level.

column2: Virtual Power Station Optimisation in Espoo
column4: The aim of the project is to implement and demonstrate the effectiveness of Virtual Power Plants (VPPs) in achieving decarbonisation and energy efficiency objectives within the smart energy grid. Led by the demonstration in the Leppävaara district of Espoo, Finland, this project is overseen by experts aiming to coordinate local energy resources including generation, storage, and consumption. The focus is on addressing challenges stemming from renewable energy variability, limited energy storage, and insufficient flexibility in energy demand. Key to the project is the development and implementation of advanced forecasting models to optimize energy balance and consumption, particularly in urban environments.

column2: AI predicts cardian arrest in Copenhagen
column4: In Copenhagen, the Emergency Medical Services (EMS) has taken a unique approach to increase the recognition of cardiac arrests. An innovative device based on artificial intelligence is trained to identify cardiac arrest from the ongoing calls to 112. By analyzing the call, ‘listening’ for patterns in the dialogue referring to cardiac arrest, it can alert the medical dispatcher who is handling the call. When analyzing the call in real-time, the solution compares the ongoing call to the history of calls and calculates the probability of a cardiac arrest. Currently, the artificial intelligence solution can correctly identify approximately 85 percent of cardiac arrests – compared to approximately 75 percent recognized by medical dispatchers. This translates into 150 more patients now being recognized prior to the ambulance arriving.

column2: Empowering Learning and well-being in the City of Helsinki using data and AI
column4: Education systems must respond to increasingly diverse learner needs while supporting teachers with limited resources and rising expectations for personalised learning. The City of Helsinki sought to rethink education as a city-wide ecosystem, using data to improve learning outcomes, student wellbeing, and teaching effectiveness. This use case involves city authorities, schools, teachers, students, and technology partners, with the objective of transforming Helsinki into one of the most impactful learning environments globally. The initiative is implemented through data-driven platforms that integrate learning data, wellbeing indicators, and school information systems, supported by advanced analytics and artificial intelligence. These tools enable personalised learning pathways, early identification of support needs, and evidence-based pedagogical decisions, while respecting ethical and governance frameworks. Teachers are supported with insights that enhance planning and classroom practice, and leadership gains a systemic view of educational performance. The approach improves student engagement, supports wellbeing, and enhances teacher effectiveness. It delivers scalable and sustainable educational innovation by aligning technology, pedagogy, leadership, and governance, creating lasting public value for learners and society.

column2: Co-creation of a Data Charter for AI in the Camden Council

Co-created with the residents, the charter forms a social contract between the council and the community, and it was designed to ensure that the council collects, processes and shares data ethically. This co-creation process showed how public bodies can build trust with the community.

column2: SAVIO - Data mining to score sickness certificates for the purpose of controlling their truthfullness
column4: Social security institutions face the challenge of ensuring that sickness benefits are granted lawfully while avoiding excessive or intrusive medical controls. INPS, the Italian National Social Security Institute, reactivated the SAVIO system to support the verification of sickness certificates submitted by workers. Stakeholders include INPS medical services, employers, employees and public health professionals. The objective is to prioritise medical checks where there is a higher likelihood of irregularities, ensuring fairness while optimising the use of inspection resources. SAVIO processes administrative and medical data related to sickness certificates, applying data mining techniques to identify patterns associated with anomalous or inconsistent claims. Based on historical cases and contextual variables, the system assigns risk scores that inform whether ex officio medical examinations should be ordered. The tool does not replace medical judgement but supports decision-making by highlighting cases requiring attention. Expected impacts include improved efficiency of medical checks, reduced unnecessary inspections, better targeting of controls and more consistent handling of sickness benefit claims, while maintaining transparency and proportionality.

column2: New Web Analytics System on the INPS official website
column4: Large public sector websites often struggle to adapt content and interfaces to the real needs of users, particularly when services are complex and audiences diverse. INPS, Italy’s national social security institute, manages one of the country’s most heavily used public service portals and requires evidence-based insights to improve usability, accessibility and service effectiveness. The objective of this initiative is to enhance the quality of digital services by identifying navigation patterns, friction points and content gaps experienced by users. The implementation focuses on the design and development of machine learning algorithms that analyse anonymous web navigation data, including page transitions, interaction sequences and usage frequency. These models generate aggregated insights and recommendations for improving information architecture, content clarity and interface design, while fully respecting data protection and anonymity requirements. The system is intended to integrate with existing web management processes and support continuous improvement cycles. Expected impacts include more user-centred digital services, reduced abandonment of online procedures and improved accessibility for different user groups. Qualitative outcomes include better-informed design decisions, increased trust in digital public services and a scalable analytics framework applicable across large public administration websites.

column2: Calendaring medical examination management, communication and medical examination
column4: Managing medical examinations for social security purposes requires balancing timely appointments, limited medical resources and citizens’ availability. INPS initiated a project to standardise and automate the scheduling of medical examinations, addressing inefficiencies caused by manual planning and fragmented communication channels. Stakeholders include INPS medical staff, administrative offices, contracted healthcare providers and citizens subject to medical assessments. The objective is to optimise resource allocation, reduce waiting times and improve communication with patients. The system introduces a centralised and automated calendar that integrates information on medical personnel, facilities and availability. Advanced scheduling logic applies prioritisation rules and planning-and-control principles to dynamically assign appointments. The platform also includes automated notification mechanisms, informing citizens of scheduled visits and enabling rescheduling through INPS digital channels. Anticipated benefits include more efficient use of medical resources, improved appointment adherence, reduced administrative workload and a more transparent and predictable experience for citizens undergoing medical examinations.

column2: Implementation of AI chatbot to guide the compilation of UNIEMENS flows
column4: Private sector employers and intermediaries are required to submit UNIEMENS reports monthly to INPS, detailing employment and contribution data. Errors in compilation can lead to delays, corrections and administrative disputes. INPS launched a project to support users in correctly completing UNIEMENS submissions. Stakeholders include employers, payroll intermediaries, INPS offices and compliance professionals. The objective is to reduce errors, improve understanding of regulatory requirements and streamline reporting processes. The solution consists of a conversational digital assistant designed to provide contextual guidance during UNIEMENS compilation. It draws on regulatory texts, procedural rules and validation logic to answer questions, explain obligations and highlight common errors. The chatbot is intended to integrate with existing INPS digital services, offering real-time support without replacing formal controls. Expected impacts include improved data quality, fewer rejected submissions, reduced support requests to INPS offices and greater transparency for employers navigating complex reporting obligations.

column2: Chatbot to support workers applying for a wage supplement support
column4: The aim of the project is to support users in their search for information on Wage Supplementation Fund (CIG) benefits. It proposes itself as a key to access a fast, immediate and convenient interaction with online services, with greater effectiveness and user satisfaction also due to its 24-hour availability. It can make use of the data of those who interact with it, and in particular of CIG recipients, allowing it to offer the right solution or answer at the right time, and incorporate Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning functions, to learn from its 'mistakes' and from user behaviour.

column2: Customer Relations Management system evolution to support decision-making
column4: Large public administrations interact with millions of users across multiple channels, often resulting in fragmented data and limited strategic insight. INPS initiated the evolution of its Customer Relationship Management system to consolidate user interactions and support more proactive service delivery. Stakeholders include INPS service departments, case officers, management and citizens. The objective is to harmonise data collection across channels, improve monitoring and enable personalised services. The project focuses on integrating all interaction channels into a unified operational CRM platform, complemented by the gradual development of an analytical CRM layer. This includes reporting dashboards, monitoring tools and segmentation models that classify users based on needs, behaviours and life events. The approach follows an iterative and incremental development model, with early releases delivered to operational offices. Expected impacts include improved decision-making, more consistent service provision, better anticipation of user needs and long-term efficiency gains through data-driven service design.

column2: Balancing tourist capacity via blockchain in Formentera
column4: Tourist destinations with fragile ecosystems face increasing pressure to balance economic benefits from tourism with the protection of natural resources and quality of life for residents. Formentera, a small island with limited carrying capacity, needed a mechanism to better distribute visitor flows and reduce overcrowding in highly frequented areas, while engaging both tourists and residents in sustainable behaviour. This use case addresses that challenge by involving the island council, tourism authorities, local businesses, residents, and visitors, with the objective of promoting more balanced tourism patterns and sustainable use of space. The solution is implemented through a decentralised incentive system based on blockchain and smart contracts, which integrates real-time data from the island’s Tourism Intelligence System via oracles. Based on current capacity and congestion levels, incentives are dynamically issued to encourage visits to less crowded areas or specific activities, such as museums, events, or public transport routes, with transactions recorded on the Alastria network. The approach improves transparency, traceability, and trust in capacity management, enables data-driven redistribution of tourist flows, and enhances visitor experience while protecting sensitive areas. It supports sustainable tourism, reduces pressure on overcrowded sites, and provides a scalable governance model for managing destination carrying capacity.

column2: Secure visitor management with blockchain
column4: This solution incorporates digitalisation and monitoring to improve safe visitor management, including capacity and crowd control, and integrating blockchain as an element of certification of the security measures implemented by destinations. The telematic system for controlling capacity and flows allows the management of visits safely, following the health recommendations in this regard, interacting directly on information panels or informing the personnel responsible for avoiding crowds or risk situations. The tool also incorporates a specific system of indicators to understand the background, behavior, characteristics and impact of tourism on heritage assets and their management, and subsequently act accordingly.

column2: Innovation and Digital Transition Service – Mobility and Urban Regeneration Project
column4: In order to monitor both public and private urban mobility, it is required to develop a model capable of predicting traffic peaks in the city’s main thoroughfares well in advance, using data from bus circulation and data from road sensors.

column2: Improving trackside worker safety
column4: Network Rail has identified geofencing technology as a potential solution to improve the safety of trackside working. Geofencing is a location-based technology where a mobile, cloud-based app or other software uses GPS, radio frequency ID (RFID), Wi-Fi or cellular data. A mobile device can be worn to alert workers when entering or exiting a virtual boundary set up around a geographical location, known as a geofence. This provides an additional ‘tap on the shoulder’ for workers if they inadvertently stray outside of safe working limits. It would be deployed alongside and in addition to existing safety protocols and procedures.

column2: Metaverse-based centre for exibitions in Valencia
column4: The new art center has more than 2,500 m2 of exhibition space divided into four warehouses, and includes an installation with more than 45 projectors. Also, the organisation states that it has the largest metaverse room in Europe. The space is equipped with expanded reality (RX) technologies to offer large-scale immersive experiences, virtual and augmented reality, holography and artificial intelligence, as well as new formats in the experimental phase.

column2: Autonomous Road Inspection for Bizkaia Regional Council
column4: Maintaining road safety requires frequent inspection of infrastructure such as signs, markings, barriers, and work zones, yet manual inspections are costly, infrequent, and expose personnel to risk. The Bizkaia Regional Council faced the challenge of improving road asset monitoring while increasing coverage and responsiveness. The Autonomous Road Inspector addresses this need by enabling continuous, automated assessment of road conditions. Stakeholders include regional authorities, infrastructure operators, and road users. The objective is to enhance safety, optimise maintenance, and support proactive road management. The solution deploys vehicle-mounted sensing systems that capture visual and environmental data during normal traffic operations. Automated analysis identifies damaged or missing signs, degraded markings, unsafe barriers, construction zone conditions, and hazards such as slippery surfaces. Predictive functions support early detection of deterioration and risk. The impact includes more frequent inspections, reduced operational costs, improved worker safety, and faster response to hazards. The pilot demonstrates how automated monitoring can modernise road management, improve public safety, and deliver scalable benefits for regional infrastructure authorities.

column2: Public AI Challenge - Hub Innovazione Trentino - Overtourism detection
column4: Overtourism places pressure on environmentally sensitive areas, leading to congestion, ecological damage, and reduced quality of life for residents and visitors. The Province of Trento sought tools to anticipate tourism peaks and manage mobility proactively. This use case addresses the need for data-driven forecasting to support sustainable tourism management. Stakeholders include regional authorities, transport planners, local communities, and visitors. The objective is to detect and predict overcrowding in specific areas and support timely mitigation measures. The solution integrates heterogeneous datasets, including road traffic data, mobile network activity, and weather information, to estimate visitor volumes and characterise tourism patterns. Data preparation and harmonisation enable effective analysis, while predictive models identify days and locations at high risk of overtourism. The impact includes improved forecasting accuracy, better traffic management decisions, and reduced environmental pressure on sensitive areas. The initiative supports evidence-based tourism governance and demonstrates how advanced analytics can help balance economic activity with environmental protection and quality of life.

column2: Municipality of Messina pilots  the Recycleye robot to select garbage and improve reciclying
column4: Messinaservizi Bene Comune and Recycleye started a collaboration aimed at installing an artificial intelligence-managed waste sorting robot, the first of its kind in Sicily, at the Pace sorting centre. Recycleye is a robotic system equipped with artificial intelligence that will be used for the selection of specific classes of materials, capable of picking with high granularity and continuity, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, guaranteeing cost containment and bringing added value to the treated flows.

column2: ETIBLOGG – Local Energy Trading in Green Microgrids
column4: The transition to decentralised energy systems requires new market mechanisms that allow small-scale producers and consumers to trade electricity efficiently without relying on central intermediaries. In local microgrids, existing market structures often make trading small quantities of energy economically unattractive. The ETIBLOGG project addresses this challenge by enabling local electricity trading among prosumers and consumers. Stakeholders include households, small businesses, and local energy communities. The objective is to make peer-to-peer energy trading economically viable and support decentralised renewable generation. The solution establishes a digital trading framework for microgrids, enabling automated settlement of small energy transactions and transparent coordination between participants. Trading rules and transactions are recorded in a shared digital infrastructure, reducing transaction costs and administrative complexity. The impact includes increased local consumption of renewable energy, improved economic returns for small producers, and reduced reliance on centralised energy markets. ETIBLOGG contributes to more resilient, decentralised energy systems and supports the development of local green grids as part of the future energy landscape.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Pebbles – Peer-to-Peer Electricity Trading in Local Microgrids
column4: Local energy systems with high shares of renewable generation face regulatory and market barriers that limit direct trading between small producers and consumers. In regions such as Wildpoldsried, where many households already generate renewable electricity, surplus energy is often fed into central grids instead of being exchanged locally. The Pebbles project addresses this challenge by designing a sustainable and economically viable local energy market within a microgrid. Stakeholders include households, small and medium-sized producers, prosumers, local companies, and distribution network operators. The objective is to establish a replicable model for regional electricity markets that strengthens local value creation and energy autonomy. The solution implements a regional digital trading platform that enables peer-to-peer exchange of green electricity within the microgrid. Transactions between participants are securely recorded and settled using distributed ledger mechanisms, ensuring transparency and trust without central intermediaries. Market rules support flexible pricing and real-time balancing of supply and demand. The project demonstrates improved local consumption of renewable energy, increased economic participation of small actors, and reduced grid dependency. Pebbles delivers a scalable blueprint for decentralised energy markets, supporting the transition towards resilient, community-based energy systems.

column2: Decide Madrid - Citizen Participation and Machine Learning
column4: Large-scale online participation platforms can suffer from information overload: thousands of proposals and comments make it difficult for citizens to find relevant initiatives, build consensus, and understand debates, while public officials struggle to synthesise input into actionable insights. Research using Madrid’s Decide Madrid platform (built on Consul) examined how to improve discovery, deliberation, and responsiveness so that participation remains meaningful as volume grows. Stakeholders include citizens submitting proposals and comments, city administrators responsible for engagement and policy feedback, and platform maintainers seeking scalable moderation and analysis tools, with the objective of improving user experience and the quality of participation outcomes. The approach applies natural language processing and machine learning to suggest proposals users may wish to support, cluster citizens by shared interests, summarise comment threads, and assist with drafting or aggregating responses and refined proposals. This is achieved by processing the text corpus of proposals and comments, extracting semantic representations, and using similarity, clustering, and summarisation techniques that can be integrated into the platform interface as recommender, navigation, and analyst-support features. Reported evaluations indicate these methods can address participation barriers by reducing noise, improving navigability, and helping users and administrators focus on salient themes, thereby increasing the practicality of digital democracy tools without requiring proportional increases in staffing.

column2: AI in Evaluating Citizens’ Contributions
column4: Public administrations increasingly rely on citizen participation processes, yet analysing large volumes of written contributions remains time-consuming and inconsistent. This research-based pilot explores how natural language processing can support public decision-making by structuring and analysing citizen input. Stakeholders include public authorities, policymakers and citizens participating in consultations. The objective is to improve the efficiency, fairness and depth of analysis of public contributions while maintaining transparency. The approach applies computational text analysis to identify duplicate submissions, cluster contributions by topic and extract deeper insights such as arguments, sentiment and stakeholder perspectives. Large collections of textual submissions serve as the primary data source, enabling scalable analysis that complements human review rather than replacing it. The results show improved capacity to handle large-scale participation processes, reduced administrative burden and more evidence-based policy insights. While exploratory, the pilot highlights how advanced language analysis can strengthen democratic participation by ensuring that citizen voices are systematically considered in policymaking processes.

This ‘learning’ takes place through artificial neural networks.
The German Museum Bonn therefore aims to acquire seven multi-part media demonstrations in order to give visitors the abstract and highly complex functioning of simple and multi-layered artificial neuronal networks in a clear and entertaining way. column2: Classification using AI in automated rail driving by DZSF
column4: DZSF – Further development of open data bases for object detection and classification using artificial intelligence in automated rail driving Automated driving in railway operations has great potential, for example in terms of increasing capacity. Technical systems must take over tasks currently performed by the driver. In the case of higher levels of automation, this includes testing for any objects in the track area and in the surrounding area, as well as the classification of defined types of objects. Different sensors collect data from the environment, which are then tested for any relevant objects using artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms. As the potential input data of sensors for such AI applications are subject to a large variance, a significant part of the algorithms are expected to be developed using machine learning techniques (ML) based on training and validation data.
