column2: AI-augmented system for predicting electricity consumption in Serbia
column4: The Electric Power Company of Serbia (EPS) is responsible for balancing electricity generation, distribution, and consumption across a national grid subject to fluctuating demand, seasonal variation, and increasing sustainability requirements. Traditional forecasting methods limited the company’s ability to anticipate demand peaks accurately, leading to inefficiencies in resource allocation and higher operational costs. This use case involves EPS grid operators, planners, and energy market stakeholders, with the objective of improving demand forecasting to support reliable supply, optimise grid operations, and reduce waste. The solution is implemented through an advanced forecasting system that combines historical consumption data, real-time grid measurements, weather information, and socio-economic indicators. Machine-learning models analyse these datasets to generate short- and medium-term demand predictions that are continuously updated as new data becomes available. The system is integrated into EPS operational platforms to support planning, dispatch, and decision-making. The impact includes more accurate demand forecasts, improved grid stability, and better optimisation of generation assets. EPS benefits from reduced operational risk, improved energy efficiency, and stronger support for sustainability objectives, contributing to smarter and more resilient national energy management.

column2: exPEDite - Enabling Positive Energy Districts through Digital Twins
column4: Achieving climate neutrality at district level requires coordinated management of energy production, consumption and mobility, which is difficult using fragmented planning tools. City planners and energy stakeholders need integrated solutions to manage complex urban systems. The objective of this pilot is to support the creation of Positive Energy Districts that produce more energy than they consume. The implementation develops digital twins of urban districts that combine real-time and historical data on buildings, energy networks and transport flows. Simulation and optimisation tools allow stakeholders to test scenarios, optimise energy balance and engage citizens through visualisation and gamification features. The pilot demonstrated improved energy efficiency, reduced emissions and enhanced planning capabilities. Qualitative impacts include better cross-sector collaboration, increased public engagement and transferable guidelines for replication in other cities. The project illustrates the value of digital twins in supporting sustainable urban transformation across Europe.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Blockchain.PT - Decentralize Portugal with Blockchain
column4: ​The BLOCKCHAIN.PT project, initiated in January 2023, is a collaborative effort led by VOID Software and involving 56 partners, including INESC-ID. Funded by the European Union through Next Generation EU as part of Portugal's Recovery and Resilience Plan (PRR), the project aims to establish a national blockchain ecosystem to drive innovation and capitalise on global business opportunities. The initiative focuses on sectors such as agriculture, digital asset management, and interoperability, with the goal of positioning Portugal at the forefront of blockchain technology in Europe.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Smart City Logistics – Automating Last-Mile Delivery in Leuven
column4: This is a use case of the EU funded project, TOKEN, implemented in Poland. It focuses on last-mile logistics in smart cities, particularly in mail and mobility services. It involves automating the delivery process through a public-private partnership between the Ministry, the City, and a business operator. The initiative aims to enhance urban logistics and mobility efficiency within smart city frameworks.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AMS Berufs Infomat -  personalized career advice for job seekers
column4: Job seekers often struggle to navigate complex information on occupations, training pathways, and reskilling opportunities, particularly in a rapidly changing labour market. Austria’s Public Employment Service (AMS) developed Berufs-Infomat to provide accessible, personalised career information to support informed decisions. The use case involves job seekers, students, career advisors, and AMS staff, with the objective of improving access to labour-market information and supporting lifelong learning. The solution is implemented as an interactive digital assistant that responds dynamically to user questions about professions, qualifications, and further education opportunities. It draws on structured labour-market data, occupational profiles, and training catalogues to generate tailored responses aligned with user interests and backgrounds. Integrated into AMS digital services, it complements human counselling rather than replacing it. The impact includes faster access to relevant career information, improved user engagement, and better alignment between individual aspirations and labour-market needs. AMS benefits from more scalable service delivery, while users gain clearer guidance and increased confidence in career planning, supporting employability and workforce adaptability.

column2: Generative AI-Enhanced Knowledge Management in Business (GAIK)
column4: Many organisations struggle with fragmented information and inefficient knowledge sharing, leading to duplicated work and reduced productivity among knowledge workers. Finnish companies participating in this pilot sought practical solutions to improve access to internal knowledge assets. The objective of the pilot is to reduce information bottlenecks and enhance organisational efficiency. The implementation delivers a modular toolkit that enables organisations to deploy intelligent knowledge services using their own business data. Reusable components, templates and process models support rapid integration into existing systems, enabling functions such as contextual search, summarisation and knowledge reuse. Early results indicate reduced time spent searching for information, improved decision-making and higher employee satisfaction. Qualitative benefits include better onboarding, stronger knowledge retention and improved customer-facing processes. The project demonstrates how flexible, reusable solutions can support scalable knowledge management across different organisational contexts.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: GENAI4ED - A Platform for Assessing and Bridging Generative AI and Human Skills in Secondary Education
column4: Secondary schools face growing pressure to adopt digital tools that enhance learning while supporting teachers rather than increasing their workload. Educators and policymakers require evidence-based guidance to select appropriate educational technologies. The objective of this pilot is to assess and recommend tools that best complement teaching practices and student needs. The implementation provides a digital evaluation platform that reviews widely used educational software against pedagogical, usability and skills-related criteria. Pilot schools involve both teachers and students in structured evaluations, generating comparative insights. The pilot improves alignment between technology adoption and educational objectives, supporting more informed procurement and classroom use. Qualitative outcomes include improved teacher confidence, better student engagement and enhanced learning experiences. The project contributes practical frameworks for responsible integration of advanced digital tools in education.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Generative-AI based chatbot for the Cypriot Government Portal
column4: Citizens interacting with public administration often face fragmented information and limited service availability outside office hours. The Cypriot Government sought to improve digital access to public information through its central portal, gov.cy. This use case involves government departments, citizens, and businesses, with the objective of enabling faster, clearer, and more immediate communication with the state on a 24/7 basis. The solution is implemented as a generative digital assistant embedded within the government portal, capable of responding to a wide range of citizen enquiries in natural language. It draws on official government content, structured service information, and continuously updated policy data to provide accurate and consistent answers. The assistant is integrated with the portal’s information architecture and designed to scale as new services are added. The impact includes improved accessibility to government information, reduced pressure on call centres, and higher user satisfaction. Citizens benefit from timely responses and simplified navigation of public services, while the administration gains efficiency and insight into user needs, supporting a more responsive and citizen-centric digital government.

column2: European AI Factories for businesses
column4: European businesses and public organisations increasingly require access to advanced computing, data, and expertise to develop and deploy competitive digital solutions, yet such resources are often fragmented or inaccessible. The European AI Factories initiative addresses this gap by creating interconnected innovation hubs across Europe. The use case involves supercomputing centres, universities, SMEs, large industry, financial actors, and public authorities, with the objective of accelerating innovation and adoption across strategic sectors. AI Factories are implemented as ecosystems that combine high-performance computing infrastructure, shared datasets, research expertise, and collaborative environments. They support the development, testing, and scaling of advanced digital models and applications across domains such as health, manufacturing, climate, finance, and space. The network includes national initiatives such as LUMI AIF in Finland, Meluxina-AI in Luxembourg, and IT4LIA in Italy, among others. The impact is increased innovation capacity, reduced barriers to entry for SMEs, and stronger cross-border collaboration. AI Factories enhance Europe’s digital sovereignty, improve competitiveness, and deliver long-term economic and societal value by enabling scalable, trustworthy, and high-impact innovation.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Norwegian University of Science and Technology testing the utility and safety of Microsoft's AI assistant in public sector organisations
column4: Public sector organisations increasingly explore productivity assistants but lack clear guidance on organisational readiness, data protection and responsible deployment. In Norway, public institutions sought evidence-based insights before large-scale adoption. The objective of this pilot is to evaluate the utility, safety and governance implications of deploying Copilot for Microsoft 365. The implementation tests the tool in controlled organisational settings, examining impacts on workflows, information handling and compliance. A structured “readiness toolbox” is developed, providing guidelines, risk assessments and best practices. The pilot delivered practical recommendations, increased organisational awareness and clearer policy guidance. Qualitative benefits include reduced uncertainty, improved governance capacity and a foundation for responsible scaling across the public sector.
column8: Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU)

column2: x-CITE: Expanding the Experience of Citizens through Extended Reality and Metaverse in the City of Tampere
column4: Cities increasingly seek new ways to engage citizens, improve access to culture, and support participatory urban development while ensuring that digital innovation remains human-centric and inclusive. The City of Tampere aims to enhance how residents and visitors experience urban spaces, cultural venues, and civic participation by blending physical and digital environments. This use case addresses the need to broaden access to culture, improve engagement in urban planning, and create new forms of social participation, involving the city administration, cultural institutions, event organisers, citizens, and visitors, with the objective of developing a shared CitiVerse as a practical tool for smart city innovation. The x-CITE project implements extended reality and metaverse technologies to create immersive experiences connected to real locations, events, and artworks, integrating physical spaces such as public areas and the Nokia Arena with digital layers. XR applications are developed for urban planning, museums, culture, and tourism, supported by city data platforms and tested through pilots such as immersive museum experiences at Vapriikki. The initiative enhances citizen engagement, reaches new audiences, and improves accessibility to cultural and civic spaces. It delivers richer user experiences, supports inclusive participation, and establishes scalable digital capabilities that strengthen Tampere’s position as a pioneer of human-centric smart cities.

column2: TwIN - Urban Digital Twin in the Region of Navarra
column4: Cities and regions increasingly need collaborative tools to address complex urban challenges while ensuring that innovation delivers tangible benefits to citizens. In the Region of Navarra, public authorities and local companies face fragmentation between policy needs, technological capabilities and skills development. The objective of this pilot is to foster structured collaboration between public administration, industry and the innovation ecosystem through the use of urban digital twin technologies. The implementation is organised around two complementary lines of work. TwIN GovTech operates as an open innovation programme that responds rapidly to concrete challenges defined by Pamplona City Council, enabling companies to test digital twin applications in real urban contexts. TwIN Lab functions as a training and experimentation environment, supporting skills development, prototyping and knowledge transfer across the regional ecosystem. Digital twin components integrate urban data, simulation tools and visualisation environments to support analysis and decision-making. The pilot has strengthened collaboration between public and private actors, accelerated experimentation cycles and increased institutional capacity to adopt advanced digital tools. Qualitative results include improved policy design, greater innovation readiness and a scalable framework for citizen-centred urban transformation.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: BasqueLLM research project launched the Llama-eus-8B neural model in Basque
column4: Digital services often underrepresent minority and regional languages, limiting accessibility and cultural inclusion. The BasqueLLM research project addresses this challenge by strengthening digital language support for Basque. This use case involves researchers, public institutions, technology developers, and Basque-speaking communities, with the objective of enabling high-quality language understanding and generation for Basque in digital applications. The solution is implemented through Llama-eus-8B, a foundational neural language model specifically trained on curated Basque-language datasets. With fewer than 10 billion parameters, it balances performance and efficiency, making it suitable for a wide range of applications. The model is designed as a base layer for downstream tools such as chatbots, content generation systems, and public-sector digital services. The impact includes improved linguistic coverage, reduced dependency on non-European language technologies, and enhanced accessibility of digital services for Basque speakers. The project strengthens digital inclusion, supports cultural preservation, and enables innovation in public and private services using the Basque language.

column2: AI tool for industries' application of EU regulations on cybersecurity
column4: Industrial companies face growing regulatory complexity in complying with EU cybersecurity requirements, particularly under new frameworks such as NIS2. Many organisations lack the internal expertise to interpret and apply regulations effectively. ZIUR, the Industrial Cybersecurity Centre of Gipuzkoa, developed a digital assistant to support companies in this process. The use case involves industrial firms, cybersecurity specialists, and regional authorities, with the objective of simplifying regulatory compliance and strengthening cyber resilience. The solution is implemented as an interactive advisory tool that guides users through regulatory requirements, assesses organisational maturity, and provides tailored recommendations based on company profiles. It draws on regulatory texts, best practices, and sector-specific risk scenarios. The impact includes improved understanding of obligations, reduced compliance costs, and more consistent application of cybersecurity measures. Companies benefit from clearer guidance and reduced risk exposure, while the region strengthens its industrial cybersecurity posture and regulatory readiness.

column2: Streamlining Environmental Permitting Processes with Multi-Agent AI in Denmark
column4: Environmental permitting processes are often complex, slow, and resource-intensive, creating uncertainty for project developers while placing pressure on regulators. Denmark’s Environmental Protection Agency modernised this process to improve efficiency and transparency. This use case involves regulators, developers, and environmental stakeholders, with the objective of accelerating permit decisions while maintaining regulatory integrity. The solution is implemented through a fully digital, centrally managed permitting platform that structures workflows, standardises documentation, and supports caseworkers with analytical tools and access to historical data. Automated checks and intelligent assistance help identify issues early and reduce bottlenecks. Applicants can track application status in real time, improving predictability. The impact includes faster processing times, improved coordination between agencies, and higher consistency in decisions. The system supports renewable energy deployment and climate goals, delivering a scalable, replicable model for modern regulatory administration.
column43: Automated reasoning and Automated Decision Making (ADM)

column2: AI-driven citizen engagement platform for the City of Prague
column4: Municipal administrations often struggle to manage large volumes of citizen reports submitted through fragmented communication channels, leading to delays, misrouting and reduced public satisfaction. In Prague, residents and city departments required a more efficient and transparent mechanism for reporting and resolving urban issues. The objective of this pilot is to streamline citizen engagement and improve responsiveness of municipal services. The implementation delivers a digital engagement platform that allows residents to report issues through a single interface, where submissions are automatically categorised and routed to the responsible departments. Natural language processing techniques interpret citizen inputs, while workflow automation integrates with municipal back-office systems. The platform is developed within the CommuniCity initiative in collaboration with the city’s ICT organisation, ensuring alignment with existing governance structures. The pilot has reduced manual handling of reports, shortened response times and improved traceability of issue resolution. Qualitative benefits include increased citizen trust, better inter-departmental coordination and more consistent service delivery. The initiative demonstrates how digital engagement platforms can enhance participation while improving operational efficiency in large cities.
column42: Automated reasoning and Automated Decision Making (ADM)

column2: Skendy - Multilingual Chatbot supporting Ukrainian refugees in the City of Amsterdam
column4: Newly arrived refugees often face significant barriers in understanding administrative requirements, particularly when dealing with unfamiliar languages and complex bureaucratic processes. In the City of Amsterdam, Ukrainian refugees required accessible support to manage official communications and documentation. The objective of this pilot is to reduce administrative stress and improve access to essential services for displaced populations. The implementation adapts an existing digital assistant platform to provide multilingual document support, including translation, organisation and reminder functions. The system processes official letters, utility bills and other documents, presenting clear explanations and deadlines in the user’s preferred language. The solution integrates with mobile devices and existing municipal support services, ensuring ease of access. The pilot improved refugees’ ability to comply with administrative obligations, reduced reliance on informal assistance and eased pressure on frontline municipal staff. Qualitative outcomes include increased autonomy for users, improved inclusion and better access to public services. The project illustrates how targeted digital tools can support vulnerable groups while enhancing the effectiveness of city-level integration policies.

column2: nShield - Predictive Maintenance for escalators downtimes in Prague
column4: Escalator failures in public transport systems cause service disruptions, safety risks, and high maintenance costs. Prague’s public transport operator sought to reduce downtime and improve maintenance efficiency. This use case involves transport operators, maintenance teams, and passengers, with the objective of predicting failures before they occur. The solution is implemented through acoustic sensors installed on escalators, continuously collecting sound data. These signals are analysed in real time using mathematical models that detect anomalies and deviations from normal behaviour. The system identifies likely component failures and provides maintenance recommendations to operators. Integrated into maintenance workflows, it enables condition-based interventions rather than reactive repairs. The impact includes reduced downtime, lower maintenance costs, improved passenger experience, and increased asset lifespan. The system enhances operational reliability and supports smarter management of critical transport infrastructure.

column2: ADLER: Local Language Models for Sensitive Data
column4: Public sector organisations frequently process confidential documents that cannot be transferred to external systems without risking data breaches or loss of sovereignty. Administrative staff require advanced text-processing capabilities while maintaining strict control over sensitive information. The objective of this pilot is to demonstrate that language models can be executed securely on local devices without network dependency. The implementation explores on-device deployment of compact language models on commercially available, resilient hardware. Documents are processed directly on the user’s workstation, enabling functions such as classification, extraction and summarisation without external data transmission. The approach is designed to comply with strict security classifications, including confidential government information. The pilot strengthens data sovereignty, reduces exposure to cyber risks and opens new usage contexts for secure document handling. Qualitative benefits include increased trust, broader applicability of advanced text processing in sensitive environments and reduced dependency on external infrastructure. The project provides a foundation for responsible deployment of advanced digital tools in security-critical administrative contexts.
column8: Bundesdruckerei Group – Federal AI Competence Center

column2: MÖVE: Benchmarking of Language Models for Public Administration
column4: Public administrations face growing pressure to adopt language technologies while ensuring reliability, fairness and regulatory compliance. Decision-makers often lack objective criteria to compare available models for administrative use. The objective of this pilot is to establish a robust evaluation framework tailored to public sector needs. The implementation defines standardised benchmarks assessing model performance on administrative tasks in German, alongside governance criteria such as social fairness, hallucination rates and alignment with the EU AI Act. The framework supports transparent comparison of different models and deployment scenarios. The pilot provides evidence-based guidance for procurement and policy decisions. Qualitative impacts include improved risk awareness, stronger governance and increased confidence in technology adoption. The framework supports scalable, responsible use of language technologies across public administrations.
column8: Bundesdruckerei Group – Federal AI Competence Center

column2: AI Language Assistant for the German Federal Administration: Efficient Information Processing
column4: Administrative decision-making often depends on analysing large volumes of unstructured documents, which places significant strain on staff resources. Federal authorities require tools to extract relevant information efficiently while maintaining accountability. The objective of this pilot is to improve information processing and support evidence-based decisions. The implementation deploys a language-based assistant capable of summarising documents, identifying key topics and mapping responsibilities. The system integrates with existing document management systems and supports staff workflows. The pilot reduced processing time, improved consistency and enhanced transparency. Qualitative benefits include increased productivity, improved staff satisfaction and better-informed decisions.
column8: Bundesdruckerei Group – Federal AI Competence Center

column2: PLAIN: a data analysis platform for the entire German federal administration
column4: Effective public policy increasingly depends on the ability to analyse large and heterogeneous datasets across institutional boundaries. Federal ministries often lack a shared infrastructure to support advanced data analysis securely. The objective of this pilot is to establish a standard, cross-departmental data platform. The implementation delivers a secure infrastructure enabling storage, processing and analysis of diverse datasets without thematic restrictions. The platform supports advanced analytical applications and future data-driven projects. The pilot improved analytical capacity, reduced duplication and strengthened inter-ministerial collaboration. Qualitative outcomes include improved policy insight, scalability and strategic alignment with national digital strategies.
column8: Bundesdruckerei Group – Federal AI Competence Center

column2: SOCIB 2030: Digital Twin of the Western Mediterranean
column4: Understanding complex marine systems is essential for addressing climate change, biodiversity loss and coastal risks. Researchers and policymakers require integrated tools that combine data, modelling and visualisation. The objective of this pilot is to develop modular digital twin prototypes tailored to specific marine challenges in the Western Mediterranean. The implementation delivers three specialised prototypes focusing on marine protected areas, climate adaptation and early warning for extreme events. These integrate observational data, forecasting models and visual analytics. The pilot enhances scientific understanding, supports decision-making and fosters international collaboration. Qualitative impacts include improved preparedness, better environmental governance and scalable contributions to global ocean initiatives.
column8: SOCIB - Balearic Islands Coastal Observing and Forecasting System

column2: VELVET - Italian AI Open Source model
column4: European administrations and organisations require language technologies that reflect linguistic diversity, regulatory requirements and sustainability goals. The objective of this pilot is to develop an open-source model prioritising Italian language and content while supporting multilingual use. The implementation trains a multimodal model on high-performance computing infrastructure, optimised for efficiency and regulatory compliance. The pilot strengthens European technological autonomy, improves language coverage and supports diverse public and private applications. Qualitative benefits include transparency, energy efficiency and alignment with European values.

column2: Smart Street Lighting
column4: Urban lighting represents a major source of energy consumption and maintenance costs for municipalities. Hafnarfjörður sought to modernise its lighting infrastructure to improve efficiency and safety. The objective of this pilot is to enable adaptive, data-driven lighting management. The implementation deploys networked controllers connected via low-power communication, allowing real-time control and monitoring of 6,000 streetlights. Lighting levels adapt to conditions and schedules, and maintenance is optimised through monitoring data. The pilot achieved a 35% reduction in energy consumption and a 42% decrease in maintenance costs, while improving public safety. Qualitative impacts include reduced emissions, operational savings and a scalable foundation for broader smart city services.

column2: Preserving History with Smart Technology: IoT Monitoring at the Colosseum
column4: Protecting world heritage sites requires continuous monitoring while avoiding invasive interventions that could compromise historical integrity. At the Colosseum in Rome, heritage authorities and conservation experts needed a reliable method to detect structural changes and environmental stress without physical alteration of the monument. The objective of this pilot is to support proactive preservation through non-invasive, real-time monitoring. The implementation deploys discreet LoRaWAN®-enabled sensors that collect structural and environmental data and transmit it continuously to a central network server. The system enables real-time detection of anomalies and supports the validation of satellite-based observations, integrating multiple data sources into a coherent monitoring framework. The pilot improved the ability to identify early warning signs of structural stress, reduced reliance on periodic manual inspections and enhanced situational awareness for conservation teams. Qualitative impacts include improved safeguarding of cultural heritage, more informed maintenance planning and a scalable approach for monitoring other sensitive historical sites while preserving their authenticity.

column2: Water Network Monitoring
column4: Ensuring reliable water supply and minimising losses is particularly challenging in areas with limited infrastructure and remote pumping stations. In Tápióság, Hungary, water utilities required better visibility of network performance without continuous on-site supervision. The objective of this pilot is to improve operational efficiency and sustainability of water distribution systems. The implementation installs autonomous monitoring devices at pumping stations to measure parameters such as pressure, energy consumption, pump operation time and water flow. Data is transmitted via low-power wide-area networks to a central management system, enabling real-time monitoring and alerts. The solution operates continuously without human intervention, even in hard-to-reach locations. The pilot enabled faster detection of faults, improved optimisation of pumping operations and reduced water losses. Qualitative benefits include lower operational costs, improved service reliability and stronger support for sustainable water management practices, demonstrating the value of remote monitoring for utilities with constrained resources.

column2: Data-driven Wildfire Prevention System for Forest
column4: Forest authorities face increasing risks from wildfires due to climate change, requiring early detection and accurate risk assessment over large areas. In Salzburg, managing forest safety demanded real-time environmental insights to support preventive action. The objective of this pilot is to enhance wildfire preparedness through continuous risk monitoring. The implementation deploys a dense network of environmental sensors across 350 square kilometres, collecting data on weather conditions and soil moisture. These inputs are processed to calculate the Fire Weather Index, providing real-time assessments of fire risk. Information is made available to authorities to guide prevention measures and resource allocation. The pilot improved situational awareness, enabled faster preventive responses and supported more informed decision-making. Qualitative impacts include increased forest resilience, improved safety for communities and a scalable framework for wildfire risk management in other regions.

column2: German Ministry of Research, Technology, and Space provides a popular chatbot to make project funding regulations more accessible
column4: Research funding regulations are complex and difficult for applicants to interpret, often leading to incomplete applications and high enquiry volumes. The German Ministry of Research, Technology, and Space introduced a digital assistant to improve accessibility of funding information. This use case involves researchers, institutions, and ministry staff, with the objective of simplifying access to funding rules and guidance. The solution is implemented as a conversational assistant built on an enterprise-grade platform that retrieves answers from official funding documents and guidance materials. It supports natural language queries and provides clear, context-specific responses. Integrated into ministry websites, it complements existing support channels. The impact includes reduced administrative burden, faster access to information for applicants, and improved quality of submissions. The ministry benefits from efficiency gains and better stakeholder engagement, while applicants gain clarity and confidence in navigating funding opportunities.
column8: German Federal Ministry of Research, Technology, and Space (BMFTR)
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Urban EVOLUTION (Earth obserVatiOn and nature-based soLUTions agaInst urban pollution)
column4: Urban EVOLUTION (Earth obserVatiOn and nature-based soLUTions agaInst urban pollution) is a 12-month research initiative combating urban air pollution and heat island effects through Nature-Based Solutions (NBS). The project is being piloted in three Italian cities—Naples, Catania, and Perugia—each offering distinct urban and climatic conditions to test NBS effectiveness. Leveraging advanced geospatial analysis, artificial intelligence, and remote sensing provided by technological partner Latitudo 40, the initiative assesses pollution levels and evaluates how green spaces can reduce CO₂ emissions, particulate matter, and urban temperatures. Through collaboration with TeamDev's R&D division, WiseTown, and the Universities of Perugia and Palermo, Urban EVOLUTION aims to develop scalable, replicable models for NBS planning aligned with Paris Agreement targets—reducing emissions by 50% by 2030 and achieving carbon neutrality by 2050—demonstrating that urban green spaces can potentially offset up to 40% of city emissions while significantly improving air quality.
column8: City of Naples, Catania, and Perugia

column2: MEDI:CUS – A Multi-Cloud-Based Platform Architecture for a Connected and Intersectoral Healthcare System
column4: MEDI:CUS is a large-scale digital transformation initiative launched in Baden-Württemberg in 2022 to address the fragmentation and inefficiencies of Germany’s decentralised healthcare IT landscape. Backed by five state ministries and a broad coalition of healthcare stakeholders—including all university hospitals and the Baden-Württemberg Hospital Association—the project aims to establish by 2026 a multi-cloud platform architecture (MEDI:core) that enables secure, scalable, and interoperable health data exchange across institutions and sectors. Functioning like an “App Store” for healthcare, the platform will provide Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) solutions through standardised interfaces and protocols, supporting maximum interoperability at technical, semantic, organisational, and legal levels. Early pilot tests have already demonstrated secure hospital connectivity and the successful deployment of initial services such as cloud storage and Nextcloud. The goal is to connect 15–20 hospitals by the end of 2026, equip them with usable digital solutions, and establish a self-sustaining target organisation capable of driving long-term innovation. MEDI:CUS is designed not only as a reference model for digital health transformation but also as a blueprint for future public-sector platforms, fostering efficiency, scalability, and trust across the healthcare system.

column2: PublicTwin for citizen engagement in Malmo for the renovation of the Nyhamnen harbor
column4: Large urban regeneration projects often struggle to meaningfully involve citizens in planning processes, leading to limited transparency and acceptance. In Malmö’s Nyhamnen harbour redevelopment, the city sought to strengthen public participation. The objective of this pilot is to empower residents to actively engage in urban planning decisions. The implementation uses a digital twin platform that visualises proposed developments and enables interactive exploration of planning data. An intuitive interface allows citizens to provide feedback and suggestions in real time, integrating public input into planning workflows. The pilot enhanced transparency, increased citizen participation and improved dialogue between planners and residents. Qualitative outcomes include stronger public trust, more inclusive decision-making and better alignment between urban development and community needs, demonstrating the role of digital twins in participatory city governance.

column2: AI-Tour guide in the City of Tampere
column4: Tourists increasingly expect personalised, real-time information when exploring new destinations, while cities aim to enhance visitor experiences and promote local businesses. In Tampere, tourism authorities sought innovative ways to deliver engaging, multilingual guidance. The objective of this pilot is to enrich the tourist experience through immersive digital assistance. The implementation introduces smart glasses equipped with a virtual assistant that provides location-based information on attractions, museums and services. Content is continuously updated by local businesses and tourism organisations, ensuring relevance and accuracy. The pilot improved visitor satisfaction, increased engagement with local services and offered new promotional channels for businesses. Qualitative benefits include enhanced accessibility, more dynamic tourism experiences and a scalable model for smart tourism solutions.

column2: Bike sharing availability optimisation in the City of Kuopio
column4: Managing shared mobility services requires ensuring that vehicles are available at the right locations and times, yet many cities struggle with uneven distribution that reduces reliability and user satisfaction. In Kuopio, the electric bike sharing scheme experienced recurring shortages in high-demand areas and surpluses elsewhere, limiting its effectiveness as a sustainable transport option. The objective of this pilot is to improve service reliability and encourage greater uptake of cycling by optimising bike availability across the city. The implementation analyses historical usage data to identify demand patterns by time and location and generates predictive forecasts of bike shortages. Based on these insights, the system issues real-time redistribution instructions to logistics operators via SMS or digital channels, guiding targeted rebalancing actions. A feedback loop allows operators to confirm completed actions and report outcomes, enabling continuous refinement of the predictive models. The pilot resulted in more consistent bike availability, reduced service disruptions and improved operational efficiency. Qualitative impacts include higher user satisfaction, increased use of sustainable transport and better utilisation of municipal mobility assets, demonstrating a scalable approach for data-driven management of shared mobility services.

column2: Regional AI Hubs/Centres for Health and Tourism
column4: Healthcare systems and tourism services face growing pressure from rising demand, limited resources and increasing expectations for personalised experiences. Several Italian regions identified common challenges in managing healthcare waiting lists and tourist flows effectively. The objective of this pilot is to test shared, regionally coordinated solutions that improve service planning, efficiency and user experience in both domains. The implementation develops analytical tools for healthcare demand analysis, forecasting and adaptive resource allocation, including appointment scheduling and booking support, while ensuring ethical and regulatory compliance. In parallel, tools for tourism flow management enable personalised visitor experiences and improved distribution of demand across destinations and seasons. The pilot operates through regional innovation hubs that foster collaboration between administrations, service providers and technology partners. Early results include improved planning accuracy, reduced bottlenecks in healthcare access and enhanced coordination of tourism services. Qualitative benefits include greater service equity, improved quality of care and visitor experience, and a replicable framework for cross-regional innovation, supporting more resilient and citizen-centred public services.

column2: Regional AI Hub/Centres for Environmental Data and Sustainable Mobility
column4: Environmental protection and mobility planning increasingly require coordinated action across administrative boundaries, yet data fragmentation often limits policy effectiveness. Lombardy and Veneto regions sought to improve environmental and transport governance through shared analytical capabilities. The objective of this pilot is to create a “smart land” by enabling transregional analysis of environmental and mobility data. The implementation establishes a federated data architecture that allows regions to retain control over their datasets while enabling advanced analytical models to operate across jurisdictions. These models support evidence-based policymaking in areas such as emissions reduction, traffic management and sustainable transport planning. The pilot enhances interoperability between regional systems and supports scalable innovation. Qualitative impacts include improved policy insight, stronger coordination between regions and more effective sustainability strategies. The initiative demonstrates how federated data approaches can support advanced analytics while respecting governance and data sovereignty requirements.

column2: Regional AI Hubs/Centres for the PA
column4: Public administrations face increasing complexity in policy design and technical-administrative procedures, placing pressure on staff capacity and decision quality. Several Italian regions recognised the need for shared innovation to support administrative effectiveness. The objective of this pilot is to enhance the quality, consistency and efficiency of public sector work by supporting human decision-making. The implementation introduces analytical and decision-support tools tailored to public policy analysis and administrative processes, integrated into existing workflows. Regional hubs provide shared infrastructure, governance and expertise, enabling experimentation and responsible adoption. The pilot improved process efficiency, supported more informed decisions and reduced administrative burdens. Qualitative benefits include improved working conditions for public servants, stronger institutional capacity and better service outcomes for citizens, offering a scalable model for digital transformation in public administration.

column2: Regional AI Hubs/Centres for Resilience and Security of the Territory with 5G pilot action
column4: Preventing environmental risks and ensuring the safety of infrastructure and communities requires timely data, advanced analysis and reliable connectivity. Tuscany and partner regions sought to strengthen territorial resilience and emergency preparedness. The objective of this pilot is to improve risk monitoring, intervention planning and emergency management across regions. The implementation combines advanced analytical models with 5G-enabled connectivity to support real-time data collection from sensors, infrastructure and construction sites. Analytical tools support risk assessment, scenario planning and coordination among authorities. The pilot enhances situational awareness, speeds up response planning and improves cross-agency collaboration. Qualitative impacts include increased safety, stronger resilience of critical infrastructure and improved spatial planning capabilities, demonstrating the value of combining advanced analytics and next-generation connectivity for territorial governance.

column2: "Roma 5G" - extension of the 5G connection across Rome
column4: Rome faces the challenge of supporting digital services, public safety, and connectivity across a large, historic, and densely used urban environment. Roma 5G addresses this by upgrading citywide digital infrastructure. This use case involves the City of Rome, infrastructure providers, transport authorities, and citizens, with the objective of enabling a fast, secure, and sustainable smart city. The solution is implemented through the deployment of advanced 5G infrastructure, including small cells, fibre networks, public Wi-Fi, IoT sensors, and surveillance cameras. Coverage is extended across the entire metro system and key public spaces. A central Security Operations Centre coordinates data flows and supports crowd management, particularly for major events such as the 2025 Jubilee. The impact includes improved connectivity, enhanced public safety, better urban monitoring, and support for future digital services. Roma 5G positions Rome as a leading European smart city, delivering long-term social, economic, and environmental value.

column2: Project Griffin - Drones to transport medical supplies in emergency situations
column4: Rapid transport of medical samples and supplies is critical in emergency healthcare, yet traditional logistics can be slow or constrained by infrastructure. In Luxembourg, emergency services seek innovative solutions to reduce response times. The objective of Project Griffin is to explore the use of drones for transporting medical supplies in urgent situations. The implementation involves testing drone-based delivery routes, operational procedures and coordination with healthcare providers. Future development plans include integrating advanced automation for route planning, obstacle avoidance and fleet coordination. Expected impacts include faster sample transport, improved emergency response and reduced dependency on road transport. Qualitative outcomes include increased system resilience, innovation in healthcare logistics and a scalable foundation for future autonomous delivery networks.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: UNIC – Blockchain- and Metaverse-Based University Course
column4: Universities are increasingly challenged to provide innovative, globally accessible education while ensuring the authenticity and portability of academic credentials. The University of Nicosia (UNIC) addressed this by redesigning how a course is delivered, accessed, and certified. The initiative involves the university, students worldwide, academic staff, and external experts from the digital economy, with the objective of experimenting with new models of online learning, certification, and learner engagement. The course “Introduction to NFTs and the Metaverse” is delivered entirely through decentralised digital environments, removing reliance on traditional learning management systems. Registration is enabled through the minting of a free course token, which grants access to learning materials and activities hosted in virtual spaces. Course participation, progress, and completion are recorded through blockchain-based mechanisms, and learners can optionally obtain a completion certificate issued as a non-fungible token on Ethereum. The impact has been significant, with more than 14,000 registrants, demonstrating global reach and strong engagement. The model enhances transparency, verifiability, and learner ownership of credentials, while reducing administrative overhead. It provides UNIC with a scalable, future-oriented education model and offers learners a portable, tamper-proof record of achievement aligned with emerging digital economies.

column2: MareNostrum-Ona, Europe's first quantum annealer
column4: Europe faces increasing demand for advanced computing capabilities to address complex optimisation problems that are difficult to solve using conventional supercomputing alone, particularly in areas such as logistics, materials science and advanced analytics. The objective of MareNostrum-Ona is to strengthen European technological sovereignty by providing researchers, industry and public sector organisations with access to novel quantum computing resources. The implementation involves the development of an analogue quantum annealer hosted at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center and integrated into the MareNostrum 5 infrastructure. The initial system will operate with at least ten physical qubits and will be progressively expanded in subsequent generations. It is designed to complement classical high-performance computing by addressing optimisation tasks through quantum annealing techniques. The project is co-funded by European and national partners and developed in collaboration with leading research institutions. Expected impacts include expanded research capabilities, early industrial experimentation with quantum optimisation and accelerated knowledge transfer within Europe. Qualitative outcomes include improved readiness for quantum adoption, strengthened collaboration between academia and industry, and a scalable foundation for future quantum–HPC hybrid systems.
column8: European High Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC JU)
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: DeepHealth - Deep-Learning and HPC to Boost Biomedical Applications for Health
column4: Biomedical research and healthcare increasingly depend on the ability to analyse large, complex datasets to support diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment, yet existing computing infrastructures often limit performance and scalability. Researchers and clinicians require integrated environments that can efficiently process imaging, genomic, and clinical data across diverse applications. This use case addresses these needs by supporting advanced biomedical applications through a unified computational framework, involving research institutions, healthcare providers, and technology partners, with the objective of accelerating innovation in diagnosis and treatment. The DeepHealth project implements a framework that combines high-performance computing infrastructures with deep learning and computer vision techniques, optimised to run on heterogeneous HPC and big data architectures. It supports data-intensive workflows and enables large-scale training and inference across multiple biomedical domains, piloted through 15 real-world applications. The project improves processing speed, scalability, and analytical accuracy, enabling more efficient analysis of complex biomedical data. It reduces time to insight, supports more precise clinical decision-making, and demonstrates how advanced computing infrastructures can be effectively leveraged to deliver tangible benefits in health research and clinical practice.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: PILOTING - PILOTs for robotic INspection and maintenance Grounded on advanced intelligent platforms and prototype applications
column4: The PILOTING project (PILOTs for robotic INspection and maintenance Grounded on advanced intelligent platforms and prototype applications) was an EU-funded initiative under the Horizon 2020 programme, running from January 2020 to December 2023. Its primary aim was to enhance the safety and efficiency of inspections and maintenance (I&M) for ageing European infrastructures, such as bridges, tunnels, and oil & gas refineries. To achieve this, PILOTING developed an integrated platform that combined aerial drones, ground robots, crawlers, and artificial intelligence. These technologies were tested in three large-scale pilot scenarios across the oil & gas and civil infrastructure sectors. The project sought to reduce operational costs, improve inspection quality, and increase worker safety, while also contributing to the development of industrial standards for robotics in I&M.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: FABULOS (Future Automated Bus Urban Level Operation Systems)
column4: The FABULOS (Future Automated Bus Urban Level Operation Systems) project was a European initiative funded under the Horizon 2020 programme, running from January 2018 to March 2021. Its goal was to develop and demonstrate autonomous last-mile public transport solutions using self-driving minibuses integrated into existing urban transport systems. Through a Pre-Commercial Procurement process, the project engaged multiple consortia to design, prototype, and pilot autonomous shuttle services in six European cities: Helsinki (Finland), Gjesdal (Norway), Tallinn (Estonia), Lamia (Greece), Helmond (Netherlands), and another city in Portugal. These pilots aimed to assess the technical, economic, and societal viability of automated public transport, addressing challenges such as integration with current infrastructure, regulatory compliance, and public acceptance. The project contributed to advancing autonomous mobility solutions and provided valuable insights for cities considering the adoption of such technologies.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI-EFFECT, Advancing AI Integration in the European Energy Sector - Multi energy and sector coupling
column4: Energy systems are becoming increasingly complex as electricity, heating, and other energy vectors must be coordinated to reduce emissions while maintaining reliability and affordability. In regions such as Jutland and Bornholm, energy operators face challenges in optimising district heating and power grid operations simultaneously. This use case addresses the need for better coordination between energy systems, involving grid operators, heating network operators, and energy stakeholders, with the objective of improving efficiency and moving towards near-zero-emission energy supply. The initiative implements advanced analytical and forecasting tools to optimise sector coupling between electricity grids and district heating networks. These tools support demand forecasting, plant operation optimisation, and coordinated control across multiple energy vectors, validated through targeted demonstrations. The approach enables more efficient use of renewable energy, lowers operational costs, and reduces CO₂ emissions. It strengthens system flexibility, improves reliability of district heating operations, and provides a validated pathway for deploying advanced optimisation tools across multi-energy systems in Europe.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI-EFFECT, Advancing AI Integration in the European Energy Sector - Transmission Congestion Management Laboratory
column4: Electricity transmission networks face growing congestion due to renewable integration, electrification, and increased demand, placing pressure on control rooms to manage grid stability in real time. Grid operators require safe environments to test and validate new operational approaches without risking live systems. This use case addresses these challenges by supporting the development and validation of congestion management solutions, involving transmission system operators, researchers, and control room specialists, with the objective of improving grid resilience and operational effectiveness. The Transmission Congestion Management Laboratory extends TU Delft’s Control Room of the Future with capabilities for synthetic grid data generation and algorithm testing. A digital-physical test environment enables rigorous verification of congestion management strategies, including sequential topological reconfigurations, under controlled conditions. The facility improves the robustness and reliability of congestion management solutions, reduces operational risk, and accelerates innovation by allowing experimentation before real-world deployment. It delivers clear value by strengthening grid security, supporting operator confidence, and enabling scalable validation of next-generation grid control tools.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI-EFFECT, Advancing AI Integration in the European Energy Sector - Energy efficiency, management and sharing for local energy communities
column4: Local energy communities are playing a growing role in the energy transition, yet effective energy sharing and optimisation are hindered by fragmented data access, privacy concerns, and limited consumer engagement. Traditional data platforms led by system operators are not fully designed for collaborative, user-centric energy services. This use case addresses these barriers by enabling secure, trusted data sharing for local energy communities, involving consumers, grid operators, service providers, and technology developers, with the objective of improving energy efficiency and supporting new community-based services. The initiative implements an Energy Data Space that provides a common framework for sharing energy data with explicit consent, supported by governance, security, and certification mechanisms. This environment enables the development and testing of data-driven services for energy management, e-mobility, and smart appliances, while respecting privacy and data sovereignty. The approach increases consumer trust and participation, supports more flexible grid management, and fosters cross-sector innovation. It delivers improved energy efficiency, better use of local renewable resources, and scalable foundations for community-driven energy systems.
column8: INESC TEC - Institute for Systems and Computer Engineering, Technology and Science
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI-EFFECT, Advancing AI Integration in the European Energy Sector - Distribution Network Congestion Management for Renewable Integration
column4: Electricity distribution networks are under increasing strain as renewable generation, electrification, and decentralised energy resources grow, leading to congestion and operational complexity at local grid level. Distribution system operators need more accurate, scalable, and real-time tools to manage congestion while maintaining high standards of safety, reliability, and security. This use case addresses these challenges by supporting the integration of advanced decision-support tools into distribution network operations, involving grid operators, engineers, and technology providers, with the objective of enabling efficient renewable integration and improved congestion management as part of business-as-usual operations. The solution is implemented through a flexible and reconfigurable laboratory environment that emulates real cyber-physical distribution systems, allowing advanced analytical and learning-based models to be developed, tested, and validated before deployment. The setup integrates simulators, synthetic data generation, and distribution system IT and OT components to assess performance, robustness, scalability, and real-time behaviour under realistic scenarios. This approach reduces operational risk, supports seamless integration with classical engineering methods, and accelerates innovation. It delivers improved grid efficiency, higher hosting capacity for renewables, stronger decision-making capabilities, and a validated framework for safely scaling advanced congestion management solutions across European distribution networks.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: ChatAmsterdam - AI assistant for civil servants
column4: Municipal employees increasingly work with large volumes of text, policy documents and complex information, which places pressure on time, consistency and quality of administrative work. At the same time, public organisations must ensure that sensitive information is handled securely and in line with public values. In the City of Amsterdam, civil servants required a trusted internal tool to support everyday tasks without relying on external commercial services that process data outside municipal control. The objective of this pilot is to improve productivity and information handling for municipal staff while maintaining full data sovereignty and compliance with public sector standards. The implementation delivers an internal digital assistant deployed within the municipality’s secure infrastructure. The system supports tasks such as drafting texts, summarising documents and analysing complex material, while ensuring that all inputs and outputs remain within the city’s digital environment and are not reused externally. The assistant was developed in alignment with the city’s Agenda Digitale Stad and integrated into existing work processes. During the six-month pilot, more than 800 civil servants used the tool for over 34,000 assignments. Results indicate time savings, improved consistency in document handling and high user acceptance. Qualitative impacts include increased trust in digital tools, enhanced staff efficiency and a reusable model that can be shared with other public administrations to support a resilient, human-centred digital public sector.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: LOQUI - participation by speech
column4: Digital public services increasingly rely on text-based interfaces, which creates significant barriers for people with language deficits, low literacy levels, visual impairments or limited digital skills, including new Dutch citizens and status holders. These barriers reduce access to essential services and limit participation in digital society. The objective of this pilot is to enable inclusive access to digital government services through speech-based interaction. The implementation develops a multilingual speech control tool supporting Dutch, English and Arabic, allowing users to navigate and complete digital services using voice commands. The system recognises speech input, identifies language difficulties in real time and adapts interactions accordingly, enabling more intuitive and accessible service use. Integration with existing digital government platforms allows speech to act as a universal interface layer rather than a separate service. The pilot demonstrated improved accessibility for vulnerable user groups, reduced dependency on intermediaries and greater autonomy in interacting with public services. Qualitative impacts include enhanced digital inclusion, improved user confidence and a scalable approach for making digital public services accessible to diverse populations.

column2: VITE-A2 Video and Text AI Analysis for Political Debates
column4: Municipal council meetings are essential democratic forums, yet their length and complexity often discourage citizen engagement and limit transparency. In the Netherlands, local governments sought ways to make political debates more accessible to the public. The objective of this pilot is to improve understanding of municipal decision-making by transforming complex discussions into clear and engaging summaries. The implementation analyses video and textual recordings of council meetings, extracting key topics, arguments and emotional cues through multimodal analysis techniques. Outputs are presented in simplified formats that highlight relevant discussion points and sentiments, supporting easier interpretation by citizens. The pilot produced a paper prototype and feasibility studies informed by stakeholder feedback. Qualitative results include improved accessibility of political information, increased civic awareness and stronger potential for citizen engagement. The project demonstrates how advanced analysis of public meetings can support transparency and democratic participation at local level.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Accelerat.AI - Accelerating digital transformation in Portugal
column4: Public administrations and businesses in Portugal face rising demand for efficient, accessible customer service across multiple channels, while language diversity, regional accents, and ageing populations limit the effectiveness of generic digital solutions. Accelerat.AI addresses this challenge by modernising customer service delivery in both public and private sectors, involving government bodies, enterprises, technology providers, and citizens, with the objective of significantly increasing automation while improving user experience in European Portuguese. The initiative is implemented through the development of advanced conversational agents and Contact Center as a Service platforms tailored to linguistic and demographic variation, trained on large-scale, high-quality language and speech datasets. These systems are integrated into existing customer service infrastructures to support voice and text interactions, automated case handling, and escalation to human agents when required. Funded with €35 million under Portugal’s Recovery and Resilience Plan and Next Generation EU, the project targets automation of up to 80% of customer interactions. The initiative delivers substantial efficiency gains, reduces operational costs, improves service accessibility, and strengthens digital sovereignty by developing language technologies aligned with European values and local needs.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: FalAI - A Dataset for End-to-end Spoken Language Understanding in a Low-Resource Scenario
column4: Many European languages remain underrepresented in voice-based digital services, limiting access to technology for speakers of minority languages. In Galicia, the lack of large, high-quality datasets constrained the development of conversational systems in Galician and Spanish. The objective of this pilot is to promote linguistic diversity and digital inclusion by creating open datasets for spoken language understanding. The implementation mobilised large-scale citizen participation through the #A30frases campaign, collecting voice recordings of prototype sentences across public sector domains such as healthcare and social care. The resulting dataset exceeds 260,000 recordings from around 12,000 contributors and is openly available for research and development. The pilot significantly improved the availability of training data for low-resource languages, supporting the development of inclusive voice technologies. Qualitative impacts include strengthened linguistic equality, increased research capacity and long-term benefits for public service accessibility across Europe.
column8: AtlanTTic research centre of the University of Vigo
