column2: iMaging Project - Beach Monitoring
column4: As part of the EU-funded iMagine project, a prototype service has been developed to enhance beach monitoring through the use of artificial intelligence. Led by SOCIB, the initiative uses deep learning image segmentation to automatically analyze video feeds from beach cameras. The system focuses on detecting and monitoring Posidonia oceanica berms, which serve as indicators of coastal health, and identifying rip currents, assessing their position and characteristics. By enabling near real-time analysis of beach imagery, the service supports coastal management efforts and improves public safety. The prototype runs from September 2022 to August 2025.
column8: SOCIB - Balearic Islands Ocean Observing and Forecasting System

column2: iMaging Project - Ecosystem Monitoring at EMSO Sites by Video Imagery
column4: Marine ecosystem monitoring relies on continuous observation, yet manual analysis of underwater video footage is time-consuming and limits the ability to detect changes in biodiversity and environmental conditions at scale. Research infrastructures such as EMSO observatories require automated tools to transform large volumes of raw video into actionable scientific insights. This use case addresses these needs by supporting ecosystem monitoring at subsea observatory sites, involving marine researchers, infrastructure operators, and data scientists, with the objective of improving real-time understanding of marine environments. The solution is implemented through a video-based monitoring service that processes underwater imagery using deep-learning models for object detection and quality filtering. Deployed across EMSO sites including Obsea, the Azores, and SmartBay, the service identifies marine species, flags low-quality footage, and detects notable events such as prawn burrows, converting unstructured video into standardised, analysable datasets. Integrated data pipelines deliver near-real-time outputs to researchers and support reuse across observatories. The approach significantly reduces manual workload, improves consistency of observations, and enhances the timeliness and scale of ecosystem monitoring. It strengthens biodiversity assessment, supports long-term environmental research, and provides a scalable foundation for data-driven marine science.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: iMaging Project - WITOIL Oil spill detection service
column4:
As part of the EU-funded iMagine project, the EyeOnWater initiative addresses the challenge of ensuring the quality and reliability of citizen-contributed data for environmental monitoring. Through a smartphone application, citizens submit photographs of natural water bodies such as rivers, lakes, and seas to support large-scale observation of water colour and related ecological indicators. Key stakeholders include citizens, environmental scientists, and research institutions that rely on consistent, high-quality visual data. The objective of the use case is to validate incoming images efficiently and at scale so that only suitable, scientifically usable photographs are incorporated into long-term environmental datasets.
The service uses computer vision techniques to automatically assess the suitability of submitted images before they enter scientific workflows. Image segmentation and object detection models, including YOLOv8, are applied to identify whether an image contains a water body and to detect obstructions or irrelevant content. The models are trained on a labeled dataset of more than 10,000 images categorized into different quality classes. The validation pipeline is integrated into the mobile application backend, enabling near real-time processing and automated decision-making on image acceptance or rejection.
The automated validation process significantly improves the consistency and reliability of citizen science data while reducing the need for manual review. Real-time filtering ensures that only high-quality images are added to long-term water colour datasets, increasing their scientific value and usability. The approach enhances scalability by allowing large volumes of submissions to be processed efficiently, supports faster data availability for researchers, and improves the user experience by providing immediate feedback on submissions. Overall, the solution strengthens the contribution of citizen science to environmental monitoring and ecological research.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: iMaging Project - EyeOnWater app
column4: As part of the EU-funded iMagine project, the Oil Spill Detection service—also known as WITOIL—aims to automate the identification and monitoring of marine oil spills using satellite imagery. Operational on the iMagine platform, the service employs deep learning models trained on labelled data from Sentinel 1, 2, and Landsat 8 satellites to enhance the detection capabilities of existing tools like the OKEANOS system. Running from September 2022 to August 2025, WITOIL supports automated spill detection, fuel spill modeling, and alerting, and offers documentation and training resources to help researchers and decision-makers adopt the system effectively .
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Consular Digital Triage - Automating consular enquiry responses
column4: British nationals abroad frequently contact consular services for urgent but routine issues such as lost passports, emergency travel documents, or eligibility for support, creating high volumes of written and telephone enquiries for the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO). The Consular Digital Triage use case was introduced to reduce this operational pressure while ensuring that citizens receive fast, accurate, and safe guidance. The primary stakeholders include British nationals overseas, consular staff, and central FCDO operations teams, with the objective of resolving common enquiries at first contact and allowing human staff to focus on complex or sensitive cases. The solution is implemented as a digital assistant that classifies incoming written enquiries and matches them to pre-approved guidance templates rather than generating open-ended responses. This rules- and model-based approach ensures consistency, policy compliance, and safety. The system integrates with existing consular contact channels and knowledge bases, enabling rapid response without manual intervention. The impact has been substantial: written enquiries were reduced by around 80% and calls by up to 50% within three months of deployment. Over time, the tool is projected to save the FCDO millions of pounds while improving response times, maintaining trust, and enhancing the overall experience for citizens abroad.

column2: Barking and Dagenham AI Search and Webchat - Streamlining access to council services and information through AI-powered search and chat
column4: Residents often struggle to navigate council websites or contact centres to find accurate information on local services, leading to high call volumes and repeated enquiries. Barking and Dagenham Council introduced AI Search and Webchat to streamline access to council services, starting with waste collection and expanding to areas such as housing and council tax. Stakeholders include residents, customer service teams, and service departments, with the objective of resolving most routine questions digitally while preserving accessibility. The solution combines an AI-powered search interface and a conversational webchat tool that uses structured service data, including postcode-based logic, to deliver personalised responses. Both interfaces are integrated with council content systems and continuously refined through community feedback and monitoring. Traditional channels, such as phone and face-to-face support, remain available to ensure inclusivity. The impact has been measurable: the AI Search tool now handles around 18,000 sessions per month with a high first-time hit rate, while the chatbot manages approximately 4,450 conversations monthly with a 93% success rate based on user feedback. Together, the tools have significantly reduced general enquiries and call volumes, improved resident satisfaction, and enabled council staff to focus on more complex cases.

column2: Aila: AI Lesson Assistant for UK teachers
column4: Teachers face significant workload pressures when preparing high-quality, curriculum-aligned lesson plans tailored to diverse student needs. Oak National Academy developed Aila to support teachers in lesson planning while maintaining pedagogical quality and alignment with national curricula. Stakeholders include classroom teachers, school leaders, and curriculum designers, with the objective of saving time, reducing stress, and improving teaching consistency. Aila is implemented as a conversational lesson assistant that combines a large language model with retrieval-augmented generation, drawing exclusively on Oak’s quality-assured lesson repository. Teachers interact with Aila via a chat interface to generate lesson outlines, which can be iteratively refined to suit class context and teaching style. The system is designed to ensure factual accuracy and curriculum relevance by grounding outputs in trusted educational content. The impact has been strong: in its first two months, Aila supported 10,000 users in creating nearly 25,000 lesson plans, with 85% of teachers rating the quality as fairly high or very high. Nearly two-thirds reported reduced workload, with average time savings of around three hours and some saving up to 15 hours. Teachers also reported improved well-being, demonstrating clear educational and workforce value.
column8: Department for Education (DfE) / Oak National Academy

column2: GCS Assist - Accelerating content creation for government communicators
column4: Government communicators must produce high-quality, timely content aligned with established standards and frameworks, often under tight deadlines. GCS Assist was developed in-house to support members of the UK Government Communication Service by accelerating routine content creation and review tasks. The key stakeholders are government communicators across departments, with the objective of saving time while maintaining consistency, accuracy, and best practice. Assist is implemented as a secure internal tool featuring pre-built, task-specific prompts tailored to communication disciplines such as press notices, stakeholder briefings, and campaign planning. The system enables users to brainstorm ideas, draft initial content, and review materials more efficiently than manual processes. Its design embeds Government Communication Service guidance and frameworks directly into workflows, ensuring outputs align with professional standards. Since going live in April 2025, following a prototype phase in late 2023, Assist has enabled communicators to complete tasks in a fraction of the time previously required. The impact includes faster turnaround times, improved productivity, and reduced administrative burden, allowing communication professionals to focus more on strategic thinking and stakeholder engagement rather than repetitive drafting tasks.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: Magic Notes - Assisting social care workers through transcription and note-taking
column4: Social care professionals spend a significant proportion of their time on administrative documentation, reducing the time available for direct engagement with clients and contributing to workload pressure and burnout. In regional social care services in Wales, there was a need to reduce administrative burden while improving the quality of assessments. The objective of this pilot is to support social workers by automating transcription and structured note-taking during client interactions. The implementation deploys a speech-based system that records conversations, produces accurate transcripts and generates structured summaries aligned with assessment requirements. The tool integrates into existing workflows and allows practitioners to review and edit outputs. During the pilot, practitioners saved on average 25 minutes per assessment and approximately 7.5 hours per week, submitting assessments up to eight hours faster. Accuracy exceeded 97%, and users reported more natural conversations and higher-quality assessments. Qualitative impacts include reduced case backlogs, improved workload distribution and enhanced practitioner wellbeing. The pilot demonstrates significant productivity gains and improved service quality in social care delivery.

column2: Logo Detection and Classification Toolkit (LDK) - Automating the detection of unauthorised logo use on websites
column4: Unauthorised use of official government logos on fraudulent or misleading websites poses risks to citizens and undermines trust in public institutions. HM Revenue & Customs, working with central government partners, developed the Logo Detection and Classification Toolkit to automate the identification of such misuse. Stakeholders include enforcement analysts, government departments, and the public, with the objective of detecting and removing infringing websites quickly and accurately. LDK is implemented as an automated pipeline that scans the web via search engines, scrapes suspicious sites, and applies a deep-learning-based computer vision model to detect government logos. The system flags likely infringements for analyst review, integrating seamlessly into existing enforcement workflows. By automating what was previously a manual and time-consuming process, LDK reduces detection time from days to hours. The impact is significant: analysts can now take down 10–20 infringing URLs per month, with accuracy exceeding 97%. This improves public protection, strengthens brand integrity, and delivers substantial efficiency gains for enforcement teams.

column2: SAM - Service AI Assistant Module - Streamlining routine queries through AI-powered contact centre tools
column4: Local authorities receive high volumes of routine enquiries about services such as waste collection, council tax and basic administrative procedures, placing sustained pressure on contact centres and frontline staff. Residents increasingly expect immediate, round-the-clock access to information. The objective of this pilot is to improve customer service efficiency while maintaining service quality and accessibility. The implementation introduces a conversational assistant that handles routine enquiries through automated dialogue and integrates with backend council systems to retrieve accurate, up-to-date information. The assistant operates continuously and escalates complex queries to human agents when necessary. Early testing showed response accuracy of approximately 93% and user satisfaction levels between 86% and 90%, based on direct feedback. The pilot reduced call centre workload, shortened response times and improved service availability for residents. Qualitative benefits include better staff focus on complex cases, improved citizen experience and a scalable solution for digital customer service in local government. The pilot highlights the value of automated assistance in enhancing public service responsiveness and operational efficiency.

column2: Mia - Streamlining breast cancer screening with AI
column4: Breast cancer screening programmes face growing demand while radiology departments experience workforce shortages and increasing workloads, which can affect screening throughput and consistency. Radiologists must review large volumes of mammograms, often under time pressure, while ensuring that cases requiring further investigation are reliably identified. The objective of this pilot is to support clinicians in breast cancer screening by improving efficiency and maintaining high diagnostic standards. The implementation introduces an assisted assessment system that automatically analyses mammography images and provides recommendations on whether a case should be recalled for further examination. The system is integrated into existing screening workflows, supporting radiologists during image review rather than replacing clinical judgement. It processes large datasets of anonymised mammograms to detect patterns associated with potential abnormalities and highlights cases that warrant closer attention. The pilot demonstrated improved workflow efficiency, reduced reading time per case and more consistent prioritisation of high-risk images. Qualitative impacts include better workload management for radiologists, increased confidence in screening decisions and enhanced capacity of screening programmes. The pilot supports scalability of breast cancer screening services while preserving clinical oversight and patient safety.

column2: Review Automoderation Tool - Automating content moderation on NHS.UK
column4: NHS.UK receives around 100,000 user reviews each year, all of which must comply with strict moderation policies on personal data, abuse, discrimination, and safeguarding. Manual moderation by third-party reviewers led to delays and high costs, and many rejected reviews were never resubmitted, reducing valuable patient feedback. NHS England introduced the Review Automoderation Tool to address these challenges. Stakeholders include patients, NHS content teams, and service providers, with the objective of accelerating moderation while maintaining safety and quality. The tool automatically assesses reviews against moderation criteria, flagging issues for human oversight where necessary. Integrated into NHS.UK workflows, it processes reviews in seconds rather than days. The impact includes expert-level accuracy, dramatically reduced moderation times, and lower operational costs. Importantly, faster moderation has improved publication rates, preserving more patient feedback and enhancing transparency. The tool supports safer, more responsive digital services while ensuring NHS standards are upheld.
column8: Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) / NHS England (NHSE)

column2: Warm Home Discount Energy Cost Predictor - Modelling energy costs to determine eligibility for Warm Home Discount scheme
column4: Identifying households eligible for energy cost support is complex, particularly when relying on self-application processes that disadvantage less informed or vulnerable citizens. The Warm Home Discount Energy Cost Predictor was developed to improve targeting of support under the UK’s Warm Home Discount scheme. Stakeholders include the Department for Energy Security & Net Zero, energy suppliers, and low-income households, with the objective of issuing rebates more fairly and efficiently. The solution uses a statistical prediction model to estimate household energy costs based on property characteristics such as size, age, and type. By integrating these estimates with income-related data, the system identifies high-cost, low-income households proactively. The impact has been substantial: in 2023/24, over 90% of the 3 million rebates were issued automatically. This removed the first-come, first-served approach and ensured vulnerable households received support even if they were unaware of the scheme. The result is a fairer, more inclusive system with improved policy effectiveness and reduced administrative burden.
column8: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)

column2: Stroke AI - Interpreting brain scans with AI to improve outcomes for stroke patients
column4: Stroke treatment is extremely time-sensitive, and delays in diagnosis can have severe consequences for patient outcomes. Stroke AI was introduced across England to support clinical teams in rapidly interpreting brain scans and making treatment decisions. Stakeholders include clinicians, hospitals, and patients, with the objective of improving speed, accuracy, and consistency in stroke care. The solution analyses brain imaging data to support identification of stroke type and severity, integrating into existing radiology and clinical workflows. It provides decision support to clinicians rather than replacing clinical judgement. The impact is national in scale: all 107 stroke centres in England now use the technology, supporting care for around 80,000 stroke patients each year. Faster and more accurate assessments enable timely interventions, improving survival rates and long-term outcomes. The system demonstrates how advanced digital tools can enhance clinical capacity and deliver significant public health benefits in critical care settings.
column8: Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) / NHS England (NHSE)

column2: ADRYX - Chatbot for real-time flight updates
column4: Passengers at large international airports often require up-to-date information on flights, services, and facilities, placing pressure on customer service teams. Aeroporti di Roma launched the AdRyX chatbot at Rome Fiumicino Airport to enhance the digital passenger experience. Stakeholders include travellers, airport operators, and commercial partners, with the objective of delivering instant, multilingual information. AdRyX is implemented as part of the Digiport platform and operates via popular messaging channels such as WhatsApp and Facebook Messenger. It provides real-time flight updates, wayfinding support, and recommendations for shops and restaurants, responding in Italian and English. Integrated with airport operational systems, the chatbot delivers accurate, timely responses without human intervention. Since its launch in June 2024, AdRyX has marked a significant step forward in digital service delivery, improving passenger satisfaction, reducing routine enquiries, and supporting smoother airport operations.

column2: Faneque Double Tunnel - improving traffic with Automatic Incident Detection (AID) system
column4: Major road infrastructure projects must ensure smooth traffic flow and rapid incident response to maintain safety and minimise congestion, particularly in confined environments such as tunnels. The completion of the Faneque double tunnel on Gran Canaria’s La Aldea highway required advanced monitoring to manage increased traffic volumes and reduce travel times. The objective of this pilot is to enhance road safety and operational efficiency through early detection of traffic incidents. The implementation deployed an automatic incident detection system that analyses live video feeds from tunnel cameras to identify events such as stopped vehicles, accidents or abnormal traffic behaviour. The system operates continuously and integrates with traffic management platforms to alert operators in real time, enabling faster intervention. The pilot improved incident response times, reduced secondary accidents and supported smoother traffic flow through the tunnel. Qualitative impacts include increased safety for drivers, reduced congestion-related delays and improved reliability of critical transport infrastructure. The project demonstrates how advanced traffic monitoring can enhance resilience and efficiency in complex road environments.

column2: Digitising university degrees with blockchain
column4: Launched in September 2019, the project by Cineca, MIT, and Italian universities (Padua and Milano-Bicocca) uses blockchain and the Blockcerts standard to issue digital, verifiable academic credentials. It supports stackable learning, international mobility, and reduces bureaucracy by replacing traditional diplomas with secure, portable digital certificates, redefining the concept of a university degree for the digital age.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: EUIPO connects to TMview and DesignView through blockchain
column4: In 2021, the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) integrated blockchain technology into its TMview and DesignView platforms to enhance the transparency, security, and efficiency of IP data exchange. By connecting national IP offices through a distributed ledger, the system ensures real-time updates, immutable records, and improved interoperability. Users can now access authenticated histories of trademarks and designs, with future features planned such as downloadable blockchain certificates and an IP Wallet.

column2: MADRE: a Banque de France blockchain project
column4: In April 2018, the Banque de France launched its pioneering MADRE project, the first blockchain system brought into production by a central bank. MADRE uses a private Ethereum-based blockchain to decentralize the SEPA Creditor Identifier (ICS) register. This shift from a centralized database allows participating banks to receive ICS codes in minutes instead of days, automates processes via smart contracts, and reduces costs, paperwork, and manual oversight.
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: Smart Ivrea - Blockchain Technology in the Management of Smart Communities
column4: Smart cities require coordination across energy, mobility and citizen services, yet fragmented systems often limit transparency and trust. Smart Ivrea, led by the Italian Agency for Digital Italy (AGiD), explores how distributed ledger technology can support integrated management of smart communities. Stakeholders include local authorities, service providers and citizens. The objective is to enhance transparency, coordination and participation in urban services. The project uses the Quadrans blockchain platform, an open-source, public and decentralised infrastructure, to record and manage community-related transactions and interactions. Blockchain enables verifiable records of service usage, resource distribution and citizen engagement, while smart contracts support automated processes across domains such as energy sharing and mobility services. The pilot demonstrates how decentralised infrastructure can reduce fragmentation, improve accountability and foster citizen trust. Although still experimental, Smart Ivrea provides a replicable model for municipalities seeking to integrate emerging technologies into sustainable urban governance.

column2: Two italian universities partner to build the quantum computers of the future
column4: Maintaining competitiveness in advanced computing technologies requires long-term investment, coordinated research and strong collaboration between academic and industrial actors. In Italy, fragmented efforts and limited shared infrastructure have slowed progress in emerging computing fields. The objective of this initiative is to establish a robust national ecosystem capable of designing, developing and scaling next-generation quantum computing technologies. The partnership, launched in June 2025, brings together Planckian—a joint spin-off of the University of Pisa and the Scuola Normale—and the QTLab centre of the Federico II University of Naples, aligning scientific research, engineering capabilities and infrastructure development. The implementation focuses on joint research programmes, shared laboratories and coordinated technology roadmaps covering hardware design, control systems and integration with existing high-performance computing environments. The initiative also fosters collaboration with industry and public research organisations to accelerate technology transfer and skills development. The expected impact includes strengthened national research capacity, improved access to cutting-edge infrastructure and increased attractiveness for industrial partnerships. Qualitative outcomes include accelerated innovation cycles, enhanced talent development and a strategic foundation for Italy’s long-term technological sovereignty in quantum computing.

column2: AI use in the City of Kastav
column4: Municipal administrations often manage competitive funding and co-financing processes that involve large volumes of documentation, manual evaluation and tight deadlines, placing strain on staff and reducing transparency. In the City of Kastav, authorities sought to modernise the tender submission and evaluation process while supporting fair and efficient decision-making. The objective of this pilot is to partially automate administrative workflows and improve the evaluation of funding applications. The implementation developed a prototype system that enables digital submission of tender documents, automatic extraction of key information and structured summarisation of project proposals. Applications are assessed against predefined criteria, generating evaluation proposals and ranked outputs that can be exported for further review by commission members. The system integrates with existing administrative tools and supports human oversight throughout the process. The pilot reduced administrative workload, shortened evaluation timelines and improved consistency in scoring. Qualitative benefits include greater transparency, improved traceability of decisions and enhanced efficiency for municipal staff, providing a scalable model for digital transformation in local public administration.

column2: AI-Code - GenAi against disinformation: Use case 3
column4: Online disinformation increasingly relies on short-form videos, posing challenges for media professionals tasked with identifying coordinated influence campaigns. AI-CODE, a Horizon Europe project, addresses this issue through dedicated use cases. Use Case 2 focuses on detecting covert foreign influence in video-based social media content. Stakeholders include journalists, fact-checkers and media organisations. The objective is to improve the efficiency and accuracy of disinformation monitoring at scale. The solution automates the analysis of large volumes of videos from platforms such as TikTok and YouTube Shorts, extracting visual, audio and contextual features. Advanced models flag anomalies, coordinated patterns and potentially harmful sources for further human investigation. By reducing manual screening effort, the tools enable faster response to emerging information attacks. The pilot strengthens media resilience by combining automation with editorial oversight, contributing to more effective protection of the public information space.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI-Code - GenAi against disinformation: Use case 2
column4: Media organisations increasingly experiment with generative tools, yet concerns remain about quality, bias and the risk of amplifying disinformation. AI-CODE Use Case 3 addresses this challenge by supporting responsible use of generative technologies in journalism. Stakeholders include journalists, editors and media trainers. The objective is to improve content quality while strengthening professional awareness of generative risks. The use case delivers tools such as an interactive simulator and an AI-based personal companion that help media professionals test scenarios, identify misinformation and refine editorial judgement. Rather than automating publishing, the tools focus on skill development and decision support. Early validation shows improved understanding of generative limitations and increased confidence in identifying misleading content. This use case contributes to building long-term trust in media by ensuring that emerging technologies reinforce, rather than undermine, journalistic standards.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Diia.Engine - Ukrainian open-source platform for the public sector digitalisation
column4: Diia.Engine is a national low-code, open-source platform developed by Ukraine to support the digitalisation of public services. It was introduced in response to challenges identified during a review of the country’s administrative systems, which included fragmentation, reliance on paper-based processes, and lack of interoperability among existing digital solutions. The platform enables the development of digital public services using modular components and emerging technologies, including AI tools such as chatbots and automated validators. These components aim to reduce development time and operational costs while supporting standardisation and scalability across government institutions. Diia.Engine is currently used by 23 government agencies and municipalities. Over 40 digital services have been developed, with more than 80% utilising standardised modules. Approximately 40% of the population has accessed services built on the platform. The platform also includes an AI-powered chatbot integrated into the state address registry, with over 4,700 active users and a reported satisfaction score of 4.69 out of 5. Environmental impact assessments suggest that reusing existing components can reduce electricity consumption and infrastructure usage by a factor of 3–5. Estimates indicate that deploying 100 registries via Diia.Engine could result in annual savings of up to USD 50 million. Diia.Engine has been incorporated into the CDTO Campus curriculum and is available for international replication through a documented toolkit. It is also part of Ukraine’s broader WINWIN Global Innovation Strategy, which includes goals related to AI integration in the public sector and the establishment of the WINWIN AI Centre of Excellence.

column2: TRACY: A big-data analytics platform for law enforcement investigations
column4: The TRACY project was implemented to address the growing need for secure, efficient, and interoperable use of electronic communications metadata in the investigation of serious and organised crime across EU Member States. Law Enforcement Authorities (LEAs) often face fragmentation in accessing and analyzing telecom data, which hinders cross-border cooperation and slows down criminal investigations. TRACY provides an AI-powered data analytics framework tailored for LEAs, improving the timeliness, accuracy, and interoperability of investigations. Preliminary results indicate a significant improvement in processing time and data correlation across jurisdictions. Pilot implementation of TRACY for 6 months in real cases demonstrated a 1000x acceleration in crime resolution. With the use of visual analytics TRACY manages to pinpoint the perpetrators in all cases outperforming manual resolution.  TRACY received positive feedback from over 80% of end-users who rated the tools as user-friendly and operationally relevant. Qualitative feedback also highlighted increased confidence in the legal and ethical compliance of the TRACY system, thanks to its embedded privacy-by-design and auditability features. The solution has achieved its core objectives of (1) demonstrating the feasibility of harmonized metadata analysis workflows, (2) equipping LEAs with scalable analytics tools, and (3) informing EU-wide training and standardisation efforts. By addressing both technological and procedural gaps, TRACY contributes to a more resilient, interoperable, and intelligence-led law enforcement ecosystem in Europe.

column2: Robic: Inclusive AI for Special Learning
column4: This pilot project explores the feasibility and educational impact of an AI-supported social robotics solution in a special education setting. Implemented at Colegio Gaudem in Madrid, the initiative aims to enhance the personal, cognitive, and emotional development of students with special educational needs through personalised, structured interactions using Inrobic Clinic and Inrobic Virtual. Between November 2024 and March 2025, 300 sessions were conducted involving 28 students, mostly in group formats. High satisfaction scores were reported by educators and families, with notable improvements in communication, motor skills, and engagement across subjects like math and language. Students responded positively, treating the robot as a peer and showing increased motivation. Teachers integrated the solution smoothly into their practices, and families expressed strong interest in its continuation. Early findings support the value and effectiveness of AI-assisted robotics in promoting inclusive and adaptive education.

column2: Interoperability Platform - Finnish metadata platform for semantic linked data specifications as a service
column4: Interoperability platform is a metadata portal, from which the users can find, what kind of data there is available, who provides it, and what is the structure of the data. When the users find useful metadata descriptions, they do not have to re-invent that information, but can reuse existing descriptions, and so save time and money. When the developers use the same datamodels and code sets, unnecessary variation can be avoided. For example, the idea of OOTS is to use data that is collected by a certain authority, so that there is no need to copy the data for other authorities. Those can request the information they need from the authority that owns the data. But one needs to know the contents of the data in order to request it in semantically correct manner.
column8: Digital and Population Data Services Agency (Finnish Digital Agency)

column2: Local.iA: promoting public and responsible AI
column4: Local.iA is an ecosystem of resources, materials, and initiatives that provides local authorities—free of charge—with training paths, guides, and communication materials to deploy AI and data governance in an organised manner. In addition, it promotes AI-based proof-of-concept pilots to address local government needs, in collaboration with municipalities. Some of these pilots are already in production, while others are in the analysis phase. All pilots are documented and shared openly.

column2: Document Review in Service Requests powered by Gen AI
column8: Italian National Institute of Social Security (INPS)

column2: Text Simplification for Citizens with GenAI
column4:
Clear and accessible communication is essential to ensure that citizens can effectively navigate public services. INPS—Italy’s National Institute of Social Security and the largest welfare institution in Europe—serves approximately 45 million citizens through nearly 500 digital services related to pensions and welfare. The clarity of these service descriptions plays a critical role in guaranteeing equitable access and a positive user experience. To address the challenges of bureaucratic language, INPS has launched a structured simplification program aimed at making content understandable to users with a middle school level of education. Over 700 texts have been manually simplified so far, resulting in an increase in the average Gulpease readability score from 57 to 64—a substantial improvement in accessibility. However, this manual process is labor-intensive, requiring specialised linguistic and legal expertise, and is not easily scalable.
The need for plain language in public administration has long been acknowledged in Italy. A 2002 directive still in force emphasizes that “all texts produced by administrations must be designed and written to be understood by those who receive them.” Yet institutional communication remains overly formal and complex, hindering citizens’ ability to fully access and understand critical services. While simplification improves transparency and public trust, it must also preserve the legal precision required for official documentation. To overcome these limitations, INPS has integrated Large Language Models (LLMs) into its simplification workflow, developing a semi-automated system—operating in a human-in-the-loop model—that is both scalable and efficient. This solution can process high volumes of content while ensuring readability, semantic accuracy, and legal compliance. A dedicated evaluation framework was introduced to measure key aspects of the output, including readability, fluency, and content preservation. User testing further validated the approach, confirming the quality of AI-generated content in comparison to both the original and manually simplified texts. The AI-based system delivers expert-level results in a fraction of the time and cost required by manual processes. It enhances INPS’s operational efficiency, standardizes quality across service pages, and ensures that content remains accessible and legally sound. Human oversight guarantees accuracy and relevance, while integrated metrics provide transparency and guide ongoing optimization. A large-scale user study involving A/B testing with 1,620 participants confirmed the model’s effectiveness. AI-generated texts were preferred in over 70% of cases when compared to the original versions and outperformed them across all measured dimensions: fluency (+14.1%), readability (+13.5%), clarity (+11.1%), and engagement (+12.3%). The AI semplified versions outperformed also the corresponding human simplified texts across all the qualitative metrics.  Citizens are the ultimate beneficiaries of this transformation. By simplifying complex institutional language, INPS reduces barriers to access for individuals with limited language skills, lower educational attainment, or reading difficulties. More inclusive communication strengthens transparency, encourages greater civic engagement, and builds trust between citizens and institutions. Beyond INPS, this AI-driven approach has strong potential for adoption in other sectors where complex language limits accessibility—such as healthcare, legal services, taxation, and social welfare. Its ability to adapt to diverse regulatory and linguistic contexts makes it a powerful tool for modernising public communication across government systems.
In summary, the integration of LLMs into INPS’s content simplification strategy demonstrates how generative AI can enhance accessibility, efficiency, and trust in public services—delivering clear, comprehensible, and citizen-centered communication at scale.
column8: Italian National Institute of Social Security (INPS)

column2: Once-Only Common Services: Croatia uses cloud native, cloud agnostic building blocks to make accessing the Single Market simple and easier for EU citizens and businesses
column4: Croatia has adopted the Once-Only Technical System (OOTS) to simplify cross-border procedures for citizens and businesses within the EU. The system allows official documents (e.g., certificates, diplomas, permits) to be exchanged securely and automatically between competent authorities, removing the need for individuals or companies to resubmit the same data multiple times. Implemented across numerous central government institutions – including ministries, agencies, and national registers – OOTS improves efficiency, transparency, and interoperability in public administration. By connecting to the EU-wide OOTS Common Services, Croatia helps reduce administrative costs and processing times, supporting a more seamless Digital Single Market and making it easier for citizens and businesses to live, work, study, and operate across borders.
column8: Ministry of Justice, Public Administration and Digital Transformation

column2: Generation of environmental technical reports using Generative AI
column4: The Generalitat de Catalunya has piloted a Generative AI–based system for drafting environmental technical reports, developed by the Centre of Telecommunications and Information Technology (CTTI) and the Department of Territory, Housing and Ecologic Transition. The solution addresses the high volume and repetitive nature of authorisations for activities in protected areas—such as mountain races, drone flights, and forest management—where more than 4,500 reports are prepared annually. By combining visual-language models, geospatial algorithms, and a vectorised legal knowledge base, the tool analyses route data and regulations to automatically generate draft reports. This has reduced report preparation time by around 45%, saving nearly 800 hours of manual work per year, while improving consistency, objectivity, and transparency in administrative processes. The system is fully integrated with the Generalitat’s secure intranet and platforms such as SINATCAT, supports Catalan language outputs, and complies with interoperability, security, and data protection standards. Designed to be modular, scalable, and reusable, it improves efficiency, standardises decision-making, and strengthens public trust by ensuring clearer, more uniform environmental reporting.
column8: Generalitat de Catalunya: Centre of Telecommunications and Information Technology (CTTI) together with the “Department of Territory, Housing  and Ecologic Transition"

column2: ELA 360: Humanising Public Services Through Collaborative Innovation
column4: ELA 360 is a pilot by the Regional Government of Andalusia to improve care for ALS patients under Spain’s new ALS Law (Law 3/2024). ALS requires rapid, coordinated support, but traditional processes caused long delays and stress for families. With automated data flows and proactive service triggers, ELA 360 has reduced waiting times from 1 year to under 3 months and eliminated paperwork, ensuring patients receive essential services faster and with less burden. Developed through co-creation with patients, families, and professionals, the solution integrates health and social systems, improves transparency, and builds trust in public administration. First piloted at Hospital Virgen Macarena, its scalable, modular design makes it adaptable to other municipalities and vulnerable groups, offering a replicable model for more empathetic public services.
column8: Regional Government of Andalusia (Junta de Andalucía)

column2: Docklands Digital Twin: Laying the Foundations for Smarter Urban Futures
column4: The Docklands Digital Twin, developed by Dublin City Council under the Smart Docklands programme, transforms complex spatial data into an interactive 3D model to support more transparent, evidence-based urban planning. Unlike static maps and siloed reports, the twin integrates multiple datasets into a shared virtual environment, accessible via desktop, web, and VR. Internally, it helps city stakeholders visualise proposed developments and coordinate planning. Externally, it improves public engagement and trust by allowing residents and community groups to explore future changes in an intuitive, accessible format. Developed with interoperability and scalability in mind, the model has already inspired replication beyond Docklands and is positioned to expand into areas such as mobility, climate resilience, and citizen participation. Delivered through collaboration between Dublin City Council, SMEs, and academia, the Docklands Digital Twin lays the groundwork for a citywide digital twin strategy, modernising governance and shaping smarter, more inclusive urban futures.

column2: Madrid, Digital Capital: City Intelligence, 5G, Interoperability, aimed at improving citizens' well-being through Emerging Technologies
column4: As part of its Digital Transformation Strategy, Madrid launched the 5G Agenda to provide advanced connectivity infrastructure, enabling smart, sustainable, and people-centered public services. The initiative modernises internal municipal operations and public-facing services, improving urban mobility, emergency response, waste management, and administrative efficiency for over 3.4 million residents, 5 million daily users, and 11 million annual visitors. Key implementations include 5G urban corridors, IoTMADLab, autonomous vehicles at Mercamadrid, and the EUGENIA emergency network, as well as enhanced connectivity at Valdemingómez Technology Park. The project has led to significant efficiency gains, including €246 million saved over five years, a 95% reduction in paper submissions, and measurable improvements in citizen satisfaction. Beyond operational benefits, the 5G Agenda has strengthened public-private collaboration, fostered innovation, and reinforced Madrid as a European benchmark in advanced connectivity and smart city services.
column8: Madrid City Council (Spain) - Directorate General of the Digital Office

column2: KIRA - AI research assistant
column4: As part of Hamburg’s digital strategy, the KIRA Research Assistant addresses the challenge of managing vast amounts of legal and social services legislation. This AI-driven knowledge tool (chatbot) retrieves relevant documentation and provides accurate answers, reducing research time, supporting consistent service quality, and easing pressure on public administration staff. Developed with support from the InnoTecHH Fonds, KIRA has demonstrated strong performance, with an 87% top-k accuracy rate, excellent usability (SUS score 9.75/10), and high satisfaction among staff. Pilot results confirm significant efficiency gains, enabling faster, reliable processing of social benefits requests and directly improving service delivery for both Hamburg’s administration and its citizens.
column8: Secretary of State of the Free and Hanseatic City of Hamburg (Senatskanzlei)

column2: ERP And CRM Costumisation for Project Management in the Public Sector
column4: The use case by the Ephorate of Antiquities of Western Attica aims to optimise project management at the Ephorate of Antiquities of Western Attica and maximise expenditure absorption per funding source. It identified key challenges including limited resources, insufficiently trained staff, incomplete procedures, rigid schedules, and ongoing legislative changes. The recommended solution was the procurement and customisation of ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) and CRM (Customer Relationship Management) software, following a thorough modelling and documentation of the Service’s processes and project objectives.
column8: Ephorate of Antiquities of Western Attica

column2: Pepper robot as an examination assistant and source of information
column4:
Pepper at TH Wildau is a deployed library assistant, based on SoftBank’s humanoid platform and the NAOqi software stack. The project adapts Pepper for a 24/7 academic library, where access is card-controlled outside staffed hours. The robot greets visitors, answers common questions, guides users to locations across three floors, gives short tours, and demonstrates equipment such as the printer–scanner–copier.
The solution combines scripted behaviors with a lightweight NLP pipeline. Behaviors are authored in Choregraphe/Python as modular, rule-based flows for gestures, tablet prompts, and navigation sequences. Speech interaction uses an open, componentised chain: automatic speech recognition (speech-to-text), intent and entity extraction for library domains (orientation, lending, services, events), response generation from curated templates and FAQs, and text-to-speech. The team prioritises transparent, locally hosted components over closed assistants. For perception and mobility, Pepper uses classical robotics methods for localisation and path planning; autonomous driving is complemented by tablet teleoperation for safety and elevators.
Two units are operated: “Wilma” serves in the library as a mobile information point; “Bernd” is used in the RoboticLab for testing and training. Content and dialogs are updated iteratively, with analytics from interactions feeding refinements to intents, utterances, and tour scripts.
column8: Technical University of Applied Sciences Wildau

column2: Vienna AI with generative search (de: WienKI mit generative Suche)
column4: The City of Vienna has proactively embraced artificial intelligence to modernise public administration and deliver tangible benefits to citizens and employees. Recognising the disruptive potential of generative AI, Vienna developed WienKI, a holistic AI platform designed to improve efficiency, safeguard data sovereignty, and foster responsible innovation. WienKI brings together secure, user-friendly tools such as multilingual assistants, knowledge databases with anonymisation functions, and image generation, supported by a strong governance framework and continuous staff training. More than 2,500 employees are already actively using the system, with over 100,000 chat interactions, 85,000 image generations, and 14,000 curated knowledge documents to date. Built on a modular, open-source architecture, WienKI ensures compliance with GDPR, the EU AI Act, and strict security standards, whilst remaining transparent, scalable, and reusable by other public institutions. It enhances internal efficiency, strengthens accessibility across 103 languages, and encourages a culture of responsible innovation throughout the administration. WienKI demonstrates how public administration can act as a pioneer in times of technological change—balancing innovation and responsibility to create sustainable value for both employees and citizens.

column2: AI-Enhanced Processing of Business Registrations for Faster Public Service Delivery
column4: The statutory accident insurer BG ETEM faced a sharp rise in electronic business registrations, causing long delays and a five-digit backlog. To resolve this, it introduced the EDA GWA AI solution, which integrates artificial intelligence and robotic process automation into its core system. The tool now automatically categorises most registrations, checks data against external registers, and reduces manual effort, enabling routine cases to be processed quickly while staff focus on complex ones. Since its rollout in 2024, over 100,000 registrations have been handled, response times have improved, and the backlog has already been cut by 13%. Built with open-source components such as Python, Scikit-learn, XGBoost and Docker, the plugin is transparent, explainable, and compliant with strict legal standards. Its modular, containerised design makes it cost-efficient, robust, and transferable to other statutory insurers. By combining automation with human oversight, the solution delivers faster services for businesses, greater efficiency for administration, and strong potential for reuse across the wider social insurance sector.
column8: Berufsgenossenschaft Energie Textil Elektro Medienerzeugnisse (BG ETEM)

column2: RehaPlus: AI-Powered Identification of Complex Rehabilitation Cases in Public Health Insurance
column4: The statutory accident insurer BG ETEM introduced RehaPlus to improve the early identification of complex rehabilitation cases requiring intensive case management. Previously, such cases were often detected late through manual reminders, leading to delayed interventions, higher treatment costs and longer recovery times. RehaPlus applies explainable machine learning models to continuously monitor case data and flag potential high-risk cases weeks earlier than before. This has replaced the outdated manual reminder process with proactive, automated monitoring, while ensuring case officers remain in control of final decisions. The result is improved efficiency, fewer documents to screen or complete, earlier interventions, and better recovery outcomes for insured workers. Since going live in 2021, RehaPlus has delivered high user satisfaction, with more than 90 per cent of staff rating it positively for improving efficiency and quality of work. Built with open-source components such as Python, XGBoost, Scikit-learn, Pandas and Docker, the solution is lightweight, explainable, cost-efficient and fully compliant with GDPR. Its modular architecture makes it portable across infrastructures and scalable to other social accident insurers, with the first reuse planned for 2026. By combining automation with transparency and human oversight, RehaPlus demonstrates how AI can create both internal efficiency gains (G2G) and societal value (G2C) through better rehabilitation outcomes.
column8: Berufsgenossenschaft Energie Textil Elektro Medienerzeugnisse (BG ETEM)

column2: AI-Based Virtual Assistant for Faster and Reliable Businesses Support
column4: The statutory accident insurer BG ETEM developed a virtual assistant (MeinAssistent) to support its “Members and Fees” department, which has seen a sharp increase in inquiries from businesses. Around 70 per cent of queries concern recurring issues such as registration or insurance fees, which were previously handled manually, causing long response times and high workloads. The assistant provides a three-tiered service: immediate answers from a legally verified FAQ catalogue, context-specific responses generated from official ETEM documents with clear source references, and transparent escalation to staff if no automated answer is possible. This ensures businesses receive reliable, accurate, and timely support while keeping human oversight for complex cases. Early results from the pilot show clear benefits: 24/7 accessibility for businesses, faster responses, reduced interruptions for staff, and high trust thanks to transparent answer paths. Pilot users have praised the clarity, reliability, and seamless handover to human officers. Built with open-source components including Python, LangChain, Hugging Face models, FastAPI, and Docker, the system is modular, containerised, and API-based, making it cost-efficient, portable, and adaptable to other contexts. Although still in pilot, the solution is being integrated into the MeineETEM portal and is designed for transfer to other public sector organisations by connecting it to their own FAQs and documents. By combining automation, explainability, and scalability, MeinAssistent strengthens efficiency and accessibility in public service delivery while reducing manual workload for staff.
column8: Berufsgenossenschaft Energie Textil Elektro Medienerzeugnisse (BG ETEM)

column2: Digital Service for Obligated Citizens to Declare the Cleaning of Their Properties to Prevent Fire Risks or Rapid Fire Spread
column4: The digital platform akatharista.gov.gr is an innovative tool designed to prevent and manage forest and grassland fires in Greece by monitoring the cleaning status of privately owned plots, a key preventive measure against fire outbreaks. The system enables citizens to declare the fire-prevention cleaning activities they have undertaken on their plots each season and allows them to report hazardous plots to supervisory authorities, supporting proactive intervention. For public administration, the platform consolidates and organises critical data—including plot location, ownership, cleaning history, and reported risks—allowing central and local authorities, such as the Fire Service and municipalities, to monitor compliance, assess risk, plan targeted inspections, and take timely preventive action. Since its launch in 2024, over 1.5 million cleaning declarations and 20,000 citizen reports have been processed, resulting in more than 18,000 on-site inspections and more efficient resource deployment. Technologically, the platform integrates with national databases, supports real-time GIS visualisation of plots, and provides automated notifications to citizens. Its interoperable and user-friendly design ensures accessibility, traceability, and transparency, while statistical analysis capabilities inform policy decisions on fire prevention and civil protection. Akatharista.gov.gr exemplifies a digital governance solution that enhances public participation, strengthens accountability, and safeguards human life, property, and ecosystems.
column8: Ministry of Climate Crisis and Civil Protection / Hellenic Fire Service / Fire Service Headquarters / Department of Preventive Fire Protection

column2: ChatAmsterdam - an internally developed generative AI chatbot tailored to the municipality's needs and values to support civil servants in their daily activities
column4: ChatAmsterdam is an internally developed generative AI assistant designed to support civil servants in the Municipality of Amsterdam. Developed through a six-month research pilot involving over 800 employees, the application provides practical assistance with daily tasks while promoting AI literacy, ensuring digital security, and safeguarding sensitive municipal data. ChatAmsterdam operates within a secure, vendor-neutral environment, prioritising data ownership, privacy, transparency, and compliance with Dutch and European regulations. The platform enables integration with internal data sources, allowing staff to pose domain-specific questions and receive contextually relevant answers. By providing a safe alternative to commercial generative AI tools, ChatAmsterdam reduces shadow use of external platforms and facilitates responsible AI adoption. Its flexible architecture allows future expansion, including additional functionalities, AI-assisted workflows, and integration of new datasets, while ensuring alignment with organisational values and operational goals. The application reflects a user-centred design approach, incorporating continuous feedback, training, and co-creation with staff to maximise usability, trust, and impact.

column2: AI tool for depersonalising sensitive data at the State Consumer Rights Protection Authority (SCRPA)
column4: The personal data depersonalisation AI tool is an internally developed solution designed to enhance data privacy, security, and regulatory compliance. By automatically identifying, masking, encrypting, or removing Personally Identifiable Information (PII), the tool ensures that datasets used for development, testing, and analysis are fully anonymised, embedding a “privacy by design” culture across the organisation. This automation has reduced manual depersonalisation time from several hours to under 15 minutes, achieving a 95% efficiency gain, while maintaining 99.8% accuracy in PII detection. The solution is fully interoperable with existing internal systems, integrates seamlessly into data pipelines, and complies with GDPR, CCPA, and organisational policies. Its advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Machine Learning (ML) capabilities allow context-aware recognition of PII in structured and unstructured data, outperforming traditional rule-based methods. The tool has delivered measurable impact, including an 85% reduction in data breaches involving unauthorised PII exposure, significant labour cost savings, and increased user confidence. By providing a reliable, scalable, and user-friendly solution, the AI tool strengthens operational efficiency, data integrity, and transparency, allowing secure data sharing with internal teams and external partners without compromising privacy.

column2: Virtual Market Assistant for the State Consumer Rights Protection Authority (SCRPA)
column4: The Virtual Market Assistant is an innovative mobile application designed to enhance consumer safety by providing instant access to information on officially listed hazardous products and enabling users to report new risks. By connecting to verified databases such as the EU Safety Gate (RAPEX) and integrating user-submitted reports, the app offers proactive protection and empowers citizens to participate directly in market surveillance. It allows barcode scanning for instant product safety checks and simplifies the submission of detailed hazard reports, including photos and descriptions, which are standardised for seamless use by regulatory authorities. Technologically, the app leverages familiar mobile, cloud, and database infrastructure, providing a scalable and user-friendly solution without requiring specialised equipment. It is designed with full interoperability in mind, adhering to the European Interoperability Framework at legal, organisational, semantic, and technical levels, ensuring seamless integration with national and transnational market surveillance systems. By increasing transparency, accountability, and public engagement, the Virtual Market Assistant strengthens trust between citizens and regulatory authorities, streamlines hazard reporting, and accelerates the removal of unsafe products from the market.

column2: DONNA: A digital assistant for IT operations
column4: DONNA is a digital assistant designed to enhance IT operations within public administration, targeting critical services such as vehicle registration and identity card issuance. Built on open-source AI generative models and service architectures, DONNA integrates with tools like JIRA and Confluence to consolidate fragmented documentation and historical incident data into actionable, contextually enriched recommendations. By doing so, it accelerates incident resolution, reduces downtime, and maintains service reliability for citizen-facing digital services. The solution leverages a modular, containerised architecture (Kubernetes/Kubeflow) with components including vLLM, Milvus, LangChain, LangGraph, Langfuse, and OpenWebUI, ensuring scalability, technical interoperability, and data sovereignty. Designed following the European Interoperability Framework (EIF), DONNA aligns with legal, organisational, semantic, and technical interoperability principles, supporting seamless integration with internal IT workflows and potential future cross-government systems. By using fully self-hosted, open-source technologies, DONNA enhances transparency, auditability, and independence from proprietary vendors, while a user-centred development process involving IT operators ensured high adoption, usability, and alignment with operational realities.

column2: AI agent for Prague 6: Simplifying communication between the office and citizens
column4: The Citymind chatbot was implemented by Prague 6 to improve citizen-office communication and streamline access to public services. Its primary function is to provide citizens with a quick, intuitive interface to find information—covering topics such as passport issuance, ID cards, and local police services—while freeing officials from answering routine inquiries. The project, funded under the Horizon Europe CommuniCity programme, combines co-creation with citizens and municipal staff to ensure a user-centred design, resulting in a high user satisfaction rating of 4.05 out of 5. Technologically, the solution is scalable, secure, and user-friendly, integrating seamlessly into municipal websites with minimal technical effort. Its structured data processing ensures semantic interoperability, translating complex administrative content into plain, human-like language. The chatbot also generates analytical reports on common queries, supporting internal service improvements and enhancing organisational efficiency. The innovation process involved participatory development, iterative testing, and a replicability guide to support adoption by other municipalities. Citymind is commercially managed, ensuring ongoing support and continuous development, while being aligned with European interoperability and accessibility principles.

column2: AI Literacy in Action: An Interactive and Reusable Prompting Workbook for Public Servants
column4: The AI Prompting Workbook was developed to rapidly build AI literacy among public administration employees, addressing the limitations of passive training formats and fulfilling capacity-building requirements under the EU AI Act. The interactive, self-paced workbook allows users to engage directly with an internal AI model (“LinzGPT”), experimenting with prompts, executing them in real time, and receiving instant feedback across 50 practical exercises. Internal metrics—voluntary enrolment, course completion, and certificate issuance—demonstrate high uptake and effectiveness, while qualitative feedback from the “AI Pioneers” focus group confirms its practical relevance and engaging format. Technologically, the solution leverages a lightweight web application and structured content (JSON-based exercises), enabling semantic, organisational, technical, and legal interoperability. It is designed for easy scaling and reuse, both internally and as a public Google Gemini Canvas, allowing other administrations to customise or expand the workbook without programming knowledge. The implementation followed a user-centric, agile process, combining in-house prototyping, co-creation with internal focus groups, and rigorous IT validation, with no external procurement required. The dual-delivery model ensures secure internal deployment while maximising public value and societal impact, supporting AI capacity-building and fostering a broader culture of digital empowerment across Europe.

column2: Regional Digital Twin Platform for Climate Change
column4: The Regional Digital Twin for Climate Change provides a data-driven platform for climate risk assessment, simulation, and decision support, addressing gaps in integrating EU-scale climate data into local and sector-specific operations. It enhances resilience across public services and critical sectors—energy, finance, agriculture, and emergency planning—by combining diverse datasets, AI and physical simulations, and a climate services marketplace. The platform also federates stakeholders, fostering collaboration and trust. Pilot users in Luxembourg report improved risk understanding, faster and more informed decisions, and strengthened public and financial resilience. Technologically, it integrates satellite, in-situ, topographic, and regional data via AI and HPC-powered cloud solutions, offering real-time insights and multi-sectoral services. Designed according to the European Interoperability Framework (EIF), the platform ensures legal, organisational, semantic, and technical interoperability. Transparency is enhanced through dashboards, open data where possible, and workshops for stakeholders. Its modular architecture allows scalable deployment across sectors, regions, and administrative levels, with the potential for broader adoption by other administrations. The project follows a user-centred innovation process, beginning with a Proof of Concept by Thales Alenia Space, LIST, and PwC, incorporating feedback from ministries and critical infrastructure operators to ensure practical, actionable outcomes.

column2: Artificial Intelligence Services Oriented to Legal Documents
column4: The Ministry of the Presidency, Justice, and Relations with the Courts has harnessed Artificial Intelligence to manage vast volumes of unstructured legal information and streamline tens of thousands of procedures each year. AI tools support civil servants, judges, magistrates, and prosecutors by extracting and linking relevant information, generating timelines, summarising documents, and assisting with rule-based processes. A key initiative is the automatic transcription of hearings and trials, achieving an estimated 60% time saving, with over 3.28 million transcriptions completed to date, saving more than one million hours of work. AI services also produce plain-language and easy-to-read documents, enhancing accessibility for citizens, including those with functional or intellectual difficulties. The AI infrastructure combines natural language processing, supervised neural networks, Transformer-based models, and GPU-enabled servers, deployed within a secure microservices architecture. Services are accessible via portals such as ADIA and Carpeta Justicia and include anonymisation, document summarisation, intelligent assistants, and translation tools. These tools are integrated across multiple internal applications and are already being reused by other administrations, demonstrating scalability and interoperability. Development followed a user-centred, iterative process involving legal experts, linguists, programmers, and administrative staff. Solutions were piloted on a small scale and then expanded strategically, supported by knowledge-sharing initiatives such as the Justice Digital Transformation Forum. Overall, AI innovations have transformed operations, improved efficiency, fostered a culture of innovation, enhanced accessibility, and strengthened transparency and public trust in the justice system.
column8: Dirección General de Transformación Digital de la Administración de Justicia
