Using advanced mobile mapping technology and 360° panoramic cameras, the initiative captured millions of images and detailed information on streets, buildings, street furniture, signage, lighting, and infrastructure, including areas previously unmapped such as CityLife and the former Expo site. The digital twin provides Milan’s administration with an accurate, up-to-date, and immersive representation of the urban environment. It is designed to support more efficient city governance, improved urban planning, smarter management of public spaces, and timely maintenance, helping to advance Milan towards becoming a fully “smart city.”
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Digital twins in cultural heritage conservation: Notre-Dame Cathedral
column4: The AI-powered digital twin of Notre‑Dame is a project led by Microsoft in collaboration with heritage digitisation firm Iconem and the French Ministry of Culture. It aims to create a hyper-detailed virtual replica of the cathedral, capturing both interior and exterior features using drone imagery, laser scanning, and thousands of high-resolution photographs. Advanced AI algorithms are used to integrate this data into a fully immersive digital model, offering a centimetre-accurate record of the cathedral’s roof, vaults, stonework, stained glass, and architectural details. The digital twin serves multiple purposes: preserving the cathedral’s heritage for future generations, supporting conservation and maintenance through detailed structural documentation, and making Notre‑Dame accessible globally via a virtual experience for those unable to visit in person.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Digital Twin in the City of Matera
column4: The Digital Twin of Matera is a project developed by the Casa delle Tecnologie Emergenti di Matera (CTE Matera) in collaboration with the Italian National Research Council (CNR). It creates a comprehensive virtual replica of the city by integrating data from sensors, geospatial systems, and software modules, forming a dynamic “living lab” to support urban governance, planning, and city management. The platform includes a data storage system, computational services, and user interfaces, allowing both city authorities and citizens to access real-time and historical information about the urban environment. Its applications include improving mobility and infrastructure management, monitoring the built environment and energy efficiency, supporting sustainable waste and resource flows, preparing for extreme weather events, and enhancing the preservation of cultural heritage. The digital twin also enables simulations and predictive analytics, helping administrators forecast urban phenomena—such as crowd movements during events, environmental impacts, or infrastructure needs—and plan interventions more efficiently and proactively.
column8: House of Emerging Technologies of Matera (CTE Matera)
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: A Digital Twin of the Italian soil
column4: The Digital Twin of Italian Soil is a collaborative project between Leonardo, through its subsidiary e‑GEOS, and the Italian Ministry of Environment and Energy Security (MASE), via its Department of Sustainable Development. The initiative aims to create a high-resolution digital model of Italy’s terrain, including soil morphology, surface features, and subsurface characteristics, as part of a national monitoring and prediction system under the PNRR investment plan. The model is generated using aerial surveys with LiDAR and aerogravimetric data, producing detailed maps of ground surfaces, terrain elevations, and a comprehensive geophysical database. Once completed, the digital twin will support terrain analysis, risk assessment for hazards such as landslides and flooding, land stability studies, environmental planning, and predictive simulations, providing a critical tool for sustainable territorial management and national resilience.
column8: Italian Ministry of Environment and Energy Security (MASE)

column2: DESIGN-IT (DEcision Science desIGN platform for digItal Twins)
column4: DESIGN‑IT is a research initiative coordinated by Spindox S.p.A. that aims to develop a “Decision Science Design Platform” — a low-code environment for creating and managing multi-level digital twins of products, machines, or industrial processes, integrated with artificial intelligence pipelines. The platform is designed for industry professionals without programming experience, allowing them to use a drag-and-drop interface to combine simulation, modelling, machine learning, optimisation, and data-management modules to build AI-powered applications for design, operation, and maintenance. By supporting digital twins at both the product/component and process levels, and integrating explainable AI, DESIGN‑IT facilitates human–machine collaboration in highly automated industrial contexts such as robotics and manufacturing, enabling predictive maintenance, process optimisation, and smarter decision-making. The project began in January 2023, is scheduled to run until December 2025, and is funded under Italy’s industrial and innovation initiatives with a total budget of approximately €5.39 million.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Adriano - first robot employee of the Italian Public Administration
column4: On 28 March 2025, the Rome Chamber of Commerce introduced Adriano, the first robot employee in the Italian Public Administration, representing a fusion of computer vision and artificial intelligence. Adriano is capable of recognising people and objects in its surroundings, adapting its behaviour through natural real-time movements, interacting safely, and engaging in conversation with visitors. The robot is continually enhancing its self-learning capabilities by processing increasingly large datasets, as part of a broader digitalisation and innovation initiative within the Public Administration. The programme envisions the deployment of robots in public spaces to support tourist reception, provide assistance to citizens, and help manage public services efficiently.

column2: RePAIR – Reconstructing the Past: Artificial Intelligence and Robotics meet Cultural Heritage
column4: The RePAIR Project — “Reconstructing the Past: Artificial Intelligence and Robotics meet Cultural Heritage” — was launched in 2021 with the goal of transforming the restoration of fragmented archaeological artefacts such as vases, amphorae, and frescoes. The project replaces slow, labour-intensive manual reconstruction with an intelligent robotic system that uses computer vision, artificial intelligence, and robotics to automatically process, match, and physically assemble large collections of fragments, even when numbering in the thousands. This approach dramatically reduces restoration time and overcomes the limitations of manual or partially automated methods. Coordinated by Ca’ Foscari University of Venice and involving multiple European research institutions, RePAIR is being tested on emblematic case studies from the UNESCO World Heritage site of Pompeii, aiming to restore iconic frescoes preserved only as thousands of fragments and to give new life to cultural heritage that would otherwise remain inaccessible.

column2: EBB_AI : Co-creation-as-a-Service
column4: The EBB_AI: Co-creation-as-a-Service project is an innovative platform that allows cultural institutions and artists to harness artificial intelligence while safeguarding copyright. Operated by the creative studio EBB, the platform engages cultural partners — including museums, art schools, a film production company, contemporary artists, and art centres — as early adopters, granting access to their databases and providing feedback to refine the service. Its business model combines a standard subscription with personalised offerings, covering both service access and the creation of posters and digital merchandise. Revenue from sales of these products is shared among cultural institutions, artists, and EBB. The platform incorporates rights-holder traceability and automated compensation during content generation, using a blockchain- and NFT-based copyright management system. Experts are involved to ensure inclusivity, reduce AI bias, and address copyright concerns. Co-creation tools are deployed in collaboration with cultural partners to facilitate user engagement. In addition, a series of conferences will be organised to promote the platform, attract new European cultural partners, and foster the exchange of knowledge and experience among stakeholders, further enhancing collaboration and innovation in the cultural sector.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: IT4LIA – Italy for Artificial Intelligence
column4: IT4LIA is a strategic Italian initiative and one of Europe’s first “AI Factories,” aimed at positioning Italy at the forefront of artificial intelligence infrastructure and services. The project involves building a new AI-optimised supercomputer at the CINECA data centre in Bologna, integrating it with existing high-performance computing and cloud systems, with the first operational partition expected in 2025. IT4LIA provides advanced computing resources, data-processing capabilities, and AI services to support startups, SMEs, research institutions, and public administration. It targets strategic sectors such as agritech, cybersecurity, climate and earth sciences, and manufacturing. Co-funded by the European High-Performance Computing Joint Undertaking and the Italian government, the initiative brings together key national stakeholders to accelerate digital transformation, enhance competitiveness, foster research and innovation, and enable wide adoption of AI across Italy.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AppLI - the Virtual Assistant for Work
column4: AppLI is a virtual-assistant platform launched by the Italian Ministry of Labour and Social Policies, in collaboration with INPS and regional employment services, designed to help young people — particularly those not in education, employment, or training (NEET) — navigate the path to work and training. The platform offers personalised guidance, helping users assess their skills and interests, suggesting suitable training courses or job opportunities, assisting with CVs and cover letters, providing mock interviews, and pointing to relevant local employment offers. Accessible 24/7, AppLI aims to bridge the gap between job demand and supply by prov
column8: Italian Ministry of Labour and Social Policies

column2: 5G4LIVES – 5G for a Better Tomorrow: Protecting Lives and the Environment in Riga
column4: The 5G4LIVES project in Riga uses 5G-enabled automatic drone flights to enhance public safety and environmental monitoring across the city. Through the Riga Municipal Police, drones equipped with high-resolution and thermal cameras patrol beaches, parks and water-adjacent areas, helping detect accidents, dangerous situations and environmental risks more quickly. The system allows authorities to cover large zones efficiently, respond faster to emergencies and reduce manual workload. By integrating 5G connectivity with automated UAV operations, Riga is improving situational awareness, strengthening prevention efforts and increasing safety for residents and visitors.
column8: Riga State City municipal Agency “Riga Digital Agency”
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: 5G4LIVES – 5G for a Better Tomorrow: Protecting Lives and the Environment in Torino
column4: The 5G4LIVES project—5G for a Better Tomorrow: Protecting Lives and the Environment—is an EU-supported initiative carried out in both Turin and Riga to enhance public safety and environmental protection through the use of advanced 5G connectivity and drone technology. In Turin, the project introduces a hybrid private 5G network enabling real-time aerial monitoring and automated vulnerability assessment of areas at risk, such as hillsides prone to flooding or landslides. The initiative makes use of beyond-visual-line-of-sight drones, infrared sensors, satellite data and secure 5G links to support emergency management, disaster prevention, environmental monitoring, public safety and rescue operations. Through 5G4LIVES, Turin aims to demonstrate how next-generation connectivity and unmanned aerial systems can strengthen urban resilience and environmental safeguards, providing a model that can be replicated in other European cities.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: 5GENIUS – A Next-Generation Network for the University College of Ghent
column4: The 5GENIUS project is creating a private 5G network at University College Ghent (HOGENT) in Belgium, transforming the campus into a next-generation “smart lab” for education, research and innovation. The new 5G non-standalone infrastructure will support advanced digital services across sectors such as healthcare, agriculture, mobility, logistics, industry, IoT, augmented and virtual reality, artificial intelligence and 3D prototyping. The project develops five main use cases: using the campus as an experimentation environment; enabling smart farming through sensors and drones; providing high-speed connectivity for complex laboratory and research activities; supporting the development of e-health applications; and enhancing digital learning through VR/AR and data-intensive computing. Funded under the EU’s Connecting Europe Facility (CEF Digital), 5GENIUS has a total budget of €1.44 million, of which €1.08 million is EU co-funding, and runs from January 2024 to December 2026.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: 5G-TERRA – 5G infrastructure and services for public interest and social inclusion
column4: The project 5G-TERRA aims to bring modern 5G network infrastructure to remote and sparsely populated rural areas and islands in Greece — places that often lack good internet connectivity. It plans to install roughly 50 new 5G base-stations (radio access points), upgrading both radio access and backhaul links, and gradually transition from a “non-standalone” 5G setup to a full “standalone” 5G network. Once operational, the enhanced connectivity will support important public interest services in three main domains: healthcare (such as telemedicine, remote monitoring, even remote ultrasound and emergency support), education (smart/“green” schools, augmented-reality learning, broader wireless access), and public safety & civil protection (environmental- and disaster-monitoring, real-time alerts, drones or sensors for wildfire/flood detection). In short: 5G-TERRA seeks to reduce the digital divide by giving underserved Greek communities high-speed, reliable connectivity — enabling digital healthcare, modern education, and safer communities even in remote areas.
column8: WINGS ICT Solutions Technologies Pliroforikis Kai Epikoinonion Anonymi Etaireai
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Hola 5G Oulu - Private 5G network at an operating European Hospital
column4: The Hola 5G Oulu project establishes a dedicated 5G infrastructure within Oulu University Hospital, aiming to become one of the first hospitals in Europe to operate a private standalone 5G network specifically for healthcare. The secure, high‑capacity network creates an environment where wireless wearable devices, including augmented‑reality and virtual‑reality headsets, can be safely used in critical hospital units such as operating theatres and selected wards. This setup allows surgeons, anaesthetists, and nurses to access real‑time patient data, medical imaging, and vital signs through AR and VR visualisation applications. It enables advanced innovations such as machine‑vision based surgical navigation, remote monitoring, and expert guidance during procedures. Beyond the operating theatre, the network supports streamlined information sharing and visualisation across hospital units, reducing the need for manual data retrieval and allowing staff to focus more on patient care. In summary, Hola 5G Oulu transforms hospital operations by providing a secure wireless backbone that supports next‑generation AR and VR healthcare solutions, enhancing efficiency, enabling advanced surgical support, and shaping a future-oriented model of patient care.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: SmartPort Ploce - Enchancing public services through a private 5G network
column4: The SmartPortPloče project aims to transform the Port of Ploče into Croatia’s first “smart port” by deploying a dedicated private standalone 5G network across the port area. The high‑capacity, secure 5G infrastructure will support several advanced applications, including a real‑time location system for tracking vehicles and ships, video and sensor‑based monitoring of cargo handling to improve operational oversight, and environmental and safety systems such as smart cameras, thermal imaging, and weather monitoring to detect and manage incidents like fires or pollution. These innovations are expected to optimise port traffic and logistics processes, enhance efficiency, and improve safety and environmental management, making port operations faster, safer, and more reliable. Led by the Port Authority of Ploče in cooperation with telecommunications partners, the project began in January 2024 and is scheduled to run for 30 months, with a total cost of approximately €1.28 million, partly funded by the EU. In summary, SmartPortPloče is set to redefine maritime logistics and port management, leveraging 5G‑enabled automation, real‑time data, and remote sensing to advance efficiency, safety, and sustainability at the Port of Ploče.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: 5G in Healthcare – Northern Region (PT) (5G Healthcare (PT))
column4: The 5G Healthcare – Northern Region project establishes a standalone 5G network across the Northern Region of Portugal, primarily in the Porto district, to support next‑generation healthcare services. The high‑capacity, secure network enables a range of advanced applications. Emergency vehicles, including ambulances and helicopters, can transmit real‑time medical data and video feeds to hospitals, allowing specialists to provide remote guidance. Robotic surgeries can be live‑streamed to medical staff and students, enhancing training and surgical innovation. The network also supports telemedicine and remote patient monitoring via IoT devices, reducing the need for hospital stays by allowing continuous observation of vital signs. In intensive care units, virtual‑reality therapies can help conscious patients manage anxiety and stress, supporting recovery and mitigating post‑intensive‑care syndrome. Additionally, digitised laboratory analyses, such as histopathology slides, can be sent to remote specialists, speeding up diagnosis and improving patient safety. Running from March 2024 to February 2027, with a total budget of approximately €3.32 million, the project aims to transform healthcare delivery in the region. By combining high‑speed connectivity, real‑time data streaming, remote diagnostics and immersive technologies, it seeks to improve patient care, enhance medical training, and strengthen the responsiveness of the health system.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: 5G.RURAL - Rural connectivity
column4:
The 5G.RURAL project aims to bring modern 5G connectivity to remote and rural communities in the Alentejo region of Portugal, benefiting around 70,000 people by 2026. Using a “neutral‑host” 5G network shared by multiple operators, the project will provide reliable broadband coverage to villages that previously lacked adequate digital infrastructure.
Beyond basic connectivity, the network will enable a range of smart‑community services, including telemedicine and remote healthcare for the elderly, modernised education with immersive learning tools, smarter agriculture and livestock monitoring, improved energy efficiency, and support for culture, tourism, and local economic development. The infrastructure combines 5G New Radio at 3.6 GHz with existing 4G where necessary, covering around 25 rural sites, often using landscape‑friendly masts to preserve the rural environment.

In summary, 5G.RURAL seeks to reduce the digital divide for rural populations by delivering high‑speed, flexible connectivity, and leveraging that backbone to provide healthcare, education, agricultural, and cultural services that would otherwise be difficult or impossible in sparsely populated areas.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: 5GMHI – smart healthcare
column4: The 5GMHI project establishes a private 5G network in Stockholm, Sweden, with the aim of improving healthcare delivery and building management efficiency. The dedicated high‑capacity 5G backbone enables a range of advanced medical and infrastructure services. Emergency ambulances and hospital emergency units can exchange real‑time data and video communications, enhancing triage, preparedness, and patient care. Within hospitals, real‑time locating systems and sensor networks track critical equipment such as beds and infusion pumps, ensuring resources are available when needed and saving staff time. The project also explores drone‑based delivery of automated external defibrillators to out‑of‑hospital cardiac arrests, paired with live video links to dispatchers and medical professionals, increasing patient survival chances. Additionally, the 5G network supports augmented‑ and virtual‑reality tools for hospital and facility management, integrating building‑information modelling with geographic information systems, and enabling improved energy efficiency through connected sensors and digital twins for HVAC and indoor‑air‑quality systems. Running from January 2024 to December 2026 with a total budget of €8.5 million, 5GMHI combines mobile healthcare, emergency response, and smart‑building technologies, enhancing patient safety, hospital efficiency, and urban infrastructure management through next‑generation 5G connectivity.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: TUKE 5GSC - TUKE 5G Infrastructure for Smart Communities
column4: The TUKE 5GSC project deploys a private standalone 5G network at the Technical University of Košice (TUKE) in Košice, Slovakia. The network supports two main use-cases: a “smart logistics” system that uses real-time location tracking to streamline manufacturing and delivery processes, and a “smart energy metering” platform that monitors energy consumption in real time and applies machine-learning tools to optimise energy efficiency. The infrastructure uses C-band spectrum and combines indoor radio units, antennas and cloud-based network management to deliver high-speed, low-latency connectivity suitable for industrial, research and energy applications. With a total budget of about €1.66 million (mostly co-funded at EU level), the project runs from January 2024 to December 2026. In short: TUKE 5GSC aims to provide a template for how 5G can boost logistics efficiency and energy management in research or industrial environments, potentially offering a replicable model for other “smart community” settings.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: ED5GE - 5G Edge Enabled Smart Communities for Green Transformation in the North Project
column4: The ED5GE project deploys a 5G-edge network across several communities in northern Sweden, including Luleå, Boden, Skellefteå and Uppsala, to support advanced smart-community and green-transition services. The network combines standalone 5G, edge-computing nodes, network slicing and precise positioning, enabling low-latency, highly reliable connectivity for real-time sensing, automation and large-scale Internet-of-Things applications. Using this infrastructure, the project supports a wide range of services: enhanced public-safety and emergency-response systems in port areas; improved port logistics and cargo handling; smart waste and recycling management; real-time urban traffic monitoring supported by sensor networks and digital-twin modelling; and precision or automated agriculture, including drone-based monitoring, autonomous machinery and data-driven farm management. The architecture places computing close to users and sensors, ensuring rapid response times and efficient data processing. Running from January 2024 to December 2026, with a total budget of around €4.1 million, ED5GE aims to accelerate the green and digital transformation of northern Sweden by demonstrating how 5G and edge computing can enhance safety, sustainability, operational efficiency and quality of life in both urban and rural communities.
column8: Cities of Luleå, Boden, Skellefteå and Uppsala
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: PIAST-Q - First European Quantum Computer
column4: PIAST-Q is the first quantum computer procured through the EuroHPC Joint Undertaking. Hosted at the Poznań Supercomputing and Networking Center in Poland, it is a trapped-ion system equipped with twenty physical qubits and designed to operate in hybrid mode alongside high-performance classical computing resources. Its deployment opens new possibilities for research in quantum optimisation, advanced materials, chemistry, machine learning and complex simulations, where quantum capabilities can significantly accelerate scientific and industrial innovation. The initiative forms part of a wider European effort to establish a distributed quantum computing infrastructure across several countries, each equipped with complementary quantum technologies. Together, these systems aim to build a robust, accessible and state-of-the-art quantum ecosystem that will strengthen Europe’s long-term leadership in this strategic field.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: EDITO-Infra project: EU Public Infrastructure for the European Digital Twin Ocean
column4: The EU Public Infrastructure for the European Digital Twin Ocean (EDITO-Infra) project, launched in 2022, aims to develop the core public infrastructure that will underpin the future EU Digital Twin Ocean. Mandated by the European Commission, the principal operators of Copernicus Marine and EMODnet are jointly building this shared framework through a co-creative and inclusive approach. EDITO-Infra will form the technological foundation on which local and thematic digital twins of the ocean can be created and operated for a wide range of users. By integrating both existing and emerging data sources, the infrastructure will combine next-generation ocean modelling, artificial intelligence and high-performance computing to enable interactive simulations and transform the way ocean knowledge is produced and shared. This digital environment will make it possible to explore the impacts of climate change and human activities on the ocean, assess possible mitigation and adaptation measures, and support informed decision-making. The project will also enhance public understanding of the marine environment, encouraging shared responsibility for the protection and restoration of coastal and ocean ecosystems. To achieve this, EDITO-Infra upgrades, merges and extends the essential components of Europe’s existing ocean observation and data systems, notably the Copernicus Marine Service and EMODnet, into a unified digital framework. Its development is guided by scientific priorities and carried out in consultation with European and international partners to ensure that it can support sophisticated, multidisciplinary analyses of marine systems and future scenarios.

column2: EDITO-ModelLab - European Digital Twin Ocean
column4: EDITO-Model Lab is a Horizon Europe project launched in 2022 which forms a central component of the European Digital Twin Ocean initiative. Its purpose is to develop the next generation of ocean numerical models and the software infrastructure that will power a highly detailed, interactive digital representation of the ocean. The project combines state-of-the-art ocean modelling, artificial intelligence, satellite and in situ observations, and high-performance or cloud computing to create a “Virtual Ocean Model Lab” where advanced simulations can be built, tested and shared. This environment will allow users to explore a wide range of scenarios, such as pollution dispersion, coastal risks, climate-change impacts, biodiversity responses and sustainable marine-resource management. EDITO-Model Lab delivers a suite of high-resolution global and coastal models, covering physical, biogeochemical and ecological processes, alongside toolkits that support both developers and end users. These resources will enable scientists, policymakers, businesses and other stakeholders to design their own local or thematic digital twins tailored to specific challenges. By providing accessible modelling tools and a unified computing and data framework, the project supports informed decision-making, environmental protection, innovation in the blue economy and broader public understanding of the marine environment.

column2: Grenada's Digital Twin for Climate Change
column4: Grenada has become the first nation in the world to develop a country-wide digital twin — a detailed, data-rich virtual replica of its entire territory designed to support sustainable planning and climate resilience. The project integrates high-resolution aerial surveys, including 3D lidar data, with advanced GIS and GeoAI tools to map and classify buildings, infrastructure, vegetation, land use, and natural features across the islands. This comprehensive model enables authorities to identify areas vulnerable to flooding, coastal erosion, sea-level rise, landslides, and soil salinisation. It also allows the simulation of future climate scenarios to assess potential impacts on communities and infrastructure, supporting informed decision-making in urban planning, environmental management, and disaster preparedness. Additionally, the digital twin serves as a long-term environmental record, helping monitor changes such as deforestation and landscape transformation over time.

column2: A Digital Twin for the Port of Rotterdam
column4: The Port of Rotterdam is developing a comprehensive digital twin of the entire harbour area in collaboration with Esri and several technology partners. This virtual model integrates real-time information on port infrastructure, ship movements, weather and water conditions, and other operational and environmental data. Initiated in 2018 with a cloud-based IoT platform, the digital twin gives port managers an up-to-date and detailed overview of activities across Europe’s busiest port, supporting more efficient logistics, safer operations, improved berth planning, and enhanced environmental monitoring. The project also forms a key step towards enabling autonomous shipping by 2030, turning the complex physical port environment into a dynamic and intelligent digital representation.

column2: Digital Twin Munich
column4: The Digital Twin Munich is a comprehensive virtual model of the city of Munich, created to support urban planning, mobility, environmental protection and civic participation. At its core is the “Urban Data Platform,” a central hub that integrates detailed geographic data (2D maps and a 3D city model), real-time sensor data (traffic, air quality, noise, weather, etc.), and administrative data from municipal services. With this infrastructure, city planners and authorities can run “what-if” simulations and scenario analyses — for example to plan future mobility systems, test urban development strategies, evaluate the effects of construction projects, or assess environmental and air-quality impacts. The tool also supports greater transparency and citizen involvement, enabling residents to visualise proposed changes and give feedback. Originally launched as a funded project to improve spatial data for traffic planning and air-quality control, Digital Twin Munich has since been embedded as a permanent part of the city’s digital infrastructure — a key building block of Munich’s ambition to become climate-neutral and efficiently planned.

column2: INCISIVE - Improving cancer diagnosis and prediction with AI and big data
column4: The INCISIVE project was an EU-funded initiative designed to enhance cancer diagnosis, prognosis and patient follow-up through the use of artificial intelligence and large-scale medical data. Running from 2020 to 2024, the project developed an AI-powered toolbox aimed at improving the accuracy, sensitivity, specificity and cost-effectiveness of current cancer imaging techniques. A central component of the initiative was the creation of a federated, interoperable repository of anonymised clinical data and medical images, enabling secure and privacy-compliant data sharing across Europe. This infrastructure allowed researchers and clinicians to train and validate AI models using extensive and diverse datasets. Focusing on major cancer types such as breast, lung, prostate and colorectal cancer, INCISIVE combined imaging with biological and health-record data to support more reliable detection, staging and monitoring of disease progression. Overall, the project brought together advanced AI tools and shared data resources to strengthen cancer imaging and clinical decision-making throughout Europe.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Diella - World's First Virtual Minister
column4: The Albanian government, led by Prime Minister Edi Rama, has appointed Diella, an artificial intelligence system, as a full member of its cabinet, making it the world’s first AI-powered government minister. Diella has been given responsibility for overseeing public procurement, with the aim of making the tendering process fully transparent and eliminating corruption. Previously introduced as a digital assistant on Albania’s national e-government portal, Diella has already helped citizens with thousands of administrative tasks and documents. This appointment positions Albania at the forefront of digital governance by embedding AI directly into governmental decision-making, while also raising questions about accountability, oversight, and public trust in such innovative applications.

column2: AI Model Combining Speech and Brain Activity for Depression Diagnosis
column4: Researchers at Kaunas University of Technology (KTU) have developed an artificial intelligence model that combines two types of data — speech and brain activity measured via EEG — to diagnose depression. They collected voice recordings while participants responded to questions, read texts, or described images, alongside EEG signals recorded when participants were relaxed with their eyes closed. These signals were converted into spectrograms, which were then analysed using a modified deep learning model based on DenseNet-121, trained to distinguish between individuals with and without depression. This multimodal approach, which integrates multiple sources of data, achieved an accuracy of approximately 97.53%. It is designed to provide a more objective and precise method of diagnosis compared with traditional techniques that rely on a single type of signal, potentially enabling faster, remote, and less subjective assessments in the future.

column2: AI4grids - AI - based planning and operational management of distribution grids
column4: The AI4Grids project at HTWG Konstanz focuses on using artificial intelligence to support the planning and operation of electricity distribution networks and micro-grids. The project develops AI-based algorithms to forecast energy demand and generation, taking into account renewable sources, weather conditions, and consumption patterns. It also continuously monitors the grid to detect changes when new users or loads are added, and in the event of faults or abnormal conditions, it proposes corrective actions to the control centre. By integrating fluctuating renewable energy sources and variable loads such as electric vehicles and heat pumps more efficiently, the project aims to stabilise the grid and reduce the need for costly over-capacity expansions. AI4Grids ran from September 2020 to December 2023 and was funded with approximately €2.53 million under the “KI-Leuchttürme” programme of the German environment ministry.

column2: KIWA, an AI-based forest monitoring system
column4: KIWA — “AI‑based forest monitoring” — is an initiative that combines drones, artificial intelligence and decision‑support systems to enable early detection and effective management of forest fires. The project uses unmanned aerial vehicles equipped with cameras to gather real‑time imagery of forested areas; those images are analysed via AI (computer vision) to detect signs of fire or smoke, allowing authorities to spot wildfires at a very early stage. On top of detection, KIWA provides a decision‑support system for emergency and disaster‑response services, helping coordinate deployment of resources based on real‑time data and AI‑driven predictions of fire spread. The aim is two‑fold: to protect people, infrastructure and natural environments — by enabling rapid, accurate wildfire response — and to support ecological sustainability by reducing the damage to forests and preventing carbon emissions from fires. The system is intended as a scalable, transferable solution for municipalities or regions, offering a modern, data‑driven approach to wildfire prevention and forest management.

column2: MarghERita: Emilia-Romagna’s High-Performance Computing and Big Data Platform
column4: MarghERita is a high-performance computing (HPC) and Big Data platform managed by Lepida for the Emilia-Romagna region. It provides public administrations—including municipalities, provinces, health agencies, and universities—with access to powerful computing infrastructure capable of handling large volumes of data. The platform supports a wide range of applications, from environmental risk assessment and public health analysis to administrative data processing and research projects, enabling regional institutions to leverage advanced technology for data-driven decision-making and innovative public services.

column2: SAVIA project: Artificial Intelligence for law quality
column4: The MarghERita and SAVIA initiative in Emilia-Romagna combines high-performance computing and artificial intelligence to enhance public administration and citizen access to information. MarghERita is a Big Data and HPC platform managed by Lepida, providing municipalities, provinces, health agencies, universities, and other regional institutions with powerful computing infrastructure capable of handling large volumes of data for a wide range of applications, from environmental risk assessment to public health and administrative data processing. Built on top of this infrastructure, SAVIA is an AI tool developed in collaboration with CINECA and the regional legislative assembly to improve the quality, accessibility, and transparency of regional legislation. It allows users to ask natural-language questions about laws and their implementing acts, including how they are applied and their effects on the territory. By combining advanced computing resources with AI-driven legal analysis, the project supports both institutional decision-making and public understanding of regional regulations.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Proof of Concept (POC) of VERA, the Regional Digital Twin
column4: The project — known as VERA Digital Twin — is a “proof‑of‑concept” (PoC) digital twin for the region Emilia-Romagna, aimed at modelling and assessing air‑quality impacts based on traffic and road‑use policies. The tool enables users (initially regional governance teams and experts) to generate in less than ten minutes a scenario of pollution impact by simulating changes in traffic patterns across the region or within individual municipalities. Three input modes make this possible: adjusting vehicle flows on the regional road network; changing the composition of circulating vehicles in selected municipalities; and applying hypothetical emission‑reduction measures. Based on these inputs, VERA computes both emissions and pollutant concentrations (e.g. NO₂, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅), using established methodologies for emissions calculation and air‑quality dispersion modelling. The result is a flexible, data‑driven decision‑support tool that can inform regional and local policy on traffic, environment and health by predicting how different traffic or regulatory scenarios would affect air quality.

column2: Chatbot for the consultation of the AI Act
column4: INAIL has developed an experimental AI‑based chatbot — built on a large language model — to support consultation of the European AI Act. The chatbot is specialised in answering questions about the regulation, and in particular about its implications for health and safety at work. By using a technique known as Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG), the system combines the model’s linguistic capabilities with a curated legal knowledge base (text of the regulation, summaries, indices) to generate clear, coherent responses to user queries. In tests, this chatbot outperformed other models in terms of clarity, completeness, relevance and accuracy. In future, the tool could be expanded or adapted to address other topics in occupational health & safety or regulatory compliance more broadly.
column8: Italian National Institute for Insurance against Accidents at Work (INAIL)

column2: Open Source AI Assistant in Polish digital-wallet app mObywatel
column4: Under Open Source Observatory (OSOR), Poland projects to integrate an open-source AI assistant into its national digital-wallet app mObywatel. The assistant, powered by the Polish Large Language Model (PLLuM), is designed to help citizens navigate administrative procedures by answering questions in natural language and guiding them through official processes. By simplifying bureaucracy, improving accessibility for people with limited digital skills or disabilities, and reducing the workload on public administrations, the initiative aims to make public services more user-friendly. Its open-source foundation also supports transparency, digital sovereignty, and long-term public value.

column2: A Web App to Support Risk Assessments for Small Businesses
column4: BG ETEM has released a new web-app designed to help very small businesses quickly and easily carry out a “Gefährdungsbeurteilung” (risk assessment) — a key requirement for workplace safety. The app walks users through a simple, questionnaire-style process in three parts: organisational safety setup; general/ basic aspects; and — where relevant — office and screen-based work. It is tailored to micro-enterprises (for example optician shops, tailoring alterations, copyshops) where risk levels tend to be low. By answering a few questions, business owners can identify potential hazards in their operations and derive suitable protective measures — improving occupational health and safety while reducing administrative burden.
column8: Berufsgenossenschaft Energie Textil Elektro Medienerzeugnisse (BG ETEM)

column2: AppLI, the Web Coach of the Ministry of Labour and Social Policies
column4: AppLI is a virtual assistant launched in September 2025 by the Italian Ministry of Labour and Social Policies in collaboration with INPS, designed to support young people, especially those aged 18–35 who are not in education, employment, or training (NEET), in their journey toward employment. Accessible 24/7 via the Ministry’s portal, AppLI provides a personalised guidance path, helping users assess their interests, skills, and goals, explore job and learning opportunities in their area, and identify relevant training or educational resources to close skills gaps. The system also offers reminders and encouragement to help transform intentions into concrete steps toward employment. AppLI represents the first multi-agent generative AI system deployed by an Italian public administration and was developed through a co-design process involving young people, regional authorities, employment centres, and sector experts to ensure it meets real needs. Its aim is to complement existing public employment services by offering a more accessible, inclusive, and technology-driven support tool to help young people navigate training and the job market more effectively.
column8: Italian Ministry of Labour and Social Policies
column11: General economic, commercial and labour affair

column2: TildeOpen LLM – AI solution for European languages
column4: TildeOpen LLM is an open‑source large language model released by the European language technology company Tilde in September 2025, specifically designed to support European languages, including those often under-represented in major commercial models. With over 30 billion parameters, the model was trained on European supercomputers using hundreds of thousands of GPU hours awarded through the EU’s “Large AI Grand Challenge.” TildeOpen can be fine-tuned and deployed on-premises or in compliant cloud environments, enabling public administrations, companies, researchers, and other users to build custom AI solutions — such as multilingual assistants or translation tools — while ensuring data sovereignty and privacy in accordance with European standards.

column2: Flynn - first AI-art university student
column4: Flynn, a non-human student, has been admitted to the digital art programme at the University of Applied Arts Vienna after successfully completing the same application process as human applicants, including portfolio submission, interviews and an artistic suitability test. Developed using large-language models and open-source generative tools, Flynn now attends classes, produces artwork and receives feedback from professors alongside other students. The project explores how AI can act not just as a tool but as an active participant in artistic creation and education, raising new questions about authorship, creativity and the role of intelligent systems in academic environments.

column2: A comprehensive language model specifically trained on Luxembourg legislation
column4: Luxembourg is developing a large language model trained specifically on national legislation, with the intention of making the country’s legal framework easier to navigate for professionals and more comprehensible for citizens. The model is being adapted to the particularities of Luxembourgish law and is designed to support legal research, improve access to information and reduce the complexity of interacting with official texts.
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: AI in Education in Luxembourg
column4: Luxembourg is deploying artificial intelligence to support personalised learning in the education system, creating tools that help pupils learn more effectively while also teaching them how to use AI responsibly. The initiative seeks to provide tailored guidance to students, accelerate their progress and ensure that all applications respect strict data-protection standards so that children’s personal information remains secure.

column2: A high-performing multilingual model that includes Luxembourgish in both written and spoken form
column4: Luxembourg is building a multilingual language and speech model that includes Luxembourgish in both written and spoken form, in order to strengthen the presence of the national language in modern digital tools. The project involves collecting high-quality local audio sources to train the system so that it can recognise and generate Luxembourgish at a level comparable to widely supported languages such as English and French.

column2: MeluXina-AI: High-performance supercomputer to test AI applications on a large scale
column4: Luxembourg is set to build an AI-optimised supercomputer, MeluXina-AI, together with a national “AI Factory” to advance artificial intelligence development and research. The initiative, led by LuxProvide with support from national research and innovation partners, aims to strengthen the country’s position among Europe’s leading AI-ready nations. Half of the supercomputer’s capacity will be shared with the wider European network, while the remainder will support domestic research, innovation, and business use. Overall, the project is designed to attract talent, support companies and start-ups, and accelerate cutting-edge AI research in Luxembourg.

column2: Discoverer+ Supercomputer
column4: Discoverer is Bulgaria’s petascale supercomputer, hosted at Sofia Tech Park as part of the European High-Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC JU) infrastructure. Originally launched in October 2021, Discoverer delivers powerful high-performance computing with sustained performance of around 4.5 petaflops. In April 2025, the system underwent a major upgrade and was renamed Discoverer+, becoming the first EuroHPC supercomputer to receive such a substantial enhancement. The upgrade introduces new GPU partitioning, expanded memory and storage, and improved AI-oriented capabilities, significantly boosting performance for data-intensive scientific and industrial workloads such as neural network training, large-scale simulations, and artificial intelligence research. Discoverer+ continues to serve a broad community of researchers, businesses, and public-sector users, reinforcing Bulgaria’s and Europe’s leadership in high-performance and AI-enabled computing.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: LitAI - Lithuania AI Factory
column4: Lithuania is establishing a national Artificial Intelligence Factory, known as LitAI, which will be coordinated by Vilnius University. The initiative will create a comprehensive centre combining high-performance computing and AI-optimised infrastructure with data resources, research capabilities and technical expertise. It brings together academia, the public sector and industry to accelerate the development and adoption of practical AI solutions across areas such as public services, cybersecurity, personalised medicine, industrial automation, the green transition and transport. LitAI is intended to function as a one-stop national platform, offering organisations access to computing power, data environments and expert support so they can develop, test and deploy AI tools more effectively. The project represents a major investment in Lithuania’s digital and innovation ecosystem—estimated at around €130 million—and is expected to create more than one hundred high-skilled jobs while strengthening the country’s capacity to compete in Europe’s AI landscape.

column2: Dìgital Twin in the City of Vilnius
column4: Vilnius has deployed an advanced digital twin platform supported by AI, GIS and drone data to optimise city services and planning. The system uses drone imagery and AI algorithms to monitor infrastructure conditions, detect full waste bins, assess street cleanliness after weather events, and evaluate pavement and building conditions. The digital twin integrates multiple data sources, enabling planners to visualise and prioritise municipal services in real time, such as clearing snow quickly or identifying areas in need of maintenance based on AI‑derived maps. It also incorporates anonymised movement data to help with transport planning and uses multi‑layered 3D city models to support long‑term urban development and participatory engagement with residents. Across these functions, Vilnius’s approach illustrates how geospatial intelligence and real‑time digital modelling can improve operational efficiency and transparency in urban governance.

column2: DVPS ('Diversibus Viis Plurima Solvo') - creating a new generation of artificial intelligence models
column4: Translated is leading DVPS (‘Diversibus Viis Plurima Solvo’), an ambitious European project funded with EUR 29 million under Horizon Europe, aiming to develop a new generation of artificial intelligence models capable of multimodal learning, integrating text, images, audio, and sensory input to understand the real world. The project seeks to create interactive and contextual AI applicable to simultaneous translation in complex environments, medical diagnostics, disaster management, and environmental monitoring. The consortium includes 20 research partners and companies from nine European countries, with the operational centre at Pi Campus in Rome, where 70 researchers are working on the first results expected within the first year. DVPS also plans to raise an additional EUR 100 million for computational development. Translated emphasises that, in translation, developing a specialised large language model for a single European language is less valuable, whereas targeted specialisation offers strategic opportunities.

column2: Mantas – chatbot created by Lithuanian Post together with Tilde
column4: Mantas is an intelligent AI‑powered chatbot developed for Lithuanian Post in collaboration with the language technology company Tilde. Launched in April 2023, Mantas was designed to handle a substantial share of customer enquiries, especially around sending and receiving parcels, as part of a broader effort to improve online customer service. The chatbot uses natural language understanding to interpret and respond to freely formulated questions — even with stylistic or grammatical variations — helping to reduce the workload on human consultants by handling hundreds of queries per day (around 300–500) and aiming to take over about 50% of written customer requests. The implementation was reported as straightforward, with Tilde supporting both the technical setup and content adaptation, and the project continues to be developed to keep responses accurate as services and user needs evolve.

column2: VRK chatbot: AI-powered support during the elections in Lithuania
column4: The VRK AI Chatbot is an AI-powered virtual assistant developed by Tilde for the Central Electoral Commission of Lithuania (VRK). Designed to provide reliable and accessible support, the chatbot answers a wide range of election-related questions from voters, candidates, and election staff, operating around the clock to ensure timely assistance. It significantly reduces the workload on human staff by handling thousands of interactions, particularly during high-traffic periods such as presidential elections. The chatbot was carefully trained in close collaboration with the VRK team to ensure accuracy, security, and compliance with official electoral information. Beyond its core functionality of answering inquiries, the VRK AI Chatbot is structured to continuously improve, with potential future enhancements including support for multiple languages, expanded features, and more personalized user interactions. By offering instant access to election guidance, the chatbot plays a key role in enhancing transparency, accessibility, and efficiency in the electoral process.

column2: Next-level eLearning: AI Chatbot with LLM revolutionises academic communication and learning processes
column4: The Digne eLearning AI Chatbot is an advanced, AI‑powered virtual assistant developed by Tilde to transform academic communication and support within university courses. Integrated directly with the Moodle learning management system and equipped with large language model (LLM) capabilities, Digne provides students with personalised, context‑aware information about their course progress, grades, and study materials, while also helping academic staff streamline communication and administrative tasks. Unlike traditional chatbots with only predefined Q&A, Digne is trained on real course content such as lecture materials, presentations, and videos, enabling it to generate comprehensive responses to open‑ended student queries. By combining guided dialogues with AI‑generated answers, the chatbot enhances learning efficiency, assists with exam preparation, and improves overall interaction between students and educators as part of a broader digitalisation initiative in higher education.

column2: Rīgas namu pārvaldnieks’ Juris – the first chatbot in the property management field
column4: Rīgas namu pārvaldnieks’ Juris is an AI‑powered chatbot developed by Tilde in collaboration with Rīgas namu pārvaldnieks (RNP), Northern Europe’s largest apartment building management company, to modernize customer communication in the property management sector. Launched as the first chatbot of its kind in this field, Juris interacts 24/7 on RNP’s website, self‑service portal, and Facebook, handling frequent queries about community decisions, payments, ownership changes, emergency reports, and more—significantly reducing repetitive customer support workload. It understands both Latvian and English, can recognize logged‑in users to provide personalized responses, and even lets customers attach images when reporting issues. Developed to simplify support and enhance digital engagement, Juris has completed tens of thousands of conversations and exemplifies an innovative AI use case in property management.

column2: Sector 3 City Hall's AI-powered chat system
column4: Since 2023, the City Hall of Sector 3 in Bucharest has implemented an AI‑powered chat system on its official website (www.primarie3.ro), making it one of the first local administrations in Romania to adopt this kind of technology for citizen engagement. The system is designed to assist residents with information and administrative queries 24/7, offering quick responses to typical questions and helping streamline access to municipal services. The AI component is described by the City Hall as being in a learning phase, improving over time based on interactions with users. Sector 3 positioned this initiative as part of its digital modernization efforts to strengthen direct communication between local authorities and citizens.
column8: City Hall of Sector 3 in Bucharest

column2: Galicea Commune's Chatbot
column4: In Galicea Commune (Vâlcea County), an “AI Galicea” chatbot has been introduced by the local municipality to support citizens with information and queries related to public services. While the official municipal website does not prominently detail the chatbot itself, research on AI usage in Romanian local governments cites AI Galicea as an example of a chatbot implemented at the commune level, reflecting early adoption of AI technologies by smaller public administrations to improve governance and service delivery. This aligns with broader efforts in Romania to explore AI for enhancing public sector interactions as part of ongoing digital transformation strategies.

column2: AI4CCAM - Trustworthy AI for Connected, Cooperative and Automated Mobility
column4: AI4CCAM (Trustworthy AI for Connected, Cooperative and Automated Mobility) is a European Union‑funded research and innovation project under the Horizon Europe programme, officially launched in 2023 and running until 2026, with a total budget of approximately €5.97 million. The project is coordinated by Simula Research Laboratory in Norway. AI4CCAM brings together 14 partners from research, industry, and user associations across Europe to address the technical, ethical, social, and safety challenges of AI in Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility (CCAM). The project develops an open digital environment that integrates trustworthy-by-design AI models for predicting the behaviour of vulnerable road users and explaining trajectory predictions of automated vehicles. It also provides an online participatory space to engage stakeholders and the public in discussions about AI risks, biases, ethical dilemmas, and user acceptance in automated driving systems. In addition to technical work on predictive models and simulation scenarios, AI4CCAM emphasises Trustworthy AI principles such as transparency, explainability, bias mitigation, and ethical decision-making to support safer and socially responsible AI deployment in urban mobility.

column2: Transforming Public Services with AI in the Var Department
column4: The Var Department in southern France has implemented AI-driven tools to enhance public service efficiency and improve citizen engagement. The project uses chatbots, automated transcription, and AI-assisted workflows to streamline administrative processes, manage citizen requests more quickly, and reduce manual workload for staff. By leveraging AI, the department aims to deliver faster, more accurate, and more responsive public services, while freeing up human resources for complex decision-making and improving overall transparency and accountability in local governance.

column2: AI Chatbot in the City of Athens
column4: The City of Athens implemented an AI-powered search engine and digital agent on its official website to improve access to municipal services. Using Google Gemini, residents and visitors can ask questions about city services and receive instant answers via a 24/7 chatbot, making information more readily available without the need for direct human assistance. All queries, responses, and error logs are anonymised and securely stored in the cloud, allowing the system to continuously learn and improve over time. This initiative aims to make public-service information faster, more efficient, and user-friendly, while enhancing transparency, reducing administrative workload, and improving the overall experience for citizens and visitors alike.

column2: A chatbot to protect the identities of those reporting school bullying
column4: The project, led by Inria’s team Petscraft, aims to develop an AI-based chatbot that allows victims or witnesses of school bullying to report incidents while preserving their anonymity. The chatbot uses privacy-enhancing technologies to rewrite or filter reports so the identity of the reporter cannot be inferred, protecting against the risk that a large language model might deduce personal traits from the text. To ensure data privacy, the approach is first tested on public datasets before applying it to sensitive bullying reports. The long-term goal is to provide a secure, anonymising tool that encourages safe reporting while helping schools detect and address bullying more effectively.

column2: mAIgov - AI-powered digital assistant of Greece's unified public administration portal
column4: mAigov is an AI-powered digital assistant integrated into Greece's unified public administration portal, gov.gr. Launched in December 2023 by the Ministry of Digital Governance, mAigov aims to streamline citizens' interactions with public services by simplifying access to information and procedures.

column2: Use of AI in Business Intelligence System - Skrinja
column4: The government of Slovenia has launched Skrinja, a central data‑warehouse and business‑intelligence (BI) platform for public administration, designed to support data-driven decision making and improve transparency, efficiency, and governance. Skrinja collects data from multiple public-sector sources, pseudonymises personal data to comply with privacy rules, and enables agencies to create dashboards, visualisations, and automated reports on public-sector information. The platform allows authorities to replace manual Excel-based repo rting with real-time analytics, “what-if” scenario simulations, predictive analytics, and more accurate, consistent reporting from a single “source of truth.” AI is used within Skrinja to enhance analytics, providing predictive insights, anomaly detection, and automated scenario simulations, helping authorities forecast trends, detect patterns, and generate actionable insights from large datasets. This allows for more accurate, consistent, and real-time reporting from a single “source of truth.”
column11: Executive and legislative organs, financial and fiscal affairs, external affairs.

column2: AI Act Chatbot
column4: The AI Act Chatbot by Digital Austria is an online service developed to assist citizens, businesses, and public institutions in understanding and complying with the European AI Act. Launched in 2024, the chatbot provides detailed guidance on the regulations governing AI systems, including the different risk categories, compliance requirements, and the rights and obligations of developers and users under the law. Designed as an interactive and user-friendly tool, it translates complex legislative language into accessible explanations, enabling organisations to implement AI technologies responsibly and in accordance with European legal standards. The service also supports Austria’s broader digital governance strategy, which aims to foster transparency, ethical AI practices, and informed citizen engagement with emerging technologies. By providing immediate, practical advice and references, the AI Act Chatbot helps reduce the barriers to understanding and applying the EU AI regulatory framework, strengthening compliance, trust, and adoption of AI across both public and private sectors.

column2: agrifoodTEF - innovative AI and robotics applications for the Austrian agricultural and food industry
column4: The AgriFood TEF (Testing and Experimentation Facility) by Digital Austria is a national initiative designed to support the application of artificial intelligence in the agriculture and food sectors. The platform provides AI infrastructure, expert guidance, and testing environments to help farmers, agritech start-ups, researchers, and public institutions experiment with AI technologies responsibly. AgriFood TEF enables users to develop, test, and implement AI solutions for precision agriculture, supply chain optimisation, food quality monitoring, and sustainability practices. It also supports the integration of AI into existing workflows, promoting innovation, efficiency, and compliance with European AI regulations. By providing a practical, hands-on environment, the initiative strengthens Austria’s digital and AI capabilities in agri-food, while fostering collaboration between industry, research, and government stakeholders.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS) - AI for urban mobility
column4: The project Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS) in Malta is an initiative aimed at advancing the use of digital technologies, data-driven tools and automation to enhance the performance of modern transport networks. It focuses on identifying, supporting and monitoring national and regional policies that encourage the adoption of smart mobility solutions, including real-time traffic management, connected and autonomous vehicles, intelligent public-transport systems and digitally enabled infrastructure. By promoting these developments, the project seeks to help governments create transport systems that are safer, more efficient, more environmentally sustainable and better able to meet the mobility needs of growing populations.

column2: Virtual civil servant - Local public administration in Cluj
column4: The project Virtual Civil Servants explores how AI-powered assistants and digital tools can support public administrations — specifically helping civil servants perform tasks more efficiently, and improving how citizens interact with government services. It aims to automate routine administrative processes (such as handling requests, sorting and verifying documents, providing information), enabling faster, more accessible and responsive public-service delivery. By doing so, it seeks to relieve human civil servants of repetitive, time-consuming tasks so they can focus on more complex or value-adding duties — while making public services more efficient, transparent and user-friendly. The creation of this 'civil servant,' based on artificial intelligence, is part of the digitisation process of procedures in the local public administration in Cluj.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Virtual civil servant - Galati City Hall
column4: The project Virtual Civil Servants explores how AI-powered assistants and digital tools can support public administrations — specifically helping civil servants perform tasks more efficiently, and improving how citizens interact with government services. It aims to automate routine administrative processes (such as handling requests, sorting and verifying documents, providing information), enabling faster, more accessible and responsive public-service delivery. By doing so, it seeks to relieve human civil servants of repetitive, time-consuming tasks so they can focus on more complex or value-adding duties — while making public services more efficient, transparent and user-friendly. Within this project, Galați City Hall also launched a virtual civil servant based on artificial intelligence. The virtual assistant is currently being trained and tested but will soon be able to provide information to citizens on town planning, taxes, fees, and other local revenues.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Digitalised Yellow Book
column4: The Digitalised Yellow Book project aims to modernise and streamline the traditional “Yellow Book,” a paper-based register historically used by citizens to submit complaints, suggestions or feedback to public authorities. By converting this system into a fully digital platform, the project seeks to create a faster, more transparent and more accessible way for citizens to communicate with government services. The digital version improves the processing and evaluation of submissions by replacing manual handling with electronic workflows, which helps reduce delays and ensures greater consistency in how cases are reviewed. It also enhances the overall quality of public-service delivery by making it easier for authorities to track issues, respond more efficiently and analyse trends in citizen feedback. Through this initiative, governments aim to strengthen public trust, increase administrative efficiency and promote more open and responsive governance.

column2: AI Factories Latvia
column4: The project “AI Factories Latvia” is a national-level initiative aimed at boosting artificial intelligence innovation in Latvia. Its goal is to support startups and established companies in developing and commercialising new AI products and services — including developing large language models (LLMs) tailored to regional languages, as well as digital-twin applications for cyber-security and regional security simulations. As of 2025, the project is active and is part of a broader strategy to strengthen Latvia’s AI ecosystem, stimulate research and industry collaboration, and accelerate deployment of AI-driven solutions across sectors.
column8: Ministry of Economics of the Republic of Latvia
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI-based feed optimisation – real-time processing of large amounts of data in pig production
column4: The project aims to develop an advanced AI‑based system that collects and processes large amounts of data from pig production farms in real time, in order to continuously optimise the feeding strategies for growing pigs. By integrating data from feed suppliers, slaughterhouses and pig producers — including data on feed composition, growth rates, health status and mortality — the system adjusts feed formulations dynamically to match the optimal nutritional needs of pigs at different growth stages. The goal is twofold: improve productivity and reduce costs for producers, and at the same time lower the environmental footprint of pig production by optimising feed efficiency and reducing waste and emissions. The project is scheduled to run from January 2025 to December 2028 and is led by IQinAbox ApS in collaboration with Københavns Universitet.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: AI Assisted Process Understanding for Management of N2O Reduction from Wastewater Treatment Plants (AIAP-N2O)
column4: The project aims to reduce emissions of the greenhouse gas nitrous oxide (N₂O) from wastewater treatment plants by using AI‑driven process understanding and control strategies. It combines large‑scale data collection (including continuous N₂O measurements via sensors) with data analysis and AI to identify the conditions under which N₂O is produced, and to develop open‑source control strategies that wastewater plants can implement to minimise emissions. The goal is to make it easier for treatment plants to cut their N₂O output — improving environmental performance and contributing to more sustainable water and wastewater management.
column47: Consortium (research institutions, private sector and other PAs)

column2: Energy-efficient de-icing with camera detection and artificial intelligence
column4: “Energy‑efficient de‑icing with camera detection and artificial intelligence” is a Danish energy innovation project funded under the Energiteknologisk Udviklings‑ og Demonstrationsprogram (EUDP). It started in 2024 and is expected to run until 2026 in Høje‑Taastrup, Denmark. The project aims to develop an AI‑based control algorithm that uses camera and sensor data to detect actual ice formation on heat exchangers in ventilation systems, enabling de‑icing only when needed. Traditional de‑icing relies on external conditions like temperature or humidity, which can trigger unnecessary and energy‑intensive processes. By using AI and advanced detection, the system will reduce unnecessary de‑icing, cut energy consumption, lower operational costs, and improve overall system efficiency. The project brings together research and industry partners to design and demonstrate this intelligent, energy‑saving solution.
column8: Danish Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Utilities

column2: HabitAi - intelligent data to support sustainable decisions for homeowners
column4: HabitAi (“Intelligent data to support sustainable decisions for homeowners”) is a Danish environmental technology project funded in 2023 under the Miljøteknologisk Udviklings‑ og Demonstrationsprogram (MUDP) as a preliminary project. Its goal is to explore the technical feasibility of collecting, integrating, and structuring diverse building‑related data, including information from the Danish Building and Residence Register (BBR) and other property datasets, to make it usable for homeowners, construction professionals, and related sectors. The project employs artificial intelligence to clean, merge, and analyse these datasets, providing predictive and analytical insights on how a specific building can be maintained or renovated sustainably. AI-driven decision support considers factors such as construction characteristics, materials, building condition, climate impact, and life-cycle costs, helping users make data-driven, sustainability-oriented choices. HabitAi represents a first step toward enabling smart, AI-assisted decision-making in residential building sustainability.
column8: Danish Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Utilities

column2: Robotic technologies for the construction industry
column4: "Robotic removal of masonry joints for lifetime extension and deconstruction" is a Danish environmental technology project funded in 2023 under the Miljøteknologisk Udviklings‑ og Demonstrationsprogram (MUDP). The project aims to develop robotic systems capable of precisely and efficiently removing mortar joints in masonry structures.The initiative seeks to extend the lifespan of masonry buildings by enabling selective repair and renovation, while also facilitating the deconstruction and recycling of masonry materials in an environmentally responsible manner. By automating a traditionally manual and labour-intensive process, the project reduces labour costs and enhances safety. It integrates robotics, computer vision, and control algorithms to identify and remove mortar joints accurately, supporting efficient maintenance, sustainable renovation, and material reuse.
column8: Danish Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Utilities

column2: Supportbot for Vejle Municipality
column4: Supportbot for Vejle Municipality is an AI-powered voice assistant implemented by CleverCall to help the roughly 8,500 employees of Vejle Municipality with IT issues 24/7. The system answers phone calls outside normal support hours, provides clear guidance, sends links to detailed instructions and video tutorials, and continuously learns from daily queries to improve its responses. This ensures staff can quickly resolve routine IT problems (like forgotten passwords or peripheral malfunctions) at any time, freeing up the human support team to focus on more complex cases and reducing workflow disruptions.

column2: CostAI Kalundborg
column4: The project CostAI Kalundborg aims to gather and unify the various AI‑tools used by a municipality into a single, user‑friendly platform. The idea arose because staff at Kalundborg Kommune found it increasingly difficult to navigate the growing number of disparate AI solutions — chatbots, specialist assistants, translation tools, meeting‑minute automation and more. To solve this, the municipality partnered with a small Danish tech startup, Promte, to build an app that works on PC, tablet and mobile, serving as a central “AI‑hub” for municipal employees. Through the app staff will be able to access intranet chat, AI‑powered assistants trained on internal policies, automatic meeting transcription and minutes, translation support, and links to other administrative or digital‑service tools. The goal is to make it easier and safer for public‑sector employees to use AI by simplifying access, ensuring data stays within secure municipal boundaries, and giving all features through one consolidated entry point — rather than having multiple separate tools.

column2: Digital assistant "Mriya" for learning
column4: In 2024, Ukraine’s Ministry of Digital Transformation launched Mriya, a digital assistant specifically designed to support education across the country. The platform harnesses the power of artificial intelligence to assist students in their learning, offering personalised educational content tailored to individual needs, helping them to better understand complex subjects and improve their academic performance. In addition to supporting students, Mriya provides teachers and parents with tools to monitor progress, manage assignments, and facilitate more effective learning experiences. The platform was initially piloted in a select number of schools in 2024, allowing the Ministry to test its functionality, gather feedback, and refine its features. Following the successful pilot, Mriya was expanded nationwide in 2025, with thousands of schools across Ukraine integrating the system into their daily educational activities. The initiative forms part of a broader effort by the Ukrainian government to modernise the education system, incorporate digital technologies, and make learning more accessible and interactive for students of all ages.

column2: Brainscan Telemedicine - AI for brain diagnostics
